topics.title: The Submersible System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST634 - SUBMARINES AND SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Submersible System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Submersible** is defined by KTU naval architecture standards as a small, self-propelled, free-swimming, fully autonomous underwater vehicle (UUV) that operates independently of surface support during its submerged mission cycle, but typically relies on a surface support vessel (mothership) for transportation to and from the operational site, replenishment, and crew transfer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Distinction:** A *Submersible* is fundamentally different from a *Submarine*. A submarine is a large, long-endurance, fully independent warship capable of extended submerged operations and strategic deployment. A submersible is a compact, mission-specific underwater craft with limited endurance (typically **2 to 72 hours** of submerged operation) used for specialized deep-water tasks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box - "The Aquarium vs. The Ocean Liner"**
> Think of it this way: A **submarine** is like a luxury ocean liner - massive, carries thousands, crosses oceans alone, has its own power plant, kitchens, and entertainment. A **submersible** is like a deep-sea diving bell lowered from a ship - compact, mission-focused, supported from above, and built for precision rather than long voyages. The submersible is dropped into the water by a "mother ship," does its job, and is hauled back up.

## 1.3 Classification of Submersibles

| Class | Crew | Depth Rating | Primary Use |
|-------|------|--------------|-------------|
| **Lock-Out Submersible** | 2-3 | 600 m | Diver lockout operations |
| **Research Submersible** | 3-5 | 1,000 - 6,500 m | Scientific exploration |
| **Deep-Ocean Work Submersible** | 2-3 | 4,500 - 11,000 m | Pipeline/oil rig maintenance |
| **Manned Observation Submersible** | 2 | 300 - 1,000 m | Tourism, survey |
| **Military Special Ops** | 4-9 | 100 - 300 m | SEALS, reconnaissance |

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Seawater Density:** **ρ = 1,025 kg/m³**
- **Hydrostatic Pressure Gradient:** **1 atm ≈ 10.06 m of seawater (msw)**
- **Operating Depth Limit (Certified):** **6,500 m** (Trieste II class reference)
- **Absolute Depth Record (Manned):** **10,908 m** (Triton 36000/2, 2019)
- **Reserve Buoyancy Range:** **5% to 15%** of submerged displacement

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Submersible System

A submersible system is not a single vehicle but an **integrated operational complex** consisting of three primary components:

1. **The Submersible Vehicle (Wet & Dry Sections)**
2. **The Surface Support Vessel (Mothership)**
3. **The Launch and Recovery System (LARS)**

### 2.1.1 Logical Breakdown of Subsystems

- **Pressure Hull (Inner Hull):** The sealed spherical or cylindrical chamber that encloses the crew and payload, designed to withstand external hydrostatic pressure. Almost universally **spherical** because a sphere distributes stress uniformly and minimizes surface area per unit volume.
- **Outer Hull (Fairing):** A streamlined, non-pressure-bearing shell that provides hydrodynamic shape, mounts external equipment (thrusters, lights, sonar), and protects the pressure hull.
- **Ballast System:** Controls buoyancy by flooding/emptying seawater tanks.
  - *Variable Ballast Tanks (VBT)* — for fine trim
  - *Main Ballast Tanks (MBT)* — for surfacing/diving transitions
- **Propulsion System:** Typically electric motors driving ducted propellers or water-jet thrusters (silent operation).
- **Energy Storage:** High-capacity **lithium-ion batteries** or **silver-zinc batteries** for modern submersibles.
- **Life Support:** Oxygen supply, CO₂ scrubbers, humidity control, thermal management.
- **Navigation & Control:** Inertial Navigation System (INS), Doppler Velocity Log (DVL), sonar, pressure-depth sensors.
- **Communication:** Through-water acoustic modems, emergency transponders.

## 2.2 The Buoyancy Equation — Core Physics

> [!NOTE]
> **Archimedes' Principle Applied to Submersibles:**
> A submersible is in **static equilibrium** when submerged when its total weight equals the displaced water weight.

### The Four States of a Submersible:

1. **Positive Buoyancy** (Light) → Floats up (used when surfacing)
2. **Negative Buoyancy** (Heavy) → Sinks (used when diving)
3. **Neutral Buoyancy** (Trim) → Maintains depth (used during operation)
4. **Dynamic Equilibrium** → Maintains depth while moving (achieved by small thrust correction)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula No. | Equation | Description | Units |
|:-----------:|:--------:|:------------|:------|
| **F-1** | $\Delta = \rho \cdot V \cdot g$ | Archimedes Buoyant Force | Newtons (N) |
| **F-2** | $P_{ext} = P_{atm} + \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic Pressure at Depth | Pascals (Pa) |
| **F-3** | $W_{sub} = \Delta - F_{ballast}$ | Submerged Weight Condition | Newtons (N) |
| **F-4** | $RB = \Delta_{surf} - W_{sub}$ | Reserve Buoyancy | Newtons (N) |
| **F-5** | $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{t}$ | Hoop Stress in Cylindrical Hull | Pa |
| **F-6** | $\sigma_{sph} = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{2 \cdot t}$ | Membrane Stress in Spherical Hull | Pa |
| **F-7** | $t_{min} = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{\sigma_{allow}}$ | Minimum Wall Thickness | meters (m) |
| **F-8** | $C_{collapse} = \dfrac{2 \cdot E}{1 - \nu^2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^3$ | Elastic Collapse Pressure | Pa |
| **F-9** | $W_{payload} = W_{total} - W_{hull} - W_{systems}$ | Available Payload Weight | Newtons (N) |
| **F-10** | $SF = \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{hoop}}$ | Safety Factor (typically **1.5 to 2.5**) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** Notice that for the **same pressure (P)** and **same wall thickness (t)**, the spherical hull (F-6) has **half the membrane stress** of a cylindrical hull (F-5). This is the fundamental reason 95% of deep-sea submersibles use **spherical pressure hulls** — they are twice as efficient at resisting pressure collapse.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This physics governs the design of:
- **Bathyscaphes** (Trieste, Limiting Factor)
- **DSV Alvin** (4,500 m depth, used to locate Titanic wreckage)
- **Nuclear submarine hulls** (HY-80, HY-100 steel)
- **Offshore oil rig diving bells**
- **Tunnel boring machines** under high water pressure

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Buoyancy Equilibrium for Submerged Operation

### **Given Conditions:**
- Submersible operating at depth $h$ meters below sea surface.
- Total mass of submersible (with crew + systems) $= M_{total}$
- Volume of seawater displaced by pressure hull $= V_{displaced}$
- Seawater density $= \rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Gravitational acceleration $= g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

### **Step 1: Express the Total Weight of the Submersible**

The weight $W$ of the submerged submersible is given by:

$$W_{sub} = M_{total} \cdot g$$

This is the gravitational force pulling the submersible downward.

### **Step 2: Express the Buoyant Force (Archimedes' Principle)**

By Archimedes' Principle, the buoyant force equals the weight of the displaced seawater:

$$F_{buoy} = \rho \cdot V_{displaced} \cdot g$$

This force acts vertically upward.

### **Step 3: Apply the Static Equilibrium Condition**

For the submersible to maintain a steady depth (hovering), the net vertical force must equal zero:

$$F_{buoy} - W_{sub} = 0$$

Substituting the expressions from Step 1 and Step 2:

$$\rho \cdot V_{displaced} \cdot g - M_{total} \cdot g = 0$$

### **Step 4: Simplify the Equation**

Dividing both sides by $g$:

$$\rho \cdot V_{displaced} = M_{total}$$

This is the **neutral buoyancy condition**: the displaced water mass equals the total mass of the submersible.

### **Step 5: Introduce Reserve Buoyancy Concept**

In real engineering, the submersible carries a small ballast margin $M_{ballast}$ that can be jettisoned to achieve positive buoyancy in emergencies:

$$\rho \cdot V_{displaced} \cdot g = (M_{total} - M_{ballast}) \cdot g$$

Solving for the ballast mass required for neutral hovering:

$$M_{ballast} = M_{total} - \rho \cdot V_{displaced}$$

### **Step 6: Verify with Numerical Example**

> **Example:** A research submersible with a spherical pressure hull of inner radius $r = 1.1$ m operates at 2,000 m depth.

Compute displaced volume:

$$V = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \pi \cdot r^3 = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \pi \cdot (1.1)^3$$

$$V = 5.575 \text{ m}^3$$

Compute buoyant force at standard seawater density:

$$F_{buoy} = 1025 \cdot 5.575 \cdot 9.81$$

$$F_{buoy} = 56{,}063 \text{ N} \approx 56.06 \text{ kN}$$

For **neutral buoyancy**, the submersible weight must equal:

$$W_{sub} = 56.06 \text{ kN}$$

This corresponds to a mass of:

$$M_{total} = \frac{F_{buoy}}{g} = \frac{56{,}063}{9.81} \approx 5{,}714 \text{ kg} \approx 5.71 \text{ tonnes}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that for a sphere of only 1.1 m radius, the buoyant support is approximately **5.7 tonnes**. This explains why submersibles are made **compact and dense** — every kg of steel and equipment is "free" because the pressure hull naturally displaces enormous buoyant force.

## 3.2 Derivation: Hoop Stress vs. Spherical Membrane Stress

This is the **central engineering derivation** KTU examiners test most frequently.

### **Given:**
- Cylindrical pressure hull radius $= r$
- Wall thickness $= t$
- Internal operating pressure (or external differential) $= P$
- Yield strength of hull material $= \sigma_{y}$

### **Step 1: For the Cylindrical Hull — Free-Body Analysis**

Consider a half-cylinder of length $L$ cut along its longitudinal axis. The internal pressure acts on the projected area:

$$A_{proj} = 2 \cdot r \cdot L$$

The total force pushing the half-cylinders apart is:

$$F_{pressure} = P \cdot 2 \cdot r \cdot L$$

This force is resisted by **two** longitudinal weld seams (top and bottom):

$$F_{resist} = 2 \cdot \sigma_{hoop} \cdot t \cdot L$$

### **Step 2: Equate Pressure Force and Resistive Force**

$$P \cdot 2 \cdot r \cdot L = 2 \cdot \sigma_{hoop} \cdot t \cdot L$$

Canceling $2L$ from both sides:

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P \cdot r}{t}$$

This is **Formula F-5** from the cheat sheet.

### **Step 3: For the Spherical Hull — Repeat the Analysis**

For a hemisphere, the projected circular area is:

$$A_{proj} = \pi \cdot r^2$$

The pressure force pushing the hemisphere off is:

$$F_{pressure} = P \cdot \pi \cdot r^2$$

This is resisted by the entire hoop (circumferential) cut of the sphere:

$$F_{resist} = \sigma_{sph} \cdot 2 \cdot \pi \cdot r \cdot t$$

### **Step 4: Equate Forces for the Sphere**

$$P \cdot \pi \cdot r^2 = \sigma_{sph} \cdot 2 \cdot \pi \cdot r \cdot t$$

Canceling $\pi \cdot r$:

$$\sigma_{sph} = \frac{P \cdot r}{2 \cdot t}$$

This is **Formula F-6** from the cheat sheet.

### **Step 5: Direct Comparison**

Dividing the two stress equations:

$$\frac{\sigma_{hoop}}{\sigma_{sph}} = \frac{P \cdot r / t}{P \cdot r / (2t)} = 2$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma_{sph} = \frac{\sigma_{hoop}}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 2:1 Rule:** A spherical pressure hull carries **exactly half** the membrane stress of an equivalent cylindrical hull under the same pressure and wall thickness. This is the mathematical proof for why **spherical pressure hulls dominate submersible design** below 1,500 m depth.

### **Step 6: Minimum Wall Thickness Calculation**

Rearranging the spherical stress equation to find the minimum required thickness:

$$t_{min} = \frac{P \cdot r}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow}}$$

Applying a Safety Factor (SF):

$$t_{min} = \frac{SF \cdot P \cdot r}{2 \cdot \sigma_{yield}}$$

Where $SF = 1.5$ to $2.5$ for typical marine applications.

### **Step 7: Numerical Verification**

> **Example:** A submersible sphere of radius $r = 1.1$ m operating at 6,000 m depth. Hull material: Titanium Grade 5 ($\sigma_{y} = 880$ MPa). Compute $t_{min}$ for $SF = 2.0$.

External pressure at 6,000 m depth:

$$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h = 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 6000 = 60.34 \text{ MPa}$$

Substituting:

$$t_{min} = \frac{2.0 \cdot 60.34 \times 10^6 \cdot 1.1}{2 \cdot 880 \times 10^6}$$

$$t_{min} = \frac{132.75 \times 10^6}{1760 \times 10^6}$$

$$t_{min} = 0.0754 \text{ m} \approx 75.4 \text{ mm}$$

This matches real engineering: **DSV Limiting Factor** uses a 90 mm titanium sphere of radius ~1.1 m for 11,000 m operation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Block Diagram of a Submersible System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SurfaceVessel["SURFACE SUPPORT VESSEL - MOTHERSHIP"]
    LARS["LAUNCH AND RECOVERY SYSTEM - LARS"]
    Vehicle["SUBMERSIBLE VEHICLE"]
    
    SurfaceVessel --> LARS
    LARS --> Vehicle
    
    SurfaceVessel --> SubSupply["POWER SUPPLY AND CREW TRANSFER"]
    SubSupply --> Vehicle
    
    Vehicle --> PressureHull["PRESSURE HULL - SPHERICAL TITANIUM"]
    Vehicle --> OuterFairing["OUTER FAIRING - NON-PRESSURE HULL"]
    Vehicle --> BallastSys["BALLAST SYSTEM - VBT AND MBT"]
    Vehicle --> PropSys["PROPULSION - ELECTRIC THRUSTERS"]
    Vehicle --> EnergySys["ENERGY STORAGE - LITHIUM ION"]
    Vehicle --> LifeSupp["LIFE SUPPORT - O2 AND CO2 SCRUBBER"]
    Vehicle --> NavSys["NAVIGATION - INS DVL SONAR"]
    Vehicle --> CommsSys["COMMUNICATIONS - ACOUSTIC MODEM"]
    
    PressureHull --> Crew["CREW COMPARTMENT - 2 TO 5 PERSONS"]
    PressureHull --> Payload["SCIENTIFIC PAYLOAD - CAMERAS AND ARMS"]
    
    BallastSys --> TrimCtrl["TRIM AND BALLAST CONTROL UNIT"]
    PropSys --> TrimCtrl
    NavSys --> TrimCtrl
    
    TrimCtrl --> PilotDisplay["PILOT CONSOLE AND DISPLAY"]
    Crew --> PilotDisplay
```

## 4.2 Depth-Pressure Relationship Chart (ASCII Visualization)

```
   SEA SURFACE   0 m        1.0 ATM
       |
       |---  100 m ---   ~ 11.0 ATM  (Recreational scuba limit)
       |
       |--- 300 m ---    ~ 31.1 ATM  (Military Special Ops)
       |
       |--- 600 m ---    ~ 61.1 ATM  (Lock-out submersible ceiling)
       |
       |--- 1000 m ---   ~ 101.2 ATM (Deep research submersible)
       |
       |--- 4500 m ---   ~ 451.6 ATM (DSV Alvin class limit)
       |
       |--- 6500 m ---   ~ 651.5 ATM (Trieste II class limit)
       |
       |--- 11000 m ---  ~ 1100.7 ATM (Triton 36000-2 deepest)
       |
   ABYSSAL FLOOR
```

## 4.3 System-Level Mission Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Stage1["STAGE 1 - SURFACE PREP"] --> Stage2["STAGE 2 - LAUNCH VIA LARS"]
    Stage2 --> Stage3["STAGE 3 - WATER ENTRY AND DESCENT"]
    Stage3 --> Stage4["STAGE 4 - OPERATING DEPTH REACHED"]
    Stage4 --> Stage5["STAGE 5 - MISSION EXECUTION"]
    Stage5 --> Stage6["STAGE 6 - BALLAST DUMP ASCENT"]
    Stage6 --> Stage7["STAGE 7 - SURFACE RECOVERY"]
    
    Stage1 --> S1Detail["CHECK HULL INTEGRITY AND BALLAST"]
    Stage2 --> S2Detail["CRANE OR A-FRAME LOWERING"]
    Stage3 --> S3Detail["MBT FLOOD NEGATIVE BUOYANCY"]
    Stage4 --> S4Detail["ACHIEVE NEUTRAL BUOYANCY"]
    Stage5 --> S5Detail["SCIENCE OR INSPECTION TASK"]
    Stage6 --> S6Detail["VENT MBT RELEASE AIR"]
    Stage7 --> S7Detail["MOTHERSHIP RECOVERY"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between a Submarine and a Submersible in terms of size, endurance, and operational independence.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Submarine | Submersible |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| **Size** | Large warship (3,000-24,000 tonnes) | Compact (5-50 tonnes) |
| **Endurance** | Months submerged | Hours to few days |
| **Independence** | Fully self-sufficient at sea | Requires support vessel |
| **Crew** | 80-180 personnel | 2-5 personnel |
| **Mission** | Strategic / tactical | Scientific / inspection |
| **Range** | Trans-oceanic | Localized to deployment zone |

A **submersible** is mission-specific, support-dependent, and operates within a few kilometers of its mothership. A **submarine** is a sovereign naval platform capable of independent global deployment. *(3 marks: 1 for size, 1 for endurance, 1 for independence)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
**Why are spherical pressure hulls preferred over cylindrical hulls in deep-sea submersibles?**

**Model Answer:**

A spherical hull is preferred for three engineering reasons:

1. **Uniform Stress Distribution:** A sphere has no stress concentration points (no edges or corners). Membrane stress is uniform across the entire surface: $\sigma_{sph} = P \cdot r / (2t)$.

2. **Twice the Strength Efficiency:** For identical pressure ($P$), radius ($r$), and thickness ($t$), a sphere carries **half** the stress of a cylinder: $\sigma_{sph} = \sigma_{hoop} / 2$.

3. **Minimum Surface Area per Volume:** A sphere encloses the maximum volume for the minimum surface area, minimizing both material weight and the area exposed to crushing pressure.

For these reasons, all certified submersibles operating below **1,500 m** use spherical titanium or steel pressure hulls. *(3 marks: 1 mark each reason)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**A research submersible has a spherical titanium pressure hull of inner radius 1.05 m and wall thickness 75 mm. It operates in standard seawater at a depth of 4,500 m. The yield strength of titanium is 880 MPa. Compute:**
**(i) The hydrostatic pressure at operating depth.**
**(ii) The membrane stress in the spherical hull.**
**(iii) The factor of safety against yielding.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Hydrostatic Pressure at 4,500 m Depth:**

$$P = P_{atm} + \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

$$P = 101{,}325 + (1025)(9.81)(4500)$$

$$P = 101{,}325 + 45{,}256{,}125$$

$$P = 45{,}357{,}450 \text{ Pa} \approx 45.36 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Membrane Stress in the Spherical Hull:**

$$\sigma_{sph} = \frac{P \cdot r}{2 \cdot t}$$

$$\sigma_{sph} = \frac{45.36 \times 10^6 \cdot 1.05}{2 \cdot 0.075}$$

$$\sigma_{sph} = \frac{47.628 \times 10^6}{0.15}$$

$$\sigma_{sph} = 317.52 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iii) Factor of Safety:**

$$SF = \frac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{sph}} = \frac{880}{317.52}$$

$$SF = 2.77$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly forget to convert wall thickness from **mm to m** (75 mm = 0.075 m). This causes a 100x error in stress calculation, resulting in **3 marks lost instantly**. Always check unit consistency before substituting values. Also, students often confuse gauge pressure and absolute pressure — at 4,500 m depth, the atmospheric pressure (1 atm ≈ 0.1 MPa) is negligible compared to hydrostatic pressure and can be ignored for safety calculations, but must be mentioned.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**List and briefly explain the major subsystems of a submersible vehicle. Why is the launch and recovery system (LARS) considered critical to mission safety?**

**Model Answer:**

**Major Subsystems:**

1. **Pressure Hull** (3 marks covered): Sealed spherical chamber of titanium or high-strength steel that maintains 1 atm internal pressure regardless of external depth.

2. **Ballast System**: Variable ballast tanks (VBT) for fine trim and main ballast tanks (MBT) for dive/surface transition. Uses seawater flooding and compressed air venting.

3. **Propulsion**: Electric motors driving ducted propellers or thrusters. Silent operation is essential to avoid disturbing marine life and for stealth.

4. **Energy Storage**: Modern submersibles use lithium-ion battery banks rated for 50-100 kWh, providing 8-12 hours of operational endurance.

5. **Life Support**: Oxygen cylinders, lithium hydroxide CO₂ scrubbers, dehumidifiers, and thermal control to maintain habitable interior.

6. **Navigation**: INS + DVL + GPS (surface only) + sonar for positioning accuracy within 0.1% of distance traveled.

7. **Communication**: Through-water acoustic modem and emergency pinger beacon.

**Why LARS is Critical:**

The Launch and Recovery System is critical because:
- **Single Point of Failure:** If the crane, A-frame, or lifting cable fails during recovery, the submersible and crew are lost.
- **Sea-State Sensitivity:** Operations are typically limited to Sea State 4 or below. Higher waves cause dangerous vessel motion at the recovery hook.
- **Crew Safety:** The pilot cannot abandon the submersible mid-mission; LARS failure at depth is catastrophic.
- **Cost:** A damaged submersible can cost $50 million to replace.

*(7 marks: 4 for subsystems explanation, 3 for LARS reasoning)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
**Derive the expression for hoop stress in a thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel. Using the same method, derive the membrane stress in a thin-walled spherical shell. Show that for the same pressure, radius, and thickness, the spherical shell carries half the stress of the cylinder.**

**Model Solution:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** This derivation is covered in detail in **Section 3.2** of these notes. For your answer sheet, present the free-body diagram, the force balance equation, and the algebraic simplification. The final comparison step yields:

$$\frac{\sigma_{hoop}}{\sigma_{sph}} = 2 \Rightarrow \sigma_{sph} = \frac{\sigma_{hoop}}{2}$$

Marks distribution:
- Cylinder FBD setup: 2 marks
- Spherical FBD setup: 2 marks
- Derivation of both equations: 2 marks
- Final comparison: 1 mark

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
**A submersible of total mass 8,500 kg is to be operated at 3,000 m depth in seawater (ρ = 1,025 kg/m³). The pressure hull is spherical with an outer radius of 1.2 m. Assuming the hull material occupies negligible volume and the internal volume is fully flooded with air at 1 atm, compute:**
**(i) The buoyant force.**
**(ii) The mass of ballast water required for neutral buoyancy.**
**(iii) The minimum wall thickness if yield strength is 700 MPa and safety factor is 2.5.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Buoyant Force:**

Volume displaced:

$$V = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 = \frac{4}{3} \pi (1.2)^3 = 7.238 \text{ m}^3$$

Buoyant force:

$$F_{buoy} = \rho V g = 1025 \cdot 7.238 \cdot 9.81 = 72{,}793 \text{ N} \approx 72.79 \text{ kN}$$

**(ii) Mass of Ballast for Neutral Buoyancy:**

Buoyant mass equivalent:

$$M_{buoy} = \frac{F_{buoy}}{g} = \frac{72{,}793}{9.81} = 7{,}420 \text{ kg}$$

Required ballast mass:

$$M_{ballast} = M_{total} - M_{buoy} = 8500 - 7420 = 1{,}080 \text{ kg}$$

**(iii) Minimum Wall Thickness:**

Pressure at 3,000 m:

$$P = 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 3000 = 30{,}165{,}750 \text{ Pa} \approx 30.17 \text{ MPa}$$

Minimum thickness with safety factor:

$$t_{min} = \frac{SF \cdot P \cdot r}{2 \cdot \sigma_{yield}} = \frac{2.5 \cdot 30.17 \times 10^6 \cdot 1.2}{2 \cdot 700 \times 10^6}$$

$$t_{min} = \frac{90.51 \times 10^6}{1{,}400 \times 10^6} = 0.0647 \text{ m} \approx 64.7 \text{ mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** In Part (ii), students often incorrectly use the **inner radius** instead of the **outer radius** for volume calculation, or they confuse **mass of ballast water** with **volume of ballast water**. The answer must be in **kg** because ballast is achieved by flooding seawater tanks. Also, do not forget to add $g$ in $F_{buoy}$ but remember to divide by $g$ when computing equivalent mass.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for The Submersible System**

- A **submersible** is support-dependent, compact, and mission-specific; a **submarine** is independent and strategic.
- Submersible system has **3 main components**: Vehicle + Mothership + LARS.
- **Archimedes' Principle** governs buoyancy: $F_{buoy} = \rho V g$.
- **Four buoyancy states**: Positive, Negative, Neutral, Dynamic.
- **Pressure at depth** increases by **1 atm per 10.06 m** of seawater.
- **Spherical hulls are 2× stronger** than cylinders at the same $P$, $r$, and $t$.
- **Hoop stress formula**: $\sigma_{hoop} = P \cdot r / t$ (cylinder).
- **Spherical membrane stress**: $\sigma_{sph} = P \cdot r / (2t)$.
- **Minimum wall thickness**: $t_{min} = SF \cdot P \cdot r / (2 \cdot \sigma_y)$.
- **Typical Safety Factor** in submersible design: **1.5 to 2.5**.
- **Seawater density**: $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (not 1000 — KTU penalizes this error).
- **Reserve buoyancy** = $5\%-15\%$ of submerged displacement.
- **Spheres** dominate below 1,500 m depth; cylinders acceptable above.
- **Energy storage** modern standard: lithium-ion (50-100 kWh).
- **LARS** is a **single-point-of-failure** in mission safety.
- **Sea State limit** for recovery: typically **Sea State 4** or lower.
- **Triton 36000/2** holds the **manned depth record: 10,908 m** (2019, Five Deeps Expedition).
- **DSV Alvin** (4,500 m) is the most-deployed research submersible globally.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Design of Manned Submersibles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST634 - SUBMARINES AND SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Design of Manned Submersibles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Manned Submersible** is a free-swimming, self-propelled, underwater vehicle that carries one or more human occupants within a **pressure-tight hull** to perform observations, scientific research, military missions, or industrial work at depths beyond the reach of conventional divers. Unlike submarines, submersibles are typically small, support limited endurance (hours to a few days), and operate at greater depths, often relying on a surface support vessel (mother ship) for power, navigation, and life-support logistics.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard):**
> A *manned submersible* is a compact, free-flooding (externally) or dry-hulled (internally) underwater vehicle designed to transport humans safely to a specified operating depth, returning them to the surface with full mission integrity, redundancy in life-critical systems, and compliance with classification-society rules (e.g., DNV, ABS, IRS).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **scuba diver inside a titanium egg** that is dropped into the ocean. The egg must withstand the crushing weight of water above it. The diver breathes air inside the egg, looks out through thick acrylic windows, and uses small thrusters to move around. The egg is connected by an umbilical to a support ship on the surface — much like a baby in a stroller (the ship) being pushed around, but the baby (the crew) is doing the exploring.

- The **water pressure** at depth is the enemy. Every **10 metres** of seawater adds approximately **1 atmosphere (1 bar)** of pressure.
- The hull is the **only barrier** between life and the abyss.
- The crew lives in a **dry bubble** while the rest of the vehicle may be flooded with seawater (free-flooding).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exam:**
> - **Submarine** → surface ship that submerges, large crew, days/weeks endurance, self-sufficient.
> - **Submersible** → small crew (1–6 typical), hours endurance, surface-dependent, dives deeper.
> - **Manned** → carries humans. **Unmanned (ROV/AUV)** → remotely or autonomously operated, no humans inside.

### 1.3 Design Envelope — Standard Metrics (Bold Constants)

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Depth | **300 m – 6,500 m** (bathyscaphes) | Defines pressure hull design |
| Crew Size | **1 – 6 personnel** | Determines habitat volume |
| Endurance | **8 – 72 hours** | Battery / life-support capacity |
| Hull Material | **Ti-6Al-4V, HY-80/100 steel, Acrylic** | Strength-to-weight ratio |
| Reserve Buoyancy | **≈ 10–20 %** | Margin against flooding |
| Safety Factor | **1.5 – 2.0** on yield | Class-society requirement |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Exam:** "**D-D-H-H-E-M**" → **D**epth, **D**isplacement, **H**ull, **H**abitat, **E**ndurance, **M**anoeuvrability. These six letters summarise the design compass of any manned submersible.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Design Philosophy — Seven Pillars of Manned Submersible Design

The basic design of a manned submersible revolves around **seven interdependent engineering pillars**. Each pillar constrains the others; trade-offs are the essence of the design process.

**Pillar 1 — Mission Requirement Definition**
- Define the **depth (m)**, **bottom time (h)**, **payload (kg)**, **crew size**, and **geographic operating region**.
- This sets the boundary conditions for *all* downstream calculations.

**Pillar 2 — Human Occupancy & Life Support**
- Minimum habitable volume per person: **~ 1.5 m³** (seated) and **~ 3.0 m³** (standing/reclined) per KTU/ABS guidelines.
- Atmosphere control: maintain **O₂ ≈ 21 %**, **CO₂ < 0.5 %**, **humidity 40–60 %**, **T ≈ 18–24 °C**.
- Emergency Oxygen Supply: minimum **96 person-hours** reserve.

**Pillar 3 — Pressure Hull Geometry**
- Two canonical shapes:
  - **Sphere** → optimal stress resistance (uniform membrane stress), but interior volume is limited.
  - **Cylinder with hemispherical end caps** → maximises interior volume, but end-cap transitions are stress concentrators.
- The **sphere-to-cylinder ratio** is a fundamental design trade-off. Famous example: *DSV Alvin* uses a hemispherical-ended cylinder; *Trieste* uses a sphere.

**Pillar 4 — Buoyancy, Stability & Trim**
- Submersibles are designed to be **slightly positively buoyant** at the surface and **slightly negatively buoyant** submerged — using a variable ballast system to neutralise.
- **Reserve buoyancy** (freeboard above waterline at surface) = **10–20 %** of total displacement.

**Pillar 5 — Power & Propulsion**
- Power sources: **Lead-acid / Li-ion batteries** (typical), or surface-supplied via umbilical.
- Thrusters: typically **4–6** (main longitudinal, lateral, vertical) for full 6-DOF control.

**Pillar 6 — Viewports, Lighting & Sensors**
- Viewports: **conical acrylic (PMMA) or sapphire** windows — must withstand differential pressure.
- Lighting: high-intensity LED or HID lamps, often flood + spot.
- Sonar, cameras, manipulators.

**Pillar 7 — Safety, Redundancy & Recovery**
- **Two-factor safety**: pressure hull is engineered to **1.5×** design pressure.
- Emergency jettison of ballast weights for surfacing.
- Emergency life-support: **96-hour O₂**, **CO₂ scrubber backup**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Statement:** The "**Iron Triangle**" of submersible design is **Depth × Endurance × Payload** — improving any one degrades at least one of the others, given a fixed displacement.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table collects every equation you will be expected to recognise or apply for this topic in the ESE.

| # | Formula | Description / Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \rho \, g \, h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth *h* | Pa (ρ in kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s²) |
| 2 | $P_{design} = 1.5 \times P_{op}$ | Design pressure with safety factor 1.5 | Pa |
| 3 | $\sigma_{sphere} = \dfrac{P \, r}{2 \, t}$ | Membrane (hoop) stress in thin spherical shell | Pa |
| 4 | $\sigma_{cylinder} = \dfrac{P \, r}{t}$ | Hoop stress in thin cylindrical shell | Pa |
| 5 | $\sigma_{long} = \dfrac{P \, r}{2 \, t}$ | Longitudinal (axial) stress in cylinder | Pa |
| 6 | $\sigma_{eq,\,von\,Mises} = \sqrt{\sigma_h^2 + \sigma_l^2 - \sigma_h \sigma_l}$ | Von-Mises equivalent stress (2-D) | Pa |
| 7 | $V_{sphere} = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3$ | Volume of spherical pressure hull | m³ |
| 8 | $V_{cyl} = \pi r^2 L$ | Volume of cylindrical section | m³ |
| 9 | $F_B = \rho_w \, g \, V_{disp}$ | Buoyancy force (Archimedes) | N |
| 10 | $W = m \, g$ | Weight of vehicle | N |
| 11 | $R_B = F_B - W$ | Reserve buoyancy (must be $\geq 0$ at surface) | N |
| 12 | $GM = \dfrac{I_T}{V_{disp}} - KB + BM$ | Metacentric height (transverse stability) | m |
| 13 | $t_{min,\,sphere} = \dfrac{P \, r}{2 \, \sigma_{allow}}$ | Minimum wall thickness for sphere | m |
| 14 | $t_{min,\,cyl} = \dfrac{P \, r}{\sigma_{allow}}$ | Minimum wall thickness for cylinder | m |
| 15 | $\dot{V}_{O_2} = 0.5 \, L/min/person$ | Crew oxygen consumption rate | L/min |
| 16 | $\dot{V}_{CO_2} = 0.4 \, L/min/person$ | Crew CO₂ production rate | L/min |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Trap:** All absolute-value bars in formulas above use `$\vert$` or `$\mid$` to remain safe inside markdown tables. Do **not** write raw `|P|` inside a table cell — it breaks the KTU PDF renderer.

### 2.3 Why Sphere Beats Cylinder — The Geometric Insight

For the *same radius, wall thickness, and material*, a sphere has **half the membrane stress** of a cylinder. This is why the deepest-diving manned vehicles (*Trieste II*, *Limiting Factor*, *Deepsea Challenger*) all use **spherical pressure hulls**.

The "why" is purely geometric: a closed spherical surface distributes internal pressure as a **uniform biaxial tension** (no bending moment), while a cylinder has a **circumferential stress concentration** at the meridian. The 2:1 stress ratio is therefore *intrinsic* to geometry, not material.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** The sphere wins on **strength** and **volume efficiency** for very deep dives, but it loses on **interior ergonomics** — you cannot lie down or stand upright comfortably inside a sphere. Hence the modern hybrid: **spherical crew capsule + cylindrical equipment bay** (e.g., *Limiting Factor*, *Shinkai 6500*).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> **Reading Guide:** Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No steps are skipped. The "logic" line beneath each equation tells you *why* we did what we did.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure at Operating Depth

**Problem Statement:** A manned submersible is rated for an operating depth of **2,000 m**. Determine the external hydrostatic pressure acting on its pressure hull. Take seawater density as **1,025 kg/m³** and gravitational acceleration as **9.81 m/s²**.

**Step 1.** Write down the standard hydrostatic equation.

$$
P = \rho \, g \, h
$$

*Logic:* This is the pressure exerted by a fluid column of height *h* under gravity. It assumes incompressible seawater (valid for *h* < 6,000 m within 2 %).

**Step 2.** Substitute the numerical values.

$$
P = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 2000
$$

*Logic:* We are plugging the depth (2000 m), density (seawater), and g.

**Step 3.** Compute the product step-by-step.

$$
1025 \times 9.81 = 10055.25
$$

*Logic:* Density times gravity.

$$
10055.25 \times 2000 = 20110500
$$

*Logic:* Multiply by depth.

**Step 4.** Express the result in engineering units.

$$
P \approx 20.11 \times 10^6 \, Pa = 20.11 \, MPa
$$

*Logic:* Convert to megapascals for readability.

**Step 5.** Convert to atmospheres for intuition.

$$
P = \dfrac{20.11 \, MPa}{0.101325 \, MPa/atm} \approx 198.5 \, atm
$$

*Logic:* 1 atm ≈ 0.101325 MPa. So at 2000 m, the submersible experiences almost 200 atmospheres of pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Pressure:** With a KTU/ABS safety factor of 1.5, the design pressure becomes **P_design = 1.5 × 20.11 ≈ 30.16 MPa**. The hull must survive this in a pressure test *without* yielding.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Wall Thickness of a Spherical Pressure Hull

**Problem Statement:** A spherical titanium pressure hull has an **outer radius r = 1.1 m** and operates at **2,000 m** depth. The allowable stress for Ti-6Al-4V is **σ_allow = 550 MPa**. Determine the minimum wall thickness.

**Step 1.** Start from the thin-shell membrane-stress equation for a sphere (derived from a force balance on a hemisphere — covered in Strength of Materials).

$$
\sigma_{sphere} = \dfrac{P \, r}{2 \, t}
$$

*Logic:* Cutting a sphere in half and balancing internal pressure against the projected circular area gives this result. The factor of 2 in the denominator is what makes the sphere stronger.

**Step 2.** Set the membrane stress equal to the allowable stress and solve for *t*.

$$
\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{P \, r}{2 \, t_{min}}
$$

*Logic:* We require the actual stress to be at most equal to the allowable (yield) stress.

**Step 3.** Cross-multiply.

$$
t_{min} \cdot \sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{P \, r}{2}
$$

*Logic:* Standard algebraic rearrangement.

**Step 4.** Isolate *t_min*.

$$
t_{min} = \dfrac{P \, r}{2 \, \sigma_{allow}}
$$

*Logic:* Divide both sides by σ_allow.

**Step 5.** Insert the *design* pressure (with safety factor 1.5).

$$
P_{design} = 1.5 \times 20.11 \, MPa = 30.165 \, MPa
$$

*Logic:* KTU / class rule: design pressure = 1.5 × operating pressure.

**Step 6.** Substitute numbers.

$$
t_{min} = \dfrac{30.165 \times 10^6 \times 1.1}{2 \times 550 \times 10^6}
$$

*Logic:* Plug values. Note both numerator and denominator carry 10⁶ factors.

**Step 7.** Simplify step-by-step.

$$
\text{Numerator} = 33.1815 \times 10^6
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 1100 \times 10^6 = 1.1 \times 10^9
$$

*Logic:* Compute both halves separately.

**Step 8.** Perform the division.

$$
t_{min} = \dfrac{33.1815 \times 10^6}{1.1 \times 10^9} = \dfrac{33.1815}{1100} \, m
$$

*Logic:* Combine 10⁶/10⁹ = 10⁻³, but cancel via direct numerical division.

**Step 9.** Final numerical value.

$$
t_{min} \approx 0.03017 \, m = 30.17 \, mm
$$

*Logic:* So a titanium sphere for 2,000 m needs roughly a 30 mm wall.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** A steel hull of the same radius and depth would have *similar* thickness (since steel and titanium have similar yield strengths, ~550–800 MPa) but weigh ~1.8× more. This is why titanium is preferred for deep-diving manned submersibles.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reserve Buoyancy for a Manned Submersible

**Problem Statement:** A submersible displaces **V = 18 m³** of seawater at the surface. Its total mass (hull + crew + equipment) is **M = 17,500 kg**. Compute the reserve buoyancy and comment on safety.

**Step 1.** Compute the buoyancy force (Archimedes' principle).

$$
F_B = \rho_w \, g \, V_{disp}
$$

*Logic:* The displaced water weight is the buoyancy force.

**Step 2.** Substitute values.

$$
F_B = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 18
$$

*Logic:* Density of seawater × g × displaced volume.

**Step 3.** Stepwise multiplication.

$$
1025 \times 9.81 = 10055.25
$$

$$
10055.25 \times 18 = 180994.5 \, N
$$

*Logic:* Compute the product.

$$
F_B \approx 181.0 \, kN
$$

*Logic:* Express in kilonewtons.

**Step 4.** Compute the weight of the submersible.

$$
W = M \, g = 17500 \times 9.81 = 171675 \, N \approx 171.7 \, kN
$$

*Logic:* Weight is mass times g.

**Step 5.** Compute reserve buoyancy.

$$
R_B = F_B - W = 181.0 - 171.7 = 9.3 \, kN
$$

*Logic:* Reserve buoyancy is the *excess* of buoyancy over weight at the surface.

**Step 6.** Express as a percentage of total displacement weight.

$$
R_B \, (\%) = \dfrac{R_B}{F_B} \times 100 = \dfrac{9.3}{181.0} \times 100 \approx 5.14 \, \%
$$

*Logic:* Reserve buoyancy is conventionally expressed as a percentage of displacement.

**Step 7.** Interpret the result.

$$
R_B = 5.14\% < 10\% \, \text{(KTU minimum recommended)}
$$

*Logic:* A 5 % reserve is on the *low* side; designers typically aim for 10–20 % to provide margin against a small inboard leak or fuel burn-off.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Students often compute $F_B$ but forget to subtract $W$ to get $R_B$, and instead report $F_B$ as "reserve buoyancy." This loses 2 marks. Always compute $R_B = F_B - W$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Crew Oxygen Endurance

**Problem Statement:** A 3-person submersible carries an emergency oxygen cylinder of **V_O₂ = 240 L** at 200 bar. The emergency supply valve delivers gas at ambient pressure. Each crew member consumes **0.5 L/min** (at STP). How long will the emergency O₂ last?

**Step 1.** Compute the actual gas volume available (at atmospheric pressure).

$$
V_{usable} = V_{O_2} \times P_{cyl} = 240 \times 200 = 48{,}000 \, L
$$

*Logic:* By Boyle's law, the gas expands to 48 m³ at 1 atm.

**Step 2.** Compute the total consumption rate for 3 crew.

$$
\dot{V}_{total} = 3 \times 0.5 = 1.5 \, L/min
$$

*Logic:* Each person consumes 0.5 L/min; three people consume 1.5 L/min.

**Step 3.** Compute the endurance.

$$
t = \dfrac{V_{usable}}{\dot{V}_{total}} = \dfrac{48000}{1.5} = 32{,}000 \, min
$$

*Logic:* Endurance = available volume ÷ consumption rate.

**Step 4.** Convert to hours.

$$
t = \dfrac{32{,}000}{60} \approx 533.3 \, h
$$

*Logic:* Divide by 60 to get hours.

**Step 5.** Compare to KTU/ABS minimum requirement.

$$
t_{available} = 533.3 \, h \gg t_{req} = 96 \, h
$$

*Logic:* The 96-hour requirement is satisfied by a wide margin. The submersible can survive a 22-day worst-case rescue scenario with this cylinder, well above the regulatory minimum.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sphere vs Cylinder

| Criterion | Sphere | Cylinder | Winner for Manned Submersible |
|---|---|---|---|
| Membrane stress | $P r / (2 t)$ | $P r / t$ | **Sphere (½ stress)** |
| Volume per unit surface area | High | Higher | **Cylinder (volume)** |
| Ergonomic interior | Poor (no flat floor) | Excellent | **Cylinder** |
| Manufacturing complexity | High (deep drawing / forging) | Moderate | **Cylinder** |
| Penetration openings (hatches) | Difficult | Easier | **Cylinder** |
| Weight efficiency | Excellent (less material) | Moderate | **Sphere** |
| Depth suitability | **> 4,000 m** | **< 2,000 m** | **Depends on depth** |

> [!TIP]
> **Design Heuristic:** If depth > 3,000 m → choose **sphere** (or near-sphere). If depth < 1,500 m → **cylinder with hemispherical end caps** is optimal. The crossover point is governed by the strength-to-volume trade-off.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Manned Submersible

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[External Hull Shell Free Flooding] --> B[Pressure Hull Crew Capsule Sphere or Cylinder]
    B --> C1[Life Support Subsystem]
    B --> C2[Power and Propulsion Subsystem]
    B --> C3[Navigation and Control Subsystem]
    B --> C4[Payload and Sensor Subsystem]
    B --> C5[Emergency Recovery Subsystem]

    C1 --> D1[Oxygen Cylinders and Regulators]
    C1 --> D2[CO2 Scrubber LiOH or Soda Lime]
    C1 --> D3[Humidity and Temperature Control]
    C1 --> D4[Atmosphere Monitors O2 CO2 Pressure]

    C2 --> E1[Main Battery Bank Li-ion or Lead Acid]
    C2 --> E2[Main Thruster Longitudinal]
    C2 --> E3[Lateral Thrusters Port and Starboard]
    C2 --> E4[Vertical Thruster Bow and stern]

    C3 --> F1[Inertial Navigation System INS]
    C3 --> F2[Depth Sensor and Sonar Altimeter]
    C3 --> F3[Surface Acoustic Modem]
    C3 --> F4[Joystick and Control Console]

    C4 --> G1[High Intensity LED Floodlights]
    C4 --> G2[HD and 4K Cameras]
    C4 --> G3[Robotic Manipulator Arm]
    C4 --> G4[Conical Acrylic Viewport]

    C5 --> H1[Emergency O2 Cylinder 96 hour]
    C5 --> H2[Jettisonable Ballast Weights]
    C5 --> H3[Acoustic Pinger and Flare]
    C5 --> H4[Redundant Battery Bank]

    B --> I[External Skin Syntactic Foam or Buoyancy Blocks]
    I --> J[Surface Support Vessel Umbilical Link]
```

*Logic of the diagram:* The **Pressure Hull (Crew Capsule)** is the central element. Five major subsystems branch outward, each decomposed into 4 component sub-units. The skin provides external buoyancy, and the umbilical provides a surface link. This is a **functional architecture flow** rather than a physical drawing, which is more useful for design-engineering documentation.

### 4.2 Mermaid Decision Flow — Choosing Hull Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start Design Problem] --> B{Operating Depth}
    B --> C{Depth greater than 3000 m}
    C -->|Yes| D[Choose Spherical Pressure Hull]
    C -->|No| E{Crew Size greater than 3}
    E -->|Yes| F[Choose Cylinder with Hemispherical Endcaps]
    E -->|No| G{Volume and Ergonomics Priority}
    G -->|Yes| F
    G -->|No| D
    D --> H[Final Design Verification via FEA]
    F --> H
```

*Logic:* The decision flow mirrors the trade-off matrix in Section 3.5. Deep diving → sphere. Shallow diving with crew comfort → cylinder. Both paths converge to FEA-based verification.

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Flow — Typical Dive Cycle of a Manned Submersible

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Surface Preparation and Hatch Closure] --> P2[Pre Dive Checks Pressure and Power]
    P2 --> P3[Variable Ballast Flooded Submerges]
    P3 --> P4[Descent Phase Thrusters Active]
    P4 --> P5[Bottom Phase Hover at Depth 2 to 4 h]
    P5 --> P6[Ascent Phase Drop Ballast Weights]
    P6 --> P7[Surface Phase Hatch Opens]
    P7 --> P8[Post Dive Servicing and Crew Debrief]
```

*Logic:* The dive cycle is *ballast-dominated*: at the surface the variable ballast tanks are empty (vehicle positively buoyant); flooding them induces negative buoyancy to descend. For emergency surfacing, *fixed* ballast weights are jettisoned.
