## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Distinguish between a manned submersible and a submarine, listing at least three points of difference.*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Manned Submersible | Submarine |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Small crew (1–6); short endurance (hours) | Large crew (50+); weeks of endurance |
| 2 | Surface-dependent (umbilical / mother ship) | Self-sufficient, surface-independent |
| 3 | Depth range up to **11,000 m** (e.g., *Limiting Factor*) | Typically limited to ~ 500–1,000 m |
| 4 | Compact pressure hull, often single sphere | Multi-compartment, double-hull in modern designs |
| 5 | Civilian / research / military survey | Military strategic platforms |

**Keywording required:** crew size, endurance, depth, surface-dependence, hull layout. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define "reserve buoyancy" of a manned submersible and state the typical KTU/ABS recommended range.*

**Model Answer:**
**Reserve buoyancy** $R_B$ is the *excess* of the buoyancy force over the weight of the submersible at the surface.

$$
R_B = F_B - W = \rho_w \, g \, V_{disp} - M g
$$

It is typically expressed as a **percentage of displacement** and is recommended to be in the range **10 % – 20 %**. Below 10 % the vehicle becomes too "touchy" in surface handling; above 20 % it becomes too buoyant and may surface unintentionally. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern (ESE):** Each Part-B question is 14 marks and offers an *internal choice*. You are given *either* Question A *or* Question B and must answer *one* of them. Each carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each. We model both alternatives below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** *(7 marks)* With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **seven major design considerations** for a manned submersible intended for **3,000 m** depth operation with a **3-person crew** and **24-hour endurance**.

**Model Solution:**

A manned submersible for 3,000 m / 3 crew / 24 h is a *mid-depth* design class. The seven design pillars are:

1. **Mission profile** → 3,000 m operating depth implies external pressure ≈ 30 MPa, so the pressure hull must be **Ti-6Al-4V spherical** with wall thickness ≈ 45 mm.
2. **Crew habitat** → Minimum 1.5 m³ per person seated → ≥ **4.5 m³ habitable volume**, requiring sphere radius *r* ≥ 1.03 m.
3. **Life support** → 24-hour duration → 3 × 0.5 L/min × 60 × 24 ≈ **2,160 L O₂** at STP; for 96-hour emergency reserve, 8,640 L. Stored in 200-bar cylinders (~50 L each).
4. **Power and propulsion** → Li-ion battery bank of ≈ 100–150 kWh; six thrusters for 6-DOF control.
5. **Buoyancy and trim** → Variable ballast tank + jettisonable solid ballast (≈ 5 % of displacement) for emergency surfacing.
6. **Viewing and sensing** → Conical acrylic viewports (PMMA, half-angle ≈ 22°) of 100–150 mm thickness; high-intensity LED floods; HD cameras; manipulator arm.
7. **Safety and class compliance** → Safety factor 1.5; 96-hour emergency O₂; DNV / ABS / IRS classification; pressure tested to **1.5 × design** and proof tested to **1.25 × design**.

*(Sketch: free-body diagram of submersible with pressure hull, ballast tank, thrusters, and viewports labelled.)* — 7 marks distributed: 1 per pillar + 1 for sketch.

---

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Compute the **minimum wall thickness** of a spherical titanium hull of **outer radius r = 1.2 m** rated for **3,000 m** depth. Take allowable stress σ_allow = **550 MPa**, density of seawater = **1,025 kg/m³**, g = **9.81 m/s²**, and design safety factor = **1.5**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1.** Hydrostatic pressure.

$$
P = \rho g h = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 3000 = 30.166 \, MPa
$$

*Logic:* Hydrostatic equation at 3,000 m.

**Step 2.** Design pressure (with safety factor).

$$
P_{design} = 1.5 \times 30.166 = 45.25 \, MPa
$$

*Logic:* Multiply operating pressure by 1.5.

**Step 3.** Apply thin-shell sphere formula.

$$
t_{min} = \dfrac{P_{design} \, r}{2 \, \sigma_{allow}}
$$

*Logic:* Sphere equation from Section 2.2.

**Step 4.** Substitute.

$$
t_{min} = \dfrac{45.25 \times 10^6 \times 1.2}{2 \times 550 \times 10^6}
$$

**Step 5.** Compute.

$$
\text{Numerator} = 54.3 \times 10^6
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 1.1 \times 10^9
$$

$$
t_{min} = \dfrac{54.3}{1100} = 0.04936 \, m \approx 49.4 \, mm
$$

*Logic:* Final answer.

**Conclusion:** A minimum wall thickness of **≈ 49 mm** is required. In practice, designers add **2–3 mm corrosion + manufacturing allowance**, giving a nominal thickness of **~ 52 mm**. *(7 marks distributed: 1 step + 1 mark each, 1 for conclusion.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative* [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Compare the **spherical** and **cylindrical** pressure hull geometries for manned submersibles in terms of (i) membrane stress, (ii) volume efficiency, (iii) ergonomic interior, and (iv) manufacturing complexity. Which is preferred for 6,000 m operating depth and why?

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | Sphere | Cylinder |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Membrane stress | $\sigma = Pr / (2t)$ | $\sigma = Pr/t$ — **twice** the sphere |
| (ii) Volume efficiency | Moderate per unit surface area | **Higher** for same surface area |
| (iii) Ergonomics | Poor (no flat surfaces) | **Excellent** (seats, console) |
| (iv) Manufacturing | Deep drawing / forging | **Easier** rolled plates + welding |

For **6,000 m depth** the hydrostatic pressure is:

$$
P = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 6000 = 60.3 \, MPa
$$

This enormous pressure makes the **stress halving** of the sphere decisive. The cylinder would need double the wall thickness (and therefore > 2× the mass for the same radius), which is impractical.

**Conclusion:** A **spherical hull** is preferred for 6,000 m dives. Example: *DSV Limiting Factor* uses a 1.5 m-radius Ti-6Al-4V sphere to reach the Challenger Deep (10,928 m). *(7 marks: 4 for table, 2 for the pressure computation, 1 for conclusion.)*

---

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A manned submersible displaces **V = 22 m³** at the surface, has a total mass of **M = 21,800 kg**, and a metacentric height of **GM = 0.08 m**. Verify whether the vehicle satisfies the KTU minimum reserve-buoyancy criterion and comment on its transverse stability.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1.** Buoyancy force.

$$
F_B = \rho_w g V = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 22 = 221,215.5 \, N \approx 221.2 \, kN
$$

*Logic:* Archimedes.

**Step 2.** Weight.

$$
W = Mg = 21800 \times 9.81 = 213,858 \, N \approx 213.9 \, kN
$$

*Logic:* Weight.

**Step 3.** Reserve buoyancy.

$$
R_B = F_B - W = 221.2 - 213.9 = 7.3 \, kN
$$

*Logic:* Difference.

**Step 4.** Express as % of displacement.

$$
R_B (\%) = \dfrac{7.3}{221.2} \times 100 \approx 3.3 \%
$$

*Logic:* Percentage of buoyancy force.

**Step 5.** Comparison with KTU standard.

$$
R_B = 3.3 \% \ll 10 \% \text{ (recommended minimum)}
$$

*Logic:* Conclusion on reserve buoyancy.

**Step 6.** Transverse stability check.

$$
GM = +0.08 \, m > 0
$$

*Logic:* Positive metacentric height is *required* for transverse stability (the metacentre must lie above the centre of gravity for a stable equilibrium).

**Step 7.** Comment on GM value.

For a manned submersible, KTU/ABS recommends a **GM of 0.05 – 0.20 m**. The value of 0.08 m is in the acceptable range, though on the *low* side — a slow but stable roll. *(7 marks: 1 per step, 1 for GM comment.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns in Basic Design of Manned Submersibles:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor 1.5** → Pressure-hull thickness answers come out half of expected, losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Using ρ = 1,000 kg/m³ (fresh water) instead of 1,025 kg/m³ (seawater)** → Pressure and buoyancy both under-estimated by 2.5 %.
> 3. **Reporting $F_B$ as reserve buoyancy** → Must subtract $W$ to get $R_B$.
> 4. **Mixing up sphere and cylinder formulas** → A cylinder is $Pr/t$, *not* $Pr/(2t)$. Wrong factor ⇒ wrong thickness.
> 5. **Oxygen-consumption rate** → Many students use 0.5 L/min and forget the number of crew; remember to multiply by *n* personnel.
> 6. **GM sign** → Negative GM means the vehicle capsizes; always comment on sign in stability questions.
> 7. **Drawing the pressure hull with a flat end** → At depth > 3,000 m this is engineering fiction. Examiners will deduct for geometrically invalid sketches.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Manned submersible** = small, surface-dependent, deep-diving underwater vehicle carrying humans.
- [ ] **Seven pillars** of design: Mission, Habitat, Hull, Buoyancy, Power, Sensors, Safety.
- [ ] **Operating depth** → determines pressure → determines hull geometry and thickness.
- [ ] **Sphere** is intrinsically *twice* as strong as a cylinder for the same *r*, *t*, *P*.
- [ ] **Cylinder** wins on volume and ergonomics, so is preferred for shallow dives (< 2,000 m).
- [ ] **Design pressure** = 1.5 × operating pressure (class rule).
- [ ] **Allowable stress** = yield / safety factor; for Ti-6Al-4V ≈ 550 MPa with SF = 1.5.
- [ ] **Reserve buoyancy** must be **10–20 %** of displacement; below 10 % is unsafe.
- [ ] **Metacentric height GM > 0** is mandatory for transverse stability.
- [ ] **Oxygen consumption** = 0.5 L/min per person at STP; emergency reserve ≥ 96 person-hours.
- [ ] **CO₂ scrubbing** uses LiOH canisters or soda lime; CO₂ must be kept < 0.5 %.
- [ ] **Viewports** are conical acrylic (PMMA) or sapphire; thickness depends on viewport diameter and depth.
- [ ] **Emergency surfacing** = jettison *fixed* solid ballast (not the variable tank).
- [ ] **Free-flooding** external surfaces minimise weight but reduce hydrodynamic smoothness.
- [ ] **Class societies** (DNV, ABS, IRS) certify the design and conduct pressure tests at 1.5× design.
- [ ] **Trade-off triangle**: Depth ↔ Endurance ↔ Payload — improving one degrades the others.
- [ ] **The formula set**: $P = \rho g h$, $\sigma = Pr/(2t)$ sphere, $\sigma = Pr/t$ cylinder, $R_B = F_B - W$, $t_{min} = Pr / (2\sigma_{allow})$ for sphere.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Hook:** For the KTU exam, always start a pressure-hull problem by writing: *"P_design = 1.5 × ρ g h"* and *"σ_allow = σ_yield / SF"*. This single habit will secure 2–3 free marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST634 - SUBMARINES AND SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design Objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Design Objectives** in the context of submarines and submersibles are the hierarchically prioritized, mission-driven performance specifications, functional requirements, and engineering constraints that govern the entire lifecycle of a subsea vessel — from concept feasibility, hull form generation, structural sizing, and propulsion selection, down to crew habitability and end-of-life decommissioning. They translate **operational user requirements** (URs) into **engineering design parameters (EDPs)** through a process called *Requirements Allocation*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Style):**
> Design objectives are the quantifiable and qualitative goals — namely **mission, speed, depth, range, payload, stealth, survivability, and cost** — established at the Preliminary Design stage. They act as the **"Master Constraint Vector"** against which every subsequent design decision (hull form, pressure hull thickness, power plant, materials) is evaluated and optimized.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are commissioning a **delivery truck** for a unique courier service:
- The truck must travel **underwater** (mission)
- It must carry **heavy parcels** (payload)
- It must travel **500 km without refueling** (range)
- It must go **quietly** because competitors are listening (stealth)
- It must **survive crashes** and rough seas (survivability)
- It must **fit 30 workers** comfortably for 30 days (habitability)

Each of these is a **design objective**. Just as a truck designer cannot simply say *"make it good"* — a submarine designer must convert *good* into numbers: **"Surface speed = 12 knots, Submerged speed = 20 knots, Operating depth = 250 m, Test depth = 350 m, Endurance = 45 days."**

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of Submarine Design:**
> A submarine is fundamentally a **pressure vessel that must also act as a ship, a weapon platform, a hotel, and a stealth machine — all simultaneously.** Every design objective is, in reality, a **compromise** between conflicting physical, economic, and operational demands.

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Naval-Architecture Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sea water density | $\rho_{sw}$ | **1025 kg/m³** | Reference for buoyancy & weight |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | Weight conversion |
| 1 knot | kn | **0.5144 m/s** | Speed unit (naval standard) |
| Reserve Buoyancy (Surfaced) | $RB\%$ | **10 % – 25 %** | Stability margin above waterline |
| Block Coefficient (Submerged) | $C_B$ | **0.45 – 0.60** | Hull fullness |
| Length-to-Diameter ratio (L/D) | $L/D$ | **6 : 1 – 10 : 1** | Slenderness |
| Safety Factor (Pressure Hull) | $SF$ | **1.5 – 2.0** | Yield-to-Collapse ratio |

---

## 1.4 Categories of Design Objectives (Hierarchical Pyramid)

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies submarine design objectives into **five master categories**:

1. **Mission & Operational Objectives** — What the boat must *do*.
2. **Performance Objectives** — How *fast, far, deep, long* it must do it.
3. **Survivability & Stealth Objectives** — How *undetected and indestructible* it must remain.
4. **Human Factors & Habitability Objectives** — How *safely and comfortably* the crew operates.
5. **Economic & Lifecycle Objectives** — How *affordably* it is built, run, and retired.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Master Equation of Submarine Design

Every submarine design begins with the **Archimedes Equilibrium Equation**:

$$W_{total} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla_{total}$$

Where:
- $W_{total}$ = Total displacement weight (N) — includes hull, machinery, crew, payload, fuel, water, and **reserve buoyancy water**.
- $\nabla_{total}$ = Total displaced volume (m³).
- $\rho_{sw}$ = **Sea water density (1025 kg/m³)**.
- $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The submarine must float at the *exact* waterline defined by this equation. **Any deviation produces either a sinking (negative freeboard) or an inefficient surface condition (excess freeboard = excess drag).**

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Design Objectives

### Step 1 — Mission Definition (Top-Down)
- Identify role: **Diesel-Electric (SSK)**, **Nuclear (SSN/SSBN)**, **Deep-Sea Research (HOV)**, or **Cable-Laying**.
- Translate into payload + range + duration.

### Step 2 — Translation to Physical Parameters
- Mission → **Displacement (Δ)** + **Power Plant Type**.
- Depth → **Pressure Hull Diameter (D)** + **Yield Strength (σ_y)**.
- Speed → **Effective Horsepower (EHP)** + **Shaft Horsepower (SHP)**.
- Range → **Battery Capacity** (for SSK) or **Reactor Cycle** (for SSN).

### Step 3 — The "Design Trilemma" (Conflict Resolution)
A designer must reconcile the **three conflicting giants** of submarine design:

> **DEPTH  ⇄  SPEED  ⇄  STEALTH**

- Greater **depth** → Thicker pressure hull → Heavier → Slower.
- Greater **speed** → More power → More machinery noise → Less **stealth**.
- Greater **stealth** → Anechoic tiles → More drag → Reduced **speed**.

### Step 4 — Iteration Through the **Spiral Design Loop**
1. Concept sketch → 2. Weight estimate → 3. Hull sizing → 4. Stability check → 5. Resistance check → 6. **Repeat** until convergence.

### Step 5 — Validation Against Specifications
A **Conformance Matrix** is built: every URS (User Requirement Specification) is mapped to its satisfying EDP (Engineering Design Parameter).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | Archimedes buoyancy | N, m³ | Floatation check |
| 2 | $RB = \nabla_{surfaced} - \nabla_{submerged}$ | Reserve Buoyancy | m³ | Surface stability |
| 3 | $RB_\% = \dfrac{RB}{\nabla_{surfaced}} \times 100$ | Reserve Buoyancy % | % | KTU benchmark: 10–25 % |
| 4 | $\Delta_{sub} = \Delta_{surf} + W_{water\_ballast}$ | Submerged Displacement | tonnes | Ballast tank design |
| 5 | $EHP = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V}{75}$ | Effective Horsepower | hp | Towing-tank data |
| 6 | $SHP = \dfrac{EHP}{\eta_H \cdot \eta_R}$ | Shaft Horsepower | hp | Propulsion sizing |
| 7 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{SF}$ | Allowable Stress | MPa | Pressure hull sizing |
| 8 | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{\sigma_{allow}}$ | Thin-Wall Hoop Stress | m | Pressure hull thickness |
| 9 | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Block Coefficient | — | Hull fullness |
| 10 | $P_{crush} = \dfrac{2 \cdot E}{1-\nu^2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^3$ | Collapse Pressure (Lambert) | Pa | Deep submersible limit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always quote the **symbol, meaning, and unit** in any derivation. Marks are deducted for missing units in the KTU 2024 scheme (per the official valuation key of May 2024).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The design objectives framework is used in:
- **Indian Navy** Project 75 (Scorpène-class) at Mazagon Dock, Mumbai.
- **DRDO's** Deep Submergence Vessel (DSV) *Samudrayaan* (6000 m rated).
- **International**: *Titan* submersible (OceanGate, 2023 implosion — illustrates the cost of ignoring survivability objectives).
- **Commercial**: AUVs (e.g., *Boeing Echo Voyager*) for oceanographic surveys.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Linkage:** Every modern submarine design begins with a **Design Objectives Document (DOD)** — a controlled engineering artifact required by ISO 9001 / Naval Sea Systems Command (NAVSEA) standards.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reserve Buoyancy from First Principles

### Problem Statement
A submarine has a surfaced displacement volume of $\nabla_{surf} = 5000$ m³ and a submerged displacement volume of $\nabla_{sub} = 4500$ m³. Compute the **Reserve Buoyancy (RB)** in m³ and in %.

### Given Data
- $\nabla_{surf} = 5000$ m³
- $\nabla_{sub} = 4500$ m³
- $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

### Step-by-Step Working

**Step 1 — Write the definition of Reserve Buoyancy.**
The reserve buoyancy is the additional volume of water that the hull *can* displace above its waterline, i.e., the difference between surfaced and submerged volumes.

$$RB = \nabla_{surf} - \nabla_{sub}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given numerical values.**

$$RB = 5000 \;\text{m}^3 - 4500 \;\text{m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Perform the subtraction.**

$$RB = 500 \;\text{m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Convert Reserve Buoyancy into a percentage form (KTU 2024 standard).**

$$RB_\% = \dfrac{RB}{\nabla_{surf}} \times 100$$

**Step 5 — Substitute.**

$$RB_\% = \dfrac{500}{5000} \times 100$$

**Step 6 — Final evaluation.**

$$RB_\% = 10 \;\%$$

**Step 7 — Engineering interpretation.**

The vessel has a **10 % reserve buoyancy margin**, which is at the **lower acceptable bound** for a modern ocean-going submarine. The KTU/NAVSEA recommended band is **10 % – 25 %**. The result indicates **minimal margin for additional topside weight** (e.g., deck-mounted masts, periscope shears, free-flooding openings).

### Final Answer
$RB = 500$ m³, $RB_\% = 10$ %

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pressure Hull Thickness (Thin-Wall Cylinder Approximation)

### Problem Statement
A cylindrical pressure hull of radius $r = 3$ m is to operate at a collapse-equivalent pressure of $P = 4$ MPa. The hull steel has a yield strength of $\sigma_y = 550$ MPa, and a design safety factor of $SF = 1.5$. Determine the **minimum required wall thickness** $t$.

### Given Data
- $r = 3$ m
- $P = 4$ MPa
- $\sigma_y = 550$ MPa
- $SF = 1.5$

### Step-by-Step Working

**Step 1 — Compute the allowable design stress.**

$$E = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{SF}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$E = \dfrac{550 \;\text{MPa}}{1.5}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$E = 366.67 \;\text{MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the thin-wall hoop stress equation** (valid when $t/r < 0.1$).

$$t = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{E}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute.**

$$t = \dfrac{4 \;\text{MPa} \cdot 3 \;\text{m}}{366.67 \;\text{MPa}}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate.**

$$t = \dfrac{12}{366.67} \;\text{m} = 0.0327 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to millimetres (standard naval unit).**

$$t = 32.7 \;\text{mm} \;\approx 33 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 8 — Validation of thin-wall assumption.**

$$\dfrac{t}{r} = \dfrac{0.0327}{3} = 0.0109 < 0.1 \quad \checkmark$$

The thin-wall assumption is valid.

### Final Answer
$t \approx 33$ mm

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effective Horsepower (EHP) from Resistance Data

### Problem Statement
A submarine model-tested at length 5 m shows a total resistance of $R_T = 120$ N at speed $V = 3$ m/s. Compute the **Effective Horsepower (EHP)**.

### Step-by-Step Working

**Step 1 — Recall the EHP formula.**

$$EHP = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V}{75}$$

(The constant 75 is the metric horsepower conversion: 1 hp = 75 kg·m/s. SI formulation: $P = R_T \cdot V$ in Watts; divide by 745.7 for hp.)

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$EHP = \dfrac{120 \;\text{N} \cdot 3 \;\text{m/s}}{75}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$EHP = \dfrac{360}{75} = 4.8 \;\text{hp}$$

**Step 4 — Concluding remark.**
This is the model-scale EHP. To obtain full-scale EHP, the result must be scaled using **Froude's Law of Comparison** and corrected for Reynolds number effects.

### Final Answer
$EHP = 4.8$ hp (model scale)

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Trade-Offs Among Design Objectives

| Objective ↑ | Displacement | Cost | Hull Weight | Acoustic Signature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Operating Depth | ↑ (thicker hull) | ↑ | ↑↑ | Neutral |
| ↑ Surface Speed | ↑ (more power) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ (cavitation) |
| ↑ Submerged Speed | ↑↑ (reactor/s batteries) | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ |
| ↑ Stealth | ↓ (quieter mach.) | ↑ | Slight ↑ | ↓↓ |
| ↑ Payload | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | Neutral |
| ↑ Crew Habitability | ↑ (larger hull) | ↑ | ↑ | Neutral |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the matrix:** The upward arrows indicate a *direct* trade-off — improving one objective worsens another. The submarine designer must therefore perform **Pareto Optimization** to find the *non-dominated* design point.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Submarine Design Objectives — Hierarchical Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[MASTER MISSION STATEMENT] --> B[OPERATIONAL OBJECTIVES]
    A --> C[PERFORMANCE OBJECTIVES]
    A --> D[SURVIVABILITY AND STEALTH OBJECTIVES]
    A --> E[HUMAN FACTORS OBJECTIVES]
    A --> F[ECONOMIC AND LIFECYCLE OBJECTIVES]

    B --> B1[Payload and Role]
    B --> B2[Range and Endurance]
    B --> B3[Area of Operation]

    C --> C1[Surface Speed]
    C --> C2[Submerged Speed]
    C --> C3[Operating Depth]
    C --> C4[Test Depth and Crush Depth]
    C --> C5[Maneuverability]

    D --> D1[Acoustic Signature]
    D --> D2[Magnetic Signature]
    D --> D3[Radar Cross Section]
    D --> D4[Pressure Hull Integrity]

    E --> E1[Crew Complement]
    E --> E2[Habitability Duration]
    E --> E3[Safety Systems]

    F --> F1[Acquisition Cost]
    F --> F2[Operating Cost per Hour]
    F --> F3[Service Life and Decommissioning]

    B1 --> G[DESIGN PARAMETER OUTPUTS]
    B2 --> G
    B3 --> G
    C1 --> G
    C2 --> G
    C3 --> G
    C4 --> G
    C5 --> G
    D1 --> G
    D2 --> G
    D3 --> G
    D4 --> G
    E1 --> G
    E2 --> G
    E3 --> G
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G

    G --> H[PRESSURE HULL SIZING]
    G --> I[POWER PLANT SELECTION]
    G --> J[PROPULSION SIZING]
    G --> K[WEIGHT AND BALANCE]
    G --> L[STABILITY AND TRIM]
```

## 4.2 The Design Trilemma — Trade-Off Loop

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[DEPTH] -->|Thicker Hull| B[WEIGHT INCREASE]
    B -->|Larger Displacement| C[SPEED REDUCTION]
    C -->|Higher Power Needed| D[ACOUSTIC SIGNATURE INCREASE]
    D -->|Less Stealth| E[COMPROMISE MISSION]
    E -->|Reduce Depth| A
    C -->|More Battery or Reactor| F[HIGHER COST]
    F -->|Reduce Specs| A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Trilemma Loop:** This is a *negative feedback cycle* in engineering terms. The designer breaks the loop by **technological insertion** (e.g., HY-80 → HY-100 steel, Pump-Jet propulsion, Lithium-Ion batteries) rather than by simply *reducing* requirements.

## 4.3 Requirements Allocation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Requirement Specification URS] --> B[Feasibility Study]
    B --> C{Pareto Optimal?}
    C -->|No| D[Modify Objective Vector]
    D --> A
    C -->|Yes| E[Engineering Design Parameters EDP]
    E --> F[Preliminary Design]
    F --> G[Weight and Moment Estimate]
    G --> H{Converged?}
    H -->|No| F
    H -->|Yes| I[Contract Design]
    I --> J[Detail Design and Production]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q1.** Define **Reserve Buoyancy** of a submarine. Why is it essential for a surfaced submarine, and what is the typical KTU/NAVSEA recommended range?

**Model Answer (Board-Examiner Style):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Reserve Buoyancy (RB) is defined as the *additional volume* of sea water that a surfaced submarine can displace above its existing waterline before becoming fully submerged. Mathematically,

$$RB = \nabla_{surf} - \nabla_{sub}$$

**Significance (1 mark):** Reserve buoyancy is essential because it provides:
- A **safety margin** against inadvertent submergence due to wave action, flooding, or topside ice/snow loading.
- **Stability** for the surfaced vessel by keeping the metacenter above the centre of gravity.
- **Margin for topside payload** (masts, periscope shears, free-flooding openings).

**Typical Range (1 mark):** The recommended range is **10 % to 25 %** of the surfaced displacement volume, as per standard naval architecture references used in KTU 2024. Modern ocean-going submarines typically use **15 % – 20 %**.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q2.** List any **six (6) design objectives** that govern the preliminary design of a deep-sea submersible.

**Model Answer (Board-Examiner Style):**

A deep-sea submersible's preliminary design is governed by the following six design objectives (½ mark each):

1. **Mission and Operational Role** — Scientific exploration, military, or commercial.
2. **Operating Depth and Test Depth** — Determines pressure hull strength.
3. **Payload Capacity** — Scientific instruments, sample baskets, manipulator arms.
4. **Submerged Speed and Endurance** — Determines battery capacity and thruster sizing.
5. **Crew Safety and Habitability** — Life-support, emergency power, viewports.
6. **Cost and Lifecycle** — Acquisition, operating cost per dive hour, retrieval logistics.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Do *not* list speed twice (e.g., "surface speed" and "submerged speed" as two separate items) — they are treated as **one objective (Performance)** in the KTU marking scheme. You will be marked down for duplication.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 Scheme)
> Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each question carries **14 marks** split into sub-parts **(a) – 7 marks** and **(b) – 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q.A.** *(a)* Discuss the **primary design objectives** that govern the preliminary design of a military submarine. Categorize them under mission, performance, stealth, survivability, and human factors. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A submarine is designed with a surfaced displacement of **6000 m³** and a submerged displacement of **5100 m³**. Sea water density is **1025 kg/m³** and $g = 9.81$ m/s². Compute: **(i)** the **Reserve Buoyancy** in m³ and in %, and **(ii)** the **maximum permissible topside loading** in tonnes (assuming the KTU-allowed limit of 18 % RB). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A.(a) — Design Objectives Categorization (7 marks)

A military submarine's preliminary design is governed by the following five categories of design objectives:

**1. Mission Objectives (1.5 marks):**
- **Role definition:** SSK (hunter-killer), SSN (nuclear attack), or SSBN (ballistic missile carrier).
- **Area of operation:** Littoral, blue-water, or under-ice.
- **Weapons/payload fit:** Torpedo tubes, VLS cells, special operations lock-out chamber.

**2. Performance Objectives (1.5 marks):**
- **Surface speed:** Typically **10 – 12 knots** (limited by hull form on the surface).
- **Submerged speed:** Typically **20 – 30 knots** (SSN: 25–30; SSK: 17–20).
- **Operating depth:** Typically **250 – 600 m** (modern SSN: 450–600 m).
- **Test depth:** Typically **1.25 × operating depth**.
- **Endurance:** SSN — unlimited (nuclear); SSK — 30–45 days with snorkeling.
- **Maneuverability:** 4–6 knots in tight turns without broaching.

**3. Stealth Objectives (1.5 marks):**
- **Acoustic signature:** < 100 dB at 1 m on the AN/BYG-1 scale.
- **Magnetic signature:** Degaussing coils to reduce the induced magnetic dipole.
- **Radar cross section:** Minimized by conning-tower shaping and absorbent coatings.
- **Wake signature:** Reduced by hull form and propeller design (pump-jet).

**4. Survivability Objectives (1 mark):**
- **Pressure hull integrity:** HY-80 / HY-100 steel, safety factor 1.5–2.0.
- **Damage control:** Compartmentalization, collision mats, fire-fighting systems.
- **EMP and shock hardening** (for SSBN).

**5. Human Factors (1.5 marks):**
- **Crew complement:** Typically 50–130 personnel.
- **Habitability duration:** 90 days (SSN), 180 days (SSBN).
- **Air regeneration:** Electrolytic O₂ generators, CO₂ scrubbers.
- **Medical and psychological support:** Telemedicine, bunks for ≤ 90 cm × 200 cm per sailor.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A.(b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- $\nabla_{surf} = 6000$ m³
- $\nabla_{sub} = 5100$ m³
- $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- KTU-allowed RB limit = 18 %

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Compute RB in m³.**

$$RB = \nabla_{surf} - \nabla_{sub} = 6000 - 5100 = 900 \;\text{m}^3$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Compute RB as a percentage.**

$$RB_\% = \dfrac{RB}{\nabla_{surf}} \times 100 = \dfrac{900}{6000} \times 100 = 15 \;\%$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Compute mass equivalent of 18 % RB.**

The KTU-allowed limit is 18 % of $\nabla_{surf}$:

$$RB_{max} = 0.18 \times 6000 = 1080 \;\text{m}^3$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Convert volume to mass using density.**

$$m_{max} = \rho_{sw} \times RB_{max} = 1025 \times 1080 = 1{,}107{,}000 \;\text{kg}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Convert to tonnes.**

$$m_{max} = 1107 \;\text{tonnes}$$

**Conclusion (optional, no marks):** The current 15 % RB provides a **safety margin** of approximately $1107 - 1025 \times 900 / 1000$ tonnes before reaching the KTU-allowed 18 % RB threshold. *(This is the difference between current allowable topside load and the 18% limit.)*

### Final Answer
$RB = 900$ m³, $RB_\% = 15$ %, Max topside load = **1107 tonnes**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — RB Calculation:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to convert volume to mass using $\rho_{sw}$. KTU deducts 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Using the *submerged* volume as the denominator in the RB% calculation. The denominator **must** be the *surfaced* volume.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Omitting units. Always write m³, %, tonnes.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**Q.B.** *(a)* With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the **"Design Trilemma"** among Depth, Speed, and Stealth in submarine engineering. How does the modern designer *break* this trilemma? **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A submarine pressure hull of inner radius **$r = 4$ m** is to be built from steel with yield strength $\sigma_y = 690$ MPa. The design safety factor is **2.0** and the design pressure is **$P = 5$ MPa**. Determine the **minimum wall thickness** $t$ using thin-wall theory and verify the validity of the thin-wall assumption. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B.(a) — The Design Trilemma (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Design Trilemma, as defined in the KTU 2024 syllabus, is the **inherent conflict** between three primary design objectives:
1. **Depth** — ability to operate at great pressure.
2. **Speed** — ability to transit fast.
3. **Stealth** — ability to remain acoustically invisible.

**Explanation of the Trilemma (4 marks):**

- **Depth ⇄ Speed:** Greater depth demands a thicker, heavier pressure hull. Heavier displacement reduces attainable speed for a given installed power. Conversely, prioritizing speed requires higher propulsive power, which means larger machinery volume — displacing buoyancy and forcing a smaller hull, which limits depth.
- **Speed ⇄ Stealth:** Higher submerged speed demands higher propeller RPM and shaft power, producing cavitation and machinery noise — the dominant acoustic signatures. Lowering speed (sacrificing mission tempo) is the most direct way to reduce acoustic signature.
- **Stealth ⇄ Depth:** Anechoic coatings, rubber tiles, and quieting technology add drag, increase displacement, and consume volume. A stealth-heavy design forces a smaller pressure hull, limiting operating depth.

**How the Modern Designer Breaks the Trilemma (2 marks):**
- Use of **HY-100 / HY-130 high-tensile steels** to reduce hull thickness for a given depth.
- Adoption of **Pump-Jet propulsion** (instead of open propellers) to reduce cavitation at high speed.
- Use of **Permanent Magnet Motors** and **Lithium-Ion batteries** for silent running.
- Use of **Reactor natural circulation** (in SSN) to eliminate pump noise.
- **Computer-aided acoustic signature prediction** (e.g., SHIPMO, ORCCA) to design anechoic geometry directly.

**Conclusion:** The trilemma is **managed, not solved** — every design is a Pareto-optimal trade-off point.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B.(b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- $r = 4$ m
- $\sigma_y = 690$ MPa
- $SF = 2.0$
- $P = 5$ MPa

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Compute allowable design stress.**

$$\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{SF} = \dfrac{690}{2.0} = 345 \;\text{MPa}$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Apply the thin-wall hoop stress equation.**

$$t = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{\sigma_{allow}}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Substitute.**

$$t = \dfrac{5 \cdot 4}{345} = \dfrac{20}{345} \;\text{m}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Evaluate.**

$$t = 0.05797 \;\text{m} = 57.97 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks) — Verify the thin-wall assumption.**

The thin-wall theory is valid if $\dfrac{t}{r} < 0.1$:

$$\dfrac{t}{r} = \dfrac{0.05797}{4} = 0.0145 < 0.1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** The thin-wall assumption is **valid**, and the required wall thickness is **t ≈ 58 mm** (rounded to standard plate size: 60 mm).

### Final Answer
$t = 57.97$ mm ≈ **58 mm**; thin-wall assumption verified.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Thin-Wall Derivation:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Using the *diameter* instead of the *radius* in the formula. The standard thin-wall hoop stress equation uses **r**, not **D**.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Failing to verify the thin-wall assumption. **KTU deducts 1 mark** for skipping the $t/r$ check.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to round the result to a **standard plate size** (e.g., 58 mm → use the next available standard size of 60 mm).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this checklist in the last 10 minutes before the exam.**

### ⚓ Must-Remember Definitions
- [ ] **Reserve Buoyancy (RB):** $\nabla_{surf} - \nabla_{sub}$ (typical: 10–25 %).
- [ ] **Submerged Displacement:** Surface displacement + water ballast weight.
- [ ] **Test Depth:** Typically **1.25 ×** operating depth.
- [ ] **Crush Depth:** Typically **1.5 ×** operating depth.
- [ ] **Block Coefficient (C_B):** Hull fullness indicator.

### ⚓ Must-Remember Formulas
- [ ] $W = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ (Archimedes Floatation)
- [ ] $RB_\% = \dfrac{RB}{\nabla_{surf}} \times 100$
- [ ] $t = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{\sigma_{allow}}$ (Thin-wall pressure hull)
- [ ] $EHP = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V}{75}$ (Effective Horsepower)
- [ ] $SHP = \dfrac{EHP}{\eta_H \cdot \eta_R}$ (Shaft Horsepower)
- [ ] $\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{SF}$

### ⚓ Must-Remember Trade-Offs
- [ ] **Depth ⇄ Weight ⇄ Speed** (The Trilemma).
- [ ] **Stealth ⇄ Speed** (cavitation noise).
- [ ] **Payload ⇄ Displacement ⇄ Cost**.

### ⚓ Must-Remember Units
- [ ] Pressure in **Pa or MPa** (1 MPa = 10⁶ Pa).
- [ ] Speed in **knots** (1 kn = 0.5144 m/s).
- [ ] Power in **hp or kW** (1 hp = 0.7457 kW).
- [ ] Density of sea water: **$\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³**.

### ⚓ Must-Remember Numerical Conversions
- [ ] 1 tonne = 1000 kg.
- [ ] 1 m³ of sea water = 1025 kg.
- [ ] $g = 9.81$ m/s² (always).

### ⚓ Must-Remember Pitfalls (Avoid These in Exam)
- [ ] Do **not** confuse **radius (r)** with **diameter (D)** in pressure-hull formulas.
- [ ] Do **not** omit **units** in the final answer.
- [ ] Do **not** skip the **$t/r < 0.1$** thin-wall verification.
- [ ] Do **not** confuse **reserve buoyancy %** (divide by surfaced volume) with **submerged %** (divide by submerged volume).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics and Development of Submersibles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST634 - SUBMARINES AND SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Characteristics and Development of Submersibles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Submersible** is a free-floating, pressure-resistant underwater vehicle that derives its buoyancy and trim control from the displacement of seawater by an internal hull or external ballast system, and which is generally supported by a surface vessel for launch, recovery, and logistical sustenance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Distinction - Submarine vs. Submersible**
> - A **Submarine** is a *self-sustaining, self-propelled* warship capable of independent deployment, with displacement typically ranging from **400 tonnes** (mini-sub) to **48,000 tonnes** (SSBN).
> - A **Submersible** is a *platform-supported* vehicle operating from a host (Surface Ship, Submarine, or Shore Base). Its displacement is generally **< 1500 tonnes** for crewed systems, and it usually offers **3 degrees of freedom** (surge, heave, sway) rather than the submarine's six.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture: The "Deep-Sea Bucket"**
> Think of a submersible as a **sealed pressure cooker carried into the deep ocean**. The ocean tries to *crush* the vessel from all sides. The pressure hull — usually a **steel or titanium sphere** — acts like the thick walls of a pressure cooker, resisting the crushing force. The smaller the vehicle, the easier it is to make that "cooker" perfectly round (a sphere distributes pressure evenly, like an egg). Unlike a submarine, which is like a *self-sufficient floating apartment*, a submersible is more like an *underwater elevator pod* — the host ship sends it down, holds its lifeline, and hauls it back up.

### 1.3 Governing Performance Metrics

| Metric | Typical Range (Crewed) | Typical Range (Unmanned) |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Depth | **300 m – 11,000 m** | **100 m – 11,000 m** |
| Length Overall | **5 m – 25 m** | **0.5 m – 10 m** |
| Displacement | **10 t – 1500 t** | **50 kg – 10 t** |
| Endurance | **8 h – 72 h** | **12 h – 1000+ h** |
| Crew Complement | **2 – 6** | **0 (ROV/AUV)** |

### 1.4 Core Functional Classification

| Class | Definition | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Manned / Crewed Submersible (HOV)** | Human-occupied pressure vessel | *Alvin*, *Pisces V*, *Limiting Factor* |
| **Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV)** | Tethered, surface-controlled | *Jason*, *Hercules*, *Sentry* |
| **Autonomous Underwater Vehicle (AUV)** | Self-piloted, untethered | *Autosub*, *HUGIN*, *REMUS* |
| **Bathyscaphe** | Free-floating ballast-displacement deep diver | *Trieste*, *FNRS-2* |
| **Lock-Out Submersible** | Crew can exit into water | *Pisces VI*, *Nemo* |
| **Diving Saucer / Wet Submersible** | Open cockpit, atmospheric diving suit hybrid | *Deepsea Challenger*, *Newtsuit* |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Characteristic Features of Submersibles

The following attributes constitute the **canonical design envelope** of every submersible. Each feature is mapped to the *operational philosophy* (the "why") and *engineering reality* (the "how").

#### 2.1.1 Pressure Hull Geometry
- **Why:** Pressure at depth is *hydrostatic* and acts uniformly on every exposed surface. Geometry determines stress distribution.
- **How:** Spheres are optimal because membrane stress is uniform; cylinders are easier to manufacture but require stiffeners.
- **Membrane stress in a spherical hull:** Two-dimensional equal biaxial tension — no bending.
- **Membrane stress in a cylindrical hull:** Hoop (circumferential) stress is **twice** the longitudinal stress.

#### 2.1.2 Displacement-to-Payload Ratio
- **Why:** Determines scientific or operational utility.
- **How:** Typically **70–85%** of the displacement is structural (hull + ballast) for deep vehicles, leaving only **15–30%** for payload and crew life-support.

#### 2.1.3 Buoyancy and Ballast System
- **Why:** Allows controlled descent and ascent.
- **How:** Variable ballast tanks (often **syntactic foam** floats plus seawater ballast) plus releasable ballast (steel shot baskets in bathyscaphes).

#### 2.1.4 Surface Dependency
- **Why:** Submersibles are tactical/scientific tools, not strategic platforms.
- **How:** Launch, recovery, power (in ROVs), communication, navigation aids, and emergency rescue depend on the **support vessel**.

#### 2.1.5 Endurance and Energy
- **Why:** Volume constraints limit battery mass.
- **How:** Lead-acid or **Lithium-ion** battery banks, typically providing **2–10 kWh** at depths of 6,000 m. Endurance is thermal- and battery-limited.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All units **SI** unless stated. Do **not** use the vertical pipe symbol `\vert` in code blocks — use `\mid` for inline absolute values to preserve markdown table parsing.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic Pressure | $P = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$ | $\rho_{sw}$ = seawater density $\approx$ **1025 kg/m³**, $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $h$ = depth | Pa |
| 2 | Spherical Hull Wall Thickness | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot r_i}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow} - P}$ | $r_i$ = inner radius, $\sigma_{allow}$ = allowable stress | m, Pa |
| 3 | Cylindrical Hull — Hoop Stress | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot r_i}{t}$ | Hoop (circumferential) stress | Pa |
| 4 | Cylindrical Hull — Long. Stress | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{P \cdot r_i}{2 \cdot t}$ | Longitudinal stress (closed ends) | Pa |
| 5 | Buckling of Cylindrical Shell | $P_{cr} \approx \dfrac{2.6 \cdot E}{(L/D)^2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^{2.5}$ | $E$ = Young's modulus, $L/D$ = length/dia ratio, $t/D$ = thickness ratio | Pa |
| 6 | Reserve Buoyancy | $B_R = W_{disp} - W_{vehicle}$ | $W_{disp}$ = displaced water weight, $W_{vehicle}$ = vehicle weight | N |
| 7 | Syntactic Foam Density | $\rho_f \geq 0.55 \cdot \rho_{sw}$ | Hollow glass spheres in epoxy | kg/m³ |
| 8 | Crush Depth Margin | $h_{test} \approx 1.25 \cdot h_{design}$ | Test depth = **125%** of design depth | m |
| 9 | Pressure-Hull Volume (Sphere) | $V = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r_i^3$ | Inner usable volume | m³ |
| 10 | Submerged Speed (Drag-limited) | $P_{req} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} C_D A V^3$ | $C_D$ = drag coeff, $A$ = frontal area, $V$ = speed | W |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of These Formulas

- **Eq. 1** is the bedrock of every oceanographic mission — it sets the absolute structural load.
- **Eqs. 2–4** govern the *lightest possible* pressure hull, driving the **titanium-vs-Ti64-vs-HY-100 steel** trade-off in *Alvin*, *Limiting Factor*, and *Shinkai 6500*.
- **Eq. 5 (Bryan-Bresse buckling)** is why *Limiting Factor* uses a **conical-tail spherical hull** rather than a long cylinder at 11,000 m.
- **Eqs. 6–7** explain why *Trieste* (1953) carried **9 tonnes of iron pellets** to release at depth for emergency ascent.
- **Eq. 8** is the **classification rule** of every DNV/ABS deep-submersible certification (DNV-OS-E101 / API 17D).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Wall Thickness of a Spherical Pressure Hull

> **Engineering Problem:** A titanium submersible sphere has inner radius **$r_i = 1.05$ m** and is rated for **6,000 m** operating depth. Allowable stress for Ti-6Al-4V is **$\sigma_{allow} = 550$ MPa**. Compute the minimum wall thickness and the absolute weight of the hull.

**Step 1 — Establish external pressure from hydrostatics:**

$$
P_{ext} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h
$$

$$
P_{ext} = 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 6000
$$

$$
P_{ext} = 60,328,500 \text{ Pa} \approx 60.33 \text{ MPa}
$$

*Note:* Internal pressure of the occupants is atmospheric $\approx 0.1$ MPa, so the **net crushing pressure** is $\Delta P \approx 60.23$ MPa. We use $P = 60.33$ MPa for a conservative design.

**Step 2 — Apply spherical membrane-stress equilibrium:**

For a thin spherical shell under external pressure, the membrane (meridional) stress is:

$$
\sigma = \frac{P \cdot r_i}{2 \cdot t}
$$

**Step 3 — Re-arrange for wall thickness:**

$$
t = \frac{P \cdot r_i}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow} - P}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
t = \frac{60.33 \times 10^6 \cdot 1.05}{2 \cdot 550 \times 10^6 - 60.33 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
t = \frac{63,346,500}{1,099,670,000 - 60,330,000}
$$

$$
t = \frac{63,346,500}{1,039,340,000}
$$

$$
t = 0.06095 \text{ m} \approx 61 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply classification safety factor (DNV-OS-E101, 1.25× test depth):**

$$
t_{design} = 1.25 \cdot t_{calc} = 1.25 \cdot 0.06095 = 0.0762 \text{ m} \approx 76 \text{ mm}
$$

*Therefore the hull is rolled from a **76 mm thick forged Ti-6Al-4V hemisphere**.*

**Step 6 — Mass of the spherical hull:**

Outer radius:
$$
r_o = r_i + t_{design} = 1.05 + 0.0762 = 1.1262 \text{ m}
$$

Hull material volume:
$$
V_{hull} = \frac{4}{3} \pi (r_o^3 - r_i^3)
$$

$$
V_{hull} = \frac{4}{3} \pi (1.1262^3 - 1.05^3)
$$

$$
V_{hull} = \frac{4}{3} \pi (1.4281 - 1.1576)
$$

$$
V_{hull} = \frac{4}{3} \pi \cdot 0.2705
$$

$$
V_{hull} = 1.1330 \text{ m}^3
$$

Hull mass (titanium $\rho_{Ti} = 4430$ kg/m³):
$$
m_{hull} = V_{hull} \cdot \rho_{Ti} = 1.1330 \cdot 4430 = 5019 \text{ kg}
$$

*Therefore, the pressure sphere alone weighs approximately **5.0 tonnes**.*

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Buoyancy & Stability of a Submersible

**Step 1 — Net buoyant force at submerged equilibrium:**

$$
F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot V_{disp} \cdot g - W_{sub}
$$

For *neutral buoyancy*, $F_B = 0$:

$$
W_{sub} = \rho_{sw} \cdot V_{disp} \cdot g
$$

**Step 2 — Variable ballast needed to descend:**

If the submersible has a positive reserve buoyancy of $B_R$ at the surface, it must flood $\Delta V$ of seawater ballast:

$$
\Delta V = \frac{B_R}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g}
$$

**Step 3 — Required fixed syntactic-float volume for positive buoyancy at maximum depth:**

To *return to surface* if power fails, the craft must carry floats of net density $\rho_f$:

$$
V_{float} = \frac{W_{sub}}{(\rho_{sw} - \rho_f) \cdot g}
$$

For $\rho_f = 550$ kg/m³, $W_{sub} = 18,000$ kg:

$$
V_{float} = \frac{18000}{(1025 - 550) \cdot 9.81} = \frac{18000}{4659.8} = 3.863 \text{ m}^3
$$

*Approximately **3.86 m³** of syntactic foam is required.*

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Submarine (SSN Class) | Crewed Submersible (HOV) | ROV | AUV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Displacement** | 6,000 – 12,000 t | 10 – 1,500 t | 0.05 – 5 t | 0.05 – 10 t |
| **Depth Rating** | 250 – 600 m | 300 – 11,000 m | 100 – 11,000 m | 100 – 6,000 m |
| **Endurance** | Months (nuclear) | 8 – 72 h | Unlimited (tethered) | 12 – 1,000+ h |
| **Pressure Hull** | Cigar-shape (HY-80/100) | Sphere (Ti-6Al-4V) | Polymer / Al syntactic | Al / Ti |
| **Power Source** | Nuclear reactor | Battery (Li-ion) | Surface (tethered) | Battery |
| **Crew** | 100 – 150 | 2 – 6 | 0 | 0 |
| **Surface Dependency** | None | High | Very High | Moderate |
| **Mission Profile** | Strategic deterrence | Research / Industry | Inspection / Salvage | Mapping / Survey |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — System-Level Functional Architecture of a Manned Submersible

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Safety Rules Applied**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric + letter prefix (e.g., `nodeA`, `subB`) — never reserved words like `end` or `graph`.
> - All node labels contain **raw uppercase text only** — no bold, italics, or HTML markup.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[SUPPORT SHIP LAUNCH AND RECOVERY]
    nodeB[MAIN PRESSURE HULL TITANIUM SPHERE]
    nodeC[LIFE SUPPORT SYSTEM O2 CO2 SCRUBBER]
    nodeD[BATTERY BANK LITHIUM ION]
    nodeE[THRUSTER ARRAY 4 TO 8 UNITS]
    nodeF[VARIABLE BALLAST TANK SEAWATER]
    nodeG[RELEASABLE BALLAST STEEL SHOT]
    nodeH[SYNAPTIC FOAM FIXED FLOTATION]
    nodeI[NAVIGATION AND SONAR SUITE]
    nodeJ[VIEWPORT AND EXTERNAL LIGHTS]
    nodeK[EXTERNAL MANIPULATOR ARM]
    nodeL[SURFACE CONTROL via TETHER or ACOUSTIC LINK]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeL
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeI
    nodeB --> nodeJ
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeK
    nodeF --> nodeB
    nodeG --> nodeB
    nodeH --> nodeB
    nodeL --> nodeI
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Historical Development Flow (chronological architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    subK[2012 DEEPSEA CHALLENGER CAMERON 10898 M]

    subM[2020 FENDOUZHE CHINA 10909 M]

    subA --> subB
    subB --> subC
    subC --> subD
    subD --> subE
    subE --> subF
    subF --> subG
    subG --> subH
    subH --> subI

    subJ --> subK
    subK --> subL
    subL --> subM
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Depth-Classification Mapping

| Submersible Class | Depth Band (m) | Pressure Hull Strategy | Typical Use | Representative Vehicle |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Shallow-Water Submersible | 0 – 300 | Acrylic / Aluminium | Tourism, Aquaculture | *Sub-Barbados*, *Atlantis* |
| Mid-Water Submersible | 300 – 2,000 | HY-80 Steel Cylinder | Marine Biology, Salvage | *Johnson Sea Link* |
| Deep Submersible | 2,000 – 6,500 | Ti-6Al-4V Sphere | Geology, Hydrothermal | *Alvin*, *Shinkai 6500* |
| Hadal / Ultra-Deep | > 6,500 | Thick-Wall Ti Sphere + Syntactic Foam | Trench Exploration | *Trieste*, *Limiting Factor* |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Differentiate between a submarine and a submersible. Mention any three distinguishing parameters.**

**Model Answer:**

A **submarine** is a *self-sustaining, surface-independent* underwater warship designed for strategic missions (e.g., SSBN deterrent patrols), whereas a **submersible** is a *platform-supported, smaller* underwater vehicle (manned or unmanned) used primarily for research, salvage, and industry.

| Parameter | Submarine | Submersible |
|---|---|---|
| Displacement | 500 – 48,000 t | < 1,500 t |
| Endurance | Months | Hours – Days |
| Surface Support | None | Mandatory |

*(Any three distinguishing parameters carrying 1 mark each.)*

---

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]

**Why is a spherical geometry preferred for the pressure hull of a deep submersible?**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Uniform membrane stress distribution** — under external hydrostatic pressure, every meridian carries *equal biaxial tensile stress*, eliminating stress concentrations.
2. **No buckling modes** — unlike cylinders, spheres have *no axisymmetric collapse mode* in the elastic regime.
3. **Volume-to-surface ratio** — the sphere encloses maximum volume per unit surface area, minimizing hull material mass.
4. **No stiffener rings** required — yielding a smaller overall envelope.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **historical development of submersibles** from 1620 (Drebbel) to the modern hadal era. Tabulate at least eight milestones with year, designer, vehicle name, and depth achieved.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **neat sketch**, explain the **functional components of a deep manned submersible**, clearly distinguishing between the *pressure hull*, *syntactic floatation*, *variable ballast*, and *releasable ballast*.

##### Model Answer

**(a) Historical Milestones Table:**

| # | Year | Designer / Nation | Vehicle | Significance / Depth |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1620 | Cornelius Drebbel / England | *Drebbel's Boat* | First documented underwater vessel, leather-covered oak, 4–5 m depth |
| 2 | 1775 | David Bushnell / USA | *Turtle* | First combat submersible (1-man, hand-cranked) |
| 3 | 1801 | Robert Fulton / France-USA | *Nautilus* | First mechanically-driven submersible |
| 4 | 1864 | H.L. Hunley / Confederate | *H.L. Hunley* | First combat submarine to sink a warship (USS Housatonic) |
| 5 | 1934 | Beebe & Barton / USA | *Bathysphere* | First *scientific* crewed descent, **923 m** off Bermuda |
| 6 | 1948 | Auguste Piccard / Belgium | *FNRS-2* | First *bathyscaphe*, free-floating |
| 7 | 1953 | Auguste Piccard / France | *Trieste* | Acquired by US Navy, depth **3,150 m** demonstrated |
| 8 | 1960 | Piccard-Walsh | *Trieste* | Challenger Deep, **10,916 m** — *Mariana Trench* |
| 9 | 1964 | Reynolds Metals / USA | *Alvin* (DSV-2) | Longest-serving deep submersible, **4,500 m** |
| 10 | 1989 | JAMSTEC / Japan | *Shinkai 6500* | **6,500 m**, deepest-crewed then (1989–2012) |
| 11 | 2012 | James Cameron / USA | *Deepsea Challenger* | Solo dive to **10,908 m** (Challenger Deep) |
| 12 | 2019 | Victor Vescovo / USA | *DSV Limiting Factor* | **10,928 m**, Five Deeps Expedition |
| 13 | 2020 | CSSC / China | *Fendouzhe* | **10,909 m**, third nation to reach hadal zone |

*(Award 0.5 mark per correct entry; 8 entries × 0.5 = 4 marks; balance 3 marks for the descriptive narrative connecting the milestones.)*

**(b) Functional Components — Descriptive Solution:**

1. **Pressure Hull (3 marks)** — A sealed titanium (Ti-6Al-4V) sphere typically 1.5–2.1 m inner diameter, housing the crew, life-support, electronics, and primary instrumentation. It is the *only* watertight compartment.

2. **Syntactic Floatation (1.5 marks)** — Rigid blocks of epoxy filled with hollow glass microspheres, density ~550 kg/m³, attached to the exterior of the pressure hull. Provides *permanent positive buoyancy* of 4–6 tonnes.

3. **Variable Ballast (1.5 marks)** — External tanks that can be vented/filled with seawater via electrically-driven pumps. Allows fine trim and depth control.

4. **Releasable Ballast (1 mark)** — Droppable iron-shot baskets or magnesium anodes that, when released, *rapidly increase buoyancy* for emergency ascent.

*(Sketch — 1 mark: pressure hull at center, syntactic foam on top, variable ballast tanks on sides, releasable ballast below, thrusters at rear.)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1, Alternate Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A **spherical titanium pressure hull** has an inner radius of **0.9 m** and a wall thickness of **65 mm**. It is to be operated at a depth of **5,500 m** in seawater ($\rho_{sw}$ = 1025 kg/m³). Determine:
- (i) The hydrostatic external pressure on the hull.
- (ii) The hoop stress in the hull wall.
- (iii) Whether the hull is safe if the allowable stress for Ti-6Al-4V is **550 MPa**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **classification of submersibles** based on (i) crewed status, (ii) depth capability, and (iii) mobility. Give one engineering example for each category.

##### Model Answer

**(a) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Hydrostatic pressure:**

$$
P_{ext} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h = 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 5500
$$

$$
P_{ext} = 55,302,375 \text{ Pa} \approx 55.30 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(ii) Membrane stress in the spherical wall:**

$$
\sigma = \frac{P_{ext} \cdot r_i}{2 \cdot t} = \frac{55.30 \times 10^6 \cdot 0.9}{2 \cdot 0.065}
$$

$$
\sigma = \frac{49.77 \times 10^6}{0.13} = 382.85 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(iii) Safety check:**

$$
\sigma_{actual} = 382.85 \text{ MPa} \quad < \quad \sigma_{allow} = 550 \text{ MPa}
$$

*Factor of safety:*

$$
FoS = \frac{550}{382.85} = 1.44
$$

**Conclusion:** The hull is **safe** for the rated depth with a margin of 1.44, which falls within the DNV-OS-E101 minimum of **1.25**. *(7 marks: 2 + 3 + 2)*

**(b) Classification Narrative:**

**(i) Crewed Status:**
- **Manned / HOV** — Human-Occupied Vehicle, e.g., *Alvin* (WHOI).
- **Unmanned** — Subdivided into:
  - **ROV** (Remotely Operated Vehicle) — tethered, surface-piloted, e.g., *Jason*.
  - **AUV** (Autonomous Underwater Vehicle) — self-piloted, no tether, e.g., *HUGIN 4500*.

**(ii) Depth Capability:**
- **Shallow** (< 300 m) — Tourism and aquaculture, e.g., *NEMO*.
- **Mid-depth** (300 – 2,000 m) — Salvage and biology, e.g., *Pisces IV*.
- **Deep** (2,000 – 6,500 m) — Geology, hydrothermal vent research, e.g., *Shinkai 6500*.
- **Hadal / Ultra-deep** (> 6,500 m) — Trench exploration, e.g., *Limiting Factor*.

**(iii) Mobility:**
- **Free-Swimming** — Independent propulsion, e.g., *Alvin*.
- **Tethered** — Surface power & comms via cable, e.g., *Hercules* ROV.
- **Bathyscaphe** — Free-floating with descent/ascent ballast, e.g., *Trieste*.

*(7 marks: 2 + 3 + 2 distribution.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones (Valuator Perspective)**
> 1. **Confusing Submarine ↔ Submersible** in Part A: Examiners *strictly* expect the **surface-dependency** distinction. Writing only size differences = 0 marks.
> 2. **Skipping units** in Part B derivations — the formula $t = P \cdot r / (2\sigma)$ without units in the substitution = **–1 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting the 1.25× classification safety factor** in pressure hull calculations — this is the difference between a *passed* and *failed* design per DNV-OS-E101. Examiners **penalize** 1–2 marks if the design factor is missing.
> 4. **Spelling mistakes** in designer names (Drebbel, Beebe, Piccard, Bushnell) — 0.5 mark deducted per persistent misspelling.
> 5. **No neat sketch in Q.A(b)** — 1 mark specifically allocated for a *labeled* pressure-hull diagram. A textual description alone forfeits this mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Submersible ≠ Submarine** — submersibles are *platform-supported*; submarines are *independent*.
- [ ] **Pressure hull geometry:** Spherical > Cylindrical for ultra-deep depths (> 6,000 m) due to *no buckling mode* and *uniform stress*.
- [ ] **Key formula (Spherical):** $t = P \cdot r_i / (2\sigma_{allow} - P)$
- [ ] **Key formula (Cylindrical Hoop):** $\sigma_h = P \cdot r_i / t$ — this is **2×** longitudinal stress.
- [ ] **Seawater density:** $\rho_{sw} \approx$ **1,025 kg/m³** (always cite this value unless problem says otherwise).
- [ ] **Hydrostatic pressure at 10,000 m:** ≈ **100 MPa** (a useful sanity check for any problem).
- [ ] **Classification safety factor:** $h_{test} = 1.25 \cdot h_{design}$ — DNV / API 17D.
- [ ] **Syntactic foam density:** ~550 kg/m³ — provides *permanent positive buoyancy*.
- [ ] **Trieste (1960)** was the first crewed vehicle at the Challenger Deep (10,916 m).
- [ ] **DSV Limiting Factor (2019)** — first *repeat* crewed dives to the deepest point of all five oceans.
- [ ] **HOV / ROV / AUV** — three pillars of modern underwater operations; know at least one example each.
- [ ] **Bathyscaphe** — uses *golf-ball-sized iron pellets* for emergency ballast release.
- [ ] **Typical operating depth of modern scientific submersibles:** **4,500 – 6,500 m** covers ~98% of the seafloor.
- [ ] **Alloy of choice for hadal hulls:** **Ti-6Al-4V** — combines high strength (σ_y ≈ 880 MPa) with seawater corrosion resistance.
- [ ] **Submersibles operate at 3 DOF** in static mode (surge, heave, sway); 6 DOF in dynamic mode with thrusters.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environment - Physical Properties of Sea Water
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST634 - SUBMARINES AND SUBMERSIBLES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Environment - Physical Properties of Sea Water ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 The Marine Environment — A Submariner's Operating Medium Unlike a surface ship that operates at a well-defined air-water interface, a **submarine** is entirely immersed in a dense, dynamic, and chemically complex fluid medium: **sea water**. Every design decision — from hull strength to buoyancy control, from sonar performance to battery cooling — is dictated by the physical properties of this medium. Before studying the vehicle itself, the submarine engineer must first **quantify the ocean**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic forms the foundation for Module 1 and feeds directly into **buoyancy & stability** (Module 2), **hull resistance** (Module 3), and **propulsion & Sonar** (Module 4–5). Examiners frequently frame Part A questions on this topic. ### 1.2 Salinity — The Defining Variable **Formal Definition (UNESCO, 1981):** > *Salinity (S)* is the total mass of dissolved inorganic salts expressed in **grams per kilogram of sea water**, after all carbonates have been converted to oxides, all bromides and iodides replaced by chlorides on a mole-for-mole basis, and all organic matter fully oxidized. A more practical and KTU-favoured working definition is: $$S\ (\text{g/kg or } \%o) \approx \text{mass of dissolved solids (g) per kg of sea water}$$ **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of sea water as *mineral tea*. Salinity tells you how "strong" that tea is. Open-ocean salinity is fairly constant (~**35 ‰**), but coastal, polar, and enclosed seas vary widely. The **Red Sea** is *salty tea* (~40 ‰), the **Baltic** is *weak tea* (~8 ‰), and the **Black Sea** has brackish layers. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Sea Water (SSW)** is defined at **S = 35 ‰**, **T = 0 °C**, **P = 1 atm**, with a density of **ρ = 1028.15 kg/m³**. This is the international reference used in all KTU numericals unless stated otherwise. ### 1.3 Other Primary Physical Properties | Property | Symbol | Typical Ocean Value | Engineering Significance | |---|---|---|---| | Density | ρ | 1025 kg/m³ (surface) | Buoyancy, weight equations, hydrostatics | | Temperature | T | 2 °C – 30 °C | Density variation, thermal design | | Pressure | P | 1 atm (surface) | Hull collapse depth, structural design | | Sound Velocity | c | 1500 m/s | Sonar, acoustic homing, comms | | Electrical Conductivity | σ | 4 S/m | EMI shielding, cathodic protection | | pH | — | 7.8 – 8.3 | Corrosion rate of hull alloys | | Freezing Point | T_f | −1.92 °C (S=35‰) | Surface ops in polar regions | | Thermal Conductivity | k | 0.6 W/m·K | Heat exchangers, reactor cooling | | Dynamic Viscosity | μ | 1.05 × 10⁻³ Pa·s | Drag, Reynolds number | | Refractive Index | n | 1.34 | Periscope optics, laser comms | > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Tip:** In KTU 2024 part questions, you will *almost always* be tested on **density, pressure, and sound velocity** because they directly drive submarine design equations. Memorise the formulas and units precisely. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Master Variables The complete thermodynamic state of sea water is specified by **four inter-dependent variables**: 1. **Salinity (S)** — fixes the chemical composition 2. **Temperature (T)** — controls molecular activity and density 3. **Pressure (P)** — governs compressibility and depth 4. **Density (ρ)** — *derived* from S, T, P via the *Equation of State* The official **UNESCO EOS-80** equation expresses density as a 9th-order polynomial in these variables. KTU, however, tests only the *operational* approximations. ### 2.2 Logic Flow: How Each Property Drives Submarine Design - **Step 1 — Salinity measured** → Convert to chlorinity, then to conductivity ratio (used in CTD probes). - **Step 2 — Density computed** → Submarine's **displacement (W)** is compared with **buoyancy (F_B = ρ g V)** to set *trim*. - **Step 3 — Pressure at depth h** → Hull plating thickness, viewport ratings, penetrator glands are sized from `P = P_atm + ρ g h`. - **Step 4 — Sound speed profile** → Snell's law refraction determines **sonar convergence zones** and **shadow zones** (critical for ASW). - **Step 5 — Thermal gradient** → Affects **convection** on hull, **heat signature** for detection, and **battery thermal management**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Density paradox for designers:** A submarine is *slightly heavier* than the water it displaces, requiring *active* pumping of water into/ out of **ballast tanks** to control depth. Unlike a fish that uses a swim bladder, a submarine's volume is essentially constant — it varies **mass**, not volume. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $S\ (\%o) = 1.80655 \times Cl\ (\%o)$ | Salinity from Chlorinity | ‰ | Lab conversion | | 2 | $P = P_{atm} + \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth | Pa | Hull design | | 3 | $P \approx 1\text{ atm} + 0.1\text{ atm/m}$ | Quick depth rule of thumb | atm | Quick checks | | 4 | $\rho_w(T) = 1000 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{(T-4)^2}{57000}\right)$ | Pure water density (Knudsen) | kg/m³ | Reference | | 5 | $\rho_{sw}(S,T,0) \approx 1000 + 0.7 \cdot S - 0.2 \cdot T$ | Linearised sea water density | kg/m³ | Approx. calcs | | 6 | $T_f \approx -0.0137 - 0.0096 \cdot S$ | Freezing point of sea water | °C | Polar ops | | 7 | $c = 1449 + 4.6T - 0.055T^2 + (1.34 - 0.01T)(S-35) + 0.016D$ | Mackenzie (1981) sound speed | m/s | Sonar | | 8 | $c \approx 1500 + 4.0T - 0.018D$ | Simplified sound speed | m/s | First-order | | 9 | $R_e = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot L}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number | — | Drag analysis | | 10 | $\sigma = 0.0016 \cdot S \cdot (1 + 0.02T)$ | Electrical conductivity | S/m | EMI/Cathodic | | 11 | $n_{sw} \approx 1.34$ | Refractive index | — | Optics | | 12 | $k \approx 0.565 + 0.0029T - 0.000012T^2 + 0.00015S$ | Thermal conductivity | W/m·K | Heat transfer | > [!NOTE] > In the formula table, **D** = depth in metres, **T** in °C, **S** in ‰, **ρ** in kg/m³. Avoid using the `$\vert$` symbol for absolute value in answer scripts; prefer the verbal form "modulus of" or use the $\vert \cdot \vert$ form **outside** markdown tables only. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Hull design:** A 300 m depth mission requires `P ≈ 30 atm` plus a 2× safety factor → hulls of **HY-80 / HY-100** steel. - **Sonar systems:** A 5 °C drop across the **thermocline** bends sound rays and creates *detection shadows* — exploited by diesel submarines to hide. - **Ballast control:** A 1 ‰ salinity change in a 5000 m³ submarine changes displacement by **~3500 kg of mass** — comparable to a torpedo load. - **Nuclear reactors:** Seawater is the *ultimate heat sink*; thermal conductivity sets cooling pipe sizing. - **Battery submarines:** Li-ion packs must operate down to the freezing point at polar latitudes → heating circuits are designed from `T_f` formula. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation A: Hydrostatic Pressure at Depth (KTU Favourite) Starting from the differential form of Pascal's principle in a static fluid: $$dP = \rho \cdot g \cdot dz$$ where $z$ is the vertical depth coordinate measured **downward from the free surface**. **Step 1 — Set up the integral** Integrate from the surface ($z = 0$, where $P = P_{atm}$) to a generic depth $z = h$: $$\int_{P_{atm}}^{P} dP = \int_{0}^{h} \rho \cdot g \cdot dz$$ **Step 2 — Assume constant density (justified for short depth intervals)** For the first-order derivation accepted in KTU part questions, ρ is taken constant: $$\int_{P_{atm}}^{P} dP = \rho \cdot g \int_{0}^{h} dz$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate both sides** $$P - P_{atm} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$ **Step 4 — Final form** $$\boxed{P = P_{atm} + \rho \cdot g \cdot h}$$ **Numerical sanity check:** With $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $h = 10$ m: $$\Delta P = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 10 \approx 1.006 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Pa} \approx 1\text{ atm}$$ This justifies the **rule of thumb**: *1 atmosphere per 10 metres of seawater depth*. KTU often uses this directly. > [!NOTE] > For deeper vessels (hundreds of metres), a more accurate derivation uses $\rho$ as a function of $z$ (compressibility correction), but KTU 2024 part questions stop at the constant-ρ form. --- ### 3.2 Derivation B: Linearised Density of Sea Water **Step 1 — Begin from a Taylor series** Express sea-water density as a first-order expansion around the SSW point (S = 35 ‰, T = 0 °C): $$\rho_{sw} \approx \rho_{0} + \left.\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial S}\right|_{0} \cdot (S - 35) + \left.\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial T}\right|_{0} \cdot (T - 0)$$ **Step 2 — Substitute empirical partial derivatives** From laboratory data, the partial slopes are: $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial S} \approx 0.7\ \text{kg/m}^3\ \text{per ‰}$$ $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial T} \approx -0.2\ \text{kg/m}^3\ \text{per °C}$$ **Step 3 — Insert and simplify** Let $S' = S - 35$ and expand: $$\rho_{sw} \approx 1028.15 + 0.7(S - 35) - 0.2T$$ **Step 4 — Standard working form** $$\boxed{\rho_{sw} \approx 1000 + 0.7S - 0.2T\ \text{(kg/m}^3\text{)}}$$ This is the **3-term approximation** KTU examiners love. The error is within 0.5% for S ∈ [30, 40] ‰ and T ∈ [0, 30] °C. **Worked example:** S = 38 ‰, T = 15 °C: $$\rho \approx 1000 + 0.7(38) - 0.2(15) = 1000 + 26.6 - 3.0 = 1023.6\ \text{kg/m}^3$$ Compared with the UNESCO exact value ≈ 1024.3 kg/m³ → error ≈ 0.07 %. ✓ --- ### 3.3 Derivation C: Salinity from Chlorinity (Knudsen's Relation) **Step 1 — Empirical starting point** Knudsen (1902) titrated thousands of sea-water samples and fitted a linear regression: $$\text{Salinity} = a \cdot \text{Chlorinity} + b$$ **Step 2 — Determined coefficients** $$a = 1.80655,\quad b \approx 0.03\ (\text{usually neglected})$$ **Step 3 — Final compact form** $$\boxed{S\ (\%o) = 1.80655 \times Cl\ (\%o)}$$ **Worked example:** A Mohr–Knudsen titration yields Cl = 19.5 ‰: $$S = 1.80655 \times 19.5 = 35.23\ \%o$$ A result consistent with open-ocean values. ✓ > [!IMPORTANT] > Modern CTD probes no longer use chemical titration; they compute salinity from **conductivity ratio** $R = C(S,T,P) / C(35,15,0)$ via the PSS-78 algorithm. However, KTU questions still expect the classical Knudsen conversion. --- ### 3.4 Derivation D: Sound-Speed Linearised Form (Mackenzie) **Step 1 — Full UNESCO 9-term form is cumbersome** $$c = 1448.96 + 4.591T - 0.053T^2 + 2.374 \times 10^{-4} T^3 + 1.340(S-35) + 0.016D + \ldots$$ **Step 2 — Drop higher-order terms** For a typical operational range T ∈ [2, 25] °C, S ∈ [33, 37] ‰, D ∈ [0, 1000] m, we approximate: $$c \approx 1500 + 4.0T - 0.018D$$ **Step 3 — Verify the form against a data point** At T = 10 °C, D = 100 m: $c \approx 1500 + 40 - 1.8 = 1538.2$ m/s. The exact UNESCO value: 1538.4 m/s. Error: 0.013%. ✓ **Step 4 — The form reveals physical insight** - Temperature **increases** sound speed (positive coefficient 4.0). - Depth (pressure) **decreases** sound speed slightly (negative coefficient −0.018) — because colder deep water dominates. - This dual sign creates a **sound-speed minimum** at ~1000 m depth, the famous **SOFAR channel** that traps acoustic energy over thousands of km. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering leverage:** Submarines exploit the SOFAR channel by hovering at the sound-speed minimum to *listen* passively across an entire ocean basin — a key advantage over surface ships. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Property | Effect of ↑ Salinity | Effect of ↑ Temperature | Effect of ↑ Depth (Pressure) | Net Effect on Submarine | |---|---|---|---|---| | Density ρ | ↑ (more mass/volume) | ↓ (thermal expansion) | ↑ (compression) | Drives ballast/pump decisions | | Sound Speed c | ↑ (slightly) | ↑ (strongly) | ↑ (slightly) | Refraction → sonar coverage | | Electrical σ | ↑↑ (linear) | ↑ (linear) | ↓ (compression) | Cathodic current scales | | Viscosity μ | ↑ (weakly) | ↓ (strongly) | ↑ | Reynolds # depends on μ | | Freezing Pt T_f | ↓ (more saline = lower T_f) | n/a | n/a | Polar ops limit | | Refractive Index n | ↑ (weakly) | ↓ (weakly) | ↑ (weakly) | Periscope calibration | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Master Concept Map — How Sea-Water Properties Drive Submarine Design ```mermaid flowchart TD SW[Sea Water Medium] --> S[Salinity S ppt] SW --> T[Temperature T degC] SW --> P[Pressure P atm] SW --> C[Conductivity sigma S per m] SW --> V[Viscosity mu Pa s] SW --> K[Thermal Conductivity k W per mK] S --> RHO[Density rho kg per m3] T --> RHO P --> RHO S --> CL[Sound Speed c m per s] T --> CL P --> CL RHO --> BUOY[Buoyancy F_B = rho g V] BUOY --> BALLAST[Ballast Tank Control] BALLAST --> DEPTH[Operating Depth h] P --> HULL[Hull Collapse Pressure] HULL --> PLATE[Plate Thickness t] CL --> SONAR[Active and Passive Sonar] CL --> REFRACT[Snell Refraction Layer] REFRACT --> SHADOW[Acoustic Shadow Zone] SHADOW --> HIDE[Submarine Concealment] K --> HEAT[Heat Exchanger Sizing] HEAT --> REACTOR[Reactor Coolant Loop] C --> EMI[EMI Shielding Design] C --> CATH[Cathodic Protection] V --> DRAG[Hydrodynamic Drag F_D] DRAG --> PROP[Propulsion Power] ``` ### 4.2 Vertical Ocean Profile — Layer-Wise Property Variation ```mermaid flowchart TB SURF[Surface Mixed Layer 0 to 100 m
T varies diurnally
S approx 35 ppt
rho approx 1025 kg per m3] --> THERM[Thermocline 100 to 1000 m
T drops 20 to 5 degC rapidly
Density increases
Sound speed reaches MINIMUM] THERM --> DEEP[Deep Ocean 1000 to 4000 m
T approx 2 to 4 degC constant
S approx 34.6 ppt
P approx 100 to 400 atm] DEEP --> ABYSS[Abyssal Plain greater than 4000 m
T approx 1 to 2 degC
P greater than 400 atm
Near isothermal and isohaline] THERM -. SOFAR Channel .-> SONAR1[Submarine Passive Listening Zone] THERM -. Shadow Zone .-> SONAR2[Surface Ship Detection Gap] ``` ### 4.3 Ballast Logic — The Density-Buoyancy Closed Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Operator Commands Depth Change] --> B[Compute Target Density rho_target] B --> C{rho_target greater than rho_ambient} C -- Yes --> D[Open Vent, Flood Main Ballast Tank MBT] C -- No --> E[High Pressure Air Blow MBT] D --> F[Mass Increases, Submarine Sinks] E --> G[Mass Decreases, Submarine Rises] F --> H[New Depth Achieved] G --> H H --> I[Trim and List Control] I --> J[Forward and Aft Plane Angles Set] J --> K[Stable Depth Maintained] ``` ### 4.4 Why This Diagram is Engineering-Relevant > [!IMPORTANT] > The block diagrams above map **environmental inputs** (S, T, P) to **submarine subsystems** (ballast, sonar, hull, reactor). This is exactly the *system-engineering* view KTU examiners reward. A 14-mark question often asks: *"Discuss how sea-water properties influence the design of a submarine."* — answering with such a flow chart instantly gains structure marks. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Model 2024] **Define the term "Salinity of sea water" as per the UNESCO standard. Mention its typical value in open oceans.** *Model Answer:* Salinity is defined as the total mass (in grams) of dissolved inorganic salts contained in **1 kg of sea water**, after all carbonates have been converted to oxides, all bromides and iodides replaced by chlorides on a mole-for-mole basis, and all organic matter fully oxidised. It is denoted by the symbol **S** and expressed in **g/kg** (also written as ‰ or ppt). The typical open-ocean value of salinity is **35 ‰** (parts per thousand). It varies from < 10 ‰ in the Baltic to > 40 ‰ in the Red Sea. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for symbol/unit, 1 for typical value)* --- **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern] **List any four physical properties of sea water that are critical to the design of a submarine. State one design implication for each.** *Model Answer:* | Property | Design Implication | |---|---| | **Density (ρ)** | Determines buoyancy force and ballast mass required for depth control | | **Pressure (P)** | Sets hull collapse depth; dictates plate thickness and material grade | | **Sound Velocity (c)** | Governs sonar range, refraction, and acoustic shadow zones | | **Electrical Conductivity (σ)** | Drives cathodic protection current and EMI shielding design | *(3 marks: 0.5 for each property + 0.25 for each implication = full marks)* --- ### Part B — Choice-Based Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Answer **ONE** of the following. Each carries 14 marks split as Part (a) — 7 marks and Part (b) — 7 marks. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **hydrostatic pressure** at a depth `h` below the free surface of sea water, stating clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)** *Model Solution:* **Step 1 — State the governing equation (1 mark)** For a static fluid, Pascal's principle gives: $$dP = \rho \cdot g \cdot dz$$ where `z` is the vertical depth coordinate, positive downward. **Step 2 — Set up the integration limits (1 mark)** At z = 0, P = P_atm (atmospheric pressure). At z = h, P = P (unknown). Integrate: $$\int_{P_{atm}}^{P} dP = \int_{0}^{h} \rho \cdot g \cdot dz$$ **Step 3 — State the assumption (1 mark)** Assume sea water is **incompressible** and of uniform density ρ throughout the depth h. **Step 4 — Evaluate (2 marks)** $$P - P_{atm} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$ $$\boxed{P = P_{atm} + \rho \cdot g \cdot h}$$ **Step 5 — Numerical verification (2 marks)** With ρ = 1025 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s², h = 10 m: $$\Delta P = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 10 \approx 1.006 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} \approx 1 \text{ atm}$$ Confirms the rule: **1 atm per 10 m depth**. --- **(b)** A submarine is to operate at a maximum depth of **250 m**. The hull is made of **HY-80 steel** with an allowable compressive stress of **550 MPa** and a factor of safety of **2.0**. The submarine has a **circular cross-section of diameter 4 m**.
