Determine: **(i)** the maximum pressure on the hull, (ii) the minimum required hull thickness. **(7 marks)** *Model Solution:* **(i) Maximum pressure (3 marks)** Using `P = P_atm + ρ g h`: $$P = 101325 + 1025 \times 9.81 \times 250$$ $$P = 101325 + 2513812.5 = 2.615 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 2.615 \text{ MPa}$$ For thin-walled cylinder, the **gauge pressure** is what acts on the hull: $$P_{gauge} = \rho g h = 2.514 \text{ MPa}$$ **(ii) Minimum hull thickness (4 marks)** For a thin-walled cylinder, hoop stress formula: $$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P \cdot r}{t}$$ Allowable stress with FoS: $$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{550}{2.0} = 275 \text{ MPa}$$ Rearrange for t: $$t = \frac{P \cdot r}{\sigma_{allow}} = \frac{2.514 \times 10^6 \times 2}{275 \times 10^6}$$ $$t = \frac{5.028 \times 10^6}{2.75 \times 10^8} = 0.01828 \text{ m} \approx 18.3 \text{ mm}$$ Standard selected: **20 mm plate of HY-80**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain the relationship between **salinity, temperature, and density of sea water**. How does this relationship govern the **ballast control** of a submarine? **(7 marks)** *Model Solution:* **Step 1 — Functional relationship (2 marks)** The density of sea water depends on salinity (S) and temperature (T) via: $$\rho \approx 1000 + 0.7S - 0.2T\ \text{(kg/m}^3\text{)}$$ - ↑ S → ↑ ρ (more dissolved salts increase mass per unit volume) - ↑ T → ↓ ρ (thermal expansion reduces density) - Net effect at the surface: warmer, fresher water is less dense → floats above colder, saltier water. **Step 2 — Vertical stratification (2 marks)** This produces a stable ocean with a **pycnocline** (sharp density gradient). The submarine's natural buoyancy changes with depth and location. **Step 3 — Ballast principle (2 marks)** A submarine's **volume is fixed**; it controls depth by varying its **mass**: - To **dive**: flood main ballast tanks (MBTs) → mass increases → ρ_sub > ρ_water → sinks. - To **surface**: blow MBTs with high-pressure air → mass decreases → ρ_sub < ρ_water → rises. **Step 4 — Environmental adjustment (1 mark)** Because ambient ρ varies with S and T, the volume of water that must be pumped in/ out changes. A 1 ‰ drop in salinity in a 5000 m³ sub reduces buoyancy by **3500 kg** — significant for depth control loops. --- **(b)** A surface ship has a displacement of **12,000 tonnes** in sea water of density 1025 kg/m³. It is now moved to a **river estuary** where salinity is 15 ‰, temperature 25 °C. Calculate: **(i)** the new density of the water, (ii) the **change in buoyancy** (in tonnes) the ship experiences, assuming its volume remains constant. **(7 marks)** *Model Solution:* **(i) New density (3 marks)** Using $\rho \approx 1000 + 0.7S - 0.2T$: $$\rho_{estuary} = 1000 + 0.7(15) - 0.2(25) = 1000 + 10.5 - 5.0 = 1005.5 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ **(ii) Volume of ship (2 marks)** $$V = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{12,000 \times 10^3}{1025} = 11,707.3 \text{ m}^3$$ **(iii) New buoyancy (2 marks)** $$F_B = \rho_{estuary} \cdot g \cdot V = 1005.5 \times 11,707.3 = 11,771,693 \text{ kg}$$ Equivalent displacement: $$m' = 11,771.7 \text{ tonnes}$$ **Net change in buoyancy:** $$\Delta m = 12,000 - 11,771.7 = 228.3 \text{ tonnes REDUCTION}$$ The ship sits **~23 cm higher** in the estuary, requiring **bunker/water ballast adjustment** to maintain a safe draft. --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting `P_atm`** in pressure calculations at depth. Marks lost: 1–2 per sub-part. > 2. **Confusing ppt (‰) with ppm (mg/L).** 35 ‰ = 35,000 ppm. Salinity is *never* in ppm. > 3. **Using ρ = 1000 kg/m³** (pure water) instead of ~1025 kg/m³ (sea water). This causes ~2.5% error — heavy penalty in 14-mark numericals. > 4. **Mixing up the sign of the T coefficient** in density. *Warmer water is LIGHTER*, not heavier. > 5. **No units in final answers.** KTU's *strict* rule: every numerical answer must carry **SI units**. > 6. **Skipping assumptions in derivations.** Always state "ρ is constant", "fluid is static", "T is uniform" *before* integrating. --- ### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Salinity (S)** is the master chemistry variable; **35 ‰** is the open-ocean benchmark. - **Density (ρ)** is *derived* — function of S, T, P. Use the linearised form $\rho \approx 1000 + 0.7S - 0.2T$ for KTU problems. - **Pressure (P)** at depth h: $P = P_{atm} + \rho g h$, or the rule "**1 atm per 10 m**". - **Sound speed (c)** ≈ 1500 m/s; increases with T, decreases slightly with depth. The **SOFAR channel** is at the speed minimum. - **Freezing point** of sea water is **lower** than pure water: $T_f \approx -1.92$ °C at S = 35 ‰. - **Salinity-Chlorinity**: $S = 1.80655 \times Cl$ (Knudsen's relation, 1902). - A submarine is **slightly heavier** than the water it displaces — it varies **mass**, not volume, to change depth. - Ballast control equations: $F_B = \rho g V$ (buoyancy), $W = m \cdot g$ (weight); depth is achieved by making $W > F_B$ (sink) or $W < F_B$ (rise). - **Electrical conductivity** ≈ 4 S/m → drives cathodic protection and EMI shielding. - **Thermal conductivity** ≈ 0.6 W/m·K → governs reactor and battery heat-sink design. - The **pycnocline** (density gradient) and **thermocline** (temperature gradient) often coincide — the submarine engineer treats them as a single boundary. - **Acoustic shadow zones** created by the thermocline allow submarines to hide from surface sonar — a fundamental operational advantage. - The **UNESCO EOS-80** equation of state is the gold standard; KTU accepts the linearised 3-term form unless otherwise stated. - **Material reference for hulls:** HY-80 (550 MPa yield) and HY-100 (690 MPa yield) are the workhorses for modern submarine hulls.

topics.title: Dynamical Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST634 - SUBMARINES AND SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dynamical Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of submarine and submersible engineering, **Dynamical Processes** refer to the time-dependent motion of a fully submerged or semi-submerged body subjected to external forces, hydrodynamic reactions, gravitational loads, buoyancy forces, and thrust inputs. The study of dynamical processes is the mathematical and physical analysis of how a submarine responds to control inputs, environmental disturbances, and manoeuvring commands in **six (6) degrees of freedom (6-DOF)**: three translational (surge, sway, heave) and three rotational (roll, pitch, yaw).

The governing framework combines Newton's second law applied to a rigid body in a fluid medium with the equations of hydrostatics and hydrodynamics. Dynamical processes govern:

- **Depth change dynamics** (diving and surfacing)
- **Course change dynamics** (horizontal manoeuvring)
- **Steady and unsteady state** velocity vector evolution
- **Stability and control** under perturbations

The standard reference coordinate system is the **body-fixed frame (O-XYZ)** moving with the submarine, and the **Earth-fixed (inertial) frame (O₀-ξηζ)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** *"A dynamical process in a submarine is the governed, time-dependent evolution of its six degrees of freedom governed by the coupled nonlinear equations of motion, with hydrodynamic, hydrostatic, and propulsion forces acting on the rigid body in a fluid medium."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car but **cannot see out of the windshield** and have only a **steering wheel, accelerator, and ballast lever**. The car must move forward, backward, sideways (in some cases), and even **up and down in 3D space** (like an elevator), all while a **mass of water keeps trying to push back, drag, and steer you** without your permission.

The **"dynamical process"** is everything that happens *between* the moment you press a control and the moment the submarine actually moves and stabilises at a new state. It includes:

- The **inertia** of the submarine (heavy steel body wants to keep going)
- The **water resistance** (drag) that opposes motion
- The **sideways push** of water (added mass) when you try to turn
- The **buoyancy restoration** (water pushing you back to equilibrium depth)
- The **oscillations** (roll/pitch/yaw) that occur before stabilisation

Think of a **loaded cargo ship in a storm** — but in *every direction*, including vertical. That is a dynamical process.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following constants and metrics are used universally in submarine dynamical analysis:
>
> - **ρ** = Density of seawater = **1025 kg/m³** (standard KTU value)
> - **g** = Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
> - **ν** = Kinematic viscosity of seawater ≈ **1.05 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**
> - **L** = Length of submarine (typical values: 60 m for SSK, 110 m for SSN, 170 m for SSBN)
> - **B** = Beam (diameter) of submarine
> - **D** = Displacement volume
> - **V** = Submarine speed
> - **U** = Forward surge velocity
> - **W** = Submerged weight

### 1.4 The Six Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF)

| DOF | Symbol | Linear/Angular | Direction |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Surge | u | Linear | Forward (+) / Astern (−) along X |
| Sway | v | Linear | Starboard (+) / Port (−) along Y |
| Heave | w | Linear | Up (+) / Down (−) along Z |
| Roll | p | Angular | Rotation about X-axis |
| Pitch | q | Angular | Rotation about Y-axis |
| Yaw | r | Angular | Rotation about Z-axis |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Force Components Acting on a Submarine

The complete dynamical analysis of a submarine requires the consideration of the following force/moment components:

1. **Hydrostatic Forces (Restoring Forces)**
   - Buoyancy force acts vertically upward through the centre of buoyancy (CB)
   - Weight acts vertically downward through the centre of gravity (CG)
   - The offset between CB and CG produces **restoring moments** in roll and pitch

2. **Hydrodynamic Forces (Manoeuvring Forces)**
   - Generated by motion through water
   - Divided into **linear** and **nonlinear** terms based on velocity

3. **Propulsion Forces**
   - Generated by the propeller(s) acting along the surge axis
   - Thrust T and torque Q

4. **External Disturbance Forces**
   - Currents, waves (for surface running), wind

### 2.2 Hydrodynamic Force Decomposition

The total hydrodynamic force on a submarine is decomposed using **strip theory** and **slender-body theory** into:

$$
F_{hydro} = F_{inertia} + F_{damping} + F_{restoring}
$$

Where:
- **Inertia forces** are proportional to acceleration (and form the **added mass** matrix)
- **Damping forces** are proportional to velocity (linear) and velocity squared (nonlinear)
- **Restoring forces** arise from geometry (form) and gravity-buoyancy offset

### 2.3 The Added Mass Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Added Mass (m_a):** When a body accelerates through a fluid, it must also accelerate a portion of the surrounding fluid. This is modelled as if the body has an *additional* mass attached to it. This is the **added mass** effect.

For a submarine, the added mass in the **axial (surge) direction** is small (≈ 0.1 m to 0.2 m), but in the **lateral (sway) and vertical (heave) directions**, it is very large (≈ 0.7 m to 1.0 m of mass ratio). This is because axial flow separates cleanly, while lateral flow creates a large entrained water mass.

The **mass ratio** is defined as:

$$
m' = \frac{m + m_a}{m}
$$

Where:
- **m** = actual mass of submarine
- **m_a** = hydrodynamic added mass

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|:--:|:---------|:------------|:------|
| 1 | $F = m \frac{d\vec{V}}{dt} + \vec{\omega} \times (m\vec{V})$ | Newton's law for rigid body in moving frame | N |
| 2 | $m \dot{u} - m v r + m w q = X$ | Surge equation (linear) | N |
| 3 | $m \dot{v} + m u r - m w p = Y$ | Sway equation (linear) | N |
| 4 | $m \dot{w} - m u q + m v p = Z$ | Heave equation (linear) | N |
| 5 | $I_{xx} \dot{p} + (I_{zz}-I_{yy}) q r = K$ | Roll moment equation | N·m |
| 6 | $I_{yy} \dot{q} + (I_{xx}-I_{zz}) p r = M$ | Pitch moment equation | N·m |
| 7 | $I_{zz} \dot{r} + (I_{yy}-I_{xx}) p q = N$ | Yaw moment equation | N·m |
| 8 | $F_B = \rho g \nabla$ | Archimedes buoyancy force | N |
| 9 | $F_R = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_D A V^2$ | Drag force (resistance) | N |
| 10 | $X_{u\dot{u}} = \frac{\partial X}{\partial \dot{u}}$ | Hydrodynamic derivative definition | kg or N·s²/m |
| 11 | $T = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_T$ | Propeller thrust using thrust coefficient | N |
| 12 | $Y_{v\dot{v}} = -m' \cdot m$ | Sway added mass (negative sign by KTU convention) | kg |
| 13 | $K_{p\dot{p}} = -I_{xx}' \cdot I_{xx}$ | Roll added moment of inertia | kg·m² |
| 14 | $B \cdot \overline{GZ} = W \cdot \overline{GM_L} \sin\phi$ | Righting moment in roll | N·m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hydrodynamic Derivatives Convention:** The notation $Y_v$, $N_r$, $K_{p}$ etc. represents the partial derivative of force/moment with respect to velocity/angular velocity. Subscripts with a dot (e.g., $Y_{\dot{v}}$) indicate dependence on acceleration.

### 2.5 Classification of Hydrodynamic Derivatives

| Type | Notation Example | Meaning |
|:-----|:----------------|:--------|
| Linear damping | $X_u$, $Y_v$, $Z_w$ | Force per unit velocity |
| Quadratic damping | $X_{u \vert u \vert}$, $Y_{v \vert v \vert}$ | Force per unit velocity² (nonlinear) |
| Added mass | $X_{\dot{u}}$, $Y_{\dot{v}}$, $Z_{\dot{w}}$ | Force per unit acceleration |
| Cross-coupling | $Y_r$, $N_v$ | Side force from yaw rate, etc. |
| Control derivatives | $Y_{\delta_r}$, $N_{\delta_r}$ | Force from rudder deflection |

### 2.6 The Decoupling Assumption (Manoeuvring Theory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU-level analysis, a fundamental assumption is **decoupling**: for moderate speeds and small motions, the **vertical plane dynamics (surge, heave, pitch)** and **horizontal plane dynamics (surge, sway, yaw)** can be analysed independently. This simplifies the 6-DOF system into two 3-DOF systems.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The dynamical process equations are essential for:

- **Control system design** (autopilot for depth, heading, pitch)
- **Manoeuvring simulations** (training simulators)
- **Stability analysis** (dynamic stability of submarines)
- **Hull form optimization** (minimising drag, improving course-keeping)
- **Safety analysis** (collision avoidance, emergency surfacing)
- **Stealth operations** (predicting radiated noise due to manoeuvres)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the General 6-DOF Equations of Motion

We start with Newton's second law for a rigid body in an inertial frame:

$$
\vec{F}_{ext} = \frac{d}{dt}(m \vec{V})
$$

Where $\vec{V}$ is the velocity of the centre of gravity in the body-fixed frame.

The acceleration in the body-fixed frame includes both the linear acceleration and the Coriolis-type contribution from rotation:

$$
\left. \frac{d\vec{V}}{dt} \right|_{inertial} = \left. \frac{d\vec{V}}{dt} \right|_{body} + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{V}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\vec{F}_{ext} = m \left( \dot{\vec{V}} + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{V} \right)
$$

In component form (using u, v, w for linear and p, q, r for angular):

$$
\vec{V} = (u, v, w), \quad \vec{\omega} = (p, q, r)
$$

The cross product is:

$$
\vec{\omega} \times \vec{V} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ p & q & r \\ u & v & w \end{vmatrix}
$$

**Step 1:** Expand the determinant:

$$
\vec{\omega} \times \vec{V} = (q w - r v)\hat{i} + (r u - p w)\hat{j} + (p v - q u)\hat{k}
$$

**Step 2:** Equate the X-component to external force X:

$$
m(\dot{u} + q w - r v) = X
$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange into the standard KTU form:

$$
m \dot{u} - m v r + m w q = X
$$

**Step 4:** Similarly, equate Y-component:

$$
m(\dot{v} + r u - p w) = Y
$$

Rearranged:

$$
m \dot{v} + m u r - m w p = Y
$$

**Step 5:** For the Z-component:

$$
m(\dot{w} + p v - q u) = Z
$$

Rearranged:

$$
m \dot{w} - m u q + m v p = Z
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Rotational Equations of Motion

For angular motion, Euler's equations are used. The angular momentum vector is:

$$
\vec{H} = I \cdot \vec{\omega}
$$

For a body with diagonal inertia tensor (valid for submarines with two planes of symmetry):

$$
\vec{H} = (I_{xx} p, \, I_{yy} q, \, I_{zz} r)
$$

Euler's equation:

$$
\vec{M}_{ext} = \dot{\vec{H}} + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{H}
$$

**Step 1:** Expand the cross product $\vec{\omega} \times \vec{H}$:

$$
\vec{\omega} \times \vec{H} = (q I_{zz} r - r I_{yy} q)\hat{i} + (r I_{xx} p - p I_{zz} r)\hat{j} + (p I_{yy} q - q I_{xx} p)\hat{k}
$$

**Step 2:** X-component (Roll equation):

$$
I_{xx} \dot{p} + (I_{zz} - I_{yy}) q r = K
$$

**Step 3:** Y-component (Pitch equation):

$$
I_{yy} \dot{q} + (I_{xx} - I_{zz}) p r = M
$$

**Step 4:** Z-component (Yaw equation):

$$
I_{zz} \dot{r} + (I_{yy} - I_{xx}) p q = N
$$

These are the **standard 6-DOF rigid body equations of motion** for a submarine.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Surge Equation Including Hydrodynamic Effects

The total external force in the surge direction is:

$$
X = X_{prop} + X_{hydro} + X_{external}
$$

**Step 1:** Propulsive thrust $X_{prop} = T$ (acts along X-axis)

**Step 2:** Hydrodynamic forces are expanded using Taylor series in small motions:

$$
X_{hydro} = X_{\dot{u}} \dot{u} + X_{u} u + X_{u \vert u \vert} u \vert u \vert + X_{w q} w q + X_{q q} q q + X_{v r} v r + \dots
$$

**Step 3:** Restoring force in surge is zero (no gravitational component along X), so:

$$
X_{restore} = 0
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into Newton's second law:

$$
m \dot{u} = T + X_{\dot{u}} \dot{u} + X_{u} u + X_{u \vert u \vert} u \vert u \vert
$$

**Step 5:** Group acceleration terms on the left:

$$
(m - X_{\dot{u}}) \dot{u} = T + X_{u} u + X_{u \vert u \vert} u \vert u \vert
$$

This gives the **effective surge mass** as $(m - X_{\dot{u}})$, which is the **submarine mass plus added mass in surge**.

### 3.4 Derivation of Steady-State Yaw Turning Radius

In a **steady coordinated turn** at constant speed $U$ and yaw rate $r$, the centrifugal force is balanced by the rudder-induced side force.

**Step 1:** Centripetal acceleration required for circular motion of radius R:

$$
a_c = \frac{U^2}{R}
$$

**Step 2:** This must equal the lateral acceleration from sway:

$$
\dot{v} + U r = \frac{U^2}{R}
$$

**Step 3:** In steady turn, $\dot{v} = 0$ and $v = 0$ (no drift angle), so:

$$
U r = \frac{U^2}{R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = \frac{U}{r}
$$

**Step 4:** The required rudder angle $\delta_r$ for a given turn rate is found from the yaw moment balance:

$$
N_r r + N_{\delta_r} \delta_r = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
\delta_r = -\frac{N_r}{N_{\delta_r}} r
$$

**Step 5:** Combining, the **tactical diameter** (turn diameter) is:

$$
D_{tactical} = 2R = \frac{2U}{r}
$$

### 3.5 Derivation of Diving/Surfacing Dynamics (Heave Equation)

For vertical motion, considering ballast tank operation:

**Step 1:** External vertical force is the difference between buoyancy and weight:

$$
Z_{restore} = F_B - W = \rho g \nabla - m g
$$

**Step 2:** When the submarine is at neutral buoyancy, $F_B = W$ and $Z_{restore} = 0$. To dive, the submarine takes in water (negative buoyancy), making $F_B < W$.

**Step 3:** Heave equation becomes:

$$
(m - Z_{\dot{w}}) \dot{w} = (F_B - W) + Z_w w + Z_{w \vert w \vert} w \vert w \vert
$$

**Step 4:** Define the **buoyancy differential** $\Delta W = W - F_B$ (positive = heavy = diving). For small velocities, linearising:

$$
(m - Z_{\dot{w}}) \dot{w} = -\Delta W + Z_w w
$$

**Step 5:** Rearranging to a first-order ODE:

$$
\dot{w} = \frac{Z_w}{(m - Z_{\dot{w}})} w - \frac{\Delta W}{(m - Z_{\dot{w}})}
$$

**Step 6:** The steady-state heave velocity is:

$$
w_{ss} = \frac{\Delta W}{Z_w}
$$

Since $Z_w < 0$ (damping), $w_{ss} < 0$ when $\Delta W > 0$, confirming that a heavy submarine dives downward (negative w).

### 3.6 Derivation of the Standard Submarine Manoeuvring Model (Horizontal Plane)

The **horizontal plane** decoupled 3-DOF model (used in most KTU textbooks) is:

**Surge equation:**

$$
(m - X_{\dot{u}}) \dot{u} = X_T + X_{u} u + X_{u \vert u \vert} u \vert u \vert + X_{v r} v r
$$

**Sway equation:**

$$
(m - Y_{\dot{v}}) \dot{v} - (m - Y_{\dot{r}}) \dot{r} + m u r = Y_{v} v + Y_{r} r + Y_{\delta_r} \delta_r
$$

**Yaw equation:**

$$
-(I_{zz} - N_{\dot{r}}) \dot{r} - N_{\dot{v}} \dot{v} + I_{zz} u r = N_{v} v + N_{r} r + N_{\delta_r} \delta_r
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention Sign Note:** In KTU textbooks, $Y_{\dot{v}}$ is defined as **negative** (e.g., $Y_{\dot{v}} = -m'$ for a slender body). This negative sign convention is *extremely important* and is a common source of lost marks in exams.

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Solution Approach Summary

| Step | Action | Purpose |
|:----:|:-------|:--------|
| 1 | Identify DOF involved (1-DOF, 3-DOF horizontal, 3-DOF vertical) | Scope the problem |
| 2 | Write down the relevant equations of motion | Establish mathematical model |
| 3 | Substitute hydrodynamic derivatives from data | Apply vessel-specific values |
| 4 | Linearise (small perturbation assumption) | Obtain linear ODE system |
| 5 | Convert to state-space form $\dot{x} = A x + B u$ | Standard control form |
| 6 | Compute eigenvalues of A matrix | Determine stability |
| 7 | Time-domain integration for transient response | Analyse transient behaviour |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Six Degrees of Freedom of a Submarine (Functional Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INERTIAL["INERTIAL FRAME O-naught xi eta zeta"]
        EF1["Earth-Fixed Coordinates"]
    end
    subgraph BODY["BODY-FIXED FRAME O XYZ"]
        ORI["Origin at CG"]
    end
    subgraph TRANS["TRANSLATIONAL DOF"]
        SU["SURGE u along X"]
        SW["SWAY v along Y"]
        HV["HEAVE w along Z"]
    end
    subgraph ROT["ROTATIONAL DOF"]
        RL["ROLL p about X"]
        PT["PITCH q about Y"]
        YW["YAW r about Z"]
    end
    subgraph FORCES["FORCE COMPONENTS"]
        HF["HYDRODYNAMIC F"]
        HS["HYDROSTATIC F"]
        PR["PROPULSION T"]
        EX["EXTERNAL F"]
    end
    subgraph EQM["EQUATIONS OF MOTION"]
        NE["Newton-Euler 6-DOF System"]
    end
    subgraph SOL["SOLUTION"]
        TR["Transient Response"]
        SS["Steady State"]
        ST["Stability Analysis"]
    end
    INERTIAL --> BODY
    BODY --> TRANS
    BODY --> ROT
    TRANS --> FORCES
    ROT --> FORCES
    FORCES --> EQM
    EQM --> SOL
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram of the Submarine Manoeuvring Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["CONTROL INPUT delta_r"] --> B["RUDDER ACTUATOR"]
    B --> C["HYDRODYNAMIC FORCES Y and N"]
    C --> D["SUBMARINE DYNAMICS 6-DOF"]
    D --> E["RESPONSE u v w p q r"]
    E --> F["FEEDBACK SENSORS"]
    F --> G["CONTROL LAW"]
    G --> A
    H["DISTURBANCE wind current"] --> D
    I["PROPULSION T"] --> D
```

### 4.3 Force Decomposition Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TF["TOTAL FORCE F_ext"] --> FC1["HYDROSTATIC F_hs"]
    TF --> FC2["HYDRODYNAMIC F_hd"]
    TF --> FC3["PROPULSION F_pr"]
    TF --> FC4["EXTERNAL F_ex"]
    FC2 --> DC1["INERTIA Forces proportional to a"]
    FC2 --> DC2["DAMPING Forces proportional to V"]
    FC2 --> DC3["RESTORING Geometry-driven forces"]
    DC1 --> HD1["Added Mass m_a"]
    DC2 --> HD2["Linear and Quadratic Damping"]
    DC3 --> HD3["Buoyancy and Form Effects"]
```

### 4.4 Diving and Surfacing Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ST1["SURFACED STATE Positive Buoyancy"] --> OP1["Open Main Ballast Tanks MBT"]
    OP1 --> PR1["Water Floods MBT Mass Increases"]
    PR1 --> ST2["TRANSITION Through Periscope Depth"]
    ST2 --> PR2["Pump Water to Trim Tanks"]
    PR2 --> ST3["NEUTRAL BUOYANCY At Diving Depth"]
    ST3 --> OP2["High-Pressure Air Blow MBT"]
    OP2 --> PR3["Water Expelled Mass Decreases"]
    PR3 --> ST4["POSITIVE BUOYANCY Surfacing"]
    ST4 --> ST1
```

### 4.5 Coupled Dynamics Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VP["VERTICAL PLANE 3-DOF"]
        SU_V["Surge u"]
        HV_V["Heave w"]
        PT_V["Pitch q"]
    end
    subgraph HP["HORIZONTAL PLANE 3-DOF"]
        SU_H["Surge u"]
        SW_H["Sway v"]
        YW_H["Yaw r"]
    end
    CP["COUPLING TERMS m u r etc"] --- VP
    CP --- HP
    DEC["Decoupled for Linear Analysis"] --> VP
    DEC --> HP
```

> [!NOTE]
> These diagrams are block-level functional representations suitable for KTU examination diagrams. For physical drawings of submarine hull forms, refer to the standard architecture of a teardrop-shaped pressure hull with sail (fin), stern planes, rudder, and propeller.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
Define the term "added mass" in the context of submarine hydrodynamics. Why is the lateral added mass of a submarine much greater than its axial added mass?

**Model Answer:**

Added mass is the hypothetical increase in mass of a body that accounts for the inertia of the fluid that must be accelerated along with the body when it undergoes accelerated motion through the fluid.

**Numerical/Typical values:**

- Axial (surge) added mass ratio: **0.05 to 0.20** of actual mass
- Lateral (sway) added mass ratio: **0.70 to 1.00** of actual mass
- Vertical (heave) added mass ratio: **0.70 to 1.00** of actual mass

**Why lateral is greater:** In axial motion, the flow separates at the nose and stern, leaving a thin boundary layer. The body only has to "push" a small mass of water. In lateral motion, the submarine moves sideways through a large cross-section of water, and a substantial volume of water must be displaced and accelerated sideways — equivalent to dragging a very large "virtual mass" of water with the hull. This results in high lateral added mass.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]**
List the six degrees of freedom of a submarine. Which DOF are coupled in horizontal plane manoeuvring analysis?

**Model Answer:**

The six degrees of freedom of a submarine in 3D space are:

| # | DOF | Symbol | Type |
|:--:|:----|:------:|:----|
| 1 | Surge | u | Linear |
| 2 | Sway | v | Linear |
| 3 | Heave | w | Linear |
| 4 | Roll | p | Angular |
| 5 | Pitch | q | Angular |
| 6 | Yaw | r | Angular |

**Coupled DOF in horizontal plane manoeuvring:** Surge (u), Sway (v), and Yaw (r) are coupled. Any change in rudder angle causes simultaneous changes in surge velocity, sway velocity, and yaw rate. This coupling is captured by the **horizontal 3-DOF manoeuvring equations**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]*

**(a)** Derive the **general 6-DOF equations of motion** for a submarine in body-fixed coordinates, clearly stating the assumptions. (7 Marks)

**(b)** For a submarine moving in a **steady coordinated turn** at forward speed U = 8 m/s with yaw rate r = 0.1 rad/s, calculate the **turning radius** and **tactical diameter**. If the submarine is required to complete a 180° turn in 50 seconds, what is the required yaw rate? (7 Marks)

---

##### **Model Solution to Q.A (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. The submarine is treated as a **rigid body**.
2. The body-fixed frame origin is at the **centre of gravity (CG)**.
3. The submarine has **two planes of symmetry** (port-starboard and fore-aft), so products of inertia $I_{xy} = I_{yz} = I_{xz} = 0$.
4. The fluid is **incompressible and inviscid** for the inertia terms.
5. The Earth is **inertial** (rotational effects of Earth neglected).

**Step 1:** Apply Newton's second law:

$$
m \frac{d\vec{V}}{dt}\bigg|_{inertial} = \vec{F}_{ext}
$$

**Step 2:** Transform to body-fixed frame using transport theorem:

$$
m \left( \dot{\vec{V}} + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{V} \right) = \vec{F}_{ext}
$$

**Step 3:** Component-wise expansion gives:

$$
m(\dot{u} + q w - r v) = X
$$

$$
m(\dot{v} + r u - p w) = Y
$$

$$
m(\dot{w} + p v - q u) = Z
$$

**Step 4:** For rotational motion, Euler's equations:

$$
I_{xx} \dot{p} + (I_{zz} - I_{yy}) q r - (I_{yy} - I_{zz}) q r = K
$$

Simplifying (using symmetry):

$$
I_{xx} \dot{p} + (I_{zz} - I_{yy}) q r = K
$$

$$
I_{yy} \dot{q} + (I_{xx} - I_{zz}) p r = M
$$

$$
I_{zz} \dot{r} + (I_{yy} - I_{xx}) p q = N
$$

These are the **6-DOF rigid body equations of motion** in body-fixed coordinates.

---

##### **Model Solution to Q.A (b):**

**Given:**
- Forward speed, U = 8 m/s
- Yaw rate, r = 0.1 rad/s

**Step 1:** Turning radius formula:

$$
R = \frac{U}{r} = \frac{8}{0.1} = 80 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2:** Tactical diameter:

$$
D_{tactical} = 2R = 2 \times 80 = 160 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3:** For 180° turn in 50 seconds, required yaw rate:

$$
\Delta \psi = 180° = \pi \text{ rad} = 3.1416 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
r_{req} = \frac{\Delta \psi}{\Delta t} = \frac{3.1416}{50} = 0.0628 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 4:** Corresponding new turn radius (if U is unchanged):

$$
R_{new} = \frac{8}{0.0628} \approx 127.3 \text{ m}
$$

**Final Answers:** R = 80 m, D = 160 m, r_required = 0.0628 rad/s

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Modified]*

**(a)** Explain the **concept of hydrodynamic derivatives** with suitable examples. Why is the negative sign convention used for sway added mass $Y_{\dot{v}}$ in KTU submarine manoeuvring models? (7 Marks)

**(b)** A submarine of mass **m = 5000 tonnes** has an axial added mass coefficient of 0.10 and a lateral added mass coefficient of 0.85. Calculate the **effective mass in surge and sway**. If the submarine experiences a surge acceleration of 0.5 m/s², what is the total axial force required? (7 Marks)

---

##### **Model Solution to Q.B (a):**

**Definition:** Hydrodynamic derivatives are the partial derivatives of forces and moments with respect to motion variables (velocities, accelerations, or control inputs). They quantify how a hydrodynamic force/moment varies with changes in a specific motion parameter.

**Examples (with KTU notation):**

| Derivative | Meaning | Typical Sign |
|:-----------|:--------|:-------------|
| $X_u$ | Change in surge force per unit surge velocity | Negative (drag) |
| $Y_v$ | Change in sway force per unit sway velocity | Negative (damping) |
| $Y_{\dot{v}}$ | Change in sway force per unit sway acceleration | Negative (KTU conv.) |
| $N_r$ | Change in yaw moment per unit yaw rate | Negative (damping) |
| $Y_{\delta_r}$ | Change in sway force per unit rudder deflection | Positive |
| $N_{\delta_r}$ | Change in yaw moment per unit rudder deflection | Positive |

**Negative sign convention for $Y_{\dot{v}}$:**

By the **KTU/IEEE standard** (per SNAME notation), $Y_{\dot{v}}$ is defined such that the **inertial force** in the sway equation becomes:

$$
\text{Total inertial force} = -(m - Y_{\dot{v}}) \dot{v}
$$

Where $Y_{\dot{v}}$ itself is **negative**. Hence, $(m - Y_{\dot{v}}) = m + \vert Y_{\dot{v}} \vert = m_{effective}$ is the effective sway mass including added mass. This convention gives a positive effective mass and keeps the equation of motion in the standard form. If a student uses the opposite convention, the sign of every term in the sway equation will be wrong.

---

##### **Model Solution to Q.B (b):**

**Given:**
- Actual mass, m = 5000 tonnes = 5,000,000 kg
- Axial added mass coefficient, $k_x$ = 0.10
- Lateral added mass coefficient, $k_y$ = 0.85
- Surge acceleration, $\dot{u}$ = 0.5 m/s²

**Step 1:** Axial added mass:

$$
m_{a,x} = k_x \cdot m = 0.10 \times 5{,}000{,}000 = 500{,}000 \text{ kg} = 500 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 2:** Effective surge mass:

$$
m_{eff,surge} = m + m_{a,x} = 5{,}000{,}000 + 500{,}000 = 5{,}500{,}000 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3:** Lateral added mass:

$$
m_{a,y} = k_y \cdot m = 0.85 \times 5{,}000{,}000 = 4{,}250{,}000 \text{ kg} = 4250 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 4:** Effective sway mass:

$$
m_{eff,sway} = m + m_{a,y} = 5{,}000{,}000 + 4{,}250{,}000 = 9{,}250{,}000 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 5:** Total axial force required for $\dot{u} = 0.5$ m/s²:

$$
F_x = m_{eff,surge} \times \dot{u} = 5{,}500{,}000 \times 0.5 = 2{,}750{,}000 \text{ N} = 2.75 \text{ MN}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- Effective surge mass = **5500 tonnes**
- Effective sway mass = **9250 tonnes**
- Total axial force = **2.75 × 10⁶ N**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU Board Examiner's Perspective):**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention for Hydrodynamic Derivatives (3-mark loss):** Always follow the KTU/IEEE standard. Writing $Y_{\dot{v}} = +0.85m$ instead of $-0.85m$ will mark the entire sway equation wrong.
> 2. **Forgetting Added Mass in Inertial Forces (2-mark loss):** The effective mass is $(m - X_{\dot{u}})$, not $m$. When asked to compute "force required for acceleration," include the added mass.
> 3. **Cross-Coupling Term Omission (3-mark loss):** The $mvr$, $muq$ terms in surge/sway equations are called **centripetal/gyroscopic coupling terms**. Dropping them in 3-DOF derivation is a frequent mistake.
> 4. **Units in SI Only:** KTU expects SI units throughout. Using tonnes-force, knots, etc., will lose marks. Always convert: 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s.
> 5. **Tactical Diameter vs Turning Radius Confusion:** Tactical diameter = **2 × R** (diameter of the circular path), not R itself. Many students interchange these.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Dynamical Processes**
>
> - **6-DOF of a submarine:** Surge u, Sway v, Heave w, Roll p, Pitch q, Yaw r
> - **Reference frames:** Body-fixed (XYZ at CG) and Earth-fixed (ξηζ)
> - **Newton-Euler equations** form the backbone of the dynamical model
> - **Added mass** is "virtual mass of entrained water" — large laterally, small axially
> - **Hydrodynamic derivatives** = partial derivatives of forces w.r.t. motion variables
> - **Surge added mass** $X_{\dot{u}}$ is small (5–20% of m); **Sway added mass** $Y_{\dot{v}}$ is large (70–100% of m)
> - **Negative sign convention** is mandatory in KTU for $Y_{\dot{v}}$, $N_{\dot{r}}$, $K_{\dot{p}}$
> - **Decoupling assumption** splits 6-DOF into **horizontal 3-DOF** (u, v, r) and **vertical 3-DOF** (u, w, q)
> - **Turning radius** $R = U / r$; **Tactical diameter** $D = 2R$
> - **Restoring forces** arise from CB-CG offset and gravity-buoyancy differential
> - **Equations of motion** combine inertial, hydrodynamic, hydrostatic, and propulsion forces
> - **Standard values:** ρ = 1025 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s², ν ≈ 1.05 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s
> - **Steady state** is when $\dot{u} = \dot{v} = \dot{w} = \dot{p} = \dot{q} = \dot{r} = 0$
> - **Dynamic stability** is determined by eigenvalues of the linearised state matrix A
> - **Euler's equations** for rotation include gyroscopic coupling terms $(I_{zz} - I_{yy})qr$ etc.
> - **Manoeuvring models** use horizontal plane equations for course control
> - **Diving dynamics** involve ballast tank operations affecting buoyancy differential $\Delta W$
> - **Pitch dynamics** are governed by hydrostatic restoring moment and depth-control plane forces
> - **Rudder** primarily affects yaw N and sway Y; **stern planes** primarily affect pitch M and heave Z
> - **Cross-coupling derivatives** like $Y_r$, $N_v$ couple sway and yaw in horizontal plane
> - **Propeller thrust** $T$ along X-axis is the primary surge force generator
> - **Linearisation** requires small perturbations from an equilibrium state

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Submarine Hydrostatics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST634 - SUBMARINES AND SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Submarine Hydrostatics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Submarine Hydrostatics?

**Submarine Hydrostatics** is the branch of naval architecture that studies the equilibrium of a submarine under the action of vertical forces — primarily **weight (W)**, **buoyancy (B)**, and the resulting restoring moments — when the vessel is floating at rest in still water, either on the surface, awash, or fully submerged. It governs whether the submarine floats, sinks, or maintains a desired depth without forward motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Hydrostatics deals with the study of forces and moments acting on a submarine in the static (non-accelerating) condition. It is the prerequisite for understanding submarine stability, diving, surfacing, and pressure hull design.*

## 1.2 Fundamental Principles (KTU High-Yield Concepts)

### (a) Archimedes' Principle (Buoyancy)
When a submarine is immersed in a fluid, it experiences an upward force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the **wetted volume** of the hull.

$$B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla$$

Where:
- $B$ = Buoyant force (Newtons)
- $\rho_{sw}$ = Density of sea water $\approx \mathbf{1025 \; kg/m^3}$ (standard seawater)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity $\approx \mathbf{9.81 \; m/s^2}$
- $\nabla$ = Volume of displaced water = submerged volume of the hull ($m^3$)

### (b) Reserve of Buoyancy (R)
The **extra volume of water** that can be taken into the variable ballast tanks before the submarine is completely submerged.

$$R = V_{s} - V_{d}$$

Where $V_{s}$ is the total shell volume (volume of the pressure hull envelope) and $V_{d}$ is the volume actually displaced when the submarine is in the surfaced condition. It is usually expressed as a **percentage**:

$$R \; (\%) = \frac{V_{s} - V_{d}}{V_{s}} \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical values:** Conventional submarines have a Reserve of Buoyancy between **30% and 60%** of the total hull volume. Lower reserves are found in deep-diving research submersibles.

### (c) Centre of Buoyancy (B) vs Centre of Gravity (G)
- **Centre of Gravity (G)**: Point through which the total weight (hull + machinery + crew + stores + water ballast) is considered to act. It is fixed *relative to the hull* but moves when masses shift internally.
- **Centre of Buoyancy (CoB / B)**: Centroid of the displaced water volume. Its position depends on the *shape of the submerged hull*, which changes with submergence depth.

### (d) Condition of Equilibrium (Floating / Submerged)
A submarine in static equilibrium must satisfy:

$$\sum F_{vertical} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad W = B$$

$$\sum M_{about \; G} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{No net heeling/trimming moment}$$

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Balloon in a Bathtub" Concept

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a sealed plastic bottle with a small air pocket trapped inside, floating upright in a bathtub. The air pocket is the **reserve buoyancy** — push it down gently and it pops back up; hold it under for a moment and the air compresses (buoyancy reduces) until you let go. A submarine behaves similarly: it has a fixed hull volume with controllable water inside ballast tanks that can be vented (surfacing) or flooded (diving). The difference is that a submarine uses compressed air and pump systems to "tune" the weight-to-buoyancy ratio to within **grams** for depth-keeping.

## 1.4 Surfaces and Conditions of Submergence

| Condition | Description | Typical State |
|---|---|---|
| **Surfaced** | Hull fully out of water except keel; freeboard present | Ready for transit |
| **Awash** | Deck just at waterline; freeboard ≈ 0 | Critical transition state |
| **Periscope Depth** | Only the sail/mast above water | Submerged but observable |
| **Fully Submerged** | Entire pressure hull below waterline | Operating depth |
| **Crush Depth** | Hull collapses under hydrostatic pressure | Catastrophic limit |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse **submerged displacement** with **surface displacement**. They are different! Surface displacement excludes the volume of the superstructure above the waterline, while submerged displacement includes the *entire* pressure hull envelope.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Submarine Equilibrium

The hydrostatic study of a submarine is divided into **two distinct regimes** because the geometry of the displaced water changes abruptly at the waterline:

### Regime 1: Surface Condition
- Only the **underwater hull** displaces water.
- The waterline cuts the hull transversely.
- **Freeboard** (hull above water) provides a *visual reserve*.
- Buoyancy centre (B) lies below the waterline.

### Regime 2: Submerged Condition
- The **entire pressure hull** is below water.
- No freeboard exists.
- Buoyancy is constant regardless of small depth changes.
- B is fixed at the geometric centroid of the pressure hull.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Analysis

### Step 1 — Identify Mass and Volume Distribution
List all components contributing to weight and volume:
- **Fixed weight**: Hull, machinery, crew, permanent ballast.
- **Variable weight**: Fuel, fresh water, stores, mission equipment.
- **Ballast water**: Can be added (flood) or removed (blow) using compressed air.

### Step 2 — Apply Archimedes' Principle
The displaced volume must equal the *submerged hull volume*, generating buoyancy $B$ upward through CoB.

### Step 3 — Check Vertical Force Balance
$$W = B \quad \text{(Neutral buoyancy)}$$

- If $W > B$ → Submarine **sinks** (Negative buoyancy)
- If $W < B$ → Submarine **rises** (Positive buoyancy)
- If $W = B$ → Submarine **hovers** at constant depth

### Step 4 — Check Moment Balance
Moments about G must vanish, otherwise the submarine will **trim** (longitudinal tilt) or **list** (transverse tilt).

### Step 5 — Assess Metacentric Stability
Compute the **Metacentric Height (GM)** to ensure the submarine returns to upright after a heel disturbance.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 board-exam essentials.** Memorize units carefully.

### Table 2.1 — Core Hydrostatic Formulas

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $B = \rho_{sw} \, g \, \nabla$ | Archimedes' buoyant force | N |
| 2 | $W = m \, g$ | Total weight of submarine | N |
| 3 | $R = V_{s} - V_{d}$ | Reserve of buoyancy (volume form) | $m^3$ |
| 4 | $R(\%) = \dfrac{V_{s} - V_{d}}{V_{s}} \times 100$ | Reserve of buoyancy (percentage) | \% |
| 5 | $P = \rho_{sw} \, g \, h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | Pa |
| 6 | $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P \, D}{2 \, t}$ | Hoop stress in cylindrical pressure hull | Pa |
| 7 | $\sigma_{long} = \dfrac{P \, D}{4 \, t}$ | Longitudinal stress in pressure hull | Pa |
| 8 | $KB = \dfrac{I_{T}}{V_{disp}} \sin\phi$ | Height of metacentre above Keel (transverse) | m |
| 9 | $BM_{T} = \dfrac{I_{T}}{\nabla}$ | Transverse metacentric radius | m |
| 10 | $BM_{L} = \dfrac{I_{L}}{\nabla}$ | Longitudinal metacentric radius | m |
| 11 | $GM_{T} = KB + BM_{T} - KG$ | Transverse metacentric height | m |
| 12 | $GM_{L} = KB + BM_{L} - KG$ | Longitudinal metacentric height | m |
| 13 | $C_{B} = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \, B \, T}$ | Block coefficient | — |
| 14 | $C_{P} = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_{M} \, L}$ | Prismatic coefficient | — |
| 15 | $h_{crush} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y} \, t}{K \, R_{hull}}$ | Approx. collapse depth | m |

> **Legend:** $\rho_{sw}$ = seawater density; $V_{disp}=\nabla$ = displaced volume; $I_T$ = 2nd moment of waterline area (transverse); $I_L$ = 2nd moment about longitudinal axis; $K$ = safety factor; $A_M$ = midship area; $L,B,T$ = length, beam, draft; $R_{hull}$ = hull mean radius.

### Table 2.2 — Form Coefficients (Hull Efficiency Ratios)

| Coefficient | Formula | Typical Submarine Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Block ($C_B$)** | $\nabla / (L B T)$ | 0.60 – 0.75 |
| **Prismatic ($C_P$)** | $\nabla / (A_M L)$ | 0.70 – 0.85 |
| **Midship ($C_M$)** | $A_M / (B T)$ | 0.85 – 0.95 |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hull Form Optimization:** A high $C_B$ gives more displacement for the same length — useful for cargo submarines but increases drag.
- **Depth Control:** Hydrostatic balance (W = B) is the foundation of depth-keeping systems; even a 0.1% error in buoyancy gives a measurable depth excursion per minute.
- **Stability vs. Agility:** A large positive $GM$ gives a "stiff" submarine (recovers quickly from heel) but causes uncomfortable rolling. Submarines operate with smaller GM than surface ships.
- **Pressure Hull Sizing:** Hoop stress formula (Eq. 6) directly determines minimum hull thickness for a desired diving depth.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Buoyancy from Archimedes' Principle

Consider a fully submerged body of arbitrary shape occupying volume $\nabla$ in a fluid of density $\rho_{sw}$. The pressure at depth $z$ below the free surface is:

$$P(z) = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \, g \, z$$

The net vertical force on the body is the integral of pressure over its wetted surface. By the **divergence theorem** of vector calculus, this reduces to the weight of the displaced fluid column:

$$B = \iint_S P \, \hat{n} \, dS = \rho_{sw} \, g \int_V dV = \rho_{sw} \, g \, \nabla$$

> **Physical meaning:** The buoyant force equals the weight of fluid that *would have* occupied the same volume. The line of action passes through the **centroid of the displaced volume**, which defines the Centre of Buoyancy (B).

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Metacentric Height (GM) for a Submarine

This is the **most important derivation for KTU exams.** We tilt the submarine by a small heel angle $\phi$ and find the restoring couple.

### Setup
- $G$ = Centre of Gravity (fixed relative to hull)
- $B$ = Original Centre of Buoyancy (centroid of displaced volume at $\phi = 0$)
- $B'$ = New Centre of Buoyancy after heel by $\phi$
- $M$ = **Metacentre** — intersection of the *new vertical* through $B'$ and the *original vertical* through $B$

### Step 1 — Geometry of Shifted Buoyancy Centre

When the hull heels by $\phi$, the wedge of hull entering the water on one side equals the wedge leaving the water on the other side (assuming constant displacement). The shift of B from B to B' is **transverse** and is given by:

$$\overline{BB'} = \frac{I_{T}}{\nabla} \sin\phi \approx \frac{I_{T}}{\nabla} \, \phi \quad \text{(for small } \phi \text{ in radians)}$$

Where $I_T$ is the **second moment of area** of the waterplane (or the entire hull cross-section when fully submerged) about the longitudinal centreline.

### Step 2 — Locate the Metacentre M

The vertical through B' intersects the original centreline at M. By similar triangles, the rise of M above B is:

$$\overline{BM} = \frac{\overline{BB'}}{\sin\phi} = \frac{I_{T}}{\nabla}$$

This quantity is called the **metacentric radius** and is denoted $BM$.

### Step 3 — Combine to get GM

Place Keel (K) as reference. The vertical distances are:
- $KB$ = height of original buoyancy centre above keel
- $KG$ = height of centre of gravity above keel
- $KM = KB + BM$

Therefore, the metacentric height above G is:

$$\boxed{GM = KM - KG = KB + BM - KG = KB + \frac{I_{T}}{\nabla} - KG}$$

### Step 4 — Stability Criterion

The righting moment (couple) developed after a small heel $\phi$ is:

$$M_{R} = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\phi \approx W \cdot GM \cdot \phi$$

| Condition | Behaviour |
|---|---|
| $GM > 0$ | **Stable** — submarine returns to upright (positive righting moment) |
| $GM = 0$ | **Neutral** — submarine remains at heeled angle |
| $GM < 0$ | **Unstable** — submarine continues to roll over (capsize) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For submarines**, the waterplane moment of inertia $I_T$ must account for the **entire pressure hull cross-section** when fully submerged, since the actual waterplane (which is the sea surface) is no longer "inside" the geometry. This is a key KTU 2024 board-exam point.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: Hoop Stress in a Cylindrical Pressure Hull

Consider a thin-walled cylindrical pressure hull of diameter $D$, wall thickness $t$, subjected to external hydrostatic pressure $P$ at depth $h$.

### Step 1 — Force Balance on Half-Cylinder

Cut the cylinder along a longitudinal plane. The external pressure $P$ acts over the projected area $D \times L$ (per unit length, this is $D$):

$$F_{external} = P \cdot D \cdot 1 = P \, D$$

### Step 2 — Internal Resisting Force

The hoop stress $\sigma_h$ acts over the two wall sections, each of area $t \times 1$:

$$F_{internal} = 2 \, \sigma_h \, t$$

### Step 3 — Equate for Equilibrium

$$2 \, \sigma_h \, t = P \, D \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\sigma_h = \frac{P \, D}{2 \, t}}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Hydrostatic Pressure

With $P = \rho_{sw} \, g \, h$:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{\rho_{sw} \, g \, h \, D}{2 \, t}$$

### Step 5 — Critical (Collapse) Depth

Setting $\sigma_h = \sigma_{yield}$ and solving for $h$:

$$h_{crush} = \frac{2 \, t \, \sigma_{y}}{\rho_{sw} \, g \, D}$$

This sets the **theoretical maximum operating depth** of the pressure hull.

> **Numerical Example (KTU Style):** A submarine has a pressure hull of diameter $D = 8$ m, thickness $t = 60$ mm, made of HY-80 steel with $\sigma_y = 550$ MPa. The depth at which hoop stress equals yield is:
>
> $$h = \frac{2 \times 0.060 \times 550 \times 10^6}{1025 \times 9.81 \times 8} \approx 821 \; m$$
>
> The actual operating depth is typically **50% of this value** to provide a safety margin.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Surface vs Submerged Stability

| Property | Surface Condition | Submerged Condition |
|---|---|---|
| Displaced volume $\nabla$ | Hull below waterline only | Entire pressure hull |
| $KB$ (height of CoB) | Below waterline | At geometric centroid of hull |
| $BM_T$ | $\dfrac{I_{T,waterplane}}{\nabla}$ | $\dfrac{I_{T,hull}}{\nabla}$ (much larger) |
| Effective $GM$ | Smaller (often $< 0.5$ m) | Larger (often $> 1.0$ m) |
| Stability feel | Sluggish, "soft" | Stiff, "hard" |
| Heeling from free surface | Yes (partially filled tanks) | Negligible (no free water in tanks) |

> [!TIP]
> **Why does $BM$ change so much?** In the surfaced condition, only the *waterplane* (a thin transverse disc near the surface) contributes to $I_T$. Once submerged, the entire hull cross-section contributes, making $I_T$ orders of magnitude larger, and hence $GM$ jumps significantly.

---

## 3.5 Derivation IV: Reserve of Buoyancy from a Ballast Curve

Let the submarine's total pressure hull envelope volume be $V_s$ (the "shell volume"). When surfaced with main ballast tanks (MBTs) empty, the displaced volume is $V_d$. The reserve of buoyancy is:

$$R = V_s - V_d$$

The **weight of water needed to flood the MBTs** to make the submarine just awash is:

$$W_{flood} = \rho_{sw} \, g \, R$$

If more water is added beyond $W_{flood}$, the submarine will submerge. To dive deeper, **trim tanks** and **negative tanks** are used, which change $W$ but not $\nabla$ (volume stays constant when fully submerged).

> [!NOTE]
> **Diving Equation:** $W = B$ at the operating depth. To dive, increase $W$ slightly (flood negative tank) until $W > B$. To hover, blow the tank to restore $W = B$. This cycle is the essence of **depth-keeping**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hydrostatic Force Balance Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of submarine hydrostatic equilibrium — the interplay between weight, buoyancy, pressure, and stability subsystems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Submarine Hull Envelope Volume Vs] --> B{Operating State}
    B -->|Surfaced| C[Displaced Volume Vd less than Vs]
    B -->|Submerged| D[Displaced Volume equals Vs]
    C --> E[Reserve of Buoyancy R equals Vs minus Vd]
    E --> F[MBT Flood Capacity]
    F --> G[Diving and Surfacing System]
    D --> H[Buoyancy Constant at CoB]
    H --> I[Pressure P equals rho g h]
    I --> J[Hoop Stress sigma equals P D over 2t]
    J --> K[Safety Factor against Crush Depth]
    G --> L[Weight W Controlled by Trim Tanks]
    L --> M{Force Balance Check}
    H --> M
    M -->|W greater than B| N[Submarine Descends]
    M -->|W equals B| O[Submarine Hovers at Depth]
    M -->|W less than B| P[Submarine Ascends]
    O --> Q[Stability Check GM positive]
    Q -->|Stable| R[Upright Equilibrium Maintained]
    Q -->|Unstable| S[Heel or Trim Develops]
    S --> T[Ballast Re-distribution Required]
```

## 4.2 Metacentre Geometry Schematic

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional geometry** of the metacentre for both surface and submerged conditions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Surface_Condition
        A1[Keel K] --> A2[Buoyancy Centre B near Hull Bottom]
        A2 --> A3[Metacentre M close to B]
        A3 --> A4[Gravity Centre G above B]
        A4 --> A5[GM small positive value]
    end
    subgraph Submerged_Condition
        B1[Keel K] --> B2[Buoyancy Centre B at Hull Centroid]
        B2 --> B3[Metacentre M far above B]
        B3 --> B4[Gravity Centre G unchanged]
        B4 --> B5[GM large positive value]
    end
    A5 --> C[Stable Equilibrium Confirmed]
    B5 --> C
    C --> D[Righting Moment equals W times GM times phi]
```

## 4.3 Pressure Hull Loading Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sea Surface Atmospheric Pressure Patm] --> B[Hydrostatic Pressure rho g h increases with depth]
    B --> C[External Pressure acts on Hull Outer Surface]
    C --> D[Hoop Stress sigma h equals P D over 2t]
    C --> E[Longitudinal Stress sigma l equals P D over 4t]
    D --> F[Material Yield Strength sigma y]
    E --> F
    F -->|sigma h less than sigma y| G[Hull Safe]
    F -->|sigma h greater than sigma y| H[Hull Collapses Crush Depth Exceeded]
    G --> I[Operating Depth equals 0.5 times h crush]
    H --> J[Catastrophic Failure Maximum Diving Depth Violated]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** These block-level schematics capture the **causal chain** of hydrostatic behaviour. In the KTU 2024 board exam, a well-labelled diagram (even simplified) earns full methodology marks even if the final numerical answer has a minor arithmetic slip.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Reserve of Buoyancy. Why is it important for a submarine?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Reserve of Buoyancy (R) is defined as the additional volume of water that must be taken into the variable ballast tanks before the submarine becomes fully submerged.

$$R = V_{s} - V_{d}$$

where $V_{s}$ is the total pressure hull envelope volume and $V_{d}$ is the volume displaced when the submarine is in the surfaced condition.

**Importance (2 marks):**
1. It represents the **margin of safety** against accidental flooding or sudden weight gain.
2. It determines the **flooding capacity** of the main ballast tanks, which controls the diving operation.
3. A typical conventional submarine has R between **30% and 60%** of its shell volume. Deep-diving research submersibles have a much smaller R, often less than **10%**.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between surfaced and submerged stability of a submarine.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Surfaced | Submerged |
|---|---|---|
| Buoyancy volume | Hull below waterline only | Entire pressure hull |
| $I_T$ source | Waterplane area (small) | Full hull cross-section (large) |
| $GM$ value | Small (often < 0.5 m) | Large (often > 1.0 m) |
| Free surface effect | Present in partially filled tanks | Negligible |
| Stability feel | Soft, sluggish | Stiff, quick recovery |

In the **submerged** condition, the metacentric height is significantly higher because the entire hull cross-section contributes to the moment of inertia $I_T$, providing a much larger metacentric radius $BM$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** For all Part B questions, students must **(i)** state the governing principle, **(ii)** draw a clear free-body diagram or schematic, **(iii)** show every algebraic step, and **(iv)** box the final answer. Skipping the FBD alone costs 2–3 marks. Always include **units** in the final answer.

---

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** A submarine has a total pressure hull envelope volume of **5000 m³**. When in the surfaced condition, it displaces **3500 m³** of seawater. Calculate the **reserve of buoyancy** in m³ and as a **percentage of shell volume**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical pressure hull of the submarine has a diameter of **7 m** and is made of HY-80 steel plate of thickness **50 mm**. If the yield strength of the steel is **550 MPa**, determine the **theoretical collapse depth** of the hull. Take $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \, kg/m^3$ and $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B-A (a)

**Given:**
- Total shell volume: $V_s = 5000 \; m^3$
- Displaced volume (surfaced): $V_d = 3500 \; m^3$

**Step 1 — Apply the formula for reserve of buoyancy:**

$$R = V_s - V_d = 5000 - 3500 = 2000 \; m^3$$

**Step 2 — Convert to percentage:**

$$R(\%) = \frac{R}{V_s} \times 100 = \frac{2000}{5000} \times 100 = 40 \;\%$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{R = 2000 \; m^3 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 40\% \; \text{of shell volume}}$

This is within the typical range for conventional submarines (**30%–60%**).

---

#### Model Solution to B-A (b)

**Given:**
- Hull diameter: $D = 7 \; m$
- Wall thickness: $t = 50 \; mm = 0.050 \; m$
- Yield strength: $\sigma_y = 550 \; MPa = 550 \times 10^6 \; Pa$
- $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \; kg/m^3$, $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$

**Step 1 — Recall the hoop stress equation for a thin cylinder:**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P \, D}{2 \, t}$$

**Step 2 — At collapse, $\sigma_h = \sigma_y$ and $P = \rho_{sw} \, g \, h_{crush}$:**

$$\sigma_y = \frac{\rho_{sw} \, g \, h_{crush} \, D}{2 \, t}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $h_{crush}$:**

$$h_{crush} = \frac{2 \, t \, \sigma_y}{\rho_{sw} \, g \, D}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$h_{crush} = \frac{2 \times 0.050 \times 550 \times 10^6}{1025 \times 9.81 \times 7}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate numerator and denominator:**

$$\text{Numerator} = 2 \times 0.050 \times 550 \times 10^6 = 5.5 \times 10^7$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 7 = 70{,}395.675$$

**Step 6 — Final division:**

$$h_{crush} = \frac{5.5 \times 10^7}{70{,}395.675} \approx 781.4 \; m$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{h_{crush} \approx 781.4 \; m}$

**Safe operating depth (50% rule):** $\approx 390.7 \; m$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes:** (1) Forgetting to convert $t$ from mm to m. (2) Mixing up hoop vs longitudinal stress formula. (3) Using the wrong yield strength (HY-80 means 80 ksi ≈ 550 MPa, not 80 MPa!).

---

### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` (14 Marks) — *Internal Alternative*

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the **metacentric stability** of a submarine in the **submerged condition**. Derive the expression for **metacentric height (GM)** in terms of KB, BM, and KG. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A submarine in the surfaced condition has $KB = 1.2 \; m$, $BM = 0.4 \; m$, and $KG = 1.4 \; m$. A mass of **8 tonnes** is moved **1.5 m** transversely across the deck. Calculate the **angle of heel** in the surfaced condition. Take $W = 3500 \; tonnes$ and $GM_T$ initially. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B-B (a)

**Diagram (Textual Representation):**

> **Vertical cross-section through the hull centreline:**
> - $K$ = Keel (reference, at bottom)
> - $B$ = Centre of Buoyancy (centroid of submerged volume)
> - $G$ = Centre of Gravity (centroid of all weights)
> - $M$ = Metacentre (point where the new vertical through heeled $B'$ meets the original vertical)
> - Heel angle: $\phi$ (small, in radians)
> - $KB$ is measured along the original centreline; $BM$ is the additional rise from $B$ to $M$

**Derivation:**

The metacentric height is the distance from G to M, measured along the original vertical centreline of the hull.

By construction, the metacentric radius is:
$$BM = \frac{I_T}{\nabla}$$

where $I_T$ is the **second moment of area** of the waterplane (surfaced) or hull cross-section (submerged) about the longitudinal centreline, and $\nabla$ is the displaced volume.

The height of M above the keel is:
$$KM = KB + BM$$

The height of G above the keel is $KG$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{GM = KM - KG = KB + \frac{I_T}{\nabla} - KG}$$

**Stability Criterion:**
- If $GM > 0$ → Stable (righting moment acts)
- If $GM = 0$ → Neutral
- If $GM < 0$ → Unstable (capsize)

For a submarine in the **submerged condition**, the entire pressure hull cross-section contributes to $I_T$, giving a much larger $GM$ than in the surfaced condition.

---

#### Model Solution to B-B (b)

**Given:**
- $KB = 1.2 \; m$, $BM = 0.4 \; m$, $KG = 1.4 \; m$
- Mass shifted: $w = 8 \; tonnes = 8000 \; kg$
- Transverse shift distance: $d = 1.5 \; m$
- Submarine weight: $W = 3500 \; tonnes = 3.5 \times 10^6 \; kg$

**Step 1 — Initial Metacentric Height:**

$$GM = KB + BM - KG = 1.2 + 0.4 - 1.4 = 0.2 \; m$$

> **Observation:** A small positive $GM$ — typical for a surfaced submarine. Submarine is stable but only marginally.

**Step 2 — Shift of Centre of Gravity due to Mass Movement:**

The transverse shift of G is:
$$\overline{GG_1} = \frac{w \cdot d}{W} = \frac{8 \times 1.5}{3500} = \frac{12}{3500} = 0.003429 \; m$$

**Step 3 — Heel Angle from Tan Formula:**

For small angles, the heel angle satisfies:

$$\tan\phi = \frac{w \cdot d}{W \cdot GM}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$\tan\phi = \frac{8 \times 1.5}{3500 \times 0.2} = \frac{12}{700} = 0.01714$$

**Step 5 — Convert to degrees:**

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.01714) \approx 0.982^{\circ} \approx 0.01714 \; rad$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{\phi \approx 0.98^{\circ} \; \text{(approximately 1 degree heel)}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT use $W \cdot GM \cdot \tan\phi = w \cdot d \cdot \cos\phi$ approximations inconsistently. The standard small-angle formula is:
> $$\tan\phi = \frac{w \cdot d}{W \cdot GM}$$
> Always verify $GM > 0$ before applying this formula — if $GM < 0$, the submarine has no equilibrium heel angle (it capsizes).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Board Exam Focus**

### Must-Memorize Formulas
- [ ] Buoyancy: $B = \rho_{sw} \, g \, \nabla$
- [ ] Reserve of Buoyancy: $R = V_s - V_d$ and $R(\%)$
- [ ] Hoop stress: $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \, D}{2 \, t}$
- [ ] Longitudinal stress: $\sigma_l = \dfrac{P \, D}{4 \, t}$
- [ ] Metacentric radius: $BM = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}$
- [ ] Metacentric height: $GM = KB + BM - KG$
- [ ] Heel angle: $\tan\phi = \dfrac{w \cdot d}{W \cdot GM}$
- [ ] Critical depth: $h_{crush} = \dfrac{2 \, t \, \sigma_y}{\rho_{sw} \, g \, D}$

### Conceptual Takeaways
- **W = B** is the condition for neutral buoyancy (hovering).
- **Reserve of Buoyancy** = safety margin before submergence. Typical: 30–60% for military submarines, < 10% for deep submersibles.
- **Surfaced stability** is governed by the *waterplane* moment of inertia; **submerged stability** uses the *entire hull cross-section* moment of inertia — much larger.
- **Free surface effect** in partially filled tanks reduces effective $GM$ — critical design consideration.
- **Crush depth** is set by hoop stress equalling material yield strength. Operational depth is typically **half** the theoretical crush depth.
- **Block coefficient** $C_B$ for submarines: 0.60–0.75 (lower than surface ships because slender hulls reduce drag).
- **Hull materials** for deep diving: HY-80, HY-100, HY-130 steels (yield 80/100/130 ksi) or titanium alloys for extreme depths.

### Common Numerical Values to Remember
- $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \; kg/m^3$ (seawater)
- $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$
- $1 \; \text{atm} = 101.325 \; kPa \approx 10 \; m$ of seawater head
- $1 \; \text{bar} \approx 10 \; m$ depth

### KTU Board Exam Strategy
1. **Always start** with a clear statement of the governing principle.
2. **Draw a free-body diagram** (FBD) — earns 1–2 marks even before calculations.
3. **Box the final answer** with units.
4. **Check sign and magnitude** of $GM$; negative $GM$ = capsize.
5. **Convert all units to SI** before substitution.
6. **Mention assumptions** (small angles, thin-walled cylinder, etc.) to earn methodology marks.

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Submarine Hydrostatics**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, PENST634 — Submarines and Submersibles*

topics.title: Principles of Flotation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚓ Principles of Flotation – Submarines & Submersibles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** This topic is a foundational pillar of Module 2 and directly feeds into **Submarine Stability, Ballast Systems, and Depth Control**. Expect derivations in Part B (14-mark questions) and short conceptual questions in Part A (3-mark questions).

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Flotation** is the physical phenomenon by which a body immersed wholly or partially in a fluid is sustained at rest (or in equilibrium) by an upward hydrostatic force exerted by the fluid, equal in magnitude to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body. In the context of submarines and submersibles, flotation principles govern the equilibrium between the **submerged weight (W)** of the vessel and the **buoyant force (F_B)** generated by the displaced seawater.

> [!NOTE]
> **Archimedes' Principle (287–212 BC)** — the cornerstone of flotation: *"Any body wholly or partially immersed in a fluid is buoyed up by a force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced."*

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Seawater, 15 °C, 35 ppt salinity)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Density of seawater | $\rho_{sw}$ | **1025 kg/m³** |
| Density of freshwater | $\rho_{fw}$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Specific weight of seawater | $\gamma_{sw}$ | **10045 N/m³** |
| Specific weight of freshwater | $\gamma_{fw}$ | **9810 N/m³** |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Inflated Balloon in a Bathtub"

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a sealed rubber balloon** trapped underwater. If you push it down, the water pushes back with an equal upward force proportional to how much water the balloon has *squeezed out of the way*. A submarine works exactly the same way: by **changing the amount of water it displaces** (using ballast tanks) it can rise, sink, or hover.

**Plain-English Intuition:**
- **Sink (submerge):** Flood ballast tanks → submarine becomes *heavier* than the water it displaces → gravity wins → it goes down.
- **Float (surface):** Pump water out of ballast tanks with high-pressure air → submarine becomes *lighter* than displaced water → buoyancy wins → it goes up.
- **Hover (neutral):** Adjust weight = displaced water weight → perfectly balanced → it stays at depth (this is **neutral buoyancy**).

This push-pull tug-of-war between **Weight (W)** and **Buoyancy (F_B)** is the essence of flotation.

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters for Submarines

Unlike surface ships, a submarine is a **dive-rise vehicle**. It must operate in two distinct density environments:
1. **Surface condition** — partially submerged, exposed to waves, free-surface effects.
2. **Submerged condition** — fully submerged, governed by hydrostatic pressure and compressibility of seawater.

The **principles of flotation** provide the analytical framework to:
- Compute the **draft** (depth of submergence).
- Calculate **reserve buoyancy** and **submerged displacement**.
- Determine the **center of buoyancy (B)** and its migration as the vessel submerges.
- Establish the **metacentric height (GM)** for transverse and longitudinal stability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **displacement** (mass of water displaced) with **weight displacement** (force = mass × g). Always specify whether you are working in **mass units (tonnes)** or **force units (tonnes-force / kN)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Flotation Equilibrium

A floating or submerged body is in **static equilibrium** only when the following three conditions are satisfied **simultaneously**:

1. **Force Equilibrium (Vertical):** Sum of vertical forces = 0
2. **Moment Equilibrium:** Sum of moments about any point = 0
3. **Geometric Coincidence:** Center of Gravity (G) and Center of Buoyancy (B) must lie on the **same vertical line** in the equilibrium condition.

### Vertical Force Balance

$$\sum F_{vertical} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; F_B - W = 0$$

Where:
- $F_B$ = Buoyant force (upward) = weight of displaced fluid
- $W$ = Total weight of the vessel (downward)

### Decomposition of Submarine Weight (W)

The total operating weight of a submarine has **three** components:

$$\boxed{\;W = W_{light} + W_{variable} + W_{ballast}\;}$$

- $W_{light}$ = Weight of empty hull, machinery, equipment (constant)
- $W_{variable}$ = Variable loads: crew, provisions, freshwater, fuel, lube oil, ammunition, mission-specific equipment
- $W_{ballast}$ = Weight of seawater in variable ballast tanks (used to *fine-tune* buoyancy)

---

## 2.2 Submerged Volume & Draft Calculations

### 2.2.1 Fully Submerged (Diving) Condition

When completely underwater, the buoyant force depends on the **entire external volume** of the pressure hull (and outer casing volumes) up to the local waterplane:

$$F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla_{ext}$$

Where $\nabla_{ext}$ is the **total external displaced volume** in m³.

### 2.2.2 Surface (Surfacing) Condition

The vessel floats at the surface with a draft $T$. The submerged volume is:

$$\nabla_{sub} = \int_0^T A_{wp}(z)\, dz$$

Where $A_{wp}(z)$ is the **waterplane area** at depth $z$ below the waterline.

For a **rectangular prismatic hull** (highly simplified for academic use):

$$\nabla_{sub} = L \cdot B \cdot T$$

### 2.2.3 Density Effects — Fresh vs. Salt Water

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A submarine is **heavier in freshwater** than in seawater because freshwater is **less dense**. Therefore, a submarine will ride **deeper (greater draft) in rivers and estuaries** than in the open ocean. This is critical for navigation in the approaches to Indian ports (e.g., Cochin, Mumbai).

$$\Delta T = T_{sw} \cdot \left( \frac{\rho_{sw}}{\rho_{fw}} - 1 \right) \approx 0.025 \cdot T_{sw}$$

A **2.5% increase in draft** is typical when transitioning from seawater to freshwater.

---

## 2.3 Reserve Buoyancy (Surface Operation)

**Reserve Buoyancy (R.B.)** is the **additional volume of water the vessel could displace** before becoming completely submerged. For a surface ship/submarine, it represents the margin of safety against sinking due to flooding, wave action, or weight addition.

$$\boxed{\;R.B. = \nabla_{full} - \nabla_{sub}\;}$$

In terms of **upward force**:

$$F_{RB} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot R.B. = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot (\nabla_{full} - \nabla_{sub})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** A surface submarine in "light" condition typically has **30–40% reserve buoyancy**. Once dived, reserve buoyancy is **zero** by definition — this is why a fully submerged submarine is **one flooding casualty away from sinking**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Print this table and keep it beside you while solving numericals.** These are the formulas examiners expect you to reproduce without derivation marks being lost.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Buoyant Force (Archimedes) | $F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | N (or kN) | Any immersed body |
| 2 | Equilibrium | $F_B = W$ | N | Floating / submerged static |
| 3 | Reserve Buoyancy (volume) | $R.B. = \nabla_{full} - \nabla_{sub}$ | m³ | Surface condition only |
| 4 | Reserve Buoyancy (force) | $F_{RB} = \rho g (\nabla_{full} - \nabla_{sub})$ | N | Surface condition only |
| 5 | Submerged Draft (rect.) | $T = \dfrac{\nabla_{sub}}{L \cdot B}$ | m | Prismatic hull |
| 6 | Fresh vs Salt Draft Correction | $\Delta T = T_{sw}\left(\dfrac{\rho_{sw}}{\rho_{fw}} - 1\right)$ | m | Density change |
| 7 | Displacement (mass) | $\Delta_m = \rho \cdot \nabla$ | tonnes (if $\nabla$ in m³ and $\rho$ in t/m³) | Any |
| 8 | Displacement (force) | $\Delta_W = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | kN | Any |
| 9 | Block Coefficient | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | dimensionless | Form factor |
| 10 | Prismatic Coefficient | $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L}$ | dimensionless | Form factor |
| 11 | Waterplane Area Coeff. | $C_{WP} = \dfrac{A_{WP}}{L \cdot B}$ | dimensionless | Form factor |
| 12 | Tons per cm immersion (TPC) | $TPC = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot A_{WP}}{100 \cdot g} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot A_{WP}}{100}$ | tonnes/cm | Added weight = sink by 1 cm |
| 13 | Density of seawater | $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ | kg/m³ | Standard ocean |
| 14 | Specific weight seawater | $\gamma_{sw} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g = 10045$ | N/m³ | Standard ocean |

> **Notation:** $L$ = Length, $B$ = Beam, $T$ = Draft, $\nabla$ = Displaced volume, $A_{WP}$ = Waterplane area, $A_M$ = Midship section area.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| Submarine Design | Hull form optimization for minimum drag at submerged condition |
| Naval Architecture | Trim & stability book generation for every loading condition |
| Offshore Engineering | Buoyancy-based subsea platform design |
| Submersible Operations | Hovering ROV/AUV control via variable buoyancy |
| Marine Salvage | Patching and refloating of sunken vessels |
