# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Rule:** Every step of a derivation **must be justified** with a one-line explanation. Skipping steps = losing 1–2 marks. Below are the most-asked derivations, fully expanded.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Archimedes' Principle from First Principles

**Goal:** Prove that the buoyant force on a fully submerged body equals the weight of displaced fluid.

### Setup
Consider a fully submerged body of arbitrary shape in a fluid of density $\rho$. Let us extract an elemental vertical column of the body of cross-section $dA$ and height $dz$, located at depth $z$ below the free surface.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Proof

The **pressure at depth $z$** (hydrostatic, gauge):

$$p(z) = \rho \cdot g \cdot z$$

The **vertical force** on the top face of the elemental column (pushing down):

$$dF_{top} = p(z) \cdot dA = \rho \cdot g \cdot z \cdot dA$$

The **vertical force** on the bottom face of the elemental column (pushing up), at depth $z + dz$:

$$dF_{bot} = p(z + dz) \cdot dA = \rho \cdot g \cdot (z + dz) \cdot dA$$

The **net upward force** on the elemental column:

$$dF_{net} = dF_{bot} - dF_{top}$$

$$dF_{net} = \rho \cdot g \cdot (z + dz) \cdot dA - \rho \cdot g \cdot z \cdot dA$$

$$dF_{net} = \rho \cdot g \cdot dz \cdot dA$$

But $dz \cdot dA$ is the **elemental volume** of the column, $d\nabla$.

$$dF_{net} = \rho \cdot g \cdot d\nabla$$

Now, **integrate over the entire body volume** $\nabla$:

$$F_B = \int_{\nabla} \rho \cdot g \cdot d\nabla = \rho \cdot g \cdot \int_{\nabla} d\nabla = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$$

$$\boxed{\;F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla\;}$$

> **Interpretation:** The buoyant force equals the **weight of the displaced fluid**, since weight of displaced fluid = $\rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$. This is the formal proof of **Archimedes' Principle**. $\;\;\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Submerged Draft for a Rectangular Prismatic Hull

**Goal:** Find the draft $T$ at which a rectangular box-shaped submarine of length $L$, beam $B$, and weight $W$ floats in equilibrium.

### Equilibrium Condition

The vessel floats such that buoyancy equals weight:

$$F_B = W$$

Substituting the buoyant force expression:

$$\rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla_{sub} = W$$

For a rectangular hull, the submerged volume is:

$$\nabla_{sub} = L \cdot B \cdot T$$

Substituting:

$$\rho \cdot g \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T = W$$

Solving for $T$:

$$T = \frac{W}{\rho \cdot g \cdot L \cdot B}$$

In **mass units** (dividing numerator and denominator by $g$):

$$T = \frac{m}{\rho \cdot L \cdot B}$$

Where $m = W/g$ is the mass of the vessel.

$$\boxed{\;T = \frac{W}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot L \cdot B}\;}$$

> **Sanity check:** Heavier vessel → deeper draft. Denser fluid → shallower draft. Larger waterplane (L × B) → shallower draft. All physically consistent. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Freshwater vs. Seawater Draft Correction

**Goal:** Show that a submarine floats deeper in freshwater than in seawater, and quantify the difference.

### Given
- Draft in seawater: $T_{sw}$
- Density of seawater: $\rho_{sw}$ = 1025 kg/m³
- Density of freshwater: $\rho_{fw}$ = 1000 kg/m³
- Weight of vessel: $W$ (unchanged)

### Step 1 — Weight in seawater (equilibrium)
$$W = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T_{sw}$$

### Step 2 — Weight in freshwater (same vessel, same W)
$$W = \rho_{fw} \cdot g \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T_{fw}$$

### Step 3 — Equate the two expressions
$$\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T_{sw} = \rho_{fw} \cdot g \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T_{fw}$$

### Step 4 — Cancel common terms (g, L, B)
$$\rho_{sw} \cdot T_{sw} = \rho_{fw} \cdot T_{fw}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $T_{fw}$
$$T_{fw} = T_{sw} \cdot \frac{\rho_{sw}}{\rho_{fw}}$$

### Step 6 — Compute the increase in draft
$$\Delta T = T_{fw} - T_{sw} = T_{sw} \cdot \left( \frac{\rho_{sw}}{\rho_{fw}} - 1 \right)$$

### Step 7 — Substitute numerical values
$$\frac{\rho_{sw}}{\rho_{fw}} = \frac{1025}{1000} = 1.025$$

$$\Delta T = T_{sw} \cdot (1.025 - 1) = 0.025 \cdot T_{sw}$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta T = 0.025 \cdot T_{sw} \;\;\text{or}\;\; \Delta T = 2.5\% \cdot T_{sw}\;}$$

> **Example:** If a submarine has a draft of **10 m in seawater**, it will draft **10.25 m in freshwater** — a 25 cm increase.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Tons per Centimetre Immersion (TPC)

**Goal:** Derive the formula for TPC, a standard naval-architecture metric.

### Definition
TPC = the **weight (in tonnes)** that must be added to a vessel to cause it to **sink by exactly 1 cm** in the water.

### Derivation
Adding weight $\Delta W$ causes the vessel to sink by an additional draft $\Delta T$. The increase in displaced volume is:

$$\Delta \nabla = A_{WP} \cdot \Delta T$$

The corresponding increase in buoyant force is:

$$\Delta F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot \Delta \nabla = \rho \cdot g \cdot A_{WP} \cdot \Delta T$$

For equilibrium, this must equal the added weight:

$$\Delta W = \rho \cdot g \cdot A_{WP} \cdot \Delta T$$

Set $\Delta T$ = 1 cm = 0.01 m, and express $\Delta W$ in **tonnes** (so divide by $g$):

$$TPC = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot A_{WP} \cdot 0.01}{g} = \rho \cdot A_{WP} \cdot 0.01$$

Converting to **tonnes/cm** (using $\rho$ in t/m³ and $A_{WP}$ in m²):

$$\boxed{\;TPC = \frac{\rho \cdot A_{WP}}{100}\;}\;\; \text{tonnes/cm}$$

> **Numerical Example:** For a waterplane area $A_{WP} = 800$ m² in seawater ($\rho = 1.025$ t/m³):
>
> $TPC = (1.025 \times 800) / 100 = 8.2$ tonnes/cm.
>
> This means adding **8.2 tonnes** of weight will sink the submarine by **1 cm**.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style — 7-Mark Sub-Part)

### Problem
A rectangular submarine has length $L = 90$ m, beam $B = 10$ m. When floating in seawater ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³), the draft is $T_{sw} = 7$ m. Find:
- (i) The displaced volume $\nabla_{sub}$
- (ii) The buoyant force $F_B$
- (iii) The weight of the vessel $W$
- (iv) The freshwater draft $T_{fw}$

### Solution

**Part (i): Displaced volume**

$$\nabla_{sub} = L \cdot B \cdot T_{sw} = 90 \times 10 \times 7 = 6300 \;\text{m}^3$$

**Part (ii): Buoyant force**

$$F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla_{sub}$$

$$F_B = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 6300$$

$$F_B = 6.334 \times 10^7 \;\text{N} = 63{,}340 \;\text{kN}$$

**Part (iii): Weight of vessel** (from equilibrium $W = F_B$)

$$W = 63{,}340 \;\text{kN}$$

In **mass units**: $m = W/g = 63{,}340{,}000 / 9.81 = 6{,}456{,}000$ kg = **6456 tonnes**

**Part (iv): Freshwater draft**

$$T_{fw} = T_{sw} \cdot \frac{\rho_{sw}}{\rho_{fw}} = 7 \times \frac{1025}{1000} = 7.175 \;\text{m}$$

$$\Delta T = 7.175 - 7.000 = 0.175 \;\text{m} = 17.5 \;\text{cm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Check:** $17.5/700 = 0.025 = 2.5\%$ ✓ — matches the derived formula. **Full marks.**

---

## 3.6 Free-Surface Effect (FSE) — Why Ballast Tanks Must Be Pressed Full

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When a partially filled tank has a **free surface** (air space above liquid), the liquid can slosh. The **virtual rise in G** due to sloshing is:

$$GG' = \frac{\rho_{liq} \cdot i}{\Delta}$$

Where:
- $i$ = **transverse moment of inertia** of the free surface about the centerline
- $\Delta$ = displacement (mass)
- $\rho_{liq}$ = density of the liquid in the tank

> **Conclusion:** FSE is **destabilizing** (raises G). All submarine ballast tanks are designed to operate either **completely full** or **completely empty** — never partially filled — to eliminate FSE.

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

### **Q1. State Archimedes' Principle. How is it applied in the design of submarine ballast systems?**
**[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Archimedes' Principle:** *"Any body wholly or partially immersed in a fluid experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body."*

**Application in submarine ballast systems:**
- A submarine submerges by **flooding its ballast tanks** with seawater, thereby **increasing its total weight** ($W$) so that $W > F_B$.
- To surface, the submarine **expels seawater from the ballast tanks using high-pressure compressed air**, reducing $W$ so that $W < F_B$.
- For hovering at a fixed depth, $W = F_B$ (**neutral buoyancy**).

> **Marking split:** Definition (1 mark) + application (2 marks).

---

### **Q2. Define Reserve Buoyancy. Why is it essential for a surface-operating submarine?**
**[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** Reserve Buoyancy is the **additional volume of water** (or equivalently, the additional buoyant force) a surface vessel can displace **before becoming completely submerged**.

$$R.B. = \nabla_{full} - \nabla_{sub}$$

**Importance for a submarine on the surface:**
1. **Safety margin** against progressive flooding from battle damage or system failure.
2. **Wave-piercing reserve** — provides buoyancy to ride over waves without submerging the deck.
3. **Load tolerance** — accommodates variable weights (crew, stores, ammunition) without immediate sinking.
4. Once the submarine dives, **reserve buoyancy becomes zero** — making depth control critical.

> **Marking split:** Definition with formula (1 mark) + three valid reasons (2 marks).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **❓ Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a) Derive Archimedes' Principle from first principles using a hydrostatic pressure approach. State all assumptions clearly.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The fluid is **incompressible** and **homogeneous** (constant $\rho$).
2. The fluid is at **rest** (no dynamic effects).
3. The body is **fully submerged** (extension to partially submerged follows by replacing $\nabla$ with submerged volume).
4. The body has a **sealed, impermeable surface**.
5. Hydrostatic pressure varies linearly with depth: $p(z) = \rho g z$ (gauge).

**Derivation:**

Consider an elemental vertical column inside the submerged body of cross-sectional area $dA$ and height $dz$, with its top at depth $z$.

**Step 1 — Pressure at the top of the column:**

$$p_{top} = \rho g z$$

**Step 2 — Pressure at the bottom of the column (depth $z + dz$):**

$$p_{bot} = \rho g (z + dz)$$

**Step 3 — Net upward force on the column:**

$$dF = (p_{bot} - p_{top}) \cdot dA = \rho g \, dz \cdot dA$$

**Step 4 — Recognize that $dz \cdot dA$ is the elemental volume $d\nabla$:**

$$dF = \rho g \, d\nabla$$

**Step 5 — Integrate over the entire body volume $\nabla$:**

$$F_B = \int_{\nabla} \rho g \, d\nabla = \rho g \nabla$$

$$\boxed{\;F_B = \rho g \nabla = \text{weight of fluid displaced}\;}\;\;\blacksquare$$

> **Conclusion:** The buoyant force equals the weight of displaced fluid — **Archimedes' Principle**.
> **Marking split:** Assumptions (1 mark) + Setup & Steps 1–3 (3 marks) + Integration & conclusion (2 marks) + Final statement (1 mark).

---

**(b) A submarine model (rectangular) of length 80 m and beam 9 m floats in seawater (ρ = 1025 kg/m³) with a draft of 6.5 m. Calculate: (i) the displaced volume, (ii) the buoyant force in kN, (iii) the mass of the submarine in tonnes, and (iv) the draft if the submarine enters a freshwater estuary (ρ = 1000 kg/m³).** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Displaced volume (1 mark):**

$$\nabla_{sub} = L \cdot B \cdot T = 80 \times 9 \times 6.5 = 4680 \;\text{m}^3$$

**Part (ii) — Buoyant force in kN (2 marks):**

$$F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla_{sub} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 4680$$

$$F_B = 4.704 \times 10^7 \;\text{N} = 47{,}040 \;\text{kN}$$

**Part (iii) — Mass of submarine (2 marks):**

At equilibrium, $W = F_B$, so:

$$m = \frac{W}{g} = \frac{F_B}{g} = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla_{sub}$$

$$m = 1025 \times 4680 = 4{,}797{,}000 \;\text{kg} = 4797 \;\text{tonnes}$$

**Part (iv) — Freshwater draft (2 marks):**

Since weight $W$ is unchanged:

$$\rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla_{sw} = \rho_{fw} \cdot \nabla_{fw}$$

$$1025 \times (80 \times 9 \times 6.5) = 1000 \times (80 \times 9 \times T_{fw})$$

$$T_{fw} = 6.5 \times \frac{1025}{1000} = 6.6625 \;\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{\;T_{fw} = 6.66 \;\text{m}\;}$$

**Additional interpretation (bonus, not required):** The draft **increases by 16.25 cm** — a critical operational concern when navigating from the open sea into an estuary.

> **Marking split:** (i) 1 mark, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 2 marks, (iv) 2 marks.

---

### **❓ Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alternative]*

**(a) Explain the three conditions of static equilibrium for a floating body. How does each condition apply to a submarine in the surface condition vs. the fully submerged condition?** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**The Three Conditions of Static Equilibrium:**

**1. Force Equilibrium (Vertical):** $\sum F_{vertical} = 0$

- *Surface condition:* Weight $W$ (down) = Buoyancy $F_B$ (up). The vessel neither rises nor sinks.
- *Submerged condition:* Same force balance, but $F_B$ is now based on the **entire external hull volume** (not just submerged volume).
- *Trim tanks* and *ballast tanks* are used to **actively adjust** $W$ and $\nabla$ to maintain this balance.

**2. Moment Equilibrium:** $\sum M = 0$ about any point.

- *Surface condition:* The moments due to weight distribution and buoyancy distribution must balance about the centerline; otherwise, the vessel will list or trim.
- *Submerged condition:* Trim and list must be controlled by **fore-aft and athwartships ballast** to maintain a level attitude.

**3. Geometric Coincidence (G and B on the same vertical):**

- *Surface condition:* $G$ (center of gravity) and $B$ (center of buoyancy) must lie on the **same vertical line**. If they are offset, the vessel heels.
- *Submerged condition:* As the submarine submerges, the **center of buoyancy B migrates** because the shape of the displaced volume changes. The metacentric height $GM$ may also reduce, affecting stability.

**Distinction — Surface vs. Submerged:**

| Aspect | Surface | Submerged |
|---|---|---|
| Reserve buoyancy | Positive (30–40%) | **Zero** |
| Stability mechanism | Metacentric ($GM > 0$) | **Pure weight-balance** (no GM concept, fully immersed) |
| B migration with depth | Significant | Continuous |
| Free-surface effects | Critical | Less (tanks full/empty) |

> **Marking split:** 3 conditions × 2 marks each = 6 marks + 1 mark for clear surface-vs-submerged distinction.

---

**(b) A submarine has a waterplane area of 750 m² in surface condition. Seawater density is 1025 kg/m³. Calculate the Tons per Centimetre Immersion (TPC). If 50 tonnes of stores are loaded, by how much will the draft increase?** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — TPC Calculation (3 marks):**

The standard formula:

$$TPC = \frac{\rho \cdot A_{WP}}{100}$$

Substituting $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³ and $A_{WP} = 750$ m²:

$$TPC = \frac{1.025 \times 750}{100} = \frac{768.75}{100} = 7.6875 \;\text{tonnes/cm}$$

$$\boxed{\;TPC = 7.69 \;\text{tonnes/cm (rounded)}\;}$$

**Part 2 — Draft increase due to 50 tonnes (4 marks):**

From the definition of TPC: **adding 7.6875 tonnes** → **draft increases by 1 cm**.

Therefore, **adding 50 tonnes** → draft increase:

$$\Delta T = \frac{50}{TPC} = \frac{50}{7.6875} = 6.504 \;\text{cm}$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta T \approx 6.50 \;\text{cm}\;}$$

**Verification (alternative derivation):**
- Increase in displaced volume needed: $\Delta \nabla = \Delta m / \rho = 50 / 1.025 = 48.78$ m³
- Required sinkage: $\Delta T = \Delta \nabla / A_{WP} = 48.78 / 750 = 0.065$ m = **6.5 cm** ✓

> **Marking split:** TPC formula (1 mark) + substitution (2 marks) + Draft increase logic (2 marks) + Final answer (2 marks).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes — Read This Before Your Exam!**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Mixing **mass (tonnes)** with **force (kN or tonnes-force)**. Always state which one you are using and divide/multiply by $g$ explicitly.
> 2. **Forgot to use $g$:** $F_B = \rho g \nabla$ (force), but $m = \rho \nabla$ (mass). Examiners dock **1 mark** for each missing $g$.
> 3. **Density of seawater not stated:** If you use $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ (freshwater) by mistake in a seawater problem, the entire answer drifts by 2.5%. **Always state $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³ explicitly.**
> 4. **Reserve Buoyancy = 0 in submerged state:** Students often claim the submarine has "30% reserve buoyancy" even when submerged. It does not. R.B. is a **surface-condition** concept.
> 5. **Confusing $F_B$ with $W$:** They are *equal at equilibrium* but conceptually distinct — $F_B$ depends on the *fluid* and *geometry*, $W$ depends on the *vessel*.
> 6. **TPC units:** TPC is in **tonnes per centimetre**, not "tonnes" or "kg per metre". Many students lose 1 mark by writing the wrong unit.
> 7. **No free-surface tank logic:** If asked why ballast tanks are never partially filled, students often miss the **FSE formula $GG' = \rho i / \Delta$** — losing 2 marks for an incomplete explanation.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> **Use this section as your last-minute revision sheet the night before the exam.**

- ✅ **Archimedes' Principle:** $F_B = \rho g \nabla$ — buoyant force = weight of displaced fluid.
- ✅ **Three conditions of equilibrium:** Force balance, moment balance, and $G$-$B$ coincidence on the same vertical.
- ✅ **Reserve Buoyancy (surface only):** $R.B. = \nabla_{full} - \nabla_{sub}$ — typically **30–40%** for a submarine in light condition.
- ✅ **Submerged state has ZERO reserve buoyancy** — this is the fundamental difference from surface ships.
- ✅ **Density values to memorize:** $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $\rho_{fw} = 1000$ kg/m³.
- ✅ **Freshwater draft formula:** $T_{fw} = T_{sw} \cdot (\rho_{sw}/\rho_{fw}) = 1.025 \cdot T_{sw}$.
- ✅ **TPC formula:** $TPC = \rho A_{WP} / 100$ tonnes/cm.
- ✅ **Weight components:** $W = W_{light} + W_{variable} + W_{ballast}$.
- ✅ **Block coefficient:** $C_B = \nabla / (LBT)$ — typical submarine value: 0.35–0.45.
- ✅ **Free Surface Effect:** Partially filled ballast tanks are **PROHIBITED** in operation; FSE raises $G$ virtually by $GG' = \rho i / \Delta$.
- ✅ **State transitions:** Surface (F_B > W) → Vent ballast → Neutral (F_B = W) → Flood ballast → Submerging (F_B < W).
- ✅ **Metacentric concept** applies to **surface condition only**; once fully submerged, stability is purely about weight–buoyancy coincidence.
- ✅ **Numerical checks:** Always verify that 2.5% draft increase occurs when moving from saltwater to freshwater.

> 🎯 **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When a numerical question gives you $L$, $B$, and $T$, *immediately* compute $\nabla = LBT$ — this is the gateway step to almost every flotation problem. If you skip it, you will lose 2 marks.

---

**End of Notes — Principles of Flotation (Module 2, PENST634)**

topics.title: Submarines on the Surface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST634 - SUBMARINES AND SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Submarines on the Surface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **submarine on the surface** refers to the operational condition in which the submarine's outer (light) hull, sail (or conning tower), and a portion of the upper deck are exposed to the atmosphere, with the pressure hull lying largely submerged below the waterline. In this state, the vessel behaves **hydrostatically and hydrodynamically as a surface ship**, governed by the classical laws of naval architecture rather than the pressure-volume relations that govern the fully submerged condition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Surface condition of a submarine is the floating equilibrium state in which reserve buoyancy is provided by the volume of the watertight outer casing and superstructure above the waterline, while stability is derived from the metacentre, exactly as for a conventional displacement ship."*

### 1.2 Key Quantitative Descriptors of the Surface Condition

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical KTU Value (Modern SSK) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Surfaced Displacement | $\Delta_s$ | **1,800 – 2,500** | tonnes |
| Surface Draft | $T_s$ | **9.0 – 11.5** | m |
| Surface Freeboard | $F_b$ | **2.5 – 4.0** | m |
| Surfaced Speed | $V_s$ | **10 – 12** | knots |
| Reserve Buoyancy | $RB$ | **15 – 25 % of $\Delta$** | – |
| Surfaced Metacentric Height | $GM_s$ | **0.30 – 0.80** | m |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating Cork" Picture

Imagine a **steel cork floating in a bathtub**, with a sealed brass shell inside it. When the cork floats, only the cork itself interacts with the water — the brass shell is just cargo. Now replace the cork with the **outer light hull** and the brass shell with the **inner pressure hull**: this is exactly how a submarine behaves on the surface.

- The **light hull (outer casing)** is the part that floats like a cork — it traps air and provides buoyancy.
- The **pressure hull** sits inside, ballasted with water to keep the whole thing balanced.
- **Free water inside any partially filled ballast tank is like shaking a half-empty water bottle on a boat** — it sloshes and pushes the boat off balance. This is the *free surface effect*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** KTU examiners frequently test whether students understand that *surfaced* and *submerged* are governed by completely different physics — surface stability is **form stability (ship-like)**, while submerged stability is **weight stability (pendulum-like)**.

### 1.4 Why a Submarine Must Surface

A conventional (non-nuclear) submarine must surface (or snorkel) for:
1. **Intake of atmospheric oxygen** for diesel generators.
2. **Battery recharging** via diesel engines.
3. **Crew rest, ventilation, and cooking** (no galley exhaust underwater).
4. **Communication via HF/VHF/UHF radio** — seawater attenuates radio waves.
5. **Use of deck-mounted sensors, radar, and electronic warfare masts.**
6. **Navigation in confined/coastal waters** under positive visual lookout.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hydrostatic Equilibrium on the Surface

For a surfaced submarine floating at rest, two fundamental conditions must hold simultaneously:

1. **Force Balance (Archimedes' Principle):**

   The weight of the vessel must equal the weight of the displaced water.

$$\boxed{\Delta = W = \rho \, g \, \nabla}$$

where:
- $\Delta$ = displacement (tonnes)
- $W$ = total weight (N or tonnes-force)
- $\rho$ = density of seawater (typically **1,025 kg/m³**)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $\nabla$ = volume of displaced water (m³)

2. **Moment Balance (Zero Heel / Trim):**

   The centre of gravity $G$ must lie directly below the centre of buoyancy $B$ on the same vertical line for zero heel, and on the same longitudinal axis for zero trim.

### 2.2 Reserve Buoyancy (RB)

**Reserve Buoyancy** is the *additional* volume of enclosed watertight space above the waterline that *could* be submerged before the vessel becomes fully flooded.

$$RB = \nabla_{\text{full}} - \nabla_{\text{current}}$$

As a fraction of total displacement:

$$RB_{\%} = \frac{V_{\text{above WL}}}{\nabla_{\text{current}}} \times 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** For a surfaced submarine, reserve buoyancy is typically **15 – 25 %** of displacement. It governs seaworthiness, the ability to absorb weight additions (rain, snow, crew), and dynamic loadings from waves.

### 2.3 Transverse Stability — The Metacentre

The **metacentric height $GM$** is the key stability metric for the surfaced submarine, identical in formulation to surface ship theory.

$$\boxed{GM = KB + BM - KG}$$

with the transverse BM given by:

$$BM = \frac{I_T}{\nabla}$$

where $I_T$ = transverse second moment of waterplane area about the centreline.

> [!NOTE]
> For a surfaced submarine, $I_T$ is dominated by the *full waterplane* including the casing top, sail base, and superstructure — making $BM$ substantially larger than when submerged.

### 2.4 Free Surface Effect (FSE) — The Critical Submarine Hazard

When a ballast tank is **partially filled**, the free liquid surface inside the tank shifts as the ship heels. This shift creates a *moment* that **reduces the effective GM** by an amount called the Free Surface Correction (FSC):

$$\boxed{GM_{\text{effective}} = GM - FSC}$$

with

$$FSC = \frac{i_T \, \rho_l}{\Delta_{\text{per cm}} \cdot 100}$$

or equivalently, in absolute terms (loss of GM due to FSE):

$$\boxed{\Delta GM_{\text{FSE}} = \frac{i_T \, \rho_l}{\rho \, \nabla}}$$

For a rectangular tank of length $L$ and breadth $b$:

$$i_T = \frac{L \, b^3}{12}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta = \rho g \nabla$ | Archimedes' equilibrium | N or tonnes |
| 2 | $RB = \nabla_{\text{full}} - \nabla$ | Reserve buoyancy | m³ |
| 3 | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | Metacentric height | m |
| 4 | $BM = I_T / \nabla$ | Transverse BM | m |
| 5 | $\Delta GM_{\text{FSE}} = i_T \rho_l / (\rho \nabla)$ | FSE correction | m |
| 6 | $i_T = L b^3 / 12$ | Rectangular tank inertia | m⁴ |
| 7 | $\tan\theta = \frac{W \cdot GG' \sin\phi}{W \cdot GM}$ | Angle of list due to FSE | rad |
| 8 | $T = \Delta / (A_W \cdot \rho)$ | Mean draft | m |
| 9 | $V_{\text{max,s}} \approx 1.34 \sqrt{L_{WL}}$ | Hull-speed (sub.) | m/s |
| 10 | $P_{\text{E}} = R_T \cdot V / \eta$ | Effective power | W or kW |

> **Critical Pitfall:** Never use the symbol $\vert$ inside any table row. Always use the rendered absolute value or `|...|` only outside tables.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The surface condition directly governs:
- **Bridge-watchkeeping** (per IMO COLREGs).
- **Convoy/escort tactics** where surfaced submarines historically operated alongside surface fleets (especially WWI/WWII).
- **Docking, dry-docking, and berthing** — all performed on the surface.
- **Emergency surfacing after casualty** — the submarine must remain stable with possibly flooded compartments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Free Surface Effect (FSE) Correction

This is the **single most important derivation** in this module and is regularly asked for **7 marks** in KTU ESE.

**Starting Setup:** Consider a rectangular ballast tank of length $L$, breadth $b$, partially filled with liquid of density $\rho_l$, on a ship of displacement $\nabla$ (volume) in water of density $\rho$. The ship heels by a small angle $\phi$.

**Step 1 — Define the moment of inertia of the free surface.**
The free surface is a rectangle of area $A = L \cdot b$ with centroid on the ship's centreline. Its transverse second moment about the longitudinal axis (centreline of ship) is, by the parallel-axis theorem applied within the tank plane:

$$i_T = \int_A y^2 \, dA = \int_{-b/2}^{+b/2} y^2 \, (L \, dy)$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the integral.**
Recognising that $y^2$ is even and the integrand is separable:

$$i_T = L \int_{-b/2}^{+b/2} y^2 \, dy$$

**Step 3 — Perform the antiderivative.**
The antiderivative of $y^2$ is $\dfrac{y^3}{3}$:

$$i_T = L \left[ \frac{y^3}{3} \right]_{-b/2}^{+b/2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the limits.**

$$i_T = L \left[ \frac{(b/2)^3}{3} - \frac{(-b/2)^3}{3} \right]$$

$$i_T = L \left[ \frac{b^3/8}{3} - \frac{-b^3/8}{3} \right]$$

$$i_T = L \cdot \frac{b^3}{24} \cdot 2$$

$$\boxed{i_T = \frac{L \, b^3}{12}}$$

**Step 5 — Virtual shift of $G$ when the ship heels.**
When the ship heels through $\phi$, the free liquid surface remains *horizontal* (gravity). The liquid wedge therefore shifts; its centre of gravity moves laterally by an amount $\delta y$, and the moment about the original liquid centroid equals:

$$M_{\text{FSE}} = \rho_l \, g \, i_T \, \sin\phi$$

(an application of the small-heel theory of liquid shift — this is also the basis of the well-known **"fluid in a U-tube" analogy**).

**Step 6 — Equate to virtual shift of $G$.**
For the equivalent *solid* body to produce the same moment about the original metacentre, $G$ must effectively shift laterally by $GG'$ such that:

$$W \cdot GG' \cdot \cos\phi = \rho_l \, g \, i_T \, \sin\phi$$

In small-angle approximation ($\cos\phi \to 1$, $\sin\phi \to \phi$):

$$W \cdot GG' = \rho_l \, g \, i_T \, \phi \quad \text{(moment-arm form)}$$

Equivalently, the *virtual loss* of metacentric height is the height $GG'$ that $G$ effectively drops when $\phi$ is unit:

$$\boxed{GG' = \frac{\rho_l \, i_T}{\rho \, \nabla}}$$

**Step 7 — Final statement of effective GM.**

$$\boxed{GM_{\text{eff}} = GM - GG' = GM - \frac{\rho_l \, i_T}{\rho \, \nabla}}$$

This is the **complete FSE correction formula**, ready for numerical substitution in KTU problems.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Style Sub-Question)

> **Problem:** A surfaced submarine has displacement $\Delta = 2{,}400$ tonnes, waterplane area $A_W = 320$ m², and $KG = 4.80$ m. A rectangular ballast tank with $L = 12$ m and $b = 4$ m is half-filled with seawater. Compute the effective metacentric height.

**Step 1 — Volume of displacement.**

$$\nabla = \frac{\Delta}{\rho} = \frac{2{,}400 \times 1{,}000}{1{,}025} = 2{,}341.46 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Transverse moment of waterplane (assume $I_T \approx L_{WL} B^3/12$).**
Assume $L_{WL} \approx 92$ m, $B \approx 10$ m:

$$I_T \approx \frac{92 \times 10^3}{12} = \frac{92{,}000}{12} = 7{,}666.67 \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 3 — Compute $BM$.**

$$BM = \frac{I_T}{\nabla} = \frac{7{,}666.67}{2{,}341.46} = 3.274 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $KB$.**
Assume block coefficient $C_B = 0.55$, draft $T = 8.0$ m:

$$KB \approx 0.53 \, T = 4.24 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $GM$ without FSE.**

$$GM = KB + BM - KG = 4.24 + 3.274 - 4.80 = 2.714 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $i_T$ of ballast tank free surface.**

$$i_T^{\text{tank}} = \frac{12 \times 4^3}{12} = \frac{12 \times 64}{12} = 64 \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 7 — Compute FSE correction.**

$$GG' = \frac{\rho_l \, i_T^{\text{tank}}}{\rho \, \nabla} = \frac{1{,}025 \times 64}{1{,}025 \times 2{,}341.46} = \frac{64}{2{,}341.46} = 0.0273 \text{ m}$$

**Step 8 — Effective GM.**

$$GM_{\text{eff}} = 2.714 - 0.027 = 2.687 \text{ m}$$

> **Conclusion:** The half-filled ballast tank reduces $GM$ by only ~2.7 cm. With **more tanks partially filled**, the reductions are cumulative — this is why surfacing/diving is sequenced carefully.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Surface vs Submerged Stability Comparison

| Attribute | Surface Condition | Submerged Condition |
|---|---|---|
| Governing physics | Ship hydrostatics | Pressure-volume + pendulum |
| Stability type | Form stability (positive $BM$) | Weight stability (pendulum) |
| Stability metric | $GM > 0$ (typically 0.3 – 0.8 m) | Restoring moment from shiftable ballast |
| Critical hazard | Free Surface Effect | Compressible volume loss |
| Heel moment arm | Lever arm $GZ$ | Lever arm from ballast shift |
| Speed limit | Hull speed | Limited by drag only |
| Resistance regime | Wave + frictional | Frictional + form (laminar possible) |
| Buoyancy control | Variable ballast tanks | Variable ballast tanks |
| Trim control | Forward/aft groups | Forward/aft groups |
| Datum reference | Waterline | Pressure hull centreline |
| Equilibrium equation | $\Delta = W$, $G$ below $B$ | $\Delta = W$, neutral |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — Surfaced Submarine

The following diagram maps the **physical architecture and functional relationships** of a surfaced submarine, focusing on the watertight superstructure (casing), free-flooding regions, and ballast systems that together generate reserve buoyancy and form stability.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["ATMOSPHERE AND EXTERNAL ENVIRONMENT"] --> node2["WATERLINE WL"]
    node2 --> node3["HULL VOLUMES BELOW WL"]
    node2 --> node4["HULL VOLUMES ABOVE WL - RESERVE BUOYANCY"]

    node4 --> node5["SAIL OR CONNING TOWER"]
    node4 --> node6["OUTER LIGHT HULL OR CASING TOP"]
    node4 --> node7["SUPERSTRUCTURE DECK AND FAIRINGS"]

    node3 --> node8["PRESSURE HULL - INNER"]
    node3 --> node9["MAIN BALLAST TANKS MBT"]
    node3 --> node10["FREE FLOODING TANKS"]
    node3 --> node11["SOL BALLAST AND TRIM TANKS"]

    node8 --> node12["CREW COMPARTMENT"]
    node8 --> node13["BATTERY COMPARTMENT"]
    node8 --> node14["DIESEL GENERATOR ROOM"]

    node14 --> node15["DIESEL ENGINE AIR INTAKE"]
    node14 --> node16["EXHAUST TO ATMOSPHERE"]

    node9 --> node17["MAIN BALLAST TANK FILL VALVE"]
    node9 --> node18["MAIN BALLAST TANK BLOW VALVE"]
    node17 --> node19["SEA CHEST AND SEAWATER INTAKE"]
    node18 --> node20["LP BLOWER OR COMPRESSED AIR"]

    node11 --> node21["FORWARD TRIM TANK"]
    node11 --> node22["AFT TRIM TANK"]
    node11 --> node23["SAFETY OR EMERGENCY BALLAST"]

    node5 --> node24["PERISCOPE AND MAST SHEATH"]
    node5 --> node25["ATTACK PERISCOPE"]
    node5 --> node26["SEARCH PERISCOPE"]
    node5 --> node27["RADAR AND ESM MAST"]
    node5 --> node28["COMMUNICATION ANTENNA"]

    node10 --> node29["DECK DRAINAGE SUMP"]
    node10 --> node30["BOW AND STERN FREE FLOODING"]

    style node1 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px

```

### 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Surfacing Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stateA["FULLY SUBMERGED"] -->|Open MBT blow valves| stateB["BLOWING MAIN BALLAST"]
    stateB -->|Air displaces water| stateC["BUOYANCY EXCEEDS WEIGHT"]
    stateC -->|Vessel rises| stateD["PERISCOPE DEPTH"]
    stateD -->|Continue blowing| stateE["BROWSING DEPTH"]
    stateE -->|Continue blowing| stateF["WATERLINE BREACH"]
    stateF -->|Stop blowing at WL| stateG["SURFACED - ESTABLISH POSITIVE GM"]
    stateG -->|Start diesels| stateH["SNORKEL OR FULL SURFACE TRANSIT"]

    stateG --> checkA{"GM positive and adequate"}
    checkA -->|Yes| stateH
    checkA -->|No - FSE risk| stateI["EMERGENCY BALLAST TRANSFER"]
    stateI --> stateG

    style stateA fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff
    style stateB fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff
    stateC fill:#22d3ee,stroke:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff
    stateD fill:#67e8f9,stroke:#0c4a6e,color:#000000
    stateE fill:#a5f3fc,stroke:#0c4a6e,color:#000000
    stateF fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0c4a6e,color:#000000

    stateH fill:#86efac,stroke:#14532d,color:#000000
    stateI fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000000
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Stability Decision Tree — Why GM Matters

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    calc["Compute GM = KB + BM - KG"] --> check{"GM greater than 0"}
    check -->|No| capsize["CAPSIZE - DANGEROUS CONDITION"]
    check -->|Yes| range{"Range of GM"}
    range -->|Too low GM less than 0.15| stiff["VERY STIFF - POOR SEAKEEPING"]
    range -->|Adequate 0.15 to 1.5| good["STABLE - NORMAL OPERATION"]
    range -->|Too high GM greater than 1.5| tender["TENDER - HIGH GZ - STRESS"]

    fse["Apply FSE correction"] --> calc

    style capsize fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px
    style stiff fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#7c2d12,stroke-width:2px
    style good fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px
    style tender fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]**

**Q1. Define Reserve Buoyancy. Why is it critical for a submarine on the surface?**

**Model Answer:**
Reserve Buoyancy is the volume of watertight enclosed spaces above the waterline that can be submerged before the vessel is completely flooded. For a surfaced submarine, it is provided by the air spaces in the outer casing, sail, and superstructure above the waterline. It is critical because:
- It enables the submarine to absorb added weights (fuel, water, crew, snow) without sinking.
- It provides the dynamic buoyancy response required to ride over waves.
- It permits safe operation in adverse weather by allowing the submarine to submerge if necessary.
- For a typical surfaced submarine, reserve buoyancy is **15 – 25 %** of the displacement.

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Q2]**

**Q2. What is Free Surface Effect (FSE)? Mention its impact on submarine stability during transition.**

**Model Answer:**
The Free Surface Effect is the phenomenon by which a partially filled liquid in any tank acts to reduce the effective metacentric height $GM$ of a vessel. When the ship heels, the liquid shifts to maintain a horizontal surface, creating a heeling couple that reduces stability.
For a submarine, this is critical during the **surfacing and diving transitions**, when main ballast tanks are partially filled. The combined effect of multiple partially filled tanks can dramatically reduce $GM$, sometimes to zero, leading to a dangerous loss of transverse stability. Correct sequencing of tank filling/blowing is therefore essential.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Internal Choice Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2, Q1A]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive, with full steps, the expression for the loss in metacentric height due to free surface effect for a rectangular tank partially filled with liquid. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
(See Section 3.1 above for the complete derivation. The student must:
- Start by defining the tank geometry.
- Derive $i_T = Lb^3/12$.
- Apply the small-angle shift of liquid.
- Equate to virtual shift of $G$.
- Arrive at $GG' = \rho_l i_T / (\rho \nabla)$.
- State the effective GM.

**(b)** A surfaced submarine of displacement 2,500 tonnes floats in seawater. A rectangular ballast tank, 10 m long × 5 m wide, is 40 % filled with seawater. The waterplane area moment of inertia is $I_T = 8{,}500$ m⁴. The vertical centre of gravity is at $KG = 5.20$ m and $KB = 4.40$ m. Calculate the effective metacentric height. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Volume of displacement.**

$$\nabla = \frac{\Delta}{\rho} = \frac{2{,}500{,}000}{1{,}025} = 2{,}439.02 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute $BM$.**

$$BM = \frac{I_T}{\nabla} = \frac{8{,}500}{2{,}439.02} = 3.485 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $GM$ without FSE.**

$$GM = KB + BM - KG = 4.40 + 3.485 - 5.20 = 2.685 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $i_T$ of free surface.**

$$i_T = \frac{L b^3}{12} = \frac{10 \times 5^3}{12} = \frac{10 \times 125}{12} = 104.17 \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 5 — FSE correction.**

$$GG' = \frac{\rho_l i_T}{\rho \nabla} = \frac{1{,}025 \times 104.17}{1{,}025 \times 2{,}439.02} = \frac{104.17}{2{,}439.02} = 0.0427 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Effective GM.**

$$GM_{\text{eff}} = 2.685 - 0.0427 = 2.642 \text{ m}$$

> **Conclusion:** The effective GM remains positive and adequate; however, multiple such tanks would compound the reduction.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the transverse stability of a surfaced submarine, clearly marking the metacentre, centre of gravity, and centre of buoyancy. Derive the formula for metacentric height. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

For a surfaced submarine floating at rest, the **centre of buoyancy $B$** lies on the centreline directly below the **centre of gravity $G$**. When the vessel is tilted by a small angle $\phi$, $B$ shifts to a new position $B_1$, and the vertical through $B_1$ meets the original centreline at the **metacentre $M$**. The distance $GM$ is the metacentric height.

```
              G
              |
              |       phi (heel angle)
              |  ____________
              | /            +
              |/             B1
        ------+--------------+-----  original WL
             /|
            / |
           /  |
          M   B
         (metacentre on original centreline)
```

Derivation (transverse BM):

By definition, $BM = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}$, where $I_T$ is the transverse second moment of the waterplane area about the longitudinal centreline. The full metacentric height is:

$$GM = KB + BM - KG = KB + \frac{I_T}{\nabla} - KG$$

Stability requires $GM > 0$, and an *adequate* $GM$ for a surfaced submarine is typically **0.30 – 0.80 m**.

**(b)** Compare in detail the stability of a submarine on the surface and in the fully submerged condition. Highlight the role of ballast tanks and the metacentre. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Surfaced | Submerged |
|---|---|---|
| Stability mechanism | **Form stability** from hull shape and metacentric height | **Weight stability** from shiftable ballast (pendulum) |
| Restoring couple | $W \cdot GZ$ where $GZ = GM \sin\phi$ | From shift of mercury or solid ballast in tanks |
| Role of metacentre | Critical — $GM > 0$ required | Not defined — no waterplane |
| Effect of MBT | Provides variable buoyancy | Provides variable buoyancy |
| Heel moment | Standard $GZ$ curve | Restoring from shifted ballast moment |
| Free surface effect | Major concern during transition | None (tanks either full or empty) |
| Critical hazard | FSE when tanks partially full | Loss of neutral buoyancy (compressibility) |
| Speed limit | Hull speed (~10–12 knots) | Much higher (~25–30 knots) |

The surfaced submarine relies on the conventional metacentre, while the submerged submarine relies on gravity acting through a shifted ballast mass — essentially a pendulum with the centre of gravity below the centre of buoyancy, producing a self-righting moment whenever the vessel tilts.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $BM$ with $GM$:** $BM$ is only the contribution from the waterplane moment; $GM$ also includes $KB$ and $KG$. Marks are lost if you forget $KB - KG$.
> 2. **Ignoring the 40 % / 50 % partial fill:** FSE applies even at low fill levels — even a thin film of free liquid causes the effect. Do not write "negligible" without a numerical estimate.
> 3. **Wrong density:** Use $\rho = 1{,}025$ kg/m³ for seawater, not 1,000 kg/m³ (freshwater).
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Always convert displacement in tonnes to kg before dividing by $\rho$ in kg/m³.
> 5. **Forgetting the parallel-axis theorem:** For non-rectangular tanks, $i_T = i_{\text{centroidal}} + A \cdot d^2$ — KTU expects this in irregular-shaped tanks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Submarines on the Surface**

- [ ] **Surface condition** = submarine behaves as a surface ship; reserve buoyancy comes from the casing top, sail, and superstructure above the waterline.
- [ ] **Reserve buoyancy** is typically **15 – 25 %** of displacement; provides margin for added weights and wave action.
- [ ] **Surface stability** is governed by $GM = KB + BM - KG$, with $BM = I_T/\nabla$.
- [ ] **Adequate $GM$** for a surfaced submarine: **0.30 – 0.80 m**; too low = stiff ship, too high = tender ship with high GZ.
- [ ] **Free Surface Effect** reduces $GM$ by $\Delta GM_{\text{FSE}} = \rho_l \, i_T / (\rho \, \nabla)$.
- [ ] For a rectangular tank: $i_T = L \, b^3 / 12$.
- [ ] FSE is the **#1 hazard during surfacing/diving** — multiple partially filled tanks can cumulatively reduce $GM$ to zero.
- [ ] **Surfaced speed** is limited by hull speed: $V_{\max} \approx 1.34 \sqrt{L_{WL}}$ m/s.
- [ ] **Submerged vs surface** are governed by *different physics*: surface = form stability; submerged = weight (pendulum) stability.
- [ ] Submarine must surface (or snorkel) for **diesel operation, battery charging, radio communication, and crew habitability**.
- [ ] The **outer light hull** is free-flooding below WL and watertight above WL — a unique submarine feature.
- [ ] **Diving planes:** small fairwater planes work on surface; large main planes only effective when submerged (require flow over them).
- [ ] KTU-favourite derivation: **FSE correction**, $i_T$ of a rectangular tank, and **effective $GM$** with numerical substitution.
- [ ] Common unit conversions: 1 tonne = 1,000 kg; $\rho_{\text{sea}} = 1{,}025$ kg/m³; $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- [ ] Always state the **density of seawater** explicitly in your numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
