Submarine Submerged
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST634 - SUBMARINES AND SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Submarine Submerged

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Submarine Submerged Condition

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Submerged Condition of a Submarine:**
> The *submerged condition* is the operating state in which a submarine is fully under the water surface, with the **sail (fin/conning tower)** and **upper hull deck** completely below the waterline. In this state, the submarine operates as a true *underwater vehicle*, controlled entirely by **hydroplanes (planes)**, **trim tanks**, and **depth-control systems**, while maintaining **neutral buoyancy** (Weight = Buoyancy) for a prescribed operating depth.

Mathematically, the submerged condition satisfies the classical hydrostatic equilibrium:

$$W = B$$

where $W$ is the displacement (weight) of the submarine and $B$ is the buoyant force equal to the weight of the displaced seawater.

The **reserve buoyancy** $R_B$, which is positive on the surface, **becomes zero (or slightly negative with controlled flooding)** when submerged:

$$R_B = B - W = 0 \quad \text{(ideal submerged)}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture — "The Submerged Submarine as a Suspended Balloon":**
> Imagine holding a sealed glass bottle filled with just enough air to float *neutrally* inside a swimming pool. The bottle neither sinks nor rises — it is *suspended*. A submerged submarine behaves the same way. To go down, you add a little water to a tiny ballast chamber (making it slightly heavier than the water it displaces). To go up, you blow that water out with high-pressure air, restoring the upward push. The depth at which it hangs is governed by the *delicate balance* between weight and buoyancy, while the *hydroplanes* act like the fins of a fish, allowing the pilot to "fly" through the water.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Density of seawater (standard):** $\rho_{sw} = \mathbf{1025\ kg/m^3}$ (vs. fresh water $\rho_{fw} = 1000\ kg/m^3$)
- **Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$:** $P(h) = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$
- **Standard gravity:** $g = \mathbf{9.81\ m/s^2}$
- **Sea-level atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = \mathbf{101.325\ kPa}$
- **Submerged speed regimes:** Silent running (5–8 knots), patrol speed (8–15 knots), sprint (>20+ knots, nuclear)
- **Operational depth vs. crush depth:** Modern nuclear SSNs typically operate at $\sim 250$–$300\ m$ with crush depths of $\sim 450$–$600\ m$.

### 1.4 Why "Submerged" Is a Distinct Module

> [!NOTE]
> On the surface, a submarine is essentially a *surface ship with a collapsible float*. Submerged, it is a *completely different vehicle*: dynamics, control, stability, resistance, and survivability all change. Module 2 of PENST634 focuses on this critical transition and the steady/unsteady physics of the fully underwater state.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Submersion Process (Transition Surface → Submerged)

A submarine transitions from surface to submerged through a controlled, multi-stage operation:

1. **Surface Trim (Awash):** Ventilate MBTs (Main Ballast Tanks) to fill them with air → submarine floats higher.
2. **Flooding the MBTs:** Open sea-water flooding valves at the top of the MBTs and vent the compressed air. Seawater enters, displacing air. As weight $W$ increases, **reserve buoyancy $R_B$** drops.
3. **Blowing the Main Ballasts (or "Blowing Down"):** For emergency surfacing, high-pressure air (HPA) at 200–300 bar is injected, expelling water.
4. **Submerging (Sinking Below Freeboard):** When $R_B \leq 0$, the deck edge submerges.
5. **Underway Depth Keeping:** Hydroplanes are used to drive the boat to its ordered depth.
6. **Steady Submerged:** Trim tanks (forward/after) fine-tune fore-aft attitude; fairwater planes control depth.

## 2.2 Hydrostatic Equilibrium in Submerged Condition

The fundamental equilibrium of a freely floating/submerged body is:

$$W = B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla$$

where $\nabla$ (displaced volume) is the **submerged volume** of the pressure hull (almost equal to the *total* hull volume once fully submerged, minus small external free-flooding spaces).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reserve Buoyancy in Submerged State:**
> On the surface, $R_B$ is typically **20 %–30 %** of the displacement. When fully submerged, the MBTs are full of seawater — the *variable buoyancy* system is exhausted. $R_B$ becomes $\approx 0$ (for true neutral trim at a fixed depth) or **negative** if the submarine needs to *sink* to a deeper depth, then trimmed back to neutral by deballasting.

## 2.3 Hydrostatic Pressure & Pressure Hull

The hydrostatic pressure acting uniformly on the hull at depth $h$ is:

$$P(h) = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$$

Modern submarines use a **cylindrical pressure hull** of high-tensile steel (HY-80, HY-100) or titanium (e.g., Russian Alfa class). The hoop stress in a thin-walled cylinder of radius $r$ and wall thickness $t$ is:

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P(h) \cdot r}{t}$$

> [!CAUTION]
> This sets the **crush depth** — beyond this depth, the hull yields. Designers enforce a **1.5× safety factor** between operating depth and collapse depth.

## 2.4 Submerged Stability

### 2.2.1 Transverse (Rolling) Stability
Transverse stability in the submerged state is governed by the **metacentric height** $\overline{GM}$:

$$\overline{GM} = \overline{KB} + \overline{BM} - \overline{KG}$$

Because the waterplane area $A_w \to 0$ when fully submerged (only tiny free-flooding areas remain), $\overline{BM} \to 0$. Therefore:

$$\overline{GM} \approx \overline{KB} - \overline{KG}$$

This is *much smaller* than in surface condition → submarines have **poor transverse stability when submerged**. Designers counter this with **active attitude control** (planes).

### 2.2.2 Longitudinal (Pitching) Stability
Longitudinal metacentric height $\overline{GM_L}$ remains finite even when submerged because the longitudinal second moment of area is non-zero (the hull is long and slender). The submarine is naturally stable in pitch when trimmed properly.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Meaning / Units |
|---|---------|----------|-----------------|
| 1 | Hydrostatic equilibrium | $W = B$ | Weight = Buoyancy, $\lbrack N \rbrack$ |
| 2 | Buoyancy force | $B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | $\nabla$ = displaced volume $\lbrack m^3 \rbrack$ |
| 3 | Reserve buoyancy | $R_B = B - W$ | $\lbrack N \rbrack$ or $\lbrack tonnes \rbrack$ |
| 4 | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | $P(h) = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw}\,g\,h$ | $\lbrack Pa \rbrack$ |
| 5 | Hoop stress in cylindrical hull | $\sigma_{hoop} = P \cdot r \mid t$ | $\lbrack Pa \rbrack$ |
| 6 | Critical (collapse) pressure | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2\,E}{1 - \nu^2}\left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^3$ | Elastic buckling, $\lbrack Pa \rbrack$ |
| 7 | Metacentric height | $\overline{GM} = \overline{KB} + \overline{BM} - \overline{KG}$ | $\lbrack m \rbrack$ |
| 8 | $\overline{BM}$ for waterplane | $\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I_T}{V}$ | $I_T$ = 2nd moment of waterplane $\lbrack m^4 \rbrack$ |
| 9 | Submerged $\overline{BM_T}$ | $\overline{BM_T} \to 0$ as $A_w \to 0$ | Waterplane area vanishes |
| 10 | Submerged speed–power | $P = R_T \cdot V$ / $\eta_{prop}$ | $R_T$ = total resistance $\lbrack N \rbrack$ |
| 11 | Submerged trim equation | $W \cdot \overline{LCG} = B \cdot \overline{LCB} + M_{trim}$ | Moment balance |
| 12 | Depth from hydroplane force | $\Delta h \approx \dfrac{F_{plane} \cdot \Delta t}{m_{sub} \cdot g}$ | Quasi-steady depth change |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The submerged condition is the *defining operational mode* of a submarine. Stealth, sonar performance, propulsive efficiency at low speed, and survivability all depend on maintaining controlled depth and trim. Naval architects, sonar engineers, and control-systems designers all rely on these formulas.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure on the Submerged Pressure Hull

We start from the fundamental relation of fluid statics. Consider a small fluid element of seawater at depth $h$ below the free surface. The vertical force balance on the element under equilibrium (no shear, no acceleration) gives:

$$\frac{dP}{dh} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g$$

Integrating from the free surface ($h = 0$, $P = P_{atm}$) to an arbitrary depth $h$:

$$\int_{P_{atm}}^{P(h)} dP = \int_{0}^{h} \rho_{sw}\,g\,dh$$

Since $\rho_{sw}$ and $g$ are treated as constants for shallow-to-medium operating depths (incompressible assumption valid for $h < 1000$ m):

$$P(h) - P_{atm} = \rho_{sw}\,g\,h$$

$$\boxed{P(h) = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw}\,g\,h}$$

**Engineering note:** At $h = 300$ m, $P(h) \approx 101.3 + (1025)(9.81)(300)/1000 \approx 101.3 + 3017 \approx 3.12$ MPa (≈ 31 atm). This is the actual pressure on every square metre of the hull.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hoop Stress in the Cylindrical Pressure Hull

Take a half-cylinder of length $L$, radius $r$, wall thickness $t$, internally pressurised to $P$. The internal pressure pushes outward on the curved surface. Projecting all forces in the horizontal direction:

- **Outward force on the cut faces (two end caps projected):** $2 \cdot P \cdot r \cdot L$
- **Resisting force from hoop tension in the two walls (each of cross-section $t \cdot L$):** $2 \cdot \sigma_{hoop} \cdot t \cdot L$

Equilibrium requires the resisting force to balance the outward force:

$$2\,P\,r\,L = 2\,\sigma_{hoop}\,t\,L$$

Solving for $\sigma_{hoop}$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P\,r}{t}}$$

**Validity check:** Valid for thin-walled cylinders where $t \mid r \ll 0.1$. For a typical SSN with $r = 4$ m and $t = 0.08$ m, $t/r = 0.02$ → thin-wall assumption excellent.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Submerged Reserve Buoyancy and Why It Vanishes

Reserve buoyancy on the surface is:

$$R_{B,\,surface} = B_{max} - W = \rho_{sw}\,g\,\nabla_{max} - W$$

where $\nabla_{max}$ is the *full* displaced volume (hull + non-flooded MBTs).

When the submarine submerges, the MBTs fill with seawater. Let $V_{MBT}$ be the total volume of the main ballast tanks. The new displacement is:

$$W_{sub} = W + \rho_{sw}\,g\,V_{MBT}$$

The buoyancy when fully submerged (entire external envelope underwater) is:

$$B_{sub} = \rho_{sw}\,g\,(\nabla_{max} + V_{MBT,\,ext})$$

where $V_{MBT,\,ext}$ is the *external* envelope of the MBTs (the same $V_{MBT}$ in the typical single-hull case). The *net* reserve buoyancy in the ideal fully submerged condition is:

$$R_{B,\,sub} = B_{sub} - W_{sub} = \rho_{sw}\,g\,V_{MBT} - \rho_{sw}\,g\,V_{MBT} = 0$$

Hence, in the **ideal fully submerged case**:

$$\boxed{R_{B,\,sub} = 0}$$

This is the foundation of the operational concept: the variable-buoyancy system (MBTs) is **exhausted** when submerged. Any further depth change must be made by the **fixed-displacement** control (planes + fine trim).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Transverse Stability When Submerged

The general metacentric formula is:

$$\overline{GM} = \overline{KB} + \overline{BM} - \overline{KG}$$

For a surface ship, $\overline{BM} = I_T / \nabla$ where $I_T$ is the transverse second moment of the *waterplane area*. In submerged condition, the only "waterplane" is the tiny free-flooding area at appendages (sail planes, limber holes). As $A_w \to 0$:

$$\overline{BM_T} = \frac{I_T}{\nabla} \to 0 \quad \text{as} \quad A_w \to 0$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\overline{GM_T^{sub}} \approx \overline{KB} - \overline{KG}}$$

This value is small (often $< 0.5$ m for a submerged submarine). It means:
- The submarine has **little passive righting moment**.
- Roll oscillations are **lightly damped** and slow.
- **Active control** (planes, gyros) is required to manage roll.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Surface vs. Submerged Comparison

| Parameter | Surface Condition | Submerged Condition |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| Reserve buoyancy $R_B$ | **20–30 %** of $W$ | $\approx 0$ |
| Waterplane area $A_w$ | Large (deck) | $\approx 0$ |
| $\overline{BM_T}$ | Significant | $\approx 0$ |
| $\overline{GM_T}$ | Large → very stable roll | Small → weakly stable |
| Depth control | Ballast tank flooding | Hydroplanes + fine trim |
| Speed regime | Surface ship hydrodynamics | Submerged body hydrodynamics |
| Pressure hull load | $\sim 0$ gauge | $\rho_{sw}\,g\,h$ gauge |
| Visibility | Optical/radar | Acoustic only |
| Propulsor | Surface-piercing (screw) cavitates | Cavitation limits speed |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Submerged Submarine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Command: Order Depth] --> B[Depth Error Calculator]
    B --> C{Hydroplane Controller}
    C --> D[Fairwater Planes]
    C --> E[Stern Planes]
    D --> F[Depth Change]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Submarine Hull Body]
    G --> H[Pressure Hull]
    H --> I[Hydrostatic Pressure Sensor]
    I --> B
    G --> J[MBT Flood Valves]
    J --> K[Seawater In Out]
    G --> L[Trim Tanks Fwd Aft]
    L --> M[Trim Pump]
    M --> N[Fore Aft Attitude]
    N --> G
    G --> O[Sonar Array]
    O --> P[Tactical Computer]
    P --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** This block-level functional architecture shows the closed-loop depth-control system. The *command depth* is compared with the *measured depth* (from pressure sensor); the error drives the *fairwater* and *stern planes*; meanwhile the *trim tanks* independently maintain fore-aft attitude. The *sonar* feeds the tactical computer, which can override the commanded depth (e.g., bottom-avoidance, evasion).

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — Submersion Sequence

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Surface
    Surface --> Awash: Flood MBT partially
    Awash --> Submerging: MBT vents open, water floods
    Submerging --> Submerged: Freeboard zero, planes take over
    Submerged --> DepthChange: Hydroplane deflection
    DepthChange --> Submerged: Hydroplane trimmed to zero
    Submerged --> Surfacing: Emergency or scheduled ascent
    Surfacing --> Surface: MBT blown with HPA
    Surface --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pressure Hull Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Sea Pressure P] --> B[Outer Fairing]
    B --> C[Pressure Hull Cylinder]
    C --> D[Internal Atmosphere 1 atm]
    C --> E[Ballast Tank Outer]
    E --> F[Sea Water in MBT]
    C --> G[Internal Equipment and Crew]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This simplified cross-section illustrates the *double-hull* concept: the **pressure hull** is the watertight, load-bearing cylinder; the **outer fairing / MBTs** carry external hydrostatic pressure but are not the primary load-bearing structure. This is essential for understanding how a submarine can be "fully submerged" with internal compartments at 1 atm.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]
**Define the "submerged condition" of a submarine. What is the role of Main Ballast Tanks (MBTs) in achieving this condition?**

**Model Answer:**
The submerged condition is the operating state in which the entire pressure hull, sail, and external appendages lie below the waterline, with the submarine controlled exclusively by hydroplanes and trim systems while maintaining neutral buoyancy ($W = B$). MBTs are the *variable buoyancy* tanks. On the surface they are filled with air, contributing to reserve buoyancy. To submerge, the MBT vents are opened and flooding valves admit seawater, replacing the air. The added mass of water eliminates the reserve buoyancy, and the submarine sinks below the surface. To surface, high-pressure air (HPA) blows the seawater out, restoring positive reserve buoyancy.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
**Why does a submarine have very low transverse stability when fully submerged? How is this problem overcome in practice?**

**Model Answer:**
When fully submerged, the waterplane area $A_w$ effectively vanishes, so the transverse metacentric radius $\overline{BM_T} = I_T / \nabla \to 0$. The metacentric height reduces to $\overline{GM_T} \approx \overline{KB} - \overline{KG}$, a small positive value that gives a weak passive righting moment. In practice, submarines use **active control systems** — hydroplanes (sail planes / fairwater planes and stern planes) operating as control surfaces, and **gyroscopic / inertial reference units** to detect and correct roll — to maintain transverse attitude. High-speed submerged manoeuvring also generates hydrodynamic restoring moments from the planes.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Submerged Buoyancy & Reserve Buoyancy Analysis

> **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** A submarine of surface displacement $W = 6000$ tonnes and total enclosed volume $V = 6100$ m³ operates in seawater ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³). When fully submerged with MBTs flooded, 95 % of the MBT volume is seawater. The MBT volume is $V_{MBT} = 800$ m³. Calculate:
> (i) the reserve buoyancy in surface condition,
> (ii) the reserve buoyancy in fully submerged condition, and
> (iii) the additional weight of seawater required to submerge to a slightly negative $R_B$ of $-50$ tonnes.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**(i) Surface reserve buoyancy:**

On the surface, MBTs are full of air. Buoyancy is the weight of the total displaced volume:

$$B_{surf} = \rho_{sw}\,g\,V = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 6100$$

$$B_{surf} = 6{,}128{,}032.5 \times 9.81 \approx \text{(recheck with mass form)}$$

Working in **mass units** (tonnes-force, $g$ cancels):

$$B_{surf} = 1025 \times 6100 / 1000 = 6252.5\ \text{tonnes}$$

Surface displacement (weight):

$$W = 6000\ \text{tonnes}$$

Reserve buoyancy:

$$R_{B,surf} = B_{surf} - W = 6252.5 - 6000 = 252.5\ \text{tonnes}$$

**(ii) Submerged reserve buoyancy:**

Submerged weight (MBTs 95 % flooded):

$$W_{sub} = 6000 + 0.95 \times 1025 \times 800 / 1000 = 6000 + 779 = 6779\ \text{tonnes}$$

Submerged buoyancy (full envelope displaced):

$$B_{sub} = 1025 \times 6100 / 1000 = 6252.5\ \text{tonnes}$$

$$R_{B,sub} = B_{sub} - W_{sub} = 6252.5 - 6779 = -526.5\ \text{tonnes}$$

(Negative — the submarine would *sink* on its own.)

**(iii) Additional water for $R_B = -50$ tonnes:**

We want $B_{sub} - W_{target} = -50$, so:

$$W_{target} = B_{sub} + 50 = 6252.5 + 50 = 6302.5\ \text{tonnes}$$

Required seawater mass:

$$m_{sw} = W_{target} - W = 6302.5 - 6000 = 302.5\ \text{tonnes}$$

Corresponding MBT fill fraction (relative to $1025$ kg/m³):

$$V_{fill} = \frac{302.5 \times 1000}{1025} = 295.1\ \text{m}^3$$

So the MBTs must be filled to $295.1 / 800 = 36.9\%$ of their total volume to achieve $R_B = -50$ tonnes. The submarine is then **slightly heavy** and will sink slowly; planes control the rate of depth change.

> **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** With the same submarine at operating depth $h = 250$ m, the pressure hull is a cylinder of mean radius $r = 4.0$ m, wall thickness $t = 80$ mm, made of HY-80 steel. Calculate:
> (i) the hydrostatic pressure on the hull,
> (ii) the hoop stress,
> (iii) the factor of safety relative to HY-80 yield strength $\sigma_y = 550$ MPa.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Hydrostatic pressure:**

$$P = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw}\,g\,h = 101{,}325 + 1025 \times 9.81 \times 250$$

$$P = 101{,}325 + 2{,}513{,}812.5 = 2{,}615{,}137.5\ \text{Pa} \approx 2.62\ \text{MPa}$$

**(ii) Hoop stress:**

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P\,r}{t} = \frac{2.615 \times 10^6 \times 4.0}{0.080}$$

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{10.46 \times 10^6}{0.08} = 130.75\ \text{MPa}$$

**(iii) Factor of safety:**

$$FoS = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{hoop}} = \frac{550}{130.75} = 4.21$$

The factor of safety is comfortably above 1.5 — operating depth is well within the hull's elastic regime.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Submerged Stability & Depth Control (Alternative Choice)

> **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** For a fully submerged submarine, the waterplane area $A_w$ is $0.4$ m² (only the small free-flooding area around the sail planes). The transverse second moment of area of this waterplane is $I_T = 0.05$ m⁴. The displacement volume is $\nabla = 5800$ m³. The vertical centre of buoyancy KB is at $2.30$ m above the keel, and the centre of gravity KG is at $2.45$ m above the keel. Calculate:
> (i) the transverse metacentric radius $\overline{BM_T}$ in the submerged condition,
> (ii) the metacentric height $\overline{GM_T}$,
> (iii) comment on the stability of the submarine in roll.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**(i) Metacentric radius:**

$$\overline{BM_T} = \frac{I_T}{\nabla} = \frac{0.05}{5800} = 8.62 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m} \approx 0.0000086\ \text{m}$$

This is essentially **zero** (microscopic) — confirming that the submerged submarine has negligible waterplane contribution to transverse stability.

**(ii) Metacentric height:**

$$\overline{GM_T} = \overline{KB} + \overline{BM_T} - \overline{KG} = 2.30 + 0.0000086 - 2.45 = -0.15\ \text{m}$$

The metacentric height is **negative** (≈ –0.15 m). The submarine is *unstable* in pure roll in the passive sense.

**(iii) Comment:**

A negative $\overline{GM_T}$ in the passive case means that, without active control, the submarine would *capsize* in roll. In practice, this is acceptable because:
1. The fairwater / sail planes are deliberately designed to provide a **hydrodynamic restoring moment** in roll.
2. Modern submarines have **active attitude control systems** (gyros, planes) operating continuously.
3. The negative $\overline{GM_T}$ can be **tuned by shifting the centre of gravity** using the trim tanks — designers can move $G$ below $M$ to give positive $\overline{GM_T}$.

> **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A submarine is trimmed to neutral buoyancy at $h = 200$ m and travels at $V = 8$ knots with total resistance $R_T = 80$ kN. The propulsive efficiency is $\eta = 0.70$. The fairwater planes have a planform area $S_p = 6$ m² and produce a lift coefficient $C_L = 0.6$ at angle of attack $\alpha = 10°$. Density of seawater $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³. Calculate:
> (i) the shaft power required,
> (ii) the force generated by the planes,
> (iii) the steady-state depth rate of change if the planes are held deflected (assume the net downward force is fully balanced by the buoyancy correction; use $\dot{h} \approx 0$ for steady state and find the *required* plane force to maintain depth in the presence of a 10 kN residual negative buoyancy).

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Shaft power:**

$$V = 8\ \text{knots} = 8 \times 0.5144 = 4.115\ \text{m/s}$$

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{\eta} = \frac{80{,}000 \times 4.115}{0.70}$$

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{329{,}200}{0.70} = 470{,}286\ \text{W} \approx 470\ \text{kW}$$

**(ii) Plane force:**

$$F_{plane} = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^2\,S_p\,C_L = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (4.115)^2 \times 6 \times 0.6$$

$$F_{plane} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 16.93 \times 6 \times 0.6 = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 16.93 \times 3.6$$

$$F_{plane} \approx 31{,}235\ \text{N} \approx 31.2\ \text{kN}$$

**(iii) Steady-state required plane force:**

If the residual negative buoyancy is 10 kN downward, the planes must produce a net **upward** force of 10 kN to maintain depth. The available plane force is 31.2 kN, which is more than sufficient (about 3× margin). The submarine can therefore maintain $h = 200$ m steadily.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in PENST634 Module 2:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 95 % fill rule:** In Question A(a)(ii), the MBTs are *not* 100 % full in real submerged condition — typically 95 % due to entrapped air. Always re-read the problem statement for the fill percentage.
> 2. **Mixing up mass and weight units:** When $\rho$ is given in kg/m³ and $V$ in m³, the product $\rho V$ is in **kilograms**. Convert to tonnes ($/1000$) or to Newtons ($\times g$) **consistently**. Do not mix kN with kg mid-calculation.
> 3. **Hoop stress without atmospheric pressure:** Some students forget to add $P_{atm}$ to the gauge hydrostatic pressure. At deep depth, $P_{atm}$ is small, but for an exact answer always include it.
> 4. **Stability sign convention:** A *negative* $\overline{GM_T}$ in the submerged condition is normal — do not flag it as an "error" in your answer. Just explain how it is handled.
> 5. **Plane force direction:** Planes can produce both upward and downward force depending on deflection. The sign of $C_L$ is dictated by the deflection sign. Show the direction explicitly.
> 6. **Units in $g$:** KTU prefers $g = 9.81$ m/s². Using 10 m/s² will lose 0.5 mark in long calculations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Submarine Submerged (Module 2)**

- ☐ Submerged condition = $W = B$ (neutral buoyancy) + submarine fully under waterline.
- ☐ Reserve buoyancy **vanishes** in ideal fully submerged condition: $R_{B,sub} = 0$.
- ☐ Main Ballast Tanks (MBTs) provide the *variable* buoyancy for transition; once full, they are exhausted.
- ☐ Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$: $P(h) = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw}\,g\,h$.
- ☐ Hoop stress in cylindrical pressure hull: $\sigma_{hoop} = P \cdot r \mid t$.
- ☐ Submerged $\overline{BM_T} \to 0$ as waterplane area $\to 0$.
- ☐ Submerged $\overline{GM_T} \approx \overline{KB} - \overline{KG}$ — small, often negative.
- ☐ Longitudinal (pitch) stability remains good (long slender hull).
- ☐ Depth control = **hydroplanes** (fairwater + stern); fore-aft trim = **trim tanks**.
- ☐ Pressure hull material: HY-80/HY-100 steel or titanium; crush depth = 1.5× operating depth.
- ☐ Standard seawater density $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³; standard $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- ☐ Submerged resistance & power: $P_{shaft} = R_T \cdot V / \eta$.
- ☐ Plane force: $F = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^2\,S_p\,C_L$.
- ☐ Active attitude control is mandatory — passive stability alone is insufficient.
- ☐ Always state **direction of force/moment** explicitly in numerical answers.
- ☐ Units: be consistent (N vs. tonnes vs. kg; gauge vs. absolute pressure).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Buoyancy Elements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST634 - SUBMARINES AND SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Buoyancy Elements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the discipline of submarine and submersible engineering, **Buoyancy Elements** are defined as the *integrated collection of structural compartments, tanks, voids, and low-density materials* within a pressure hull and outer hydrodynamic envelope that collectively govern the static and dynamic equilibrium of the vehicle between the upward hydrostatic supporting force and the downward gravitational load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim style):** *Buoyancy elements are the engineered assemblies — including main ballast tanks, trim tanks, variable ballast tanks, free-flooding spaces, and buoyancy modules (syntactic foam / buoyancy blocks) — whose controllable volumetric displacement of seawater and internal air/water ratios permit the submarine to operate in the three classical states: surfaced (positive buoyancy), submerged at neutral buoyancy, and submerged with negative or variable buoyancy.*

## 1.2 The Three Cardinal Buoyancy States

| State | Net Force Condition | Operational Meaning |
|-------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Positive Buoyancy** | $F_B > W$ | Vessel floats upward / on surface |
| **Neutral Buoyancy** | $F_B = W$ | Hovering at constant depth |
| **Negative Buoyancy** | $F_B < W$ | Vessel sinks / submerges |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cartesian Diver Inside a Ship"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy for a First-Time Reader:**
> Imagine a sealed plastic water bottle with a small glass pipette (a "diver") floating inside. When you squeeze the bottle, the diver sinks; when you release, the diver floats back. A submarine works on the *exact same physical principle*, but at industrial scale. Instead of squeezing the hull, the submarine deliberately **lets seawater in** to its ballast tanks (like air leaving the pipette) to submerge, and **blasts high-pressure air out** to push the water back (like air re-entering the pipette) to surface. The buoyancy elements are the *bottle, the pipette, and the air pump combined*.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Density of seawater (standard):** $\rho_{sw} = \mathbf{1025\ kg/m^3}$
- **Density of freshwater:** $\rho_{fw} = \mathbf{1000\ kg/m^3}$
- **Gravitational acceleration (standard):** $g = \mathbf{9.80665\ m/s^2}$
- **Specific weight of seawater:** $\gamma_{sw} = \mathbf{10.05\ kN/m^3}$
- **Standard operating depth classification (KTU):** Shallow < 300 m, Deep 300–3000 m, Ultra-Deep > 3000 m.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Archimedes' Principle — The Foundation

The hydrostatic supporting force exerted on any fully or partially submerged body equals the **weight of the fluid displaced** by that body. For a submarine this translates to:

$$F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla$$

Where $\nabla$ is the **submerged displaced volume** of the vehicle.

## 2.2 The Two-State Weight Philosophy

A submarine is engineered to have **two distinct weights** that the buoyancy elements must balance:

1. **Surfaced (Light) Weight $W_L$** — Total mass when tanks are full of *air*.
2. **Submerged (Loaded) Weight $W_S$** — Total mass when main ballast tanks (MBTs) are full of *seawater*.

The transition between $W_L$ and $W_S$ is precisely the **submergence / surfacing cycle**.

## 2.3 Operational Logic — Why We Need Each Buoyancy Element

### Step 1 — Main Ballast Tanks (MBTs)
- The **largest buoyancy-control components**, located in the outer hull (saddle tanks) or as free-flooding hard tanks.
- **Function:** Rapidly shift the vessel from positive to slightly negative buoyancy.
- **Operation:** Flooding = submerge; Blowing = surface.

### Step 2 — Trim Tanks (Forward / Aft)
- Small, **variable-volume water tanks** used to balance fore-aft moments.
- **Function:** Prevent the submarine from diving nose-first or tail-first uncontrollably.

### Step 3 — Variable Ballast Tanks (VBTs) / Trim Compensation
- **Fine-tuning chambers** used to compensate for fuel consumption, weapon expenditure, or payload changes during a mission.
- Critical for **maintaining neutral buoyancy** at depth for prolonged periods.

### Step 4 — Auxiliary / Safety Ballast Tanks
- Provide **emergency positive buoyancy** (Emergency Main Ballast Tank Blow — EMBT Blow) in casualty conditions.

### Step 5 — Buoyancy Modules (Syntactic Foam / Blocks)
- **Solid, non-compressible buoyancy** in deep submersibles where rigid air spaces would collapse under hydrostatic pressure.
- Typically glass microballoons embedded in epoxy matrix.

## 2.4 The Three Classical Buoyancy States — Mathematical Form

### State 1 — Surfaced (Equilibrium on Free Surface)

$$F_{B,\,surfaced} = W_L$$

### State 2 — Neutral Buoyancy (Hovering Underwater)

$$F_{B,\,sub} = W_S \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla = W_S$$

### State 3 — Negative Buoyancy (Controlled Descent)

$$F_B < W_S \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \frac{W_S - F_B}{m} \quad \text{(downward)}$$

## 2.5 Reserve Buoyancy — The Hidden Safety Margin

> [!NOTE]
> **Reserve Buoyancy (RB)** is the *additional* displacement a submarine possesses when surfaced, beyond what is required to support its surfaced weight. It is the safety margin against flooding casualties.

$$RB = \nabla - \frac{W_L}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g}$$

Expressed as a **fractional safety factor**:

$$SF = \frac{F_{B,\,surfaced}}{W_L} = 1 + \frac{RB}{W_L/(\rho_{sw}\,g)}$$

For military submarines, **$SF$ typically lies between 1.10 and 1.30**, meaning 10–30% reserve buoyancy.

## 2.6 Ballast Volume Calculation for Submergence

The volume of seawater that must enter the MBTs to shift the submarine from surfaced to submerged is:

$$V_{MBT} = \frac{W_S - \rho_{sw}\,g\,V_{hull}}{\rho_{sw}\,g} = \frac{W_S - F_{B,\,surfaced}}{\rho_{sw}\,g}$$

Equivalently, written as a mass-balance:

$$m_{ballast} = \frac{W_S - F_B}{g}$$

## 2.7 Free-Surface Effect and Stability

When ballast tanks are partially filled, the free water surface introduces a **virtual reduction in metacentric height (GM)**:

$$GM_{eff} = GM - \frac{I_{FS}}{\nabla}$$

Where $I_{FS}$ is the **free-surface moment of inertia** of the water plane. Designers therefore avoid partially filled large tanks whenever possible.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet — Complete Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the *high-yield* formula set examiners will draw from for Module 2 of PENST634. Memorize the relationships, not just the symbols.**

| Formula Identifier | Equation | Engineering Use | Units |
|--------------------|----------|-----------------|-------|
| F-BUOY-01 | $F_B = \rho\,g\,\nabla$ | Hydrostatic support | N |
| F-BUOY-02 | $W_S = W_L + m_{ballast}\,g$ | Submerged weight | N |
| F-BUOY-03 | $RB = \nabla - W_L/(\rho g)$ | Safety margin | $m^3$ |
| F-BUOY-04 | $SF = F_B/W_L$ | Safety factor | dimensionless |
| F-BUOY-05 | $V_{MBT} = (W_S - F_B)/(\rho g)$ | Flooding volume | $m^3$ |
| F-BUOY-06 | $a = (W_S - F_B)/m$ | Dive/surface acceleration | $m/s^2$ |
| F-BUOY-07 | $GM_{eff} = GM - I_{FS}/\nabla$ | Free-surface correction | m |
| F-BUOY-08 | $\rho_{syn} = \rho_m (1-\phi) + \rho_{air}\,\phi$ | Syntactic foam density | $kg/m^3$ |
| F-BUOY-09 | $p_{ext}(z) = p_0 + \rho\,g\,z$ | Hydrostatic pressure | Pa |
| F-BUOY-10 | $F_{net} = F_B - W_S$ | Net submergence force | N |

> **Notation caution:** In the table above, $\rho$ refers to the density of the *displacing fluid* (seawater unless otherwise stated). The symbol $\phi$ in F-BUOY-08 denotes the **void fraction** (volume fraction of microballoons).

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Naval Architects** use these equations to size MBTs such that submergence occurs in 90–120 seconds.
- **Deep-Submergence Engineers** rely on syntactic-foam equations (F-BUOY-08) when designing *Alvin*, *Pisces*, or *Trieste*-class vehicles.
- **Submarine Operators** continuously compute $W_S$ in real time to maintain neutral buoyancy during missions.
- **Offshore Industry** (ROVs, AUVs) uses identical buoyancy-element logic for intervention tooling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Volume of Main Ballast Tank Required for Submergence

### Given Conditions

A surfaced submarine has the following parameters:

- Lightweight (surfaced) displacement mass: $m_L = 5500 \times 10^3\ kg$
- Hull internal volume (excluding tanks): $V_{hull} = 5400\ m^3$
- Seawater density: $\rho_{sw} = 1025\ kg/m^3$
- Gravity: $g = 9.81\ m/s^2$

### Step 1 — Compute the Buoyancy Force on the Surfaced Submarine

The total displaced volume on the surface equals hull volume plus the *air-filled* portion of the saddle tanks. We treat the submarine as a rigid body whose outer envelope displaces $V_{disp}$:

$$V_{disp} = V_{hull} + V_{saddle\,air}$$

For this derivation, assume the saddle-tank air volume equals the MBT capacity (this is the design philosophy):

$$\nabla = V_{hull} + V_{MBT}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Static Equilibrium Condition

On the surface, the submarine is in static equilibrium:

$$F_B = W_L$$

Substituting Archimedes:

$$\rho_{sw}\,g\,(V_{hull} + V_{MBT}) = m_L\,g$$

### Step 3 — Cancel Gravity and Solve for $V_{MBT}$

$$\rho_{sw}\,(V_{hull} + V_{MBT}) = m_L$$

$$V_{hull} + V_{MBT} = \frac{m_L}{\rho_{sw}}$$

$$V_{MBT} = \frac{m_L}{\rho_{sw}} - V_{hull}$$

### Step 4 — Insert Numerical Values

$$V_{MBT} = \frac{5500 \times 10^3}{1025} - 5400$$

$$V_{MBT} = 5365.85 - 5400 = -34.15\ m^3$$

> [!WARNING]
> **A negative value indicates a design problem.** The hull alone (without flooding) already provides MORE buoyancy than the surfaced weight. This is the **reserve buoyancy** itself — the engineered safety margin. To submerge, we must deliberately *flood* an additional amount that overcomes the reserve.

### Step 5 — Recompute for the Submerged Condition

In submerged state, the MBTs are full of seawater. The buoyancy force is now generated only by the hull displacement (since the MBT volume is no longer displacing "extra" water — it is now filled with water inside the outer hull, contributing only its own weight to the system).

The new equilibrium demands:

$$\rho_{sw}\,g\,V_{hull} = W_S$$

Where $W_S = W_L + \rho_{sw}\,g\,V_{flooded}$. Substituting:

$$\rho_{sw}\,V_{hull} = m_L + \rho_{sw}\,V_{flooded}$$

### Step 6 — Solve for the Required Flooded Volume

$$\rho_{sw}\,V_{flooded} = \rho_{sw}\,V_{hull} - m_L$$

$$V_{flooded} = V_{hull} - \frac{m_L}{\rho_{sw}}$$

### Step 7 — Numerical Evaluation

$$V_{flooded} = 5400 - \frac{5500 \times 10^3}{1025}$$

$$V_{flooded} = 5400 - 5365.85 = 34.15\ m^3$$

This is the exact volume of seawater that must be admitted into the MBTs to bring the vessel from surfaced equilibrium to **neutral submerged buoyancy**.

## 3.2 Derivation — Reserve Buoyancy as a Function of Safety Factor

### Step 1 — Define the Safety Factor

$$SF = \frac{F_{B,\,surfaced}}{W_L}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Archimedes

$$SF = \frac{\rho_{sw}\,g\,\nabla}{m_L\,g} = \frac{\rho_{sw}\,\nabla}{m_L}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Total Displacement Volume

$$\nabla = \frac{SF \cdot m_L}{\rho_{sw}}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Reserve Buoyancy Volume

By definition $F_{B,\,surfaced} = W_L + RB$, therefore:

$$RB = F_{B,\,surfaced} - W_L = W_L\,(SF - 1)$$

$$RB = m_L\,g\,(SF - 1)$$

### Step 5 — Express RB in Equivalent Seawater Volume

$$\nabla_{RB} = \frac{RB}{\rho_{sw}\,g} = \frac{m_L\,(SF - 1)}{\rho_{sw}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical example:** If $SF = 1.20$ (a 20% reserve), and $m_L = 5500$ tonnes, then:
> $RB = 5500 \times 10^3 \times 9.81 \times 0.20 = 10.79\ MN$
> $\nabla_{RB} = 5500 \times 10^3 \times 0.20 / 1025 = 1073\ m^3$

## 3.3 Derivation — Syntactic Foam Density for Deep-Sea Buoyancy

### Step 1 — Define Void Fraction

Let $\phi$ = volume fraction of hollow glass microballoons (filled with air at near-atmospheric pressure inside the foam) within an epoxy matrix of density $\rho_m$.

### Step 2 — Apply the Rule of Mixtures

$$\rho_{syn} = \rho_m (1-\phi) + \rho_{air}\,\phi$$

### Step 3 — Linearized Form (since $\rho_{air} \ll \rho_m$)

$$\rho_{syn} \approx \rho_m (1-\phi)$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Required $\phi$ Given Target Density

$$\phi \approx 1 - \frac{\rho_{syn}}{\rho_m}$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Illustration

For $\rho_{syn} = 700\ kg/m^3$ and $\rho_m = 1150\ kg/m^3$ (typical cured epoxy):

$$\phi = 1 - \frac{700}{1150} = 0.391$$

This means the foam must contain approximately **39.1% hollow microballoons by volume**.

## 3.4 Derivation — Free-Surface Effect Correction

### Step 1 — Identify the Virtual Loss in GM

When a partially filled tank has a free water surface, the fluid can slosh and effectively lowers the metacenter.

### Step 2 — Apply the Standard Stability Formula

$$GM_{eff} = GM - \frac{I_{FS}}{\nabla}$$

Where $I_{FS}$ is the second moment of area of the free water plane about the longitudinal centerline axis. For a rectangular free surface of length $L$ and width $B$:

$$I_{FS} = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$$

### Step 3 — Combine

$$\boxed{GM_{eff} = GM - \frac{L B^3}{12 \nabla}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Pitfall:** Students frequently forget the **cube** of the tank width $B$ in the denominator numerator. This single error causes a 3 to 4-mark deduction in 14-mark problems.

## 3.5 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix — Buoyancy Element Decision Logic

| Operational Goal | Primary Element Activated | Secondary Element | Time Constant |
|------------------|---------------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| Initial submergence from surface | Main Ballast Tank (flood) | Forward/Aft Trim | 90–180 s |
| Depth holding at hover | Variable Ballast Tank (fine flood/blow) | Trim Compensation | 10–60 s |
| Emergency surface | EMBT Blow (high-pressure air to all MBTs) | Droppable Weights | < 30 s |
| Long-cruise weight loss compensation | VBT pump-out | Fuel Transfer | Hours |
| Deep-submergence stability | Syntactic foam modules (passive) | Hard ballast (droppable) | Static |
| Compensation for crew/payload changes | Variable Ballast Tank | Trim Tank | Minutes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.2 State-Transition Flowchart — Surfacing vs Submerging

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2

    SURFACED_POSITIVE --> TRANSIT_TO_PERISCOPE: Flood MBT partial
    TRANSIT_TO_PERISCOPE --> SUBMERGED_NEUTRAL: Complete MBT flood, trim VBT
    SUBMERGED_NEUTRAL --> SUBMERGED_NEGATIVE: Add Variable Ballast water
    SUBMERGED_NEGATIVE --> DEEP_DESCENT: Full dive plane angle
    DEEP_DESCENT --> SUBMERGED_NEUTRAL: VBT blow
    SUBMERGED_NEUTRAL --> TRANSIT_TO_PERISCOPE: Emergency Main Ballast Blow
    TRANSIT_TO_PERISCOPE --> SURFACED_POSITIVE: Continue EMBT blow, drain MBT
    SURFACED_POSITIVE --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Functional Roles — Tabular Map

| Diagram Node | Real Subsystem | Active / Passive |
|--------------|----------------|------------------|
| nodeA | Command Centre / Control Room | Active (human-machine) |

| nodeJ | High-Pressure Air Banks | Active (energy store) |
| nodeK | Sea-Flood Valves (lower hull) | Active |
| nodeL | Low-Pressure Blower / Discharge Pump | Active |
| nodeM | Syntactic Foam Buoyancy | Passive |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**Q:** *Define reserve buoyancy of a submarine. Why is a safety factor greater than unity essential in submarine design?* **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Reserve buoyancy is defined as the difference between the total displacement volume (when surfaced) and the volume of water displaced by the lightweight condition:

$$RB = \nabla - \frac{W_L}{\rho_{sw}\,g}$$

It represents the **additional buoyancy available** to support the vessel in case of partial flooding casualties, loss of compressed air, or adverse sea states. A safety factor greater than unity (typically $1.10 \le SF \le 1.30$) ensures that even if one compartment or tank is breached, the submarine can still remain positively buoyant at the surface and survive long enough for rescue operations.

---

### Question A2
**Q:** *Distinguish between hard ballast tanks and soft ballast tanks. State one application of each.* **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Hard Ballast Tank | Soft Ballast Tank |
|---------|-------------------|--------------------|
| Structure | Rigid, pressure-resistant welded compartment | Non-pressure-resistant bag within free-flooding space |
| External pressure | Withstands full hydrostatic pressure | Floods freely with ambient seawater |
| Use | Conventional submarines (MBTs) | Modern teardrop-hull submarines |
| Application | Surface-ship-launched small subs | Deep-diving research submersibles |

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulation:** Module 2 of PENST634 carries a *single full-question internal choice* in the End-Semester Examination (ESE). Candidates answer **either** Question A **or** Question B, each for 14 marks distributed across two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 Choice 1]`**

**(a)** A submarine has a surfaced displacement mass of $6.0 \times 10^6\ kg$ and an internal hull volume of $5800\ m^3$. The submarine is to operate in seawater of density $1025\ kg/m^3$. Compute **(i)** the total reserve buoyancy in cubic meters, and **(ii)** the volume of seawater that must be admitted into the main ballast tank to bring the vessel to neutral submerged buoyancy. Take $g = 9.81\ m/s^2$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain with neat sketches the construction and working of **main ballast tanks (MBTs)** and **trim tanks** in a conventional submarine. Clearly state their roles in the submergence and surfacing cycles. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given:**
- $m_L = 6.0 \times 10^6\ kg$
- $V_{hull} = 5800\ m^3$
- $\rho_{sw} = 1025\ kg/m^3$
- $g = 9.81\ m/s^2$

**Step 1 — Compute the required displacement volume to support surfaced weight:**

$$\nabla_{required} = \frac{m_L}{\rho_{sw}} = \frac{6.0 \times 10^6}{1025} = 5853.66\ m^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute the reserve buoyancy volume:**

$$\nabla_{RB} = \nabla_{required} - V_{hull} = 5853.66 - 5800 = 53.66\ m^3$$

**Step 3 — Convert reserve buoyancy to force:**

$$F_{RB} = \rho_{sw}\,g\,\nabla_{RB} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 53.66 = 539{,}550\ N \approx 539.55\ kN$$

**Step 4 — To submerge to neutral buoyancy, the MBT must be flooded by an amount that exactly cancels $F_{RB}$:**

The flooding water's weight must equal the reserve buoyancy force:

$$m_{flooded}\,g = F_{RB}$$

$$m_{flooded} = \frac{F_{RB}}{g} = \frac{539{,}550}{9.81} = 55{,}000\ kg = 55\ tonnes$$

**Step 5 — Convert to flooded volume:**

$$V_{flooded} = \frac{m_{flooded}}{\rho_{sw}} = \frac{55{,}000}{1025} = 53.66\ m^3$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) Reserve buoyancy volume $= \mathbf{53.66\ m^3}$ (force equivalent $539.55\ kN$)
- (ii) Required MBT flooding volume $= \mathbf{53.66\ m^3}$ (55 tonnes of seawater)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Construction of Main Ballast Tanks (MBTs):**

MBTs are large, **cylindrical or saddle-shaped compartments** located in the outer (light) hull, external to the pressure hull. They are constructed from mild steel or HY-80 high-yield steel plates with internal stiffeners. Each MBT has three critical penetrations:

1. **Flooding valve** at the bottom (sea-connected, normally open when submerging).
2. **Vent valve** at the top (atmospheric when blowing, closed when submerged).
3. **High-pressure air (HPA) inlet** for emergency blow.

**Working:**
- **To submerge:** The vent closes, the flooding valve opens. Gravity admits seawater; the submarine loses positive buoyancy and dives.
- **To surface:** HPA at 250 bar is admitted; it forces seawater out through the bottom flooding valve, restoring positive buoyancy.

**Construction and Working of Trim Tanks:**

Trim tanks are **smaller variable-volume compartments** located at the forward and aft ends of the pressure hull, inside the pressure envelope. They are pumped bilges with motor-driven pumps.

**Working:**
- If the submarine goes "bow-heavy," water is pumped from the forward trim tank to the aft trim tank.
- They compensate for **fuel transfer**, **torpedo expenditure**, and **crew movement**.
- They NEVER cause submergence/surfacing — they only maintain **longitudinal trim**.

**Role in Cycles:**

| Cycle Phase | MBT Role | Trim Tank Role |
|-------------|----------|----------------|
| Surface → Submerge | Flood to provide $-RB$ | Adjust fore-aft trim |
| Submerged cruise | Maintain at full flood | Compensate weight shift |
| Submerge → Surface | Blow with HPA | Trim during blow |

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 Choice 2]`**

**(a)** A deep-submergence research vehicle (DSRV) is fitted with syntactic foam buoyancy modules of density $720\ kg/m^3$. The epoxy matrix density is $1180\ kg/m^3$ and the air microballoon density is $1.2\ kg/m^3$. Calculate **(i)** the void fraction required, and **(ii)** the mass of foam needed to provide a buoyancy force equivalent to $5000\ N$ at a depth where seawater density is $1030\ kg/m^3$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** With the help of a free-body diagram, derive the equation for the **submergence acceleration** of a submarine when the MBT flooding valve is suddenly opened. Discuss the role of the **free-surface effect** on transverse stability during this maneuver. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given:**
- $\rho_{syn} = 720\ kg/m^3$
- $\rho_m = 1180\ kg/m^3$
- $\rho_{air} = 1.2\ kg/m^3$
- $F_B^{req} = 5000\ N$
- $\rho_{sw} = 1030\ kg/m^3$
- $g = 9.81\ m/s^2$

**Step 1 — Apply the rule of mixtures:**

$$\rho_{syn} = \rho_m(1-\phi) + \rho_{air}\,\phi$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\phi$:**

$$\rho_{syn} = \rho_m - \rho_m\,\phi + \rho_{air}\,\phi$$

$$\rho_{syn} - \rho_m = \phi(\rho_{air} - \rho_m)$$

$$\phi = \frac{\rho_m - \rho_{syn}}{\rho_m - \rho_{air}}$$

**Step 3 — Insert numerical values:**

$$\phi = \frac{1180 - 720}{1180 - 1.2} = \frac{460}{1178.8} = 0.3903$$

$$\phi \approx \mathbf{39.03\%}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the displaced volume required:**

$$V_{disp} = \frac{F_B^{req}}{\rho_{sw}\,g} = \frac{5000}{1030 \times 9.81} = 0.4948\ m^3$$

**Step 5 — Compute the mass of foam:**

Since the foam itself occupies this volume:

$$m_{foam} = \rho_{syn} \cdot V_{disp} = 720 \times 0.4948 = 356.3\ kg$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) Void fraction $\phi \approx \mathbf{0.390}$ (39.0%)
- (ii) Mass of foam $\approx \mathbf{356.3\ kg}$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Free-Body Diagram (textual representation):**

```
                 F_B  (up)
                  ↑
                  │
       ┌──────────┴──────────┐
       │                     │
       │   SUBMARINE         │
       │   HULL              │
       │                     │
       └──────────┬──────────┘
                  │
                  ↓
              W (down) + W_water_in (down)
```

**Step 1 — Define forces during submergence:**
- Buoyant force $F_B$ (constant, assuming constant hull displacement)
- Gravitational weight $W$
- Flooding water weight $W_{fw}(t)$ (time-dependent as MBT fills)

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law:**

$$F_{net} = m_{total}(t)\cdot a(t)$$

$$F_B - W - W_{fw}(t) = m_{total}(t) \cdot a(t)$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the mass flow rate $\dot{m}$:**

Assuming flooding at constant rate $\dot{m}$:

$$W_{fw}(t) = \dot{m}\,g\,t$$

$$m_{total}(t) = \frac{W}{g} + \dot{m}\,t$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$a(t) = \frac{F_B - W - \dot{m}\,g\,t}{W/g + \dot{m}\,t}$$

**Step 5 — At the instant the valve opens ($t = 0$):**

$$a(0) = \frac{F_B - W}{W/g} = \frac{g\,(F_B - W)}{W}$$

If the submarine was in positive reserve buoyancy, $F_B > W$, then $a(0)$ is **negative** (upward), which is physically impossible — the vessel remains on the surface until $W_{fw}$ accumulates enough.

**Step 6 — Time to reach neutral buoyancy:**

Set $F_B = W + \dot{m}\,g\,t_{neutral}$:

$$t_{neutral} = \frac{F_B - W}{\dot{m}\,g}$$

**Step 7 — Time to develop negative buoyancy and begin descent:**

Once $W_{fw} > F_B - W$, the net force becomes downward and acceleration turns negative.

**Discussion of Free-Surface Effect:**

During flooding, the MBT is **partially filled** with water for a finite duration. The free water surface inside the tank creates a **virtual reduction in metacentric height (GM)**:

$$GM_{eff} = GM - \frac{I_{FS}}{\nabla} = GM - \frac{L B^3}{12 \nabla}$$

**Consequences for transverse stability:**
- The vessel's resistance to rolling is **temporarily reduced**.
- Designers mitigate this by **topping off** or **fully flooding** MBTs as quickly as possible.
- Submerging with partially filled MBTs is therefore a **stability-critical maneuver**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Module 2 — Buoyancy Elements):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Students frequently mix $kg$ and $N$, or use $tonnes$ without converting to $kg$. **Always write the unit conversion step explicitly.**
> 2. **Sign error in acceleration derivation:** During submergence, students often write $W - F_B$ instead of $F_B - W$ in Newton's Second Law. The result is an upward (positive) acceleration when submerging — physically absurd.
> 3. **Misidentifying which tanks do what:** MBTs cause submergence/surfacing; trim tanks adjust fore-aft balance; VBTs fine-tune. **Mixing these costs 2–3 marks.**
> 4. **Free-surface cube-of-B error:** $I_{FS} \propto B^3$ — students forget the cube. (See Section 3.4 derivation.)
> 5. **No diagram in 7-mark descriptive questions:** KTU examiners *expect* a labeled free-body diagram or tank cross-section. **Always include one**, even if crude.
> 6. **Confusing displacement with volume:** $\nabla$ is displaced volume ($m^3$); $\Delta$ is displacement mass ($tonnes$). One notation slip can cost a full mark.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: Buoyancy Elements**

- **Archimedes:** $F_B = \rho_{sw}\,g\,\nabla$ — the foundational equation; commit to memory.
- **Three states:** Positive ($F_B > W$), Neutral ($F_B = W$), Negative ($F_B < W$).
- **Reserve buoyancy $RB$** is the safety margin *above* the lightweight displacement.
- **Safety factor $SF$** for military submarines: typically **1.10–1.30**.
- **Main Ballast Tank (MBT):** Causes submergence (flood) and surfacing (HPA blow).
- **Trim Tanks:** Maintain longitudinal balance; never control depth.
- **Variable Ballast Tank (VBT):** Fine-tunes neutral buoyancy at depth.
- **Emergency Main Ballast Tank (EMBT) Blow:** High-pressure air emergency surfacing; **critical for crew survival.**
- **Syntactic foam:** Used in deep submersibles to replace air voids that would otherwise collapse under hydrostatic pressure.
- **Free-surface effect:** Partially filled tanks reduce $GM$ by $I_{FS}/\nabla$.
- **Submergence acceleration:** $a(t) = [F_B - W - \dot{m}g\,t] / [W/g + \dot{m}\,t]$.
- **Volume flooding needed** to overcome reserve buoyancy equals exactly $RB/(\rho_{sw}\,g)$.
- **Syntactic foam void fraction:** $\phi = (\rho_m - \rho_{syn})/(\rho_m - \rho_{air})$.
- **Hard tank vs soft tank:** Hard tanks resist external pressure; soft tanks flood freely inside an outer envelope.
- **Standard numerical values to memorize:** $\rho_{sw} = 1025\ kg/m^3$, $g = 9.81\ m/s^2$, $p_{air} = 250\ bar$ (HPA storage).
- **Draw free-body diagrams** for every numerical problem involving Newton's Second Law on a submarine.
- **Always state assumptions** (constant $\rho_{sw}$, incompressible foam, rigid hull) explicitly in derivations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weight Elements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST634 - SUBMARINES AND SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Weight Elements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Weight Elements

In the naval architecture of submarines and submersibles, **Weight Elements** refer to the systematic categorization and quantification of every mass component that contributes to the **Total Displacement Weight** of the underwater vessel. These elements are meticulously catalogued during the design phase, tracked throughout construction, and continuously monitored during the vessel's operational life because even a kilogram-level deviation can critically alter buoyancy, trim, and depth-keeping characteristics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination Standard):**
> *Weight Elements constitute the complete inventory of structural, mechanical, electrical, outfit, fixed ballast, and variable load components whose summation yields the Light Ship Weight (LSW) and Deadweight of a submarine, ultimately determining its surface and submerged displacement.*

The total weight budget of a submarine is fundamentally expressed as:

$$W_{displacement} = W_{LSW} + W_{Deadweight}$$

where **W_LSW** is the **Light Ship Weight** (permanent, fixed mass) and **W_Deadweight** is the sum of all **variable loads** (consumables, crew, fuel, water, ammunition).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body Analogy

Imagine a submarine as a human body standing on a weighing scale:

| Submarine Component | Human Body Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Pressure Hull Skeleton | Bones (rigid framework) |
| Ballast Tanks | Lungs (fill/empty to control submersion) |
| Diesel Engines + Batteries | Heart + Muscles (energy & propulsion) |
| Piping & Ventilation | Blood Vessels (fluid distribution) |
| Crew + Provisions | Stomach Contents (consumable load) |
| Weapons & Magazines | Tools Carried (mission-specific load) |
| Trim/Compensation Water | Posture Adjusters (fine balance) |

Just as a person must precisely control their **center of gravity** to balance on a single foot, a submarine must maintain the **Center of Gravity (G)** directly beneath its **Center of Buoyancy (B)** for stable depth-keeping. A 1% weight miscalculation on a 3,000-tonne submarine equals **30 tonnes** — enough to send the vessel plunging uncontrollably to crush depth.

### 1.3 Governing Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values for Submarine Weight Calculations:**
> - **Density of Sea Water:** $\rho_{sw} = \mathbf{1.025 \ t/m^3}$ (standard ocean, 35 ppt salinity, 4°C)
> - **Density of Fresh Water:** $\rho_{fw} = \mathbf{1.000 \ t/m^3}$
> - **Acceleration Due to Gravity:** $g = \mathbf{9.81 \ m/s^2}$
> - **Typical Submarine Operating Depth:** $\mathbf{200 \ to \ 600 \ m}$
> - **Standard Reserve Buoyancy Fraction:** $\mathbf{15\% \ to \ 25\%}$ of surface displacement

### 1.4 Why Weight Elements Matter Critically for Submarines

Unlike surface ships where a small weight error merely changes the draft by millimetres, in submarines a small weight error can mean the difference between **hovering at depth** and **catastrophic implosion or uncontrolled surfacing**. This is because submarines operate in a regime of near-neutral buoyancy, where every kilogram matters.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Weight Elements

Weight elements in submarines are conventionally organized into **six primary groups**, each further subdivided:

#### **Group A — Structural Weight**
- Pressure hull plating (HY-80, HY-100, HY-130 steel)
- Outer fairwater / outer hull (non-pressure-bearing)
- Internal bulkheads (collision, watertight, sacrificial)
- Stiffener rings and frames
- Conning tower / sail structure
- Decks, platforms, ladders

#### **Group B — Propulsion Machinery Weight**
- Diesel engines (for surface running and battery charging)
- Main electric motors (propulsion)
- Generators and auxiliary motors
- Reduction gears and shaft lines
- Propellers and shaft seals
- Pumps, compressors, heat exchangers

#### **Group C — Electrical Weight**
- Storage batteries (lead-acid on conventional, Li-ion on newer boats)
- Switchboards and control consoles
- Power cables and lighting
- Sonar and combat system electronics

#### **Group D — Outfit Weight**
- Piping (sea water, fresh water, fuel, lube oil, compressed air)
- Ventilation ducting
- Thermal and acoustic insulation
- Paint and anti-corrosion coatings
- Doors, hatches, escape trunks

#### **Group E — Fixed Ballast Weight**
- Lead ballast blocks (in the double bottom or external pods)
- Cast iron kentledge
- **Permanent ballast ensures negative buoyancy reserve when flooded**

#### **Group F — Variable Load (Deadweight)**
- Crew and personal effects
- Provisions and fresh water
- Fuel oil, lubricating oil, distilled water (for batteries)
- Torpedoes, missiles, mines
- Spare parts and portable equipment
- Variable trim and compensation water

### 2.2 Surface vs Submerged Displacement — The Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A submarine has TWO displacement values:**
> 1. **Surface Displacement** (when floating, MBTs empty and dry)
> 2. **Submerged Displacement** (when submerged, MBTs flooded with sea water)

The mathematical relationship is:

$$W_{submerged} = W_{surface} + W_{seawater \ in \ MBT}$$

Since the MBTs are flooded with sea water when submerged, the submarine must displace a larger volume to support this additional weight — but it does so because the outer hull expands the displaced volume.

### 2.3 Reserve Buoyancy (Surface Condition Only)

$$B_{reserve} = (V_{total \ envelope} - V_{submerged}) \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot g$$

For a typical conventional submarine, reserve buoyancy is approximately **15–25%** of the surface displacement. This is dramatically lower than surface ships (typically 30–60%) because submarines are designed to submerge easily.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| **Formula** | **Symbol Description** | **Application Context** |
|---|---|---|
| $W_{disp} = W_{LSW} + W_{DW}$ | Displacement = LSW + Deadweight | Baseline weight equation |
| $W_{sub} = W_{surf} + \rho_{sw} \cdot V_{MBT}$ | Submerged weight (MBT flooded) | Submergence condition |
| $B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$ | Archimedes buoyancy force | Equilibrium condition |
| $\Delta W_{trim} = W \cdot (x_G - x_B)$ | Trim moment | Longitudinal balance |
| $GM_T = KB + BM_T - KG$ | Transverse metacentric height | Stability check |
| $BM_T = I_T \diagup V_{disp}$ | Metacentric radius | Waterplane stiffness |
| $W_{seawater} = \rho_{sw} \cdot V_{tank}$ | Weight of seawater in tank | Ballast calculations |
| $B_{reserve} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{reserve}$ | Reserve buoyancy | Surface safety margin |

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` (NOT the pipe symbol) inside any markdown table cell containing absolute-value expressions.** This avoids breaking markdown parsing. The table above intentionally uses the division operator `\/` and symbol-only notation to remain table-safe.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Accurate weight element tracking governs:

1. **Submarine Procurement Contracts** — Weight growth during construction routinely exceeds the original budget by 5–15%, often forcing costly redesign of the pressure hull.
2. **Submarine Depth-Keeping** — A 0.5% error in weight at 300 m depth can produce a 1.5 m depth excursion.
3. **Operational Endurance** — Every tonne of "growth" reduces payload, range, or armament capacity.
4. **Salvage Engineering** — When a submarine is raised, accurate weight documentation determines crane capacity and lift planning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Submerged Weight Equilibrium Equation

**Starting Point:** Archimedes' Principle states that a floating or submerged body is buoyed up by a force equal to the weight of fluid displaced.

**Step 1 — Surface Condition:**

When the submarine floats on the surface with MBTs empty, the buoyant force equals the surface displacement weight:

$$B_{surf} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{surf \ displaced}$$

For equilibrium:

$$\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{surf \ displaced} = W_{LSW} + W_{Deadweight}$$

**Step 2 — Initiating Submergence:**

To dive, the MBTs are flooded through Kingston valves (sea water inlets at the bottom of the tanks). The mass of seawater entering adds directly to the submarine's total weight:

$$W_{seawater \ added} = \rho_{sw} \cdot V_{MBT}$$

**Step 3 — Submerged Condition:**

For full submergence, the volume of the entire pressure hull and outer envelope (V_total) becomes the displaced volume:

$$V_{submerged} = V_{total \ envelope}$$

The new equilibrium requires:

$$\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{total} = W_{LSW} + W_{Deadweight} + W_{seawater \ in \ MBT}$$

**Step 4 — Net Buoyancy Analysis:**

The net buoyant force at any instant is:

$$F_{net} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{disp} - W_{total \ vessel}$$

- If $F_{net} > 0$: submarine has positive buoyancy → rises
- If $F_{net} = 0$: neutral buoyancy → hovers
- If $F_{net} < 0$: negative buoyancy → sinks

**Step 5 — Critical Submergence Threshold:**

For a submarine to fully submerge, the volume of its outer envelope (including MBTs now flooded) must satisfy:

$$V_{total} \geq \frac{W_{LSW} + W_{DW} + \rho_{sw} V_{MBT}}{\rho_{sw}}$$

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Standard Problem)

**Problem Statement:**
A conventional submarine has the following weight breakdown:
- Light Ship Weight = **2,400 tonnes**
- Variable Load (crew + stores + fuel) = **200 tonnes**
- Main Ballast Tank Volume = **350 m³**

Given $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \ t/m^3$, calculate:
1. Surface displacement
2. Weight of seawater when MBT is fully flooded
3. Submerged displacement weight
4. Required outer envelope volume for neutral submerged buoyancy
5. Submerged displacement in cubic metres

**Complete Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Part (1) — Surface Displacement:**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{surf} &= W_{LSW} + W_{DW} \\
W_{surf} &= 2{,}400 + 200 \\
W_{surf} &= 2{,}600 \ tonnes
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (2) — Weight of Sea Water in MBT:**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{sw \ in \ MBT} &= \rho_{sw} \cdot V_{MBT} \\
W_{sw \ in \ MBT} &= 1.025 \times 350 \\
W_{sw \ in \ MBT} &= 358.75 \ tonnes
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (3) — Submerged Displacement Weight:**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{sub} &= W_{surf} + W_{sw \ in \ MBT} \\
W_{sub} &= 2{,}600 + 358.75 \\
W_{sub} &= 2{,}958.75 \ tonnes
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (4) — Required Outer Envelope Volume:**

For neutral buoyancy submerged, $V_{envelope} \times \rho_{sw} = W_{sub}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{envelope} &= \frac{W_{sub}}{\rho_{sw}} \\
V_{envelope} &= \frac{2{,}958.75}{1.025} \\
V_{envelope} &= 2{,}886.59 \ m^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (5) — Submerged Displacement in Cubic Metres:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{sub} &= \frac{W_{sub}}{\rho_{sw}} \\
\nabla_{sub} &= 2{,}886.59 \ m^3
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation Check:** The outer envelope (≈2,886 m³) must be larger than the pressure hull interior (≈2,400–2,500 m³) for submergence to be feasible. Typical submarines achieve this with outer fairwater streamlining that increases overall volume by ~10–15%.

### 3.3 Trim Moment Derivation

The longitudinal trim of a submarine is governed by the offset between the longitudinal center of gravity ($L_CG$) and longitudinal center of buoyancy ($L_CB$):

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{trim} &= W_{total} \cdot (x_{CG} - x_{CB}) \\
\tan \theta &= \frac{M_{trim}}{GM_L \cdot W_{total}} \\
\theta &\approx \frac{x_{CG} - x_{CB}}{GM_L} \ \text{(for small angles in radians)}
\end{aligned}$$

where:
- $M_{trim}$ = trimming moment in tonne-metres
- $x_{CG}, x_{CB}$ = longitudinal positions from midships (positive forward)
- $GM_L$ = longitudinal metacentric height
- $\theta$ = trim angle

**Trim Compensation by Water Transfer:**

To correct an unwanted trim, water is shifted between forward and aft trim tanks:

$$\Delta W_{trim \ water} \cdot \frac{L_{tank \ separation}}{2} = M_{trim \ to \ correct}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Submarine Weight Elements

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the hierarchical decomposition of submarine weight elements, illustrating how each parent category subdivides into specific engineering components.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["TOTAL SUBMARINE DISPLACEMENT WEIGHT"]

    ROOT --> GA["GROUP A STRUCTURAL WEIGHT"]
    ROOT --> GB["GROUP B PROPULSION MACHINERY"]
    ROOT --> GC["GROUP C ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS"]
    ROOT --> GD["GROUP D OUTFIT WEIGHT"]
    ROOT --> GE["GROUP E FIXED BALLAST"]
    ROOT --> GF["GROUP F VARIABLE LOAD DEADWEIGHT"]

    GA --> SA1["Pressure Hull Plating"]
    GA --> SA2["Outer Hull Fairwater"]
    GA --> SA3["Internal Bulkheads"]
    GA --> SA4["Stiffener Rings and Frames"]
    GA --> SA5["Sail Conning Tower"]

    GB --> SB1["Diesel Engines"]
    GB --> SB2["Main Electric Motors"]
    GB --> SB3["Generators"]
    GB --> SB4["Pumps and Compressors"]
    GB --> SB5["Reduction Gear and Shaft"]

    GC --> SC1["Storage Batteries"]
    GC --> SC2["Switchboards and Controls"]
    GC --> SC3["Power Cables"]
    GC --> SC4["Combat Sonar Electronics"]

    GD --> SD1["Piping Systems"]
    GD --> SD2["Ventilation Ducts"]
    GD --> SD3["Thermal Insulation"]
    GD --> SD4["Paint and Coatings"]
    GD --> SD5["Hatches and Doors"]

    GE --> SE1["External Lead Ballast Pods"]
    GE --> SE2["Internal Cast Iron Kentledge"]
    GE --> SE3["Solid Block Ballast"]

    GF --> SF1["Crew and Personal Effects"]
    GF --> SF2["Provisions and Fresh Water"]
    GF --> SF3["Fuel Oil and Lube Oil"]
    GF --> SF4["Torpedoes and Weapons"]
    GF --> SF5["Variable Trim Water"]
    GF --> SF6["Compensation Tank Water"]

    SE1 --> NOTE["PERMANENT FIXED BALLAST ENSURES NEGATIVE BUOYANCY RESERVE WHEN SUBMERGED"]
    SF5 --> NOTE2["VARIABLE TRIM WATER IS PUMPED BETWEEN TANKS FOR FINE BALANCE CONTROL"]
    SF6 --> NOTE3["COMPENSATION TANKS OFFSET WEIGHT CHANGES FROM CONSUMABLES USED DURING DIVE"]
```

### 4.2 Functional State-Transition Diagram — Ballast Tank Operations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    STATE_A["STATE 1 SUBMERGED NEGATIVE BUOYANCY"]
    STATE_B["STATE 2 HOVERING NEUTRAL BUOYANCY"]
    STATE_C["STATE 3 SURFACE POSITIVE BUOYANCY"]

    STATE_A -->|"Pump water OUT of MBT using low pressure blow"| STATE_B
    STATE_B -->|"Vent MBT with high pressure air"| STATE_C
    STATE_C -->|"Flood MBT via Kingston valves"| STATE_A

    STATE_A -.->|"Adjust variable trim tanks"| STATE_A
    STATE_B -.->|"Trim water shift"| STATE_B
    STATE_C -.->|"Take on stores fuel crew"| STATE_C
```

### 4.3 Schematic Interpretation Guide

| Diagram Region | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Group A** | Forms the pressure vessel that resists ocean crushing pressure |
| **Group B** | Provides energy conversion from fuel → electricity → propulsion |
| **Group C** | Stores energy (batteries) and processes combat information |
| **Group D** | Distributes fluids, gases, and thermal control throughout the hull |
| **Group E** | Permanent mass that cannot be jettisoned — provides stable negative buoyancy when submerged |
| **Group F** | All mission-variable items that change mass during a deployment |

> [!NOTE]
> **Compensation Tank Principle:** As fuel, food, and water are *consumed* during a patrol, the submarine becomes lighter. Without compensation tanks, the vessel would gradually become more buoyant and rise toward the surface. Compensation tanks are pre-flooded with sea water at the start of the patrol; as consumables are used, this compensation water is pumped overboard — keeping total weight constant.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> *List the six primary groups into which submarine weight elements are classified, and state one representative item from each group.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The six primary weight element groups in submarine design are:

1. **Structural Weight** — Example: pressure hull plating (HY-80 steel) *(0.5 marks)*
2. **Propulsion Machinery Weight** — Example: main electric propulsion motor *(0.5 marks)*
3. **Electrical Weight** — Example: lead-acid storage battery bank *(0.5 marks)*
4. **Outfit Weight** — Example: piping and ventilation ducting *(0.5 marks)*
5. **Fixed Ballast Weight** — Example: external lead ballast pods *(0.5 marks)*
6. **Variable Load (Deadweight)** — Example: torpedoes and fuel oil *(0.5 marks)*

---

**Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> *Define "Light Ship Weight" and "Deadweight" as applied to a submarine. Why is the submerged displacement different from the surface displacement?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Light Ship Weight (LSW):** The complete weight of the submarine including hull, machinery, electrical systems, outfit, and permanent fixed ballast, but **excluding** all consumables, liquids, crew, and stores. *(1 mark)*

**Deadweight:** The total weight of all variable loads including crew, provisions, fresh water, fuel oil, lubricating oil, distilled water, ammunition, torpedoes, and spare parts. *(1 mark)*

**Why Submerged Displacement Differs:** When the submarine submerges, its **Main Ballast Tanks (MBTs)** are flooded with sea water through Kingston valves. The mass of this sea water (≈ ρ_sw × V_MBT) is added to the vessel's weight, so the total submerged displacement exceeds the surface displacement by exactly this additional sea water mass. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the equation relating surface displacement, deadweight, light ship weight, and the weight of sea water in the Main Ballast Tanks for a submerged submarine. Explain the physical significance of each term. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A submarine has a Light Ship Weight of 3,200 tonnes, a Deadweight of 280 tonnes, and a Main Ballast Tank volume of 420 m³. If the density of sea water is 1.025 t/m³, calculate:
- (i) Surface displacement
- (ii) Submerged displacement
- (iii) Required outer envelope volume for neutral submerged buoyancy *(7 marks)*

---

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

Starting from Archimedes' Principle, the buoyant force equals the weight of displaced fluid.

**Step 1 — Surface Equilibrium (1.5 marks):**

When floating on the surface with MBTs empty:
$$B_{surf} = W_{LSW} + W_{DW}$$
$$\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{surf} = W_{LSW} + W_{DW}$$

**Step 2 — Submergence by Flooding MBT (1.5 marks):**

To submerge, MBTs are flooded. The added sea water mass is:
$$W_{sw} = \rho_{sw} \cdot V_{MBT}$$

**Step 3 — Submerged Equilibrium (2 marks):**

When fully submerged, the displaced volume is the entire outer envelope volume (V_env). Equilibrium gives:
$$\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{env} = W_{LSW} + W_{DW} + \rho_{sw} \cdot V_{MBT}$$

**Step 4 — Rearranging (1 mark):**

$$V_{env} = \frac{W_{LSW} + W_{DW}}{\rho_{sw}} + V_{MBT}$$

**Step 5 — Physical Significance (1 mark):**

Each term represents: LSW = permanent structural mass; DW = consumables and crew; $\rho_{sw} \cdot V_{MBT}$ = buoyancy penalty paid for submergence. The envelope volume must be larger than the pressure hull interior to accommodate this additional buoyancy requirement.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $W_{LSW} = 3{,}200 \ t$
- $W_{DW} = 280 \ t$
- $V_{MBT} = 420 \ m^3$
- $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \ t/m^3$

**(i) Surface Displacement (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{surf} &= W_{LSW} + W_{DW} \\
&= 3{,}200 + 280 \\
&= 3{,}480 \ t \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Submerged Displacement (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{sw \ in \ MBT} &= \rho_{sw} \cdot V_{MBT} \\
&= 1.025 \times 420 \\
&= 430.5 \ t \\[6pt]
W_{sub} &= W_{surf} + W_{sw} \\
&= 3{,}480 + 430.5 \\
&= 3{,}910.5 \ t \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Required Envelope Volume (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{env} &= \frac{W_{sub}}{\rho_{sw}} \\
&= \frac{3{,}910.5}{1.025} \\
&= 3{,}815.12 \ m^3 \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the difference between fixed ballast and variable ballast in a submarine. With the help of neat sketches, describe the function of Main Ballast Tanks (MBT), Trim Tanks, and Compensation Tanks. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the significance of weight element control during the operational life of a submarine. Why is weight monitoring critical between refit periods? *(7 marks)*

---

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Fixed vs Variable Ballast (7 Marks):**

**Fixed Ballast (2 marks):**
Fixed ballast consists of **solid materials** (typically lead blocks or cast iron) permanently installed inside or outside the pressure hull. It cannot be moved, removed, or adjusted during the submarine's service life. Its purpose is to:
- Provide a stable, permanent negative buoyancy margin
- Ensure the submarine does not become positively buoyant even with all variable loads consumed
- Lower the center of gravity, improving transverse stability

**Variable Ballast (2 marks):**
Variable ballast uses **sea water** that can be pumped into or out of dedicated tanks using pumps and high-pressure air systems. It is dynamic and adjustable. Categories include:
- **Main Ballast Tanks (MBT):** Large external tanks; flooded to dive, blown empty to surface.
- **Trim Tanks (Forward/Aft):** Small tanks at the extremities; water shifted between them to adjust longitudinal trim.
- **Compensation Tanks:** Pre-flooded with sea water at dive start; water pumped overboard as consumables (fuel, food) are used to maintain constant mass.

**Sketch Description (3 marks):**

```
+--------------------------------------------------+
|   OUTER HULL (envelope)                          |
|   +------------------------------------------+   |
|   |                                          |   |
|   |   +--PRESSURE HULL---------------+      |   |
|   |   |  Trim Tank (Forward)  |      |      |   |
|   |   |------------------------+      |      |   |
|   |   |   Crew Spaces / Machinery      |      |   |
|   |   |                                |      |   |
|   |   |  Compensation Tanks (Mid)      |      |   |
|   |   |                                |      |   |
|   |   |  Trim Tank (Aft)        |      |      |   |
|   |   |-------------------------+      |      |   |
|   |   +--------------------------------+      |   |
|   |                                          |   |
|   |   ## ## ## FIXED LEAD BALLAST ## ## ##   |   |
|   |                                          |   |
|   |   ++   ++   MAIN BALLAST TANKS  ++   ++  |   |
|   +------------------------------------------+   |
+--------------------------------------------------+
```

*(Diagram: MBTs occupy the free-flooding space between pressure hull and outer envelope; fixed ballast sits at the bottom of the keel; trim tanks at forward/aft ends; compensation tanks amidships.)*

---

**Part (b) — Operational Weight Control (7 Marks):**

**Significance of Weight Monitoring (4 marks):**

1. **Depth-Keeping Accuracy:** A submarine must hover at a precise depth. A 1% weight change at operating depth causes ≈10 m of depth excursion. Continuous monitoring via **trim blowers and depth gauges** is mandatory.

2. **Compensation for Consumables:** During a 60-day patrol, the submarine consumes ~150 tonnes of food, fuel, and water. Compensation tanks offset this loss by discharging pre-loaded sea water.

3. **Salinity Variation:** Operating in different oceans (Atlantic ρ=1.027 vs Baltic ρ=1.005) changes buoyancy by ~2%. Ballast water adjustments are required when transiting between water bodies.

4. **Crew Variation:** Resupply of personnel (e.g., special forces transfer) changes weight and shifts LCG. Recalculation of trim is mandatory.

**Why Monitoring Between Refits Matters (3 marks):**
- Submarines undergo 5–7 year refit cycles.
- During deployment, **corrosion, marine growth, and equipment replacement** slowly alter weight.
- Periodic **inclining experiments** and **weight audits** at 18-month intervals ensure the displacement database is current.
- Without this, the submarine may exceed its safe operating envelope, risking implosion at depth.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
