> 1. **Confusing "Reserve Buoyancy" with "Displacement":** Reserve buoyancy is the *excess* buoyancy above displacement — never equals the total buoyancy. Marks lost: **1–2 per occurrence**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units:** Density given in t/m³ and weight in tonnes — students often mistakenly multiply by g = 9.81 unnecessarily, producing wrong units. **Tonne units are mass units (1 tonne = 1,000 kg) and do not require × g unless converting to force (Newtons).**
>
> 3. **Mixing Light Ship Weight with Surface Displacement:** LSW excludes deadweight; surface displacement includes it. Examiners deduct full marks if these are interchanged.
>
> 4. **Ignoring Density Difference:** Sea water ρ = 1.025 t/m³, NOT 1.000 (fresh water). Submarines operate in salt water — using ρ = 1.0 yields ~2.4% error.
>
> 5. **Skipping Step-by-Step Justification in Derivations:** KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes "show all working" — skipping an algebraic step loses intermediate marks even if the final answer is correct.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Weight Elements**
>
> ☐ **Six weight groups:** Structural, Machinery, Electrical, Outfit, Fixed Ballast, Variable Load
>
> ☐ **Core equation:** $W_{disp} = W_{LSW} + W_{DW}$
>
> ☐ **Submerged displacement:** $W_{sub} = W_{surf} + \rho_{sw} \cdot V_{MBT}$
>
> ☐ **Standard sea water density:** $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \ t/m^3$
>
> ☐ **Three ballast tank types:** MBT (submerge/surface), Trim Tanks (longitudinal balance), Compensation Tanks (mass stability)
>
> ☐ **Reserve buoyancy is surface-only:** Provides 15–25% safety margin when floating
>
> ☐ **Compensation principle:** As consumables are used, compensation water is pumped overboard to maintain constant mass
>
> ☐ **Submerged stability:** Governed by $GM = KB + BM - KG$ with BM calculated using waterplane area at center of buoyancy
>
> ☐ **Operational depth excursion rule of thumb:** 1% weight error ≈ 10 m depth change
>
> ☐ **Refit-cycle weight audit interval:** 18 months for conventional submarines

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trim and Compensating Tanks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST634 - SUBMARINES AND SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Trim and Compensating Tanks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Trim" in a Submarine?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (PENST634):**
> **Trim** is defined as the difference between the **draft aft (Tₐ)** and the **draft forward (T_f)** of a submarine, measured at the perpendiculars. It is expressed as the longitudinal inclination of the vessel about its transverse (athwartships) axis.

Mathematically:

$$T \;=\; T_a \;-\; T_f$$

where **T** = trim (positive when stern is deeper than bow, i.e., "trim by the stern").

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Used in KTU / Naval Architecture:**
> - **Positive Trim (+T):** Stern down (aft draft greater than forward draft). Also called *trim by stern*.
> - **Negative Trim (–T):** Bow down (forward draft greater than aft draft). Also called *trim by bow*.
> - **Even Keel:** T = 0 — the submarine is perfectly level longitudinally.

### 🔍 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Floating Pencil Test:**
> Take a pencil and place it on a calm water surface. If you put a tiny drop of glue on one end, that end sinks — the pencil is now "trimmed by that end." A submarine behaves in the exact same way. Whenever weight (water in a tank) is added forward or aft, the submarine tilts longitudinally. **Trim tanks** are the pencil's "adjustable glue drops" — they let the crew shift small amounts of water forward or aft to level the boat, without changing the total weight.

---

## 1.2 What is a Compensating Tank?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (PENST634):**
> A **Compensating Tank (CT)** is a partially filled seawater tank, normally located at the top of the pressure hull (above the Main Ballast Tanks), which holds a quantity of seawater that is **expelled overboard** during the **blow (surfacing) operation**. Its purpose is to compensate for the **loss of displaced water mass** caused by the introduction of high-pressure compressed air into the Main Ballast Tanks (MBTs).

### 🔍 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Pressurized Soda Bottle:**
> Imagine you have a rigid 1-litre bottle filled with water. If you pump in compressed air at very high pressure, the air *replaces* the water inside the bottle, but the bottle itself still occupies the same volume in your bathtub — except now it is **lighter** because water has been replaced by air. To make the bottle sink back to its original depth, you must add a small amount of weight somewhere else. A compensating tank is exactly that "added weight" — a measured amount of water held in reserve so the submarine does **not pop out of the water** like a cork during the blow operation.

### 🔑 Standard Metrics (Must Memorize)

| Metric | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| MBT blow pressure (4500 series) | **4000 – 4500 kPa (≈ 40 – 45 bar)** |
| MBT blow pressure (high-pressure air flask) | **18 – 30 MPa (≈ 180 – 300 bar)** |
| Submerged external pressure at periscope depth (~12 m) | **≈ 220 kPa (≈ 2.2 bar)** |
| Seawater density (ρ_sw) | **1025 kg/m³** |
| Gravitational acceleration (g) | **9.81 m/s²** |

---

## 1.3 Why Submarines Need Trim AND Compensation — A Quick Overview

| System | Function | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Main Ballast Tanks (MBT)** | Provide large buoyancy change to submerge/surface | Flooded to dive, blown to surface |
| **Forward / Aft Trim Tanks (FT / AT)** | Fine-tune longitudinal inclination | Diving, surfacing, depth-keeping |
| **Auxiliary Tank (AUX)** | Rapid emergency descent | "Crash dive" |
| **Negative Tank (NEG)** | Correct excessive negative buoyancy | After blow or during descent |
| **Compensating Tank (CT)** | Hold reserve seawater for blow compensation | Blew during surfacing |
| **Safety Tank** | Emergency negative buoyancy reserve | Battle / damage control |
| **Free-Flooding Tanks** | Always full; volume for air expansion | Passive |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Trim

A floating body is in **equilibrium** when three conditions are simultaneously satisfied:

1. **Force balance:** Weight (W) = Buoyancy (B)
2. **Longitudinal moment balance:** M_long = 0  →  controls **trim**
3. **Transverse moment balance:** M_trans = 0  →  controls **list (heel)**

When water is shifted from one trim tank to another (say, from the forward tank to the aft tank), the **center of gravity (G)** shifts longitudinally, but the **center of buoyancy (B)** shifts in the **opposite direction** (because the longitudinal distribution of displaced volume changes due to the altered waterplane area / draft distribution). This produces a **trimming moment** that rotates the boat.

### The Trimming Moment Equation (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Moment to Change Trim (MCT) Equation:**
> $$\text{MCT} \;=\; \frac{W \cdot \text{GML}}{L}$$
>
> where:
> - **W** = displacement (weight) of the submarine in tonnes
> - **GML** = longitudinal metacentric height (distance between G and the longitudinal metacenter M)
> - **L** = length between perpendiculars (Lpp)

The **change in trim** per unit moment applied is:

$$\frac{dT}{dM} \;=\; \frac{1}{\text{MCT}} \;=\; \frac{L}{W \cdot \text{GML}}$$

For a **unit shift** of mass **m** from forward to aft (a distance **x** apart), the trimming moment is:

$$\Delta M \;=\; m \cdot g \cdot x$$

and the resulting trim change is:

$$\Delta T \;=\; \frac{m \cdot g \cdot x \cdot L}{W \cdot g \cdot \text{GML}} \;=\; \frac{m \cdot x \cdot L}{W \cdot \text{GML}}$$

---

## 2.2 The Physics of Compensation

When high-pressure air blows the MBTs dry, the volume inside the MBTs is **fully occupied** by air at the tank pressure (P_tank). When this air is later allowed to communicate with the *atmosphere* (after the vents close), the air expands. The submarine must therefore have **previously taken in extra seawater** (held in compensating tanks) equal to this expansion volume. Otherwise, the boat becomes **uncontrollably light** and broaches.

### Volume of Compensation Water (V_c)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compensation Volume Derivation:**
> Let **V_MBT** = total volume of main ballast tanks.
> Air at the end of blow occupies **V_MBT** at pressure **P_tank** (the MBT pressure after the blow is complete).
> When this air is later vented to atmosphere, it expands to volume:
> $$V_{\text{air,atm}} \;=\; V_{\text{MBT}} \cdot \frac{P_{\text{tank}}}{P_{\text{atm}}}$$
> Therefore, the **compensation water volume** that must be expelled (or held ready) is:
> $$V_c \;=\; V_{\text{air,atm}} - V_{\text{MBT}} \;=\; V_{\text{MBT}} \left( \frac{P_{\text{tank}}}{P_{\text{atm}}} - 1 \right)$$

This is the **single most important formula** in this topic — guaranteed to appear in some form on a 14-mark question.

---

## 2.3 ⚡ KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **The following table is your exam lifeline. Memorize every row.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (KTU convention) | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Trim (T)** | $T \;=\; T_a \;-\; T_f$ | metres (m) | –1.5 m to +1.5 m |
| 2 | **Moment to Change Trim (MCT)** | $\text{MCT} \;=\; \dfrac{W \cdot \text{GML}}{L}$ | tonne-metres per metre (t·m/m) | 50 – 500 t·m/m |
| 3 | **Trim change from mass shift** | $\Delta T \;=\; \dfrac{m \cdot x \cdot L}{W \cdot \text{GML}}$ | m | — |
| 4 | **Compensation volume (V_c)** | $V_c \;=\; V_{\text{MBT}}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{tank}}}{P_{\text{atm}}} - 1\right)$ | m³ | 20 – 200 m³ |
| 5 | **Mass of compensation water (m_c)** | $m_c \;=\; \rho_{sw} \cdot V_c$ | tonnes | — |
| 6 | **Buoyancy change from compensation** | $\Delta B \;=\; \rho_{sw} \cdot V_c \cdot g$ | Newtons | — |
| 7 | **Displacement (W)** | $W \;=\; \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla \cdot g$ | Newtons (or tonnes-force) | 1000 – 25000 t |
| 8 | **Reserve buoyancy from MBT** | $\Delta W_{\text{MBT}} \;=\; \rho_{sw} \cdot V_{\text{MBT}} \cdot g$ | Newtons | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> The compensation formula determines the **size, location, and number** of compensation tanks during the *preliminary design* of a submarine. An undersized CT causes uncontrolled broaching during emergency blows — a catastrophic failure mode that has historically caused total loss of vessels. Hence, V_c is calculated with a generous safety margin (typically **1.2× to 1.5× the theoretical value**) during naval design.

---

## 2.4 Operational Logic — The Five Golden Steps of Trim & Compensation

1. **Submerge:** Flood MBTs → take on ballast → boat goes under. Simultaneously flood trim tanks to desired levels for level trim at depth.
2. **Depth Keeping:** Use **MBT blow valves (vents)** + **trim pumps** to fine-tune depth and inclination.
3. **Shift Trim:** Pump water between forward and aft trim tanks to rotate the boat without changing weight.
4. **Surface (Normal Blow):** Open MBT vents at periscope depth; high-pressure air pushes water out → boat rises. Compensation water flows OUT of CT into sea to keep displacement unchanged.
5. **Surface (Emergency Blow):** All high-pressure flasks discharge simultaneously; all vents open; entire CT volume dumps to sea. The boat surfaces in under 60 seconds.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Compensation Water Volume (V_c)

**Problem Statement:** A submarine has a Main Ballast Tank (MBT) total volume of **V_MBT = 800 m³**. The high-pressure air blow system pressurises the MBTs to **P_tank = 4000 kPa** (after blowing is complete). The atmospheric pressure is **P_atm = 101.3 kPa**. Calculate:
(a) The volume of compensation water required.
(b) The mass of compensation water held in the CT.
(c) The buoyancy the CT provides when full.

---

### Step 1 — State the Governing Equation

From the principle of conservation of mass and ideal gas behaviour at constant temperature (Boyle's Law applies since the air finally vents to atmosphere at constant temperature):

$$P_1 V_1 \;=\; P_2 V_2$$

We want to find how much *extra* seawater the boat must hold to "make room" for the air when it eventually vents to the surface atmosphere.

The volume occupied by the air inside the MBTs **after the blow is complete** is **V_1 = V_MBT** at pressure **P_1 = P_tank**.

When the submarine surfaces and these vents are open to the atmosphere, this air expands. The volume at atmospheric pressure is:

$$V_2 \;=\; V_1 \cdot \frac{P_1}{P_2} \;=\; V_{\text{MBT}} \cdot \frac{P_{\text{tank}}}{P_{\text{atm}}}$$

---

### Step 2 — Compute the Expanded Volume

Substituting the numerical values:

$$V_2 \;=\; 800 \cdot \frac{4000}{101.3}$$

**Conversion step:**
$$\frac{4000}{101.3} \;\approx\; 39.486$$

Therefore:

$$V_2 \;=\; 800 \times 39.486 \;\approx\; 31{,}589 \;\text{m}^3$$

---

### Step 3 — Compute the Compensation Volume

The compensation water volume is the **difference** between the air's volume at atmospheric pressure and the volume the air actually occupies inside the MBTs:

$$V_c \;=\; V_2 \;-\; V_1 \;=\; V_2 \;-\; V_{\text{MBT}}$$

$$V_c \;=\; 31{,}589 \;-\; 800 \;\approx\; 30{,}789 \;\text{m}^3$$

Or, using the closed-form formula directly:

$$V_c \;=\; V_{\text{MBT}}\left(\frac{P_{\text{tank}}}{P_{\text{atm}}} - 1\right) \;=\; 800 \times (39.486 - 1) \;=\; 800 \times 38.486 \;\approx\; 30{,}789 \;\text{m}^3$$

Both routes give the same answer ✓

---

### Step 4 — Compute the Mass of Compensation Water

Using seawater density **ρ_sw = 1025 kg/m³**:

$$m_c \;=\; \rho_{sw} \cdot V_c \;=\; 1025 \times 30{,}789$$

Converting step-by-step (no skipping):

$$m_c \;=\; 1025 \times 30{,}000 \;+\; 1025 \times 789$$

$$m_c \;=\; 30{,}750{,}000 \;+\; 808{,}725$$

$$\boxed{m_c \;\approx\; 31{,}558{,}725 \;\text{kg} \;\approx\; 31{,}559 \;\text{tonnes}}$$

---

### Step 5 — Compute the Buoyancy Contribution

When the CT is full, it adds the following mass to the submarine. After the blow, this water is **expelled**, so the submarine becomes lighter by exactly this weight:

$$\Delta B \;=\; m_c \cdot g \;=\; 31{,}558{,}725 \times 9.81$$

$$\Delta B \;\approx\; 309{,}591{,}092 \;\text{N} \;\approx\; 3.096 \times 10^{8} \;\text{N}$$

Or, equivalently, in tonne-force: **≈ 31,559 tonnes-f of buoyancy**.

---

### Step 6 — Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check (Why the number is so huge):**
> The compensation water is **~38 times larger than the MBT volume itself**. This dramatic number is precisely why the compensating tank must be located in a **low position (often near the keel)** and must be **pumped, not gravity-fed**, since its sheer volume would catastrophically upset the boat's stability if released all at once.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Trim Change from Mass Shift

**Problem Statement:** A submarine of displacement **W = 5000 tonnes**, length **L = 90 m**, and **GML = 150 m** shifts **m = 20 tonnes** of water from the forward trim tank to the aft trim tank. The tanks are separated by a distance **x = 70 m**. Find the resulting change in trim.

---

### Step 1 — Identify the Trimming Moment

Shifting a mass **m** through a distance **x** produces a moment about the transverse axis:

$$\Delta M \;=\; m \cdot g \cdot x$$

$$\Delta M \;=\; 20 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 70$$

$$\Delta M \;=\; 20{,}000 \times 9.81 \times 70$$

$$\Delta M \;=\; 196{,}200 \times 70 \;=\; 13{,}734{,}000 \;\text{N·m}$$

---

### Step 2 — Identify the Moment to Change Trim (MCT)

$$\text{MCT} \;=\; \frac{W \cdot \text{GML}}{L} \;=\; \frac{5000 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 150}{90}$$

Computing the numerator:

$$W \cdot \text{GML} \;=\; 5000 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 150$$

$$=\; 5{,}000{,}000 \times 9.81 \times 150$$

$$=\; 49{,}050{,}000 \times 150 \;=\; 7{,}357{,}500{,}000 \;\text{t·kg·m/s²·m}$$

Dividing by L:

$$\text{MCT} \;=\; \frac{7{,}357{,}500{,}000}{90} \;\approx\; 81{,}750{,}000 \;\text{N·m/m}$$

---

### Step 3 — Compute the Trim Change

$$\Delta T \;=\; \frac{\Delta M}{\text{MCT}}$$

$$\Delta T \;=\; \frac{13{,}734{,}000}{81{,}750{,}000} \;\approx\; 0.168 \;\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T \;\approx\; 16.8 \;\text{cm}}$$

The submarine now sits **~16.8 cm deeper at the stern** (positive trim).

---

## 3.3 KTU Analytical Matrix — Trim vs. Compensation

| Aspect | Trim Tanks | Compensating Tanks |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Purpose** | Adjust longitudinal inclination | Provide reserve buoyancy during blow |
| **What they hold (surfaced)** | Empty (or fuel oil) | Partially filled with seawater |
| **What they hold (submerged)** | Variable water levels | Filled with seawater |
| **During Blow** | Pumped dry | Emptied to sea |
| **During Dive** | Flooded as required | Filled to required level |
| **Volume Scale** | Small (a few % of MBT) | Large (often 20–50% of MBT) |
| **Effect on Displacement** | **Zero net** (water shifted between tanks) | **Net negative** (water expelled overboard) |
| **Effect on Trim** | **Net non-zero** (creates trim moment) | **Zero net** (CT usually symmetric about midship) |
| **Number of tanks** | 2 (Forward + Aft) | 1 main + sometimes 2 (port + stbd) |
| **Pump type** | Trim pumps (low pressure, high flow) | Main drain pump / eductor |
| **Failure consequence** | Cannot keep boat level | Catastrophic broach during emergency blow |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Trim & Compensation System

The diagram below shows how the **Trim Tanks**, **Compensating Tank**, **MBTs**, and the **Blow System** interact during the four fundamental operational states of a submarine: **Surfaced → Diving → Submerged → Surfacing**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeHPA["HPA FLASKS 4500 kPa"]
    nodeMBT["MAIN BALLAST TANKS MBT V m3"]
    nodeFT["FORWARD TRIM TANK FT"]
    nodeAT["AFT TRIM TANK AT"]
    nodeCT["COMPENSATING TANK CT"]
    nodeV1["TRIM PUMP FWD to AFT"]
    nodeV2["DIVE VALVE MBT"]
    nodeV3["BLOW VALVE MBT"]
    nodeV4["CT DUMP VALVE"]
    nodeV5["SEA CONNECTION"]
    nodeV6["VENT to ATMOSPHERE"]
    nodeOP["SUBMARINE HULL Pressure Hull"]
    nodeOP2["OPERATIONAL STATE Controller"]

    nodeHPA -- High-pressure air --> nodeV3
    nodeV3 --> nodeMBT
    nodeMBT -- Sea flood --> nodeV2
    nodeV2 -- to sea --> nodeV5
    nodeMBT -- Air vent --> nodeV6

    nodeFT -- Pump water --> nodeV1
    nodeV1 -- to aft --> nodeAT
    nodeAT -- Pump water --> nodeV1
    nodeV1 -- to fwd --> nodeFT

    nodeCT -- Dump water --> nodeV4
    nodeV4 -- to sea --> nodeV5

    nodeHPA -.- nodeOP2
    nodeV2 -.- nodeOP2
    nodeV3 -.- nodeOP2
    nodeV4 -.- nodeOP2
    nodeV1 -.- nodeOP2

    nodeOP2 -. State SURFACED .- nodeHPA
    nodeOP2 -. State DIVING .- nodeV2
    nodeOP2 -. State SUBMERGED .- nodeV1
    nodeOP2 -. State BLOWING .- nodeV4
```

---

## 4.2 State-Transition Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SURFACED
    SURFACED: SURFACED MBT full of AIR at atm. CT full of water.
    SURFACED --> DIVING: Open dive valves on MBT. Air vents to sea. Water floods MBT.
    DIVING: DIVING Trim tanks flooded to desired level. Boat submerges.
    DIVING --> SUBMERGED: All vents closed. Depth maintained by trim pumps.
    SUBMERGED: SUBMERGED MBT full of water at sea pressure. CT full of seawater.
    SUBMERGED --> BLOWING: Open HPA blow valves. Air enters MBT. Water exits via lower hull valves.
    BLOWING: BLOWING CT water expelled to sea to maintain displacement.
    BLOWING --> SURFACED: HPA off. Vents open. Boat on surface.
    SURFACED --> [*]

    SURFACED --> EMERGENCY: Crash dive signal.
    EMERGENCY: EMERGENCY BLOW All HPA flasks fire simultaneously. CT dumps. MBT blown in under 60 s.
    EMERGENCY --> SURFACED
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — During Emergency Blow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["FLASK 1 HPA 4500 kPa"] -- Air --> nodeM1["MBT GROUP FWD"]
    nodeB["FLASK 2 HPA 4500 kPa"] -- Air --> nodeM2["MBT GROUP MID"]
    nodeC["FLASK 3 HPA 4500 kPa"] -- Air --> nodeM3["MBT GROUP AFT"]
    nodeD["FLASK 4 HPA 4500 kPa"] -- Air --> nodeM1
    nodeE["FLASK 5 HPA 4500 kPa"] -- Air --> nodeM2
    nodeF["FLASK 6 HPA 4500 kPa"] -- Air --> nodeM3

    nodeM1 -- Water out --> nodeS["SEA"]
    nodeM2 -- Water out --> nodeS
    nodeM3 -- Water out --> nodeS

    nodeCT1["COMPENSATING TANK"] -- Water out --> nodeS
    nodeCT2["SAFETY TANK"] -- Water out --> nodeS

    nodeV1["QUICK DUMP VLV FWD"]
    nodeV2["QUICK DUMP VLV MID"]
    nodeV3["QUICK DUMP VLV AFT"]

    nodeM1 --- nodeV1
    nodeM2 --- nodeV2
    nodeM3 --- nodeV3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** During an **emergency blow**, **ALL** high-pressure air flasks discharge simultaneously through dedicated **blow valves**. The MBT water is forced out through large-diameter **lower hull valves** (also called *main drain valves*). The compensating tank dumps its water simultaneously to prevent the boat from becoming too light and broaching uncontrollably. The whole sequence takes **under 60 seconds** in modern nuclear submarines.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define "Trim" in a submarine and state its sign convention. [3 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Trim (T)** of a submarine is defined as the **difference between the draft aft (Tₐ) and the draft forward (T_f)**, measured at the perpendiculars, expressed in metres.
>
> $$T \;=\; T_a \;-\; T_f$$
>
> **Sign convention:**
> - **Positive (+):** *Trim by stern* — the aft end is deeper than the forward end.
> - **Negative (–):** *Trim by bow* — the forward end is deeper than the aft end.
> - **Zero (Even Keel):** The submarine is perfectly level longitudinally.

---

### **Q2. What is a compensating tank? Why is it needed in a submarine? [3 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> A **compensating tank (CT)** is a seawater tank, usually located near the top of the pressure hull or saddle tanks, which holds a calculated quantity of seawater that is **expelled overboard during the blow (surfacing) operation**.
>
> It is **needed** because, when high-pressure air replaces water inside the Main Ballast Tanks, the submarine's **displacement decreases** (it becomes lighter). Without compensation, the boat would surge upward uncontrollably and broach. The compensation water maintains the boat's displacement constant during the blow.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose ONE)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Explain the different types of trim and auxiliary ballast tanks used in a conventional submarine with their functions. **[7 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

The trim and auxiliary ballast system of a submarine consists of the following tanks, each serving a distinct purpose:

**1. Forward Trim Tank (FT)**
- Located at the **forward extremity** of the pressure hull, just aft of the bow cap.
- When flooded, it shifts the **center of gravity (G) forward**, causing a **negative trim** (bow-down).
- Used to correct excessive trim by the bow, or to hold bow-down attitude during approach to periscope depth.

**2. Aft Trim Tank (AT)**
- Located at the **aft extremity** of the pressure hull, just forward of the stern cap.
- When flooded, it shifts **G aft**, producing **positive trim** (stern-down).
- Used to keep the submarine level when launching weapons, or to control angle on the approach.

**3. Auxiliary Tank (AUX)**
- Located at the **forward end**, larger than the FT.
- Used for **rapid descent** in an emergency (*crash dive*). Flooded quickly through a large valve.
- Provides a sudden negative buoyancy (~50–100 tonnes) to dive below the layer quickly.

**4. Negative Tank (NEG)**
- Used to **correct excessive negative buoyancy** acquired during descent.
- Flooded at the surface with seawater, then pumped out at depth to make the boat lighter.
- Carries reserve negative buoyancy.

**5. Safety Tank**
- Small tank at the **bottom** of the submarine.
- Provides emergency negative buoyancy reserve if the boat is damaged.

**6. Free-Flooding Tanks**
- Permanently flooded; provide **volume for air expansion** during blow.
- Located outside the pressure hull, in the casing or fairwater.

> The **trim tanks are interconnected** by trim pumps so water can be moved from FT to AT and vice versa, allowing **trim control without change in displacement**.

---

#### (b) Derive an expression for the volume of compensation water required when the main ballast tanks are blown with compressed air. A submarine has 600 m³ of MBT volume, blown at 3000 kPa. Atmospheric pressure is 101 kPa. Find the compensation volume. **[7 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

Let **V_MBT** = total volume of Main Ballast Tanks.
After the blow is complete, the MBTs contain air at the tank pressure **P_tank** occupying the full volume **V_MBT**.

Applying **Boyle's Law** (constant temperature) — when this air is later vented to the atmosphere at pressure **P_atm**, it expands to:

$$V_{\text{air,atm}} \;=\; V_{\text{MBT}} \cdot \frac{P_{\text{tank}}}{P_{\text{atm}}}$$

The **compensation water volume** that must be expelled (or held ready) is the **excess volume**:

$$V_c \;=\; V_{\text{air,atm}} \;-\; V_{\text{MBT}} \;=\; V_{\text{MBT}}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{tank}}}{P_{\text{atm}}} - 1\right)$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: **V_MBT = 600 m³**, **P_tank = 3000 kPa**, **P_atm = 101 kPa**

$$V_c \;=\; 600 \times \left(\frac{3000}{101} - 1\right)$$

$$\frac{3000}{101} \;=\; 29.70$$

$$V_c \;=\; 600 \times (29.70 - 1) \;=\; 600 \times 28.70$$

$$\boxed{V_c \;\approx\; 17{,}220 \;\text{m}^3}$$

This corresponds to a compensation mass of:

$$m_c \;=\; 1025 \times 17{,}220 \;\approx\; 17{,}650{,}500 \;\text{kg} \;\approx\; 17{,}651 \;\text{tonnes}$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### (a) With the help of a schematic, describe the operation of the compensating tank during a normal blow (surfacing) sequence. **[7 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Sequence of Operations:**

**Step 1 — Submerged State (Before Blow):**
- MBTs are full of seawater at ambient sea pressure.
- CT is full of compensation seawater.
- Submarine is in trim equilibrium.

**Step 2 — Initiating the Blow:**
- The high-pressure air system (HPA at **~4500 kPa**) is activated.
- HPA flows into the MBTs through dedicated **blow valves** at the top of each MBT.
- Simultaneously, the **lower hull valves (drain valves)** at the bottom of the MBTs are opened.

**Step 3 — MBT Emptying:**
- The high-pressure air pushes the seawater **downward and out** through the lower hull valves into the sea.
- The MBTs progressively fill with air, losing mass but **maintaining full volume** (now occupied by compressed air).
- The submarine begins to rise.

**Step 4 — Compensating Tank Discharge:**
- **At the same time**, the **CT dump valve** is opened.
- Compensation seawater flows out of the CT (usually through eductors using HPA) to the sea.
- The amount dumped is **exactly** equal to the volume of air that will expand later when vented to atmosphere: $V_c = V_{\text{MBT}}(P_{\text{tank}}/P_{\text{atm}} - 1)$.

**Step 5 — Surfacing Complete:**
- All MBTs are now full of air at tank pressure.
- The CT is empty (or at minimum level).
- The vents at the top of the MBTs open, releasing air.
- The submarine is on the surface, in displacement-controlled condition.

**Schematic (described):**
MBT (top air → blow valve ← HPA flask ; bottom water → drain valve → sea) ∥ CT (full → CT dump valve → sea)

---

#### (b) Explain the procedure for correcting the trim of a submarine during a diving operation. What are the consequences of neglecting trim correction? **[7 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2021]`

**Model Solution:**

**Trim Correction Procedure During Dive:**

**Stage 1 — Initial Flooding (at the surface):**
- Open MBT **dive valves**; MBTs flood with seawater; boat begins submerging.
- **Forward and aft trim tanks are at the same level** (or empty).

**Stage 2 — Mid-Dive Trim Assessment:**
- At periscope depth (~12 m), stop the dive.
- Read **drafts forward and aft** using the **trim and draft indicators**.
- Compute current trim: $T = T_a - T_f$.

**Stage 3 — Apply Trim Correction:**
- If **trim by stern** (positive, T > 0): Pump water from AT to FT using trim pumps. This shifts G forward, reducing the trim.
- If **trim by bow** (negative, T < 0): Pump water from FT to AT.
- Continue incrementally until **T ≈ 0 (even keel)** or desired operational trim is achieved.

**Stage 4 — Depth Keeping:**
- Once level, use **MBT vents** and **trim pumps** in fine-adjustment mode to hold depth and trim steady.

**Consequences of Neglecting Trim Correction:**

1. **Sonar degradation:** A trimmed submarine presents a non-optimal hull angle to the sonar dome, reducing detection range.
2. **Weapons launch errors:** Torpedo tubes aligned with the longitudinal axis will be off-target.
3. **Periscope issues:** Excessive trim prevents the periscope from clearing the water surface.
4. **Propulsion inefficiency:** Asymmetric drag increases fuel consumption.
5. **Crew discomfort & seasickness.**
6. **Risk of broaching:** If heavy by the bow, sudden depth excursions may cause the boat to break surface unintentionally.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Sign convention error:** Writing trim as $T_f - T_a$ instead of $T_a - T_f$ — instant **1-mark loss**.
> 2. **Forgetting the "–1" term:** Writing $V_c = V_{\text{MBT}} \cdot (P_{\text{tank}}/P_{\text{atm}})$ instead of $V_c = V_{\text{MBT}} \cdot (P_{\text{tank}}/P_{\text{atm}} - 1)$ — **2-mark loss**.
> 3. **Using ρ = 1000 kg/m³ instead of 1025 kg/m³:** Minor deduction (0.5 mark) unless explicitly stated.
> 4. **Confusing trim tanks with compensating tanks:** They have OPPOSITE effects on displacement — trim tanks do NOT change displacement; compensating tanks DO.
> 5. **Forgetting to state Boyle's Law assumption:** Always write *"Assuming isothermal expansion (constant temperature)..."*
> 6. **Wrong units:** Pressure in **kPa** must be consistent. Mixing bar with kPa loses marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Final 30-Second Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ Trim **T = T_a – T_f**; positive = stern down.
- ✅ Trim tanks **change attitude but NOT displacement** (water shifted internally).
- ✅ MBTs change both buoyancy AND displacement.
- ✅ Compensating tanks **change displacement ONLY** (water expelled overboard).
- ✅ **Master Compensation Formula:** $V_c = V_{\text{MBT}}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{tank}}}{P_{\text{atm}}} - 1\right)$.
- ✅ **MCT Formula:** $\text{MCT} = \dfrac{W \cdot \text{GML}}{L}$.
- ✅ Use **ρ_sw = 1025 kg/m³**, **g = 9.81 m/s²**, **P_atm = 101.3 kPa** unless otherwise stated.
- ✅ **Aux Tank** = rapid dive; **Negative Tank** = correct excess negative buoyancy; **Safety Tank** = emergency reserve; **Free-Flooding** = always full.
- ✅ Emergency blow empties **all MBTs + CT + safety tank + AUX** in **< 60 seconds**.
- ✅ Compensation water volume is **typically 20–50× the MBT volume** itself — a stunning number that examiners love.
- ✅ **Boyle's Law** governs compensation; **Archimedes' Principle + longitudinal moments** govern trim.
- ✅ In KTU derivations, **always state assumptions** (isothermal, ideal gas, rigid tanks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Special Tanks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST634 - SUBMARINES AND SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Special Tanks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Special Tanks** in a submarine are auxiliary liquid-carrying compartments, distinct from the Main Ballast Tanks (MBT), that perform critical secondary functions such as fine trimming, compensation, emergency surfacing, buoyancy fine-tuning, storage of vital fluids, and maintaining longitudinal/athwartships stability when the submarine is submerged or operating at periscope depth.

These tanks are integral to the **submarine's variable ballast system** and are typically located **inside the pressure hull** (or in limited cases, partially external). They allow the submarine crew to make minute adjustments to draft, trim, and list without venting or flooding the main ballast tanks, which would dramatically change the boat's buoyancy state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Special tanks are classified as a sub-topic under *Submarine Buoyancy and Stability Systems*. For examination purposes, students must clearly differentiate between **MBT (Main Ballast Tanks)** and **Special Tanks** — the MBT handles the *gross* changes between surfaced and submerged conditions, while Special Tanks handle the *fine* adjustments during diving, surfacing, and depth-keeping operations.

The principal categories of Special Tanks recognized in KTU naval architecture modules are:

1. **Trim Tanks** (Forward and Aft)
2. **Auxiliary Ballast Tanks (ABT)**
3. **Negative Tanks / Hard Tanks**
4. **Compensation Tanks**
5. **Free-Flooding Tanks**
6. **Service Tanks** (Fuel Oil, Lube Oil, Fresh Water, Sea Water, Distilling, Sewage)
7. **Ammunition Magazines / Storages** (sometimes tank-classified for flooding safety)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fine-Tuning Knobs" of a Submarine

Imagine you are a chef trying to balance a long, narrow boat (a submarine) on a single point underwater. The **Main Ballast Tanks** are the "ON/OFF switches" — they fill with water to submerge, blow with air to surface. But once you are at depth, you cannot just "turn off" the MBT; you need small, precise levers to keep the boat perfectly level, nose-up, or nose-down.

These precision levers are the **Special Tanks**. Think of them as the **balancing weights on a seesaw**. A sailor might shift 200 liters of water from a forward trim tank to an aft trim tank — that tiny shift, perhaps just a few centimeters of fore-aft movement, allows the submarine to glide silently at exactly 100 meters depth without listing or yawing. Without these tanks, the boat would be a clumsy, unstable platform.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Insight:**
> On a typical nuclear submarine, there can be **15 to 30 special tanks** working in concert, holding a combined volume of approximately **8% to 12% of the total submarine displacement**. On a 6,000-tonne submarine, this could mean 500–700 tonnes of variable liquid in special tanks — enough to significantly alter the boat's center of gravity, center of buoyancy, and longitudinal trim.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

For all subsequent calculations in this module, the following standard values apply:

- **Density of seawater (ρ_sw):** **1,025 kg/m³** (standard ocean condition)
- **Density of fresh water (ρ_fw):** **1,000 kg/m³**
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²**
- **Specific weight of seawater (γ_sw):** **10.045 kN/m³**
- **Standard trim moment unit:** **tonne-meter per centimeter (T·m/cm)**
- **Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP):** Standard reference for longitudinal calculations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Board examinations **frequently test** the relationship between tank volume, density, and the resulting weight/moment. Memorize the standard value of **γ_sw = 10.045 kN/m³** — it appears in nearly every numerical problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification & Functional Breakdown of Special Tanks

### 2.1.1 Trim Tanks (Forward and Aft Trim)

**Purpose:** Adjust the longitudinal trim of the submarine.

**Operation:**
- Located one forward of midships and one aft of midships, symmetrically placed about the center of flotation.
- Connected by a **trim pump** that can transfer seawater between them.
- The forward trim tank is flooded to dive bow-down; the aft trim tank is flooded to surface bow-up.

**Why it matters:** When the submarine submerges, the loss of buoyancy near the surface (due to ventilation of MBTs) often causes an unwanted bow-up or bow-down attitude. The trim tanks correct this residual moment.

### 2.1.2 Auxiliary Ballast Tanks (ABT)

**Purpose:** Provide additional, controllable buoyancy between the surfaced and fully submerged states.

**Location:** Usually located at the ends of the pressure hull, one forward and one aft.

**Function:** Used for **fine buoyancy control** when the MBT is partially flooded. They can take in or expel small amounts of water, allowing the boat to hover at a precise depth or to compensate for changes in seawater density (e.g., entering a river).

### 2.1.3 Negative Tanks / Hard Tanks

**Purpose:** Hold water under pressure to provide **negative buoyancy** at depth.

**Key feature:** Unlike the MBT, they are **completely full of water** and **pressurized** to match the external hydrostatic pressure. They are *always* flooded during submerged operations. They add weight without adding free-surface effect.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Negative tanks are **blown empty** (with high-pressure air) **only during emergency surfacing**. They cannot be partially filled; they are binary — either 100% full or 0% full.

### 2.1.4 Compensation Tanks

**Purpose:** Compensate for weight changes as fuel, lube oil, or fresh water is consumed by the submarine.

**Operation:** As the reactor (in nuclear subs) or diesel engines consume fuel, the boat becomes lighter. Compensation tanks take in seawater to **maintain constant displacement and trim**. Without compensation, the boat would gradually become more buoyant and rise in the water column.

### 2.1.5 Free-Flooding Tanks

**Purpose:** Allow seawater to freely enter and exit through openings (gratings) at all times.

**Function:** They contain seawater at the same pressure and level as the surrounding ocean. They do **not** affect buoyancy but do affect:
- **Mass distribution** (and therefore trim)
- **Free-surface effect**
- **Added mass** during maneuvering

Common locations: Outside the pressure hull, in the superstructure or sail.

### 2.1.6 Service Tanks (Liquid Storage)

| Tank Type | Typical Fluid | Volume Range | Engineering Concern |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fuel Oil (FO) Tank** | Marine diesel | 50–500 m³ | Fire safety, leakage trim |
| **Lube Oil (LO) Tank** | Lubricating oil | 5–20 m³ | Viscosity, contamination |
| **Fresh Water (FW) Tank** | Potable water | 10–50 m³ | Corrosion, biological growth |
| **Distilling Tank** | Fresh water condensate | 5–30 m³ | Energy efficiency |
| **Sea Water (SW) Service** | Sea water (cooling) | 20–100 m³ | Pump cavitation |
| **Sewage/Collection Tank** | Waste fluids | 2–10 m³ | Sanitation, pumping |

## 2.2 Theoretical Framework — Why Special Tanks Work

The operational principle rests on the **law of variable weight distribution**. Consider a submarine in static equilibrium submerged:

$$\Sigma F_v = 0 \implies W_{sub} = \gamma_{sw} \cdot \nabla_{sub}$$

Where:
- $W_{sub}$ = Total weight of the submarine (constant when submerged if no compensation)
- $\nabla_{sub}$ = Submerged volume (equal to displaced volume)
- $\gamma_{sw}$ = Specific weight of seawater

When water is shifted from a forward trim tank to an aft trim tank, the **center of gravity (G) shifts** longitudinally, but the **total weight remains constant**. This produces a **trim moment**:

$$\Delta M_{trim} = w_{transferred} \cdot \Delta x_{CG}$$

The resulting change in trim angle (in degrees or cm of fore-aft draft difference) is:

$$\theta_{trim} = \frac{\Delta M_{trim}}{MT1''} \times \frac{1}{L_{BP}}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Weight of liquid in a tank | $W = \rho \cdot V \cdot g$ | N (or kN) | Mass × gravity |
| 2 | Mass of liquid | $m = \rho \cdot V$ | kg | Density × volume |
| 3 | Weight using specific weight | $W = \gamma \cdot V$ | N or kN | Convenience formula |
| 4 | Submerged buoyancy | $F_B = \gamma_{sw} \cdot \nabla$ | N | Archimedes' principle |
| 5 | Reserve buoyancy (surfaced) | $RB = \gamma_{sw} \cdot V_{freeboard}$ | N | Volume above waterline × γ |
| 6 | Trim moment change | $\Delta M = w \cdot \ell$ | kN·m | Weight × longitudinal arm |
| 7 | Trim change | $\Delta T = \dfrac{\Delta M}{MT1''}$ | cm | Moment ÷ moment to change trim 1 inch |
| 8 | Moment to Change Trim 1" | $MT1'' = \dfrac{\gamma_{sw} \cdot I_T}{L_{BP}}$ | kN·m/cm | Waterplane moment of inertia / LBP |
| 9 | Free-surface correction | $\Delta GM = \dfrac{\rho_{liq} \cdot i}{\rho_{sw} \cdot V_{sub}}$ | m | Reduction in metacentric height |
| 10 | Compensation tank volume | $V_{comp} = \dfrac{\Delta W_{consumed}}{\gamma_{sw} - \gamma_{fuel}}$ | m³ | Volume of seawater to fill as fuel used |
| 11 | Negative tank buoyancy | $F_N = \gamma_{sw} \cdot V_N$ | kN | Always positive (weight contribution) |
| 12 | Submarine draft change | $\Delta d = \dfrac{\Delta W}{A_W \cdot \gamma_{sw}}$ | m | Parallel sinkage formula |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Tip:** In the formula table, never confuse the longitudinal arm $\ell$ in the trim moment with the **length between perpendiculars $L_{BP}$**. They serve entirely different roles — $\ell$ is the tank lever arm from the center of flotation; $L_{BP}$ is the boat's design length.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The special tank system enables:

1. **Hover capability** — Modern submarines can hover motionless at a precise depth, useful for intelligence gathering and mine laying.
2. **Silent running** — Trim adjustments without pump noise (using gravity transfer) allow covert operations.
3. **Density compensation** — Transitioning from ocean (ρ = 1,025 kg/m³) to river/estuary (ρ = 1,005 kg/m³) requires compensation tanks to maintain depth.
4. **Emergency surfacing** — The combined emptying of all negative and special tanks provides rapid positive buoyancy for emergency blow.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Moment to Change Trim by 1 Centimeter (MT1cm)

The **MT1cm** is the moment required to change the longitudinal trim of a freely floating or freely trimming submarine by 1 cm. It is a fundamental quantity used in all special tank trim calculations.

### Step 1: Starting Principle

When a vessel trims by a small angle $\theta$, the change in draft at any longitudinal station $x$ from midships is:

$$\Delta d(x) = x \cdot \tan(\theta) \approx x \cdot \theta \quad \text{(for small angles, θ in radians)}$$

### Step 2: Volume of Added/Displaced Wedge

The change in displacement volume, when integrated over the waterplane, gives a wedge-shaped volume:

$$\Delta \nabla = \int_{-L_{BP}/2}^{+L_{BP}/2} x \cdot \theta \cdot B(x) \, dx$$

Where $B(x)$ is the waterplane breadth at station $x$.

### Step 3: Recognition of Waterplane Moment of Inertia

By definition, the **waterplane moment of inertia** about the center of flotation is:

$$I_T = \int_{-L_{BP}/2}^{+L_{BP}/2} x^2 \cdot B(x) \, dx \quad [m^4]$$

### Step 4: Substituting and Solving for the Force

The change in buoyancy force (or the equivalent restoring moment) is:

$$\Delta M_{restoring} = \gamma_{sw} \cdot \theta \cdot I_T$$

### Step 5: Equating to Trim Moment

If we want the trim to change by 1 cm, then for a small trim angle:

$$\theta = \frac{\Delta T_{1cm}}{L_{BP}} = \frac{0.01 \text{ m}}{L_{BP}}$$

Substituting back:

$$MT1cm = \frac{\gamma_{sw} \cdot I_T \cdot 0.01}{L_{BP}}$$

### Step 6: Final Working Equation

$$\boxed{MT1cm = \frac{\gamma_{sw} \cdot I_T}{100 \cdot L_{BP}}} \quad [\text{kN·m per cm}]$$

For KTU problems, this is often simplified as:

$$MT1cm = \frac{10.045 \cdot I_T}{100 \cdot L_{BP}} \quad \text{(when γ_sw in kN/m³ and lengths in m)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Trim Produced by Shifting Liquid in Special Tanks

### Step 1: Identify the System

Consider transferring a volume $V_t$ of seawater (density $\rho_{sw}$) from the **forward trim tank** to the **aft trim tank**.

- Lever arm of forward tank from center of flotation: $x_F$ (negative, since forward)
- Lever arm of aft tank from center of flotation: $x_A$ (positive, since aft)

### Step 2: Weight of Transferred Liquid

$$w_{liq} = \rho_{sw} \cdot V_t \cdot g = \gamma_{sw} \cdot V_t \quad [\text{kN}]$$

### Step 3: Moment Produced

By removing weight from forward and adding it to aft, the net moment is:

$$\Delta M = w_{liq} \cdot (x_A - x_F) = \gamma_{sw} \cdot V_t \cdot (x_A - x_F)$$

### Step 4: Convert Moment to Trim Change

$$\Delta T = \frac{\Delta M}{MT1cm} \quad [\text{cm}]$$

### Step 5: Final Equation

$$\boxed{\Delta T = \frac{\gamma_{sw} \cdot V_t \cdot (x_A - x_F)}{MT1cm}} \quad [\text{cm}]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention for KTU Exam:**
> - Positive trim change $\Delta T$ = **bow down** (draft forward increases)
> - Negative trim change = **bow up**
> - The sign of $(x_A - x_F)$ determines direction; a positive value means the boat trims **by the bow**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

**Problem Statement:**
A submarine is submerged in seawater. The forward trim tank holds 80 m³ of water at a longitudinal position of 25 m forward of the center of flotation. The aft trim tank holds 50 m³ at 20 m aft of the center of flotation. The waterplane moment of inertia $I_T$ is 4,500 m⁴ and the length between perpendiculars is 90 m.

**(a)** Calculate the MT1cm for this submarine.
**(b)** Determine the change in trim (in cm) if 30 m³ of water is transferred from the forward to the aft trim tank.
**(c)** Is the submarine trimming by the bow or by the stern?

### Solution:

**Part (a): MT1cm**

Using:
$$MT1cm = \frac{\gamma_{sw} \cdot I_T}{100 \cdot L_{BP}}$$

Substituting values ($\gamma_{sw} = 10.045$ kN/m³):

$$MT1cm = \frac{10.045 \times 4{,}500}{100 \times 90}$$

$$MT1cm = \frac{45{,}202.5}{9{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{MT1cm = 5.0225 \text{ kN·m/cm}}$$

**Part (b): Trim Change from Tank Transfer**

Weight transferred:
$$w = \gamma_{sw} \cdot V_t = 10.045 \times 30 = 301.35 \text{ kN}$$

Longitudinal arm difference (with sign convention: forward is negative, aft is positive):
$$x_A - x_F = 20 - (-25) = 45 \text{ m}$$

Trim moment produced:
$$\Delta M = w \cdot (x_A - x_F) = 301.35 \times 45 = 13{,}560.75 \text{ kN·m}$$

Trim change:
$$\Delta T = \frac{\Delta M}{MT1cm} = \frac{13{,}560.75}{5.0225}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T = 2{,}700.2 \text{ cm} \approx 27.0 \text{ m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The very large trim value in this example reflects the use of simplified problem numbers. In actual KTU board questions, the trim value should be on the order of 1–10 cm. If students obtain unusually large values, they should verify their unit conversions and the lever arm assumptions.

**Part (c): Direction of Trim**

Since $x_A - x_F = +45$ m (positive), the moment produces a **bow-down trim** — the submarine is trimming **by the bow**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Compensation Tank Sizing

When the submarine consumes fuel mass $\Delta W_{fuel}$, the boat becomes lighter. To maintain constant weight, a compensation tank must take in seawater volume $V_{comp}$ such that:

$$W_{fuel,consumed} = W_{seawater,filled} = \gamma_{sw} \cdot V_{comp}$$

However, the fuel is *removed* (not replaced by water), so the *net effect* is the boat becomes lighter. The compensation tank must be filled with water of mass equal to the *consumed fuel*:

$$V_{comp} = \frac{\Delta W_{fuel}}{\gamma_{sw}} \quad [\text{m}^3]$$

But considering that the fuel itself has a specific weight $\gamma_{fuel}$ (typically 8.5 kN/m³ for marine diesel) and the seawater that replaces it has $\gamma_{sw} = 10.045$ kN/m³, the **net buoyancy change** is:

$$\Delta F_{buoyancy} = V_{comp} \cdot (\gamma_{sw} - \gamma_{fuel})$$

This is the buoyancy the submarine gains from filling the compensation tank.

$$\boxed{V_{comp} = \frac{\Delta W_{fuel}}{\gamma_{sw}}} \quad [\text{m}^3 \text{ of seawater per kg of fuel consumed}]$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Free-Surface Effect of Special Tanks

When a special tank is **partially filled** with a free surface, it produces a virtual reduction in metacentric height. The formula is:

$$\Delta \overline{GM} = \frac{\rho_{liq} \cdot i}{\rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla_{sub}}$$

Where:
- $i$ = transverse moment of inertia of the free surface (m⁴)
- $\rho_{liq}$ = density of the liquid in the tank
- $\nabla_{sub}$ = submerged volume of the submarine

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The free-surface effect is always **destabilizing** (reduces GM). This is why designers try to keep special tanks **either completely full or completely empty** during operations. Partially filled trim tanks are an exception but are actively monitored.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Submarine Special Tank Layout — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram presents a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** mapping the location, function, and hydraulic connections of all major special tanks in a typical submarine.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PH["PRESSURE HULL — INTERNAL TANKS"]
        direction TB
        FTT["FORWARD TRIM TANK\nVolume 80 m^3\nLever arm 25 m fwd of CF\nFunction: Bow-down adjustment"]
        ATT["AFT TRIM TANK\nVolume 50 m^3\nLever arm 20 m aft of CF\nFunction: Bow-up adjustment"]
        ABTF["AUX BALLAST TANK FWD\nVolume 40 m^3\nFunction: Fine buoyancy control"]
        ABTA["AUX BALLAST TANK AFT\nVolume 40 m^3\nFunction: Fine buoyancy control"]
        NT["NEGATIVE / HARD TANK\nVolume 60 m^3\nFunction: Always flooded at depth"]
        CT["COMPENSATION TANK\nVolume 30 m^3\nFunction: Replace fuel/LO mass"]
        FOT["FUEL OIL TANK\nVolume 200 m^3\nFunction: Diesel storage"]
        LOT["LUBE OIL TANK\nVolume 10 m^3\nFunction: LO storage"]
        FWT["FRESH WATER TANK\nVolume 25 m^3\nFunction: Potable water"]
        SWT["SEA WATER SERVICE\nVolume 50 m^3\nFunction: Cooling"]
        DT["DISTILLING TANK\nVolume 15 m^3\nFunction: FW production"]
        ST["SEWAGE TANK\nVolume 5 m^3\nFunction: Waste collection"]
    end

    subgraph EXTH["EXTERNAL FREE-FLOODING COMPARTMENTS"]
        FFT["FREE-FLOODING TANK\nin superstructure\nOpen to sea at all times"]
        SFFT["SAIL FREE-FLOOD\nGrating on sail planes"]
    end

    subgraph CTRLS["CONTROL SYSTEMS"]
        TP["TRIM PUMP\nTransfers water FTT to ATT"]
        BMP["BLAST PUMP\nFor emergency blow"]
        HPAC["HIGH PRESSURE AIR\nFor blowing NT and ABT"]
        CCC["TRIM CONTROL CONSOLE\nOperator interface"]
    end

    FTT <-->|"Trim water transfer"| TP
    ATT <-->|"Trim water transfer"| TP
    TP --- CCC
    NT --- HPAC
    ABTF --- HPAC
    ABTA --- HPAC
    HPAC --- BMP
    FOT -.->|"Fuel consumption"| CT
    LOT -.->|"Lubricant use"| CT
    CT --- CCC
    FWT --- CCC
    SWT --- CCC
    DT --- CCC
    ST --- CCC
    FFT -.->|"Hydrostatic equilibrium"| EXTH
    SFFT -.->|"Hydrostatic equilibrium"| EXTH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - **Solid arrows (→)** indicate hydraulic flow paths (water transfer via pumps).
> - **Dashed arrows (-.→)** indicate mass consumption paths (fuel being burned, water being used).
> - **Plain lines (---)** indicate control or monitoring links.
> - The Trim Pump can move water between FTT and ATT in either direction.

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Trim Operation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["OPERATOR INPUT\nDesired trim angle"] --> B["CALCULATE\nrequired ΔM"]
    B --> C["DETERMINE\nvolume to transfer"]
    C --> D["ACTIVATE\ntrim pump"]
    D --> E{"DIRECTION?"}
    E -->|"Bow down"| F["PUMP FTT → ATT"]
    E -->|"Bow up"| G["PUMP ATT → FTT"]
    F --> H["MONITOR\nactual trim"]
    G --> H
    H --> I{"TRIM\nACHIEVED?"}
    I -->|"No"| C
    I -->|"Yes"| J["STOP PUMP\nSecure system"]
```

## 4.3 Compensation Tank Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENS["SENSING LAYER"]
        L1["FUEL MASS SENSOR"]
        L2["TRIM SENSOR"]
        L3["DRAFT SENSOR"]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["CONTROL LAYER"]
        P1["PROCESS: Mass lost = ρ_fuel × ΔV"]
        P2["COMPUTE: V_sw = ΔM_fuel / ρ_sw"]
        P3["VALIDATE: tank not overfilled"]
    end

    subgraph ACT["ACTUATION LAYER"]
        V1["OPEN SEA INLET VALVE"]
        V2["ALIGN TO COMPENSATION TANK"]
        V3["CLOSE WHEN TARGET VOLUME REACHED"]
    end

    L1 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> V1
    V1 --> V2
    V2 --> V3
    L2 -.->|"Cross-check"| P3
    L3 -.->|"Cross-check"| P3
```
