# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between Main Ballast Tanks (MBT) and Special Tanks in a submarine. List any four types of special tanks with their functions.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Main Ballast Tanks (MBT) | Special Tanks |
|---|---|---|
| Primary function | Gross buoyancy change (surface ↔ submerged) | Fine adjustments and storage |
| Location | External to pressure hull (typically) | Internal to pressure hull |
| Operational state | Vented to sea surface when surfaced | Sealed; operated by pumps and valves |
| Volume contribution | 25–35% of total displacement | 8–12% of total displacement |
| Free-surface effect | Yes (when partially filled) | Minimized (kept full or empty) |
| Examples | Main ballast tanks (fore/aft) | Trim, ABT, Negative, Compensation, Service |

**Four types of special tanks:**
1. **Forward Trim Tank** — At 20–30 m forward of midships. Flooded to produce bow-down attitude; emptied to surface.
2. **Aft Trim Tank** — Symmetrically placed aft. Opposite function to forward trim tank.
3. **Compensation Tank** — Takes in seawater to replace consumed fuel/lubricant mass, maintaining constant displacement.
4. **Negative / Hard Tank** — Always fully flooded at depth, providing permanent negative buoyancy; blown only during emergency surfacing.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Explain the concept of "compensation" in a submarine. Why is it necessary, and what tank is used to achieve it?**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept:** Compensation is the active process of replacing the mass of consumable fluids (fuel, lube oil, fresh water) with seawater of equivalent mass, so that the submarine's total displacement and trim remain essentially constant throughout a submerged mission.

**Necessity:** As the propulsion plant consumes fuel, the submarine becomes progressively lighter. Without compensation, it would become increasingly buoyant and rise toward the surface, compromising depth control and stealth. Compensation maintains the boat at a constant depth and on an even keel without requiring large-scale MBT adjustments.

**Tank used:** The **Compensation Tank** is the dedicated special tank for this purpose. It has a sea inlet, vent, and pump. As fuel mass $M_f$ is consumed, an equal mass of seawater $\rho_{sw} \cdot V_{sw}$ is admitted, where $V_{sw} = M_f / \rho_{sw}$. This maintains the design submerged weight $W_{sub}$ at the constant value required for the operating depth.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A (Choice Option 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: (a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the location and function of the various special tanks in a submarine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A submarine of length between perpendiculars 95 m has a waterplane moment of inertia of 5,200 m⁴. The forward trim tank is located 28 m forward of the center of flotation and the aft trim tank is 22 m aft. The MT1cm of the boat is 5.5 kN·m/cm. Calculate the change in trim (in cm) when 25 m³ of seawater is transferred from the forward trim tank to the aft trim tank. Also state whether the submarine trims by the bow or by the stern. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a):**
*Sketch:* [Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram for the block-level architecture; in written exams, students should draw a longitudinal profile view of the submarine marking FTT, ATT, ABT, NT, CT, and service tanks with their approximate positions and capacities.]

*Functional description:*

1. **Forward Trim Tank** — Located 20–30 m forward of midships. By flooding or emptying this tank, the operator can adjust the longitudinal trim. Filling causes the bow to sink; emptying causes the bow to rise.
2. **Aft Trim Tank** — Located aft, mirrors the forward trim tank. The trim pump moves water between them.
3. **Auxiliary Ballast Tank (ABT)** — Small-volume tanks at the ends, used for fine buoyancy control when the MBT is in a partially flooded state.
4. **Negative Tank** — Always flooded at depth; provides constant negative buoyancy; can be blown for emergency surfacing.
5. **Compensation Tank** — Replaces mass of consumed fuel and lubricants.
6. **Service Tanks** — FO, LO, FW, SW, Distilling, Sewage — store essential consumables and provide buoyancy-stability balance.

**Part (b):**

Given:
- $L_{BP} = 95$ m
- $I_T = 5{,}200$ m⁴
- $x_F = -28$ m (forward, negative)
- $x_A = +22$ m (aft, positive)
- $MT1cm = 5.5$ kN·m/cm
- $V_t = 25$ m³
- $\gamma_{sw} = 10.045$ kN/m³

**Step 1: Weight transferred**

$$w = \gamma_{sw} \cdot V_t = 10.045 \times 25 = 251.125 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Lever arm difference**

$$\Delta x = x_A - x_F = 22 - (-28) = 50 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Trim moment produced**

$$\Delta M = w \cdot \Delta x = 251.125 \times 50 = 12{,}556.25 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 4: Trim change**

$$\Delta T = \frac{\Delta M}{MT1cm} = \frac{12{,}556.25}{5.5} = 2{,}283.0 \text{ cm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> As noted in the worked example, this simplified set of problem numbers yields an unrealistically large trim. In a clean KTU problem, the answer would be expressed in cm and should be in the order of 1–10 cm. Students are expected to present the final numerical value with the correct units and sign.

$$\Delta T \approx 2{,}283 \text{ cm} \quad \text{(using given problem values)}$$

**Step 5: Direction**

Since $\Delta M$ is positive (water moved from forward to aft), the boat trims **by the bow** (i.e., bow goes deeper, stern rises).

$$\boxed{\text{Trim change} = 2{,}283 \text{ cm bow-down (by the bow)}}$$

---

### Question B (Choice Option 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Derive the expression for the moment to change trim by 1 cm (MT1cm) for a freely floating submarine. State clearly all the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A nuclear submarine consumes 8 tonnes of fuel per day during submerged operations. The fuel has a density of 850 kg/m³. Calculate the volume of seawater that must be admitted to the compensation tank each day to maintain constant displacement. Also find the net change in buoyancy (in kN) due to this compensation, given $\rho_{sw} = 1{,}025$ kg/m³ and $\gamma_{sw} = 10.045$ kN/m³. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a):** Derivation of MT1cm

*Assumptions:*
1. The submarine is in static equilibrium.
2. Trim changes are small (small angle approximation valid).
3. The center of flotation does not shift appreciably during the small trim change.
4. Seawater density is constant.

*Derivation (already covered in Section 3.1):*

For a small trim angle $\theta$, the draft at station $x$ changes by $\Delta d(x) = x \cdot \theta$.

The change in displacement volume is:

$$\Delta \nabla = \int x \cdot \theta \cdot B(x) \, dx = \theta \cdot I_T$$

The corresponding change in buoyancy (or restoring moment about the center of flotation) is:

$$\Delta F \cdot L = \gamma_{sw} \cdot \theta \cdot I_T$$

If the trim is to change by 1 cm:

$$\theta = \frac{0.01}{L_{BP}}$$

Substituting:

$$MT1cm = \frac{\gamma_{sw} \cdot I_T \cdot 0.01}{L_{BP}} = \frac{\gamma_{sw} \cdot I_T}{100 \cdot L_{BP}}$$

**Part (b):**

Given:
- Fuel consumed per day: $\Delta m_{fuel} = 8{,}000$ kg/day
- $\rho_{fuel} = 850$ kg/m³
- $\rho_{sw} = 1{,}025$ kg/m³
- $\gamma_{sw} = 10.045$ kN/m³

**Step 1: Volume of fuel consumed per day**

$$V_{fuel} = \frac{\Delta m_{fuel}}{\rho_{fuel}} = \frac{8{,}000}{850} = 9.412 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 2: Mass of seawater to admit (for mass replacement)**

$$V_{sw} = \frac{\Delta m_{fuel}}{\rho_{sw}} = \frac{8{,}000}{1{,}025} = 7.805 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 3: Net change in buoyancy**

This is the key conceptual point. The boat has *lost* 8,000 kg of fuel (mass = 8,000 kg → weight = 80 kN). The compensation tank has *gained* 7.805 m³ of seawater (weight = $\gamma_{sw} \cdot V_{sw} = 10.045 \times 7.805 = 78.40$ kN).

The boat's total weight change per day is:

$$\Delta W_{net} = W_{fuel,lost} - W_{seawater,gained} = 80 - 78.40 = +1.60 \text{ kN}$$

The net change in buoyancy is therefore a **gain of 1.60 kN** (the boat becomes slightly more buoyant because the seawater replacing the fuel is denser, occupying less volume but mass-balanced).

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Student Error:** Many students incorrectly compute the net buoyancy change as $\rho_{sw} \cdot V_{sw} \cdot g = $ weight of seawater admitted. The *correct* interpretation is the **difference** between the lost weight of fuel and the gained weight of seawater, since both occur simultaneously.

$$\boxed{\text{Net buoyancy gain} = 1.60 \text{ kN/day}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign errors** in the lever arm — always assign $x_F$ as negative and $x_A$ as positive in the standard convention. Reversing signs changes the trim direction in the answer.
> 2. **Forgetting the MT1cm factor** — many students compute the trim moment but forget to divide by MT1cm to get the actual trim in cm. The MT1cm conversion is **mandatory** in KTU solutions.
> 3. **Confusing density and specific weight** — using $\rho_{sw}$ (1,025) instead of $\gamma_{sw}$ (10.045) gives a 10× error in the moment calculation.
> 4. **Skipping the sketch** in part (a) — KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks for missing diagrams even when the written answer is correct.
> 5. **Wrong units in the final answer** — KTU strictly requires kN·m for moments, cm for trim, and m³ for volumes. Mixing units loses 1 mark per affected step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Special Tanks (Module 2)**

- [ ] **Special Tanks ≠ MBT** — Special tanks are *internal, sealed, auxiliary* tanks for fine control. MBT are *external, gross* buoyancy tanks.
- [ ] **Seven core types** — Trim (Fwd/Aft), ABT, Negative, Compensation, Free-Flooding, Service (FO/LO/FW/SW/DT/Sewage).
- [ ] **Trim tanks** are always in pairs (forward and aft) and connected by a **trim pump** that transfers water.
- [ ] **MT1cm formula** = $\gamma_{sw} \cdot I_T / (100 \cdot L_{BP})$ — memorize it.
- [ ] **Trim moment** = $w \cdot \Delta x$ = weight transferred × arm difference.
- [ ] **Trim change** = Trim moment ÷ MT1cm.
- [ ] **Sign convention:** Bow-down = positive trim, Bow-up = negative trim.
- [ ] **Negative tanks** are *always full* at depth — they provide constant negative buoyancy.
- [ ] **Compensation tanks** maintain constant displacement as fuel is consumed.
- [ ] **Compensation volume** = Mass of fuel consumed / Seawater density.
- [ ] **Free-surface effect** in partially filled tanks is **destabilizing** (reduces $\overline{GM}$).
- [ ] **Free-flooding tanks** contain seawater in equilibrium with surroundings — no buoyancy effect, but contribute to mass distribution.
- [ ] **Standard values** to memorize: $\gamma_{sw} = 10.045$ kN/m³, $\rho_{sw} = 1{,}025$ kg/m³, $\rho_{fw} = 1{,}000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- [ ] **Trim change of 1 cm** implies an angular change of $\theta = 0.01 / L_{BP}$ radians.
- [ ] **Emergency surfacing** uses the high-pressure air blow system to empty negative and other special tanks simultaneously.
- [ ] **Density compensation** — when moving from ocean to less-dense estuary water, compensation tanks take in more water to maintain depth.
- [ ] **KTU exam focus areas** — Definitions, sketch + labels, MT1cm derivation, trim moment numerical, compensation tank sizing.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Submarine Hydrodynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST634 - SUBMARINES AND SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Submarine Hydrodynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Submarine Hydrodynamics** is the branch of marine engineering and naval architecture that studies the interaction between a fully submerged or surface-piercing submarine hull and the surrounding fluid medium (seawater). It encompasses the analytical, empirical, and computational investigation of forces, moments, and energy losses experienced by the vessel during steady-state and maneuvering operations.

The discipline is governed by the principles of **Conservation of Mass**, **Conservation of Momentum (Navier-Stokes equations)**, and **Conservation of Energy**, applied to a body of revolution moving through a stratified, viscous, and often cavitating flow field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Definition to memorize**
> Submarine hydrodynamics deals with the determination of resistance, propulsion, maneuverability, and stability of a submarine operating below, at, or near the free surface, where the governing dimensionless parameters are the **Reynolds Number ($Re$)**, **Froude Number ($Fr$)**, **Cavitation Number ($\sigma$)**, and **Depth Froude Number ($Fr_h$)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Underwater Airplane" Intuition

Imagine a sealed, pressurized metal cylinder (a submarine) traveling through honey instead of air. The honey is denser and stickier, so:
- The **skin** of the cylinder experiences **friction** as honey drags along its surface.
- The **shape** of the cylinder determines how much honey has to be pushed aside (pressure drag).
- If honey flows fast enough around the propeller or sail, the pressure may drop so low that dissolved gases form **bubbles** (cavitation).
- The cylinder, once moving, has an "invisible bubble" of honey that also has to be accelerated with it (added mass effect).

> **A submarine is essentially an underwater aircraft, but flying through a fluid ~**800 times denser** than air. This density amplifies all hydrodynamic effects by nearly three orders of magnitude compared to an equivalent airborne vehicle.**

## 1.3 Primary Forces & Moments Acting on a Submarine

| Symbol | Quantity | Direction | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $R_T$ | Total Resistance | Opposes forward motion | Determines power required |
| $F_L$ | Hull Lift (submerged) | Perpendicular to motion | Affects vertical trim |
| $M_{yaw}$ | Yawing Moment | About vertical axis | Affects course-keeping |
| $M_{pitch}$ | Pitching Moment | About transverse axis | Affects depth control |
| $R_{wave}$ | Wave-making Resistance | Opposes motion (surface) | Dominant near surface |

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Constants in Submarine Hydrodynamics**
> - Density of seawater: $\rho \approx \mathbf{1025\ kg/m^3}$
> - Kinematic viscosity of seawater: $\nu \approx \mathbf{1.35 \times 10^{-6}\ m^2/s}$
> - Gravitational acceleration: $g = \mathbf{9.81\ m/s^2}$
> - Speed of sound in seawater: $c \approx \mathbf{1500\ m/s}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Total Resistance

The total resistance experienced by a submarine arises from three fundamentally distinct physical mechanisms. Understanding their relative dominance at different operating conditions is essential for KTU problems.

### 2.1.1 Viscous (Frictional) Resistance — $R_V$

Originated by the **shearing action** of water along the wetted hull surface. Governed by the boundary layer physics.

$$R_V = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot S \cdot V^2 \cdot C_F$$

Where $S$ is the **wetted surface area** and $C_F$ is the **frictional drag coefficient** obtained from the **ITTC 1978 correlation**:

$$C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2}$$

### 2.1.2 Pressure (Form) Resistance — $R_P$

Caused by the **pressure differential** between the bow and stern due to flow separation and boundary layer thickening. For a streamlined body of revolution, form drag is much smaller than for a bluff body.

$$R_P = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot S \cdot V^2 \cdot C_P$$

### 2.1.3 Wave-Making Resistance — $R_W$

Only present when the submarine operates **at or near the free surface**. Generated by the energy radiated as **surface gravity waves** (Kelvin wake pattern). Disappears completely when deeply submerged.

$$R_W = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot S \cdot V^2 \cdot C_W$$

### 2.1.4 Total Resistance Decomposition

$$R_T = R_V + R_P + R_W + R_{appendage} + R_{excrescence}$$

## 2.2 Governing Dimensionless Parameters

### 2.2.1 Reynolds Number ($Re$)

Represents the **ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces** in the flow. For a submarine:

$$Re = \dfrac{V \cdot L}{\nu}$$

Typical values: $Re \approx \mathbf{10^8}$ to $\mathbf{10^9}$ for a fully submerged submarine at cruising speed.

### 2.2.2 Froude Number ($Fr$)

Represents the **ratio of inertial forces to gravitational forces** — governs wave-making.

$$Fr = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L}}$$

### 2.2.3 Depth Froude Number ($Fr_h$)

A unique parameter for submarines, comparing speed to the wave celerity at hull depth $h$:

$$Fr_h = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot h}}$$

> **Why this matters:** When $Fr_h > 1$, the submarine outruns its own wave system and surface effects are **dramatically reduced** — exploited in **super-cavitating** and high-speed submerged operations.

### 2.2.4 Cavitation Number ($\sigma$)

The most critical parameter for propeller and control-surface design:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{P_\infty - P_v}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2}$$

Where $P_\infty$ is the ambient pressure at depth and $P_v$ is the vapor pressure of seawater.

- $\sigma < \sigma_{critical}$: **Cavitation occurs** → performance drops, erosion, noise.
- $\sigma \to 0$: **Super-cavitation regime** → entire body is enveloped in a vapor bubble.

### 2.2.5 Added Mass (Virtual Mass) Coefficient — $C_a$

When a body accelerates through a fluid, an apparent **additional mass of fluid** is accelerated with it:

$$F_{added} = m_{added} \cdot \dfrac{dV}{dt}, \quad m_{added} = C_a \cdot m_{displaced}$$

For a **prolate ellipsoid** (idealized submarine hull) with axis ratio $a/b$:

$$C_a \approx 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{2 \cdot \rho_{body}}{\rho_{body} + \rho_{fluid}}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Compilation

| Quantity | Formula | Key Variables | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Total Resistance | $R_T = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho S V^2 C_T$ | $C_T = C_F + C_P + C_W$ | N |
| Frictional Coeff. (ITTC) | $C_F = 0.075 / (\log_{10} Re - 2)^2$ | Valid for $Re > 10^6$ | dimensionless |
| Reynolds Number | $Re = V L / \nu$ | $L$ = length, $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity | dimensionless |
| Froude Number | $Fr = V / \sqrt{g L}$ | $g = 9.81\ m/s^2$ | dimensionless |
| Depth Froude Number | $Fr_h = V / \sqrt{g h}$ | $h$ = depth below free surface | dimensionless |
| Cavitation Number | $\sigma = (P_\infty - P_v) / (\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2)$ | $P_v \approx 2.3\ kPa$ at 20°C | dimensionless |
| EHP (Effective Horsepower) | $EHP = R_T \cdot V$ | Power to overcome resistance | W |
| Wake Fraction | $w = (V_A - V) / V_A$ | $V_A$ = advance velocity | dimensionless |
| Thrust Deduction | $t = (T - R_T) / T$ | $T$ = thrust delivered | dimensionless |
| Propulsive Coeff. | $\eta_D = \dfrac{EHP}{DHP} = \dfrac{\eta_H \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_O}$ | Hull, Relative, Open-water | dimensionless |
| Added Mass (prolate) | $C_a = \alpha \cdot \dfrac{\rho_{body}}{\rho_{body} + \rho_{fluid}}$ | $\alpha \approx 0.5$ (sphere) | dimensionless |
| Critical Cavitation | $\sigma_c = f(\text{blade design}, Re)$ | Determined experimentally | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> - **Hull form optimization** uses Reynolds scaling laws to predict full-scale resistance from model tests.
> - **Cavitation control** dictates propeller tip-speed, blade-section selection (e.g., NACA 16, NACA 66).
> - **Added mass** is critical in emergency blow-up dynamics and vertical-plane autopilot design.
> - **Wake fraction** directly affects the shaft power required and propeller design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Resistance Coefficient from First Principles

The drag of a body moving through a fluid is fundamentally a momentum-deficit problem. We start from **d'Alembert's paradox** for inviscid flow (zero drag) and then incorporate viscosity and free-surface effects.

### Step 1 — Define the Reference Force Quantity

The dynamic pressure at the bow stagnation point of a body moving at speed $V$ is:

$$q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2$$

This is the **reference pressure scale** for all pressure-induced forces.

### Step 2 — Define the Reference Area

For a streamlined body of revolution (submarine hull), the wetted surface area is taken as the reference area:

$$S = \pi \cdot D \cdot L \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{L/D}{2 \cdot (C_p)}\right)$$

Where $D$ is the maximum hull diameter, $L$ is the length, and $C_p$ is the prismatic coefficient.

### Step 3 — Write the General Drag Equation

By **dimensional analysis** (Buckingham $\Pi$ theorem), any force on a body must scale as:

$$F = q_\infty \cdot S \cdot f(\text{geometry}, Re, Fr, \sigma)$$

For a submarine, this is decomposed into viscous, pressure, and wave components:

$$\boxed{R_T = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho S V^2 \left(C_F + C_P + C_W\right)}$$

### Step 4 — Extract the Frictional Component via ITTC Correlation

For a turbulent boundary layer (typical at submarine $Re$), the ITTC 1978 formula gives:

$$C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** As $Re$ increases, the boundary layer becomes thinner, surface friction per unit area decreases, and total $C_F$ falls — but $V^2$ rises faster, so the **total frictional force still grows with speed**.

### Step 5 — Compute the Form (Pressure) Component

The form factor $k$ accounts for pressure drag due to body shape:

$$C_P = k \cdot C_F$$

For a streamlined submarine hull, $k \approx 0.1$ to $0.2$. For a teardrop hull, $k$ can approach 0.05.

### Step 6 — Compute the Wave-Making Component

Using the **thin-ship theory (Michell integral)**:

$$R_W = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S \cdot C_W, \quad C_W = f(Fr, L/B, C_P)$$

For deeply submerged operation ($h / L > 2$), $C_W \to 0$ exponentially.

## 3.2 Derivation: Cavitation Number for Submarine Control Surfaces

Consider a control surface (rudder or sternplane) operating at depth $h$ with ambient pressure:

### Step 1 — Compute Ambient Pressure at Depth

Using hydrostatic pressure:

$$P_\infty = P_{atm} + \rho g h$$

For example, at $h = 100\ m$:

$$P_\infty = 101325 + 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 100 \approx 1.107 \times 10^6\ Pa$$

### Step 2 — Identify Vapor Pressure

For seawater at 20°C: $P_v \approx 2.34\ kPa = 2340\ Pa$.

### Step 3 — Apply the Cavitation Number Definition

$$\sigma = \dfrac{P_\infty - P_v}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2}$$

### Step 4 — Critical Cavitation Condition

Cavitation initiates when the local minimum pressure on the foil equals $P_v$. By Bernoulli:

$$P_{min} = P_\infty - \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 \cdot C_P^{foil}$$

Setting $P_{min} = P_v$:

$$P_\infty - \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_P^{foil} = P_v$$

### Step 5 — Solve for the Critical Speed

$$\boxed{V_{cav} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_\infty - P_v)}{\rho \cdot C_P^{foil}}}}$$

> **Insight:** A deeper-operating submarine can tolerate much higher speeds before cavitation on control surfaces becomes critical. This is one of the principal reasons high-speed operations are conducted at **periscope depth or deeper**, not at the surface.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Operating Conditions vs Dominant Resistance

| Operating Condition | Speed Regime | Dominant Resistance | Critical Parameter |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Deeply Submerged ($h/L > 2$) | Low Speed | Viscous ($R_V \approx 80\%$) | $Re$ |
| Deeply Submerged | High Speed | Viscous + Pressure | $Re$ + $C_P$ |
| Near Surface, $Fr < 0.4$ | Cruising | Wave-making dominant | $Fr$ |
| Surface, $Fr > 0.4$ | Planing | Hydrodynamic lift + wave | $Fr$ |
| Maneuvering (turning/diving) | All speeds | Viscous + inertial (added mass) | $C_a$, $Re$ |
| High-Speed (cavitating) | $V > V_{cav}$ | Viscous + cavity effects | $\sigma$ |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A submarine has $L = 80\ m$, $D = 8\ m$, cruising speed $V = 8\ m/s$ at depth $h = 50\ m$. Sea water: $\rho = 1025\ kg/m^3$, $\nu = 1.35 \times 10^{-6}\ m^2/s$. Estimate total resistance, given $C_F = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$, form factor $k = 0.15$, $C_W \approx 0.3 \times 10^{-3}$.

**Step 1 — Wetted Surface Area** (cylinder approximation):

$$S = \pi D L = \pi \cdot 8 \cdot 80 = 2010.6\ m^2$$

**Step 2 — Reynolds Number:**

$$Re = \dfrac{V L}{\nu} = \dfrac{8 \cdot 80}{1.35 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.74 \times 10^8$$

**Step 3 — Total Resistance Coefficient:**

$$C_T = C_F (1 + k) + C_W = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1.15 + 0.3 \times 10^{-3} = 1.725 \times 10^{-3} + 0.3 \times 10^{-3} = 2.025 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Total Resistance:**

$$R_T = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1025 \cdot 2010.6 \cdot 8^2 \cdot 2.025 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_T = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1025 \cdot 2010.6 \cdot 64 \cdot 2.025 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{R_T \approx 1.336 \times 10^5\ N \approx 133.6\ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Effective Horsepower:**

$$EHP = R_T \cdot V = 1.336 \times 10^5 \cdot 8 = 1.069 \times 10^6\ W \approx 1069\ kW$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hydrodynamic Force Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Submarine Hull in Motion] --> B[Hydrodynamic Interaction Zone]
    B --> C[Viscous Effects]
    B --> D[Pressure Effects]
    B --> E[Free-Surface Effects]
    B --> F[Cavitation Phenomena]

    C --> C1[Boundary Layer Formation]
    C --> C2[Skin Friction R_V]
    C1 --> C2

    D --> D1[Stagnation at Bow]
    D --> D2[Base Pressure at Stern]
    D --> D3[Form Drag R_P]
    D1 --> D3
    D2 --> D3

    E --> E1[Kelvin Wake Generation]
    E --> E2[Wave-Making R_W]
    E1 --> E2

    F --> F1[Local Pressure Drop]
    F --> F2[Vapor Bubble Formation]
    F --> F3[Super-Cavitation Regime]
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> F3

    C2 --> G[Total Resistance R_T]
    D3 --> G
    E2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Propulsive Power Required EHP]
    H --> I[Propulsion System Selection]
    I --> J[Propeller Design]
    I --> K[Pump Jet Alternative]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Resistance Build-Up as Function of Depth

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Operating Depth Selection] --> B{Depth h relative to L}
    B --> C[h < 0.5L Surface Zone]
    B --> D[0.5L < h < 2L Transition Zone]
    B --> E[h > 2L Fully Submerged Zone]

    C --> C1[Wave-Making R_W Dominant]
    C --> C2[Bow Wave + Stern Wave]
    C --> C3[High Radiated Noise]

    D --> D1[Mixed R_V and R_W]
    D --> D2[Interference Effects]
    D --> D3[Critical Speed Window]

    E --> E1[R_V Dominant Approximately 80 percent]
    E --> E2[Form Drag R_P Secondary]
    E --> E3[R_W Approaches Zero]
    E --> E4[Lowest Radiated Noise]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Cavitation Regimes on Control Surfaces

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cavitation Analysis] --> B[Compute sigma Number]
    B --> C{sigma relative to sigma critical}
    C --> D[sigma > 1.0 No Cavitation]
    C --> E[0.3 < sigma < 1.0 Sheet Cavitation]
    C --> F[0.1 < sigma < 0.3 Cloud Cavitation]
    C --> G[sigma < 0.1 Super-Cavitation]

    D --> D1[Full Hydrodynamic Efficiency]
    D --> D2[No Noise or Erosion]

    E --> E1[Partial Lift Loss]
    E --> E2[Tip Vortex Cavities]
    E --> E3[Acoustic Signature Increases]

    F --> F1[Significant Lift Oscillation]
    F --> F2[Blade Erosion Risk]
    F --> F3[Strong Acoustic Emission]

    G --> G1[Body Fully Enveloped in Vapor]
    G --> G2[Friction Reduced Dramatically]
    G --> G3[Specialty Hull Design Required]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Submarine Propulsive Efficiency Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Shaft Power DHP] --> B[Hull Efficiency eta H]
    B --> C[Effective Power EHP Available]
    A --> D[Propeller Open Water eta O]
    D --> E[Thrust Power]
    E --> F[Relative Rotative eta R]
    F --> G[Thrust Delivered]
    G --> H[Useful Work vs Total R_T]
    H --> I[Propulsive Coefficient eta D]

    C --> J[Required to Overcome R_T]
    J --> I
    I --> K[eta D equals eta H times eta R times eta O]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams in the Exam**
> - Use **Section 4.1** when a question asks *"List the components of total resistance."*
> - Use **Section 4.2** when a question asks *"Compare surface vs. submerged operation."*
> - Use **Section 4.3** when a question asks *"Explain cavitation regimes."*
> - Use **Section 4.4** when a question asks *"Define propulsive coefficient."*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**
> **Define the term "Cavitation Number" and state its significance in submarine hydrodynamics.**

**Model Answer:**
The cavitation number is a dimensionless parameter that compares the local pressure margin above vapor pressure to the dynamic pressure of the flow:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{P_\infty - P_v}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2}$$

**Significance:**
- $\sigma > 1$: No cavitation — full hydrodynamic efficiency retained.
- $\sigma < \sigma_{critical}$: Cavitation initiates → noise, erosion, and lift loss.
- It governs the **maximum permissible speed** of propellers and control surfaces at a given depth.
- For stealth operations, designers ensure $\sigma$ remains high to suppress acoustic signatures.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**
> **What is "added mass" in the context of submarine motion? Why is it important during emergency depth-change maneuvers?**

**Model Answer:**
Added mass (or virtual mass) is the **apparent additional mass of fluid** that must be accelerated along with the submarine hull when the body changes velocity. Mathematically:

$$F_{added} = C_a \cdot m_{displaced} \cdot \dfrac{dV}{dt}$$

**Importance during emergency maneuvers:**
- During a sudden **emergency blow-up** (rapid ascent), the hull accelerates vertically and must accelerate a large column of water with it.
- For a long slender hull, $C_a$ can be 50–100% of the displaced mass, effectively **doubling the inertial load** on the system.
- Autopilot and depth-control systems must incorporate added mass to avoid overshoot and instability.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model, Module 3]**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the total resistance of a fully submerged submarine, clearly identifying the viscous, pressure, and wave-making components. State the physical origin of each component. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A submarine of length $L = 90\ m$ and maximum diameter $D = 9\ m$ travels at $V = 10\ m/s$ in seawater ($\rho = 1025\ kg/m^3$, $\nu = 1.4 \times 10^{-6}\ m^2/s$). Given $C_F = 1.4 \times 10^{-3}$ and form factor $k = 0.18$, compute the total frictional-plus-pressure resistance. Take the wetted surface area as $S = \pi D L$. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

The total resistance on a body moving in a fluid is the sum of all pressure and shear forces integrated over the wetted surface, projected onto the direction of motion.

$$\boxed{R_T = R_V + R_P + R_W + R_{appendage} + R_{excrescence}}$$

| Component | Physical Origin | Magnitude (typical) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $R_V$ — Viscous/Frictional | Shear stress in turbulent boundary layer | Dominant at high $Re$ |
| $R_P$ — Pressure/Form | Pressure difference between bow and stern | $k \cdot R_V$ ($k \approx 0.1$–$0.2$) |
| $R_W$ — Wave-Making | Energy radiated as surface gravity waves | Vanishes at depth |

Each component scales as $\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S$ multiplied by an appropriate coefficient:

$$R_T = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S \left(C_F + k \cdot C_F + C_W\right)$$

For a deeply submerged submarine ($h/L > 2$), $C_W \to 0$ exponentially, leaving only viscous and pressure drag.

### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Step 1 — Wetted Surface Area:**

$$S = \pi D L = \pi \cdot 9 \cdot 90 = 2544.7\ m^2$$

**Step 2 — Reynolds Number:**

$$Re = \dfrac{V L}{\nu} = \dfrac{10 \cdot 90}{1.4 \times 10^{-6}} = 6.43 \times 10^8$$

**Step 3 — Total Coefficient (frictional + form):**

$$C_T = C_F (1 + k) = 1.4 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1.18 = 1.652 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Total Resistance:**

$$R_T = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1025 \cdot 2544.7 \cdot 10^2 \cdot 1.652 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_T = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1025 \cdot 2544.7 \cdot 100 \cdot 1.652 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{R_T \approx 2.155 \times 10^5\ N \approx 215.5\ kN}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice**

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of cavitation. Derive the expression for the critical cavitation speed of a submerged control surface operating at depth $h$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A submarine control surface is positioned at depth $h = 80\ m$ in seawater. The local pressure coefficient is $C_P = -0.8$. Estimate the critical speed at which cavitation will initiate. Take $P_{atm} = 101.3\ kPa$, $P_v = 2.3\ kPa$, $\rho = 1025\ kg/m^3$, $g = 9.81\ m/s^2$. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

Cavitation is the formation of vapor cavities in a liquid where local static pressure falls below the **vapor pressure** of the fluid. On a lifting surface (rudder, sternplane, propeller blade), the suction side experiences a pressure drop; if this drop exceeds atmospheric by enough, the pressure becomes negative in absolute terms and the water "boils" locally.

**Derivation:**

By Bernoulli's equation along a streamline reaching the suction peak:

$$P_{min} = P_\infty - \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_P$$

For cavitation inception: $P_{min} = P_v$:

$$P_\infty - \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 C_P = P_v$$

$$V_{cav} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_\infty - P_v)}{\rho \cdot \vert C_P \vert}}$$

With $P_\infty = P_{atm} + \rho g h$:

$$\boxed{V_{cav} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_{atm} + \rho g h - P_v)}{\rho \cdot \vert C_P \vert}}}$$

### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Step 1 — Compute Ambient Pressure at Depth:**

$$P_\infty = 101300 + 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 80 = 101300 + 804420 = 905720\ Pa$$

**Step 2 — Compute Pressure Differential:**

$$P_\infty - P_v = 905720 - 2300 = 903420\ Pa$$

**Step 3 — Apply Critical Speed Formula:**

$$V_{cav} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot 903420}{1025 \cdot 0.8}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1806840}{820}} = \sqrt{2203.46}$$

$$\boxed{V_{cav} \approx 46.94\ m/s \approx 91.2\ knots}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** The control surface will not cavitate at typical submarine speeds (~10 m/s), making it highly safe. Cavitation becomes a concern mainly on **propellers at high RPM** and on **bow planes during vertical maneuvers at high speed**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting $k$ (form factor)**: Many students compute only $C_F$ and miss the pressure drag. KTU examiners specifically allocate 2–3 marks for recognizing $R_P = k R_V$.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency in Reynolds number**: Using $L$ in cm or $\nu$ in $cSt$ without conversion is a common error.
> 3. **Confusing $C_P$ (pressure coefficient) with $C_P$ (prismatic coefficient)**: They are entirely different quantities! Pressure coefficient is negative on suction surfaces; prismatic coefficient is a hull-form descriptor (0.6–0.8 for submarines).
> 4. **Using $\rho = 1000\ kg/m^3$ (freshwater) instead of $1025\ kg/m^3$ (seawater)**: For naval problems, always use seawater density.
> 5. **Omitting vapor pressure**: Forgetting $P_v$ in the cavitation formula leads to inflated critical speeds and marks deducted.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Submarine Hydrodynamics — Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024)**

- **Total Resistance:** $R_T = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho S V^2 (C_F + C_P + C_W)$. For deeply submerged submarines, $C_W \to 0$.

- **ITTC 1978 Formula:** $C_F = 0.075 / (\log_{10} Re - 2)^2$ — memorize it.

- **Reynolds Number:** $Re = V L / \nu$ — governs viscous drag. Typical submarine $Re \sim 10^8$ to $10^9$.

- **Froude Number:** $Fr = V / \sqrt{g L}$ — governs wave-making. Submarines avoid high $Fr$ by submerging.

- **Depth Froude Number:** $Fr_h = V / \sqrt{g h}$ — unique to submarines. When $> 1$, the hull outruns its own wave system.

- **Cavitation Number:** $\sigma = (P_\infty - P_v) / (\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2)$. Critical for propellers and control surfaces. Always use $P_v \approx 2.3\ kPa$ for seawater.

- **Critical Cavitation Speed:** $V_{cav} = \sqrt{2(P_\infty - P_v)/(\rho \vert C_P \vert)}$. Increases with depth — deeper is faster-capable.

- **Added Mass:** Apparent extra inertia from fluid acceleration. $F_{added} = C_a \cdot m \cdot dV/dt$. Important for emergency depth change and autopilot design.

- **Form Factor ($k$):** Multiplier converting $C_F$ to $C_P$. $k = 0.1$–$0.2$ for streamlined submarine hulls.

- **Wetted Surface Area (cylinder):** $S = \pi D L$ — first-pass approximation; refined using $C_P$ corrections for end caps.

- **Hull Efficiency Chain:** $EHP = R_T \cdot V$; $DHP = EHP / \eta_D$ where $\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_O$.

- **Operating Regimes:** Surface (wave-dominated) → Snorkeling → Periscope depth → Deep submergence (viscous-dominated). Stealth increases with depth.

- **Key Constants:** $\rho_{sw} = 1025\ kg/m^3$, $\nu_{sw} = 1.35 \times 10^{-6}\ m^2/s$, $P_v = 2.3\ kPa$, $g = 9.81\ m/s^2$.

- **Exam Tip:** Always state the **physical origin** of every resistance component, not just the formula. KTU examiners reward conceptual clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST634 - SUBMARINES AND SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
In the context of **submarines and submersibles**, **Resistance (R_T)** is defined as the total hydrodynamic force component acting opposite to the direction of motion of the underwater vehicle, when it is moving through a fluid (typically seawater) at a steady velocity. Mathematically, it is the longitudinal component of the pressure and viscous forces exerted by the water on the entire wetted surface of the hull, appendages, sail planes, and free-flooding openings.

The total resistance of a submarine/submersible is conventionally split into physically meaningful components:

$$R_T = R_F + R_R + R_{APP} + R_B + R_{WA} + R_{FSH}$$

where each term represents a distinct physical phenomenon. For a *fully submerged* condition, the **wave-making resistance (R_W)** vanishes because there is no air-water interface for the pressure field to act upon, making the resistance problem fundamentally different from a surface ship.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The treatment of resistance for submarines must explicitly account for *fully submerged operation*, *free-flooding openings*, *appendage drag*, and the use of **ITTC 1978** / **Holtrop-style empirical correlations** scaled using **Reynolds Number similarity** (not Froude scaling, which is invalid without a free surface).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy
Imagine pushing a long, smooth wooden plank through a swimming pool:
- The **frictional resistance (R_F)** is the *sticky drag* of water sliding along the plank's wetted surface — like dragging a hand slowly through honey.
- The **form/pressure resistance (R_FP)** arises because water must *part around* and *close behind* the plank; if the plank has a blunt end, you feel a clear "push back" — this is shape-dependent.
- **Appendage resistance (R_APP)** is the extra drag of small control fins (like sail planes on a submarine) — small area, but they disturb the flow.
- **Free-flooding hole resistance (R_FSH)** is the drag created by the small holes (free-flooding ports) that let water in and out of external compartments (e.g., bow planes, missile tubes) — each hole acts like a tiny cup catching the flow.

> [!NOTE]
> For a *submerged* submarine, unlike a surface ship, **no waves are generated** at the hull (no free surface to disturb), so the problem reduces to a classical *incompressible viscous flow* around a body of revolution. This is why submerged resistance scales with **Reynolds number only**, not the Froude number.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics
- **Density of seawater:** $\rho \approx 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (at surface, $T = 15^\circ\text{C}$)
- **Kinematic viscosity:** $\nu \approx 1.19 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ (seawater, surface)
- **Dynamic viscosity:** $\mu \approx 1.38 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **1 metric HP (EHP unit):** $1 \text{ EHP} = 0.7355 \text{ kW}$
- **Standard reference area:** Wetted Surface Area $S$ in m²
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of Submarine Resistance — The "Why" and the "How"

**A. Frictional Resistance (R_F)**
- *Why it exists:* No-slip condition at the hull causes a velocity gradient (boundary layer) within a thin film of water; viscous shear stress integrated over the wetted area produces a tangential drag.
- *How to compute:* Uses the **ITTC 1978 frictional line** (an industry-standard empirical curve for turbulent flow over flat plates), scaled to the submarine's wetted surface.

**B. Residuary / Pressure Resistance (R_R)**
- *Why it exists:* Viscous normal stresses in the boundary layer cause early flow separation on blunt bodies → a low-pressure wake → net pressure drag.
- *How to compute:* Usually lumped together and obtained from model tests, then extrapolated via **Reynolds scaling** (since waves are absent when submerged).

**C. Appendage Resistance (R_APP)**
- *Why it exists:* Sail planes, stern planes, rudders, fairings, sonar domes all add wetted area and disrupt the boundary layer.
- *How to compute:* Estimated as a percentage of bare-hull resistance (typically **3% to 8%** depending on appendage size).

**D. Free-Flooding Hole Resistance (R_FSH)**
- *Why it exists:* Submarines have numerous small openings (free-flooding ports) that allow water to enter/leave external tanks. These act as *orifices* with discharge losses.
- *How to compute:* $R_{FSH} = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot C_{D,hole} \cdot A_{hole,total}$

**E. Wave Resistance (R_W)**
- *Why it exists for surface ships:* Energy radiated away as gravity waves. 
- *For fully submerged submarines:* **R_W ≈ 0** (no free surface) — this is the *single most important simplification* for submerged resistance prediction.

**F. Roughness Allowance (ΔC_F) and Bulwark / Bossing Resistance (R_B)**
- Standard ITTC allowance of $\Delta C_F = +0.0004$ added to friction coefficient to account for hull roughness, marine fouling, and paint degradation over a 3–5 year service life.

### 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below contains **every formula** you need for Module 3 problems. Memorize the *form* and the *unit*, not just the equation.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{V \cdot L}{\nu}$ | dimensionless; $L$ = length, $V$ = speed |
| 2 | Froude Number | $Fn = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L}}$ | negligible for fully submerged submarines |
| 3 | Frictional Coefficient (ITTC 1978) | $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2}$ | valid for $10^6 \le Re \le 10^9$ |
| 4 | Frictional Resistance | $R_F = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot S \cdot C_F$ | Newtons |
| 5 | Form Factor Method (Prohaska) | $C_T = K \cdot C_F + C_R$ | $K$ = form factor, $C_R$ = residuary |
| 6 | Total Resistance Coefficient | $C_T = \dfrac{R_T}{0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot S}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Effective Horsepower | $EHP = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V}{75}$ | metric HP ($1$ EHP $= 0.7355$ kW) |
| 8 | Reynolds Similarity Scaling | $\dfrac{R_{T,p}}{R_{T,m}} = \left(\dfrac{V_p}{V_m}\right)^2 \left(\dfrac{L_p}{L_m}\right)^2$ | only for submerged (no Froude) |
| 9 | Free-Flooding Hole Drag | $R_{FSH} = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot C_D \cdot A_{hole}$ | $C_D \approx 0.6$ to $1.2$ |
| 10 | Appendage Allowance | $R_{APP} = (0.03 \text{ to } 0.08) \cdot R_{bare\,hull}$ | empirical rule |
| 11 | Roughness Allowance | $\Delta C_F = +0.0004$ | ITTC standard for服役 coating |
| 12 | Delivered Horsepower | $DHP = \dfrac{EHP}{\eta_D \cdot \eta_S}$ | $\eta_D$ = propulsive, $\eta_S$ = shaft |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility
The resistance estimate is the **first step in the propulsion chain** for any underwater vehicle. It feeds directly into:
- **Battery / motor sizing** for an SSK (diesel-electric) or AIP-equipped boat.
- **Endurance and stealth envelope calculation** — slower speeds drastically reduce EHP, allowing longer submerged patrols.
- **Hull-form optimization** — minimizing $C_R$ while keeping $C_F$ low defines the optimum length-to-diameter ratio (typical submarines: $L/D \approx 7$ to $9$).
- **Sonar signature budgeting** — lower resistance ⇒ smaller propulsor ⇒ lower cavitation and radiated noise.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Resistance Force from Dimensional Analysis

The total resistance $R_T$ of a fully submerged body depends on: fluid density $\rho$, kinematic viscosity $\nu$, speed $V$, length $L$, and gravitational acceleration $g$.

$$R_T = f(\rho, V, L, \nu, g)$$

The 6 variables contain 3 fundamental dimensions (M, L, T), so by Buckingham's Pi theorem we have **3 dimensionless groups**:

$$\pi_1 = \frac{R_T}{\rho V^2 L^2}, \quad \pi_2 = \frac{V L}{\nu} = Re, \quad \pi_3 = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g L}} = Fn$$

**Step 1:** Write the functional dependence:
$$\frac{R_T}{\rho V^2 L^2} = f(Re, Fn)$$

**Step 2:** For a *fully submerged* body, the free surface is absent, so gravity cannot transmit wave energy away from the hull. Therefore $Fn$ drops out:
$$\frac{R_T}{\rho V^2 L^2} = f(Re)$$

**Step 3:** Defining the total resistance coefficient $C_T = \dfrac{R_T}{0.5 \rho V^2 S}$ (with $S$ being the wetted surface area, related to $L^2$ by shape):

$$C_T = f(Re) \quad \text{— Reynolds scaling law}$$

> This is the *theoretical foundation* of why model tests of submarines use **Reynolds scaling** rather than Froude scaling.

### 3.2 Derivation of Effective Horsepower (EHP) from First Principles

Effective Horsepower is the *rate at which resistance does work against the vehicle's motion*.

**Step 1:** Power = Force $\times$ Velocity:
$$P = R_T \cdot V$$

**Step 2:** In SI units, this yields Watts. To convert to metric horsepower, divide by $735.5$:
$$EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{735.5} \quad \text{[metric HP]}$$

**Step 3:** Since $1$ metric HP $= 75$ kgf·m/s (the older naval-architecture convention used in KTU textbooks):
$$EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{75} \quad \text{[metric HP, conventional]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU textbooks (Tupper, Rawson & Tupper) use the $75$ kgf·m/s convention. Always check whether your numerical answer should be in **kW or metric HP** based on the units of $R_T$ (N vs kgf).

### 3.3 Full Worked Example — Submerged Resistance Prediction

**Problem (KTU Board Style):**
> A submarine model of length $L_m = 5$ m and wetted area $S_m = 6.8$ m² is tested in a towing tank. The measured total resistance at $V_m = 3$ m/s is $R_{T,m} = 22.5$ N. The prototype submarine has $L_p = 60$ m, $S_p = 950$ m², and operates at $V_p = 7.5$ m/s. Seawater properties: $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.19 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s. Determine: (a) the form factor $K$, and (b) the prototype EHP. Assume $C_R$ is the same for model and prototype (Re-scaling).

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Form factor $K$:**

Model Reynolds number:
$$Re_m = \frac{V_m \cdot L_m}{\nu} = \frac{3 \times 5}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.26 \times 10^7$$

ITTC 1978 frictional coefficient:
$$C_{F,m} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(1.26 \times 10^7) - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(7.100 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(5.100)^2} = \frac{0.075}{26.01} = 2.884 \times 10^{-3}$$

Model total resistance coefficient:
$$C_{T,m} = \frac{R_{T,m}}{0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot V_m^2 \cdot S_m} = \frac{22.5}{0.5 \times 1025 \times 9 \times 6.8}$$

$$C_{T,m} = \frac{22.5}{31365} = 7.17 \times 10^{-4}$$

Using Prohaska decomposition $C_T = K \cdot C_F + C_R$, and assuming model and prototype share the same $C_R$ (so $C_{R,m} = C_{R,p}$):

For submarine hulls, a standard empirical starting estimate is $K = 0.18$. (This value is *assumed* and then validated against towing-tank data; here it is *given*.)

With $K = 0.18$:
$$K \cdot C_{F,m} = 0.18 \times 2.884 \times 10^{-3} = 5.19 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$C_{R,m} = C_{T,m} - K \cdot C_{F,m} = 7.17 \times 10^{-4} - 5.19 \times 10^{-4} = 1.98 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Part (b) — Prototype EHP:**

Prototype Reynolds number:
$$Re_p = \frac{V_p \cdot L_p}{\nu} = \frac{7.5 \times 60}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.78 \times 10^8$$

ITTC 1978 frictional coefficient for prototype:
$$C_{F,p} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(3.78 \times 10^8) - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(8.577 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(6.577)^2} = \frac{0.075}{43.26} = 1.733 \times 10^{-3}$$

Prototype total resistance coefficient (Prohaska form):
$$C_{T,p} = K \cdot C_{F,p} + C_{R,p} = 0.18 \times 1.733 \times 10^{-3} + 1.98 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$C_{T,p} = 3.12 \times 10^{-4} + 1.98 \times 10^{-4} = 5.10 \times 10^{-4}$$

Prototype total resistance:
$$R_{T,p} = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot V_p^2 \cdot S_p \cdot C_{T,p} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (7.5)^2 \times 950 \times 5.10 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$R_{T,p} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 56.25 \times 950 \times 5.10 \times 10^{-4} = 13{,}932 \text{ N}$$

Effective Horsepower:
$$EHP = \frac{R_{T,p} \cdot V_p}{75} = \frac{13{,}932 \times 7.5}{75} = 1393 \text{ metric HP}$$

Converting to kilowatts:
$$EHP = 1393 \times 0.7355 = 1025 \text{ kW} \approx 1.03 \text{ MW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Adding **appendage resistance** (+5%): $EHP_{total} = 1.03 \times 1.05 = 1.08$ MW — a realistic value for a medium-sized conventional submarine cruising at flank speed.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Operating Condition | Dominant Resistance | Scaling Law | Empirical Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fully submerged, cruising** | $R_F$ + $R_R$ (form) | Reynolds | ITTC 1978 + Prohaska |
| **Surfaced, calm water** | + $R_W$ (wave) | Froude | Holtrop-Mennen |
| **Surfaced, head seas** | + $R_A$ (added) | — | Sea-keeping codes |
| **High speed, near surface** | + $R_{WA}$ (wave + ventilation) | — | Special tunnel tests |
| **Submerged, with cavitation** | + cavitation drag | — | Cavitation tunnel |
| **Snorkelling depth** | partial free-surface | mixed | Special correlation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Components of Submerged Submarine Resistance

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional decomposition** of total resistance, since a literal hydrodynamic drawing is too complex for a text-based schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Resistance RT] --> B[Frictional Resistance RF]
    A --> C[Residuary / Form Resistance RR]
    A --> D[Appendage Resistance RAPP]
    A --> E[Free Flooding Hole Drag RFSH]
    A --> F[Roughness Allowance DeltaRF]
    A --> G[Wave Resistance RW = 0 SUBMERGED]

    B --> B1[Wetted Surface Area S]
    B --> B2[ITTC 1978 Cf Correlation]
    B --> B3[Re Number Scaling]

    C --> C1[Form Factor K]
    C --> C2[Prohaska Decomposition]
    C --> C3[Hull Slenderness L over D]

    D --> D1[Sail Planes]
    D --> D2[Stern Planes]
    D --> D3[Rudder]
    D --> D4[Sonar Dome Fairing]

    E --> E1[Bow Plane Holes]
    E --> E2[Free Flooding Ports]
    E --> E3[External Tank Vents]

    F --> F1[Paint Degradation]
    F --> F2[Marine Fouling]
    F --> F3[Delta Cf = 0.0004]

    G --> G1[NO Free Surface]
    G --> G2[Gravity Waves Absent]
    G --> G3[Froude Number Drops Out]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Resistance Prediction Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Define Geometry L B T S] --> S2[Step 2: Estimate Form Factor K]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Compute Re Number]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Look up Cf from ITTC 1978]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compute Frictional RF]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Add Residuary from Model Test]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Add Appendage 3 to 8 percent]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Add Free Flooding Hole Drag]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Apply Roughness Allowance]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Compute RT and EHP]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11: Compare with Sea Trial Data]
```

### 4.3 Submarine Resistance Profile vs. Speed (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Very Low Speed] -->|V2 Dominant| B[Friction RF dominant]
    B --> C[Mid Speed]
    C -->|Form Drag Rises| D[Form plus Friction Region]
    D --> E[High Speed]
    E -->|Appendage plus Cavitation| F[All Components Active]
    F --> G[Surfacing Region]
    G -->|Free Surface Reappears| H[Wave Making RW Becomes Significant]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** For a *fully submerged* submarine operating at typical patrol speeds (5–12 knots), only the **friction + form + appendage** blocks are active. The wave-making block activates **only on or near the surface**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]**
*Why does wave-making resistance vanish for a fully submerged submarine?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Wave-making resistance arises from the *continuous generation of gravity waves* at an air-water interface, where the pressure field of the moving hull does work against gravity to lift water. For a fully submerged submarine, the hull is *entirely below the free surface*, so there is no air-water interface for the pressure disturbance to radiate against. Without a free surface, gravity waves cannot form, and the energy that would otherwise become wave-making loss instead becomes a localized pressure disturbance that decays with distance via viscous dissipation. Mathematically, the Froude number $Fn = V / \sqrt{gL}$ drops out of the dimensionless resistance equation, leaving only the Reynolds number dependence. Hence $R_W \approx 0$ for a fully submerged vehicle. **[3 marks]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define the ITTC 1978 frictional resistance correlation. Why is it preferred over older correlations for submarine resistance estimation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **ITTC 1978 frictional correlation** is an empirical formula for the average frictional coefficient $C_F$ of a *turbulent flat plate*, given by:
$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2}$$
It is preferred because: (i) it is the **internationally agreed standard** of the International Towing Tank Conference, ensuring consistency between shipyards, navies, and research institutes; (ii) it is valid across a very wide range $10^6 \le Re \le 10^9$, covering both model tests and full-scale submarines; and (iii) it includes a built-in allowance for the *form roughness* typical of a sea-going hull, making it more accurate than the older Schoenherr or Prandtl–Schlichting correlations derived from laboratory-flat plates. **[3 marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — *Resistance Components & Empirical Prediction*

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]**
**(a)** List and briefly explain the **six components of total resistance** for a submarine, clearly stating which components vanish when the submarine is fully submerged. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A model submarine of length $L_m = 4$ m and wetted area $S_m = 5.2$ m² is tested at $V_m = 2.5$ m/s in fresh water ($\rho_m = 1000$ kg/m³, $\nu_m = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s). The measured resistance is $R_{T,m} = 12$ N. The prototype (seawater, $\rho_p = 1025$ kg/m³, $\nu_p = 1.19 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s) has $L_p = 50$ m, $S_p = 820$ m², and operates at $V_p = 6$ m/s. Assuming turbulent flow in both and using the ITTC 1978 line, compute the **prototype EHP in kW**, applying a 5% appendage allowance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**Step 1 — Model Reynolds Number:**
$$Re_m = \frac{V_m L_m}{\nu_m} = \frac{2.5 \times 4}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.0 \times 10^7$$

**Step 2 — Model Frictional Coefficient (ITTC 1978):**
$$C_{F,m} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(10^7) - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(7-2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{25} = 3.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Model Total Resistance Coefficient:**
$$C_{T,m} = \frac{R_{T,m}}{0.5 \rho_m V_m^2 S_m} = \frac{12}{0.5 \times 1000 \times 6.25 \times 5.2} = \frac{12}{16250} = 7.385 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — Use Prohaska form $C_T = K C_F + C_R$ with $K = 0.20$ (submarine estimate):**
$$K \cdot C_{F,m} = 0.20 \times 3.0 \times 10^{-3} = 6.0 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$C_{R,m} = 7.385 \times 10^{-4} - 6.0 \times 10^{-4} = 1.385 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 5 — Prototype Reynolds Number:**
$$Re_p = \frac{V_p L_p}{\nu_p} = \frac{6 \times 50}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.52 \times 10^8$$

**Step 6 — Prototype Frictional Coefficient:**
$$C_{F,p} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(2.52 \times 10^8) - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(8.401 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(6.401)^2} = \frac{0.075}{40.97} = 1.831 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 7 — Prototype Total Resistance Coefficient (same $C_R$ for both, Re-scaling):**
$$C_{T,p} = 0.20 \times 1.831 \times 10^{-3} + 1.385 \times 10^{-4} = 3.662 \times 10^{-4} + 1.385 \times 10^{-4} = 5.047 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 8 — Prototype Bare-Hull Resistance:**
$$R_{T,p} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 36 \times 820 \times 5.047 \times 10^{-4} = 7625.7 \text{ N}$$

**Step 9 — Add 5% Appendage Allowance:**
$$R_{T,total} = 7625.7 \times 1.05 = 8007 \text{ N}$$

**Step 10 — Effective Horsepower:**
$$EHP = \frac{R_{T,total} \cdot V_p}{75} = \frac{8007 \times 6}{75} = 640.6 \text{ metric HP}$$

**Step 11 — Convert to kW:**
$$EHP = 640.6 \times 0.7355 = 471 \text{ kW} \approx 0.47 \text{ MW}$$

> **Final Answer:** $EHP_{prototype} \approx 471$ kW. **[7 marks]**

---

#### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — *Reynolds Scaling & Appendage Drag*

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
**(a)** Starting from dimensional analysis, **derive** the Reynolds-number scaling law for the resistance of a fully submerged body, clearly showing why the Froude number drops out. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A submarine has 12 free-flooding holes, each of diameter $d = 0.08$ m, on its bow planes. When moving submerged at $V = 8$ m/s, estimate the **extra drag** due to these holes, taking $C_D = 1.0$ for sharp-edged orifices. Seawater $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**Step 1 — Total Hole Area:**
$$A_{total} = 12 \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = 12 \times \frac{\pi (0.08)^2}{4} = 12 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0603 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply the orifice drag equation:**
$$R_{FSH} = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot C_D \cdot A_{total}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**
$$R_{FSH} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (8)^2 \times 1.0 \times 0.0603$$

$$R_{FSH} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 64 \times 0.0603 = 1978 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Express as a percentage (for interpretation):**
Assuming a typical total bare-hull resistance of $R_T \approx 15{,}000$ N at this speed:
$$\text{Hole drag percentage} = \frac{1978}{15000} \times 100 \approx 13.2\%$$

> **Final Answer:** $R_{FSH} \approx 1.98$ kN, which is about **13%** of the bare-hull drag — non-negligible, illustrating why modern submarines minimize free-flooding openings. **[7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Do not apply Froude scaling to a fully submerged submarine.** Students frequently copy the surface-ship Froude scaling $R \propto L^3$ — this is **wrong** for submerged bodies. Use $R \propto L^2 \cdot V^2$ (Reynolds).
> 2. **Always state assumptions** — "fully submerged, turbulent flow, same fluid (or specify ρ, ν)".
> 3. **Unit conversion trap** — $EHP = R_T V / 75$ gives *metric HP*. To get kW, multiply by 0.7355. KTU often demands kW, but students forget to convert and lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Form factor K** must be a *given* or *measured* quantity in problems. If a problem does not provide K, use the standard submarine estimate $K = 0.18$ to $0.22$ and state the assumption.
> 5. **Roughness allowance** $\Delta C_F = +0.0004$ is **always** added for full-scale predictions; missing it costs 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Total resistance** $R_T$ for a submarine = $R_F + R_R + R_{APP} + R_{FSH} + \Delta R_{roughness}$
- ✅ **Wave resistance vanishes** when fully submerged — this is the *key difference* from surface ships
- ✅ **ITTC 1978** is the standard frictional correlation: $C_F = 0.075/(\log_{10} Re - 2)^2$
- ✅ **Re-scaling** is the correct scaling law: $R \propto V^2 L^2$ (for same fluid)
- ✅ **Form factor K** for submarines is typically **0.15 – 0.25**; Prohaska method extracts it from model tests
- ✅ **Appendage drag** is **3–8%** of bare-hull resistance — *always* account for sail planes, stern planes, rudders, fairings
- ✅ **Free-flooding hole drag** is computed as $0.5 \rho V^2 C_D A$ with $C_D \approx 0.6$ to $1.2$
- ✅ **Roughness allowance** $\Delta C_F = +0.0004$ is added at full scale
- ✅ **EHP** in metric HP $= R_T \cdot V / 75$; in kW $= R_T \cdot V / 1000$
- ✅ **DHP** $= EHP / (\eta_D \cdot \eta_S)$ where $\eta_D$ (propulsive) + $\eta_S$ (shaft) typically = 0.6 to 0.75
- ✅ **L/D ratio** of submarines is typically **7 to 9** — too low ⇒ form drag rises; too high ⇒ frictional drag dominates
- ✅ **Dimensional analysis** is the rigorous proof of why Froude number drops out for submerged bodies
- ✅ **Units check** — KTU problems always specify; verify $R_T$ in N, $V$ in m/s, $EHP$ in HP or kW
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Appendage Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST634 - SUBMARINES AND SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Appendage Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Appendage Resistance (R_A)** is defined as the additional viscous and form (pressure) resistance experienced by a submarine or underwater vehicle due to the flow disturbances and boundary-layer interactions caused by the various **appendages** — i.e., the externally protruding components mounted on, or extending from, the bare pressure hull. Mathematically, it is the net difference between the **total resistance of the complete submarine configuration** and the **resistance of the bare hull (body of revolution)** at the same speed, length, and Reynolds number.

$$
R_{\text{Total}} = R_0 + R_A + R_{I}
$$

where $R_0$ is the bare-hull frictional + form resistance, $R_A$ is the appendage resistance, and $R_I$ is the **interference resistance** arising from the mutual interaction between hull and appendage boundary layers.

### Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Insight)

Imagine walking through a swimming pool while holding a small rectangular **fin** in your outstretched hand. You will feel two things:
1. The water "gripping" your body (hull friction).
2. A noticeable **pulling force on the fin** as the water smashes into its leading edge and swirls off the trailing edge.

That extra pull on the fin is exactly what a submarine experiences from its sail, rudders, planes, and antennas. The hull is the swimmer; the fin is the appendage. Now imagine that fin is **near the swimmer's torso** — the swirls from the body hit the fin, *and* the fin's wake disturbs the body's flow. This mutual contamination is **interference**, and it is what makes appendage resistance in submarines much more troublesome than the sum of isolated parts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the revised NEP-2020-aligned syllabus for *Submarines and Submersibles*, appendage resistance is treated as a **mandatory topic** because naval architects often discover that **15 % – 30 %** of the total resistance of a fully-appended submarine comes from components that occupy less than **5 %** of the wetted surface.

### Classification of Submarine Appendages (KTU Taxonomy)

| Category | Examples | Typical Location |
|---|---|---|
| **Control Surfaces** | Stern planes, bow planes, fairwater planes (sail planes) | Hull exterior, leading & trailing edges |
| **Stability / Steering** | Rudders, X-stern cruciform surfaces | Aft section |
| **Superstructure** | Sail (conning tower / fairwater), masts | Upper hull, amidships |
| **Sensors & Domes** | Sonar domes, transducer fairings | Bow & belly |
| **Propulsion Support** | Shaft struts, bossing, Kort nozzles | Aft, around propeller |
| **Miscellaneous** | Drain plugs, limber holes (intermittent), degaussing coils | Distributed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used Throughout This Module**
> - Sea water density, $\rho \approx \mathbf{1025\ kg/m^3}$ (at standard surface conditions)
> - Kinematic viscosity, $\nu \approx \mathbf{1.19 \times 10^{-6}\ m^2/s}$
> - Standard gravitational acceleration, $g = \mathbf{9.806\ m/s^2}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Origin of Appendage Resistance

Appendages cause resistance through **three independent physical mechanisms**, all of which must be quantified for the KTU examination:

**(a) Skin-Friction Component**
The appendage has its own wetted surface $S_A$, over which a turbulent (sometimes partially laminar) boundary layer develops. The local skin-friction coefficient $C_f$ depends on the appendage's local Reynolds number and surface roughness.

**(b) Form (Pressure) Component**
A bluff or thick appendage (e.g., a sail or sonar dome) forces the streamlines to separate, creating a low-pressure wake and an asymmetric pressure distribution. This is dominant for **thick** bodies with thickness ratio $t/c > 0.20$.

**(c) Interference Component**
The boundary layer shed by the hull, plus any local cross-flow or strut effects, modifies the effective incidence and flow quality onto the appendage. This is *not* captured by analysing the appendage in isolation.

### 2.2 Hoerner's Classification (The Classic Naval-Architecture Approach)

Hoerner (1957) classified appendages into two extreme categories and proposed simple multiplier formulas that are still in KTU lecture notes and the **Principles of Naval Architecture (PNA)** reference used in Indian naval-academy curricula:

**Thick (Bluff) Appendages** — sails, sonar domes, fairings:

$$
R_{A,\text{thick}} = 1.5 \cdot \frac{S_A}{S_0} \cdot R_{f0}
$$

**Thin (Streamlined) Appendages** — control surfaces, rudders, shaft struts:

$$
R_{A,\text{thin}} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{S_A}{S_0} \cdot R_{f0}
$$

where $R_{f0}$ is the **frictional resistance of the bare hull** and $S_0$ is the bare-hull wetted surface.

### 2.3 General Drag-Coefficient Formulation (Most Useful for Numerical Problems)

The most flexible form, suitable for arbitrary appendage cross-sections, expresses the appendage resistance in terms of an **appendage-averaged drag coefficient** $\overline{C_{DA}}$:

$$
\boxed{\,R_A \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,S_A\,\overline{C_{DA}}\,}
$$

For a flat-plate-style appendage (rudders, planes) at small angle of attack $\alpha$, the drag coefficient combines frictional and form-drag contributions:

$$
\overline{C_{DA}} \;=\; 2\,\frac{C_f}{\beta}\,\left(\frac{S_w}{S_A}\right) \;+\; C_{D,\text{form}}(\alpha)
$$

where $\beta = \sqrt{1 - M^{2}\cos^{2}\alpha}$ is the **Prandtl–Glauert compressibility correction** (set $\beta=1$ for fully submerged low-speed operation), and $S_w$ is the appendage's flat-plate wetted area.

### 2.4 Interference Factor Method

For appendages mounted directly on or near the hull, the **interference factor** $K_I$ is introduced:

$$
\boxed{\,R_A^{\text{actual}} \;=\; K_I\,R_A^{\text{isolated}} \quad;\quad K_I \in [1.1,\; 2.0]\,}
$$

A widely-used empirical correlation is:

$$
K_I \;\approx\; 1.0 \;+\; 0.55\,\left(\frac{d_{\text{gap}}}{L_{\text{app}}}\right)^{-0.4}
$$

where $d_{\text{gap}}$ is the clearance between the appendage trailing edge and the hull shoulder, and $L_{\text{app}}$ is the appendage's streamwise chord.

### 2.5 Equivalent-Body-of-Revolution Method

Some KTU questions require the "revolve the appendage" trick: replace the 3-D appendage with an **axisymmetric body** of the same frontal area $A_f$ and length $L_A$, compute its resistance using standard body-of-revolution methods, and scale by an empirical factor.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_A = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S_A \overline{C_{DA}}$ | Appendage resistance (general) | All numerical problems |
| 2 | $R_{A,\text{thick}} = 1.5\,(S_A/S_0)\,R_{f0}$ | Bluff appendages (Hoerner) | Sail, sonar dome |
| 3 | $R_{A,\text{thin}} = 0.5\,(S_A/S_0)\,R_{f0}$ | Streamlined appendages (Hoerner) | Rudders, planes |
| 4 | $R_A^{\text{actual}} = K_I\,R_A^{\text{isolated}}$ | Interference correction | Near-hull mounting |
| 5 | $K_I \approx 1 + 0.55\,(d_{\text{gap}}/L_{\text{app}})^{-0.4}$ | Interference factor (corr.) | Sail-on-hull, fin-on-strut |
| 6 | $R_{f0} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S_0 C_{f0}$ | Bare-hull friction (ITTC-57) | Prerequisite for #2 & #3 |
| 7 | $C_{f0} = 0.075/(\log_{10}Re - 2)^{2}$ | ITTC-57 friction line | All submarine problems |
| 8 | $Re = VL/\nu$ | Reynolds number on length $L$ | Scaling & corr. |
| 9 | $\overline{C_{DA}}(\text{thin}) \approx 0.004$ | Avg. drag coeff. of thin planes | Quick estimates |
| 10 | $\overline{C_{DA}}(\text{thick}) \approx 0.12$ | Avg. drag coeff. of thick forms | Sail/dome problems |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Appendage-resistance predictions are used by **naval architects at the Naval Design Bureau (Visakhapatnam)** and at the **Directorate of Naval Design (DND)** to size the propulsion motor and battery bank of a submarine. A 1 % underestimate of $R_A$ on a 2,000-tonne nuclear submarine can translate to **~40 kW** of wasted shaft power at cruise speed, with serious consequences on the boat's mission endurance.

### 2.7 Why the Appendage Problem Is Hard (Conceptual Logic Flow)

1. **Hull and appendage boundary layers are coupled** — the velocity profile that the appendage "sees" is the *distorted* one shed by the hull, not a clean free-stream.
2. **Cross-flow on curved hull sections** — the local streamlines on a submarine's parallel mid-body are slightly angled to the free stream, so an appendage aligned with the hull axis is actually at a small effective angle of attack.
3. **Wake / shielding** — one appendage can sit in the wake of another, reducing or increasing the total drag non-linearly.
4. **Cavitation interaction** (at higher speeds) — the sail's leading edge can cavitate, which then *changes* its drag coefficient drastically.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hoerner's Thick-Appendage Formula from First Principles

Hoerner's reasoning begins with a dimensional observation: the **pressure drag** of a bluff appendage is roughly proportional to its **frontal area** $A_f$, while the **friction drag** of the bare hull is proportional to its **wetted area** $S_0$. The appendage's pressure-drag contribution, when expressed as an *equivalent* fraction of the hull friction, becomes a number $\varphi$ (the form factor multiplier).

**Step 1.** Express appendage pressure drag from a bluff-body drag relation:

$$
R_{A,p} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,A_f\,C_{D,\text{bluff}}
$$

**Step 2.** Express hull frictional resistance:

$$
R_{f0} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,S_0\,C_{f0}
$$

**Step 3.** Take the ratio to obtain a dimensionless coefficient:

$$
\frac{R_{A,p}}{R_{f0}} = \frac{A_f\,C_{D,\text{bluff}}}{S_0\,C_{f0}}
$$

**Step 4.** Empirical data for typical submarine appendages shows that the bracketed ratio is *approximately equal* to $1.5\,(S_A/S_0)$ for thick bodies and $0.5\,(S_A/S_0)$ for thin bodies, where $S_A$ is the *appendage wetted area*. This is the Hoerner calibration.

**Step 5.** Substituting back gives the textbook forms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{A,\text{thick}} &= 1.5\,\frac{S_A}{S_0}\,R_{f0} \quad\blacksquare\\[4pt]
R_{A,\text{thin}}  &= 0.5\,\frac{S_A}{S_0}\,R_{f0} \quad\blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Interference Factor from a Two-Stream Mixing Model

Consider the boundary-layer thickness $\delta_h$ shed by the hull at the location of the appendage leading edge. The appendage chord is $L_{\text{app}}$ and the gap clearance is $d_{\text{gap}}$.

**Step 1.** Define a non-dimensional clearance:

$$
\zeta \;=\; \frac{d_{\text{gap}}}{L_{\text{app}}}
$$

**Step 2.** The interference factor is taken as $K_I = 1 + \Delta K$, where $\Delta K$ represents the *extra* drag induced by the proximity. Empirical fits to a wide set of appendage geometries give:

$$
\Delta K \;\approx\; 0.55\,\zeta^{-0.4}
$$

**Step 3.** Therefore:

$$
K_I \;\approx\; 1.0 \;+\; 0.55\,\zeta^{-0.4} \quad\blacksquare
$$

**Physical interpretation:** as $\zeta \to \infty$ (appendage far from hull), $\Delta K \to 0$ and $K_I \to 1$ (isolated appendage). As $\zeta \to 0$ (appendage touching the hull), $K_I \to \infty$, which is why submarine designers always leave a small *shoulder fillet* or *fairing* at the root of every appendage.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard Problem)

> **Problem (KTU Style — 7 marks):**
> A submarine is cruising at $V = 8$ knots in sea water. The bare-hull wetted area is $S_0 = 1\,200\ \text{m}^{2}$, the bare-hull friction coefficient is $C_{f0} = 1.6 \times 10^{-3}$, and the total appendage wetted area is $S_A = 90\ \text{m}^{2}$ (composed of $60\ \text{m}^{2}$ of thin surfaces and $30\ \text{m}^{2}$ of thick surfaces). Compute:
> (a) The frictional resistance of the bare hull.
> (b) The appendage resistance, treating thick and thin appendages separately.
> (c) The interference-corrected total resistance for $K_I = 1.3$.

**Step (a) — Hull Friction**

Convert knots to SI: $V = 8 \times 0.5144 = 4.115\ \text{m/s}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{f0} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,S_0\,C_{f0}\\[4pt]
       &= \tfrac{1}{2}\,(1025)\,(4.115)^{2}\,(1200)\,(1.6 \times 10^{-3})\\[4pt]
       &= \tfrac{1}{2}\,(1025)\,(16.93)\,(1200)\,(1.6 \times 10^{-3})\\[4pt]
       &= 16\,648\ \text{N} \quad \approx \mathbf{16.65\ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (b) — Appendage Resistance (Hoerner's split)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{A,\text{thin}}  &= 0.5 \cdot \frac{60}{1200} \cdot 16\,648 = 0.5 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 16\,648 = 416.2\ \text{N}\\[4pt]
R_{A,\text{thick}} &= 1.5 \cdot \frac{30}{1200} \cdot 16\,648 = 1.5 \cdot 0.025 \cdot 16\,648 = 624.3\ \text{N}\\[4pt]
R_{A,\text{total}} &= 416.2 + 624.3 = \mathbf{1\,040.5\ N} \;\approx\; \mathbf{1.04\ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (c) — Interference-Corrected Total Resistance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{Total}} &= R_{f0} + K_I\,R_A\\[4pt]
                  &= 16\,648 \;+\; 1.3 \times 1\,040.5\\[4pt]
                  &= 16\,648 \;+\; 1\,352.6\\[4pt]
                  &= \mathbf{18\,000.6\ N} \;\approx\; \mathbf{18.00\ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification check:** $R_A / R_{f0} = 1040.5 / 16648 = 6.25\ \%$. This is consistent with the typical "5–10 %" rule for well-streamlined submarines. A poor design with $K_I = 1.8$ would push this to $\sim 8.5\ %$, costing the boat measurable mission endurance.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Formula Do I Use?"

| Situation in Problem Statement | Recommended Formula | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Wetted area $S_A$ of appendage given, "thick"/"bluff" mentioned | Hoerner thick: $1.5\,(S_A/S_0)\,R_{f0}$ | Standard bluff-body empirical |
| "Thin" or "aerofoil-like" appendage | Hoerner thin: $0.5\,(S_A/S_0)\,R_{f0}$ | Streamlined form drag negligible |
| Drag coefficient $\overline{C_{DA}}$ given | $R_A = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S_A \overline{C_{DA}}$ | Direct definition |
| Frontal area $A_f$ and $C_D$ given | $R_A = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} A_f C_D$ | Bluff body in cross-flow |
| Appendage "near the hull" or "interference" mentioned | Multiply by $K_I$ | Account for wake coupling |
| Multiple appendages listed separately | Sum individual $R_A$'s, then apply aggregate $K_I$ | Linear superposition valid in low-interference regime |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Appendage Resistance Sub-System

This block diagram shows how the total appendage resistance is decomposed and how each sub-component feeds the final prediction. It uses only **alphanumeric node identifiers** in compliance with Mermaid safety rules.

```mermaid

    B1 --> E2
```


### 4.3 Annotated Layout of a Submarine — Appendage Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SAIL_REGION
        node1[Mast_Antenna]

    end

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The bare pressure hull is the central reference body (node13). Every other block represents a separate appendage group whose combined resistance, plus their mutual interference, is what the rest of the module quantifies. The hull itself does **not** include the sail, planes, or rudders — those are the appendages whose resistance is the subject of this topic.

### 4.4 Schematic — Cross-Flow on an Appendage Near the Hull

