> 6. **Block diagram with no labels:** In Q(a) of 14-mark questions, an unlabelled diagram loses **2 full marks**. Always label each block.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 / Propulsion:**
> 
> - **3 propulsion families:** Diesel-Electric | Nuclear | AIP.
> - **AIP = 3 sub-types:** Stirling, PEMFC, MESMA — know one advantage of each.
> - **Key formulas (must memorize):**
>   - $EHP = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_T S V^3$
>   - $SHP = EHP / PC$
>   - $PC = EHP / SHP \approx 0.60 - 0.75$
>   - $\sigma = (p_0 - p_v) / (\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2)$
>   - $E_{batt} = P_{motor} \times t$
> - **Conversions:** 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s ; 1 HP = 745.7 W.
> - **Reactor flow path:** Core → Primary Loop → Steam Generator → HP Turbine → LP Turbine → Reduction Gear → Propeller.
> - **Shaft line order:** Motor → Damper → Compensator → Gearbox → Thrust Bearing → Stern Tube (Front Seal → Lignum Vitae → Rear Seal) → Tailshaft → Propeller.
> - **Endurance benchmarks:** Diesel-Electric submerged ≈ 1–2 days; +AIP ≈ 2–3 weeks; Nuclear ≈ months.
> - **Cavitation rule:** $\sigma \geq 0.6$ for safe operation. Deeper depth = higher $\sigma$ = safer propeller.
> - **Stealth:** Pump-jets reduce cavitation noise by ~10–15 dB vs. open propellers.
> - **Energy density recall:** Lead-acid ≈ 35 Wh/kg; Li-ion ≈ 240 Wh/kg.
> - **Submarine speed:** Diesel-electric submerged ≈ 6–8 knots; Nuclear ≈ 30+ knots.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamics and Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST634 - SUBMARINES AND SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dynamics and Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Dynamics and Control of Submarines

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Submarine Dynamics and Control** is the branch of marine engineering and naval architecture that deals with the mathematical modeling, analysis, and regulation of a submarine's motion in six degrees of freedom (6-DOF) under the influence of hydrodynamic, hydrostatic, propulsive, gravitational, and control forces and moments.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, **Dynamics and Control** specifically refers to:

1. **Kinematics & Kinetics** of submerged bodies moving through a fluid medium.
2. **Equations of motion** expressed in a body-fixed coordinate system.
3. **Stability analysis** (static and dynamic) along the longitudinal, transverse, and vertical axes.
4. **Control surface effectiveness** — how stern planes, bow planes, and rudders generate restoring and maneuvering moments.
5. **Closed-loop control architecture** for depth, heading (yaw), and trim regulation.

The submarine is governed by **Newton's second law** in translation (3 axes) and **Euler's rotational equations** (3 axes), coupled with the **Added Mass / Inertia** of the surrounding water.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Underwater Aircraft"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Think of a submarine as an *aircraft that flies underwater*. Instead of wings generating lift in air, the submarine's hull and control planes generate **lift and side force in water**. Just like a pilot trims pitch using elevators, the diving officer uses **stern planes** to control depth, and a rudder to control heading. The denser water (≈ **1000 kg/m³**) means forces are ~**800 times** larger than in air for the same speed, making control both more powerful *and* more sluggish due to added mass.

### Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Convention)

| Parameter | Typical Range | Symbol |
|-----------|---------------|--------|
| Submerged Speed | **8–35 knots** | $U$ |
| Operating Depth | **200–600 m** | $z$ |
| Displacement | **1,500–25,000 tonnes** | $\nabla$ |
| Control Plane Area | **8–20 m²** | $S_p$ |
| Plane Aspect Ratio | **1.5–3.0** | $AR$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Theoretical Framework & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Six Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF) — The Foundation

A rigid submarine possesses **six independent motions**, conventionally labelled as follows (per SNAME notation, 1950):

| DOF | Linear / Angular | Symbol | Body-axis Direction |
|-----|------------------|--------|---------------------|
| 1 | Surge (forward) | $u$ | $+x$ |
| 2 | Sway (sideways) | $v$ | $+y$ |
| 3 | Heave (vertical) | $w$ | $+z$ |
| 4 | Roll (about x) | $p$ | about $x$ |
| 5 | Pitch (about y) | $q$ | about $y$ |
| 6 | Yaw (about z) | $r$ | about $z$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Of the six motions, **depth** is governed by *heave* ($w$) coupled with *pitch* ($q$) via the stern planes. **Heading** is governed by *yaw* ($r$) and *sway* ($v$) via the *rudder*. These two coupled pairs are the **primary control loops**.

## 2.2 Coordinate Systems

Two reference frames are used:

- **Earth-fixed (inertial) frame** $O - X Y Z$ — for trajectory / position.
- **Body-fixed frame** $G - x y z$ — coincident with submarine's principal axes, origin at center of gravity.

The transformation between them uses **Euler angles** $\phi$ (roll), $\theta$ (pitch), $\psi$ (yaw).

## 2.3 General Equation of Motion (Newton–Euler Form)

Forces and moments are summed in the body-fixed frame:

$$\sum F_x = m(\dot{u} - vr + wq) - m x_G (q^2 + r^2) + m y_G (pq - \dot{r}) + m z_G (pr + \dot{q})$$

$$\sum F_y = m(\dot{v} - wp + ur) + m x_G (pq + \dot{r}) - m y_G (p^2 + r^2) + m z_G (qr - \dot{p})$$

$$\sum F_z = m(\dot{w} - uq + vp) - m x_G (pr - \dot{q}) + m y_G (qr + \dot{p}) - m z_G (q^2 + p^2)$$

## 2.4 Added Mass / Inertia Concept

Because the submarine accelerates through a fluid, a portion of the surrounding water **accelerates with it**. This appears as a virtual mass tensor:

$$\mathbf{M}_A = \begin{bmatrix} X_{\dot u} & 0 & 0 & 0 & X_{\dot q} & 0 \\ 0 & Y_{\dot v} & 0 & Y_{\dot p} & 0 & Y_{\dot r} \\ 0 & 0 & Z_{\dot w} & 0 & Z_{\dot q} & 0 \\ 0 & Y_{\dot p} & 0 & K_{\dot p} & 0 & K_{\dot r} \\ X_{\dot q} & 0 & Z_{\dot q} & 0 & M_{\dot q} & 0 \\ 0 & Y_{\dot r} & 0 & K_{\dot r} & 0 & N_{\dot r} \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **subscript-dot notation** $Y_{\dot v}$ denotes the partial derivative $\dfrac{\partial Y}{\partial \dot v}$ — i.e., the added mass in the $y$-direction due to acceleration in the $y$-direction.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation / Symbol | Physical Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|-------------------|------------------|--------------|
| 1 | $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S C_L$ | Plane lift force | N |
| 2 | $C_L = a_p \cdot \alpha_e$ | Lift coefficient (linear) | — |
| 3 | $a_p = \dfrac{\partial C_L}{\partial \alpha} = \dfrac{2\pi AR}{2 + \sqrt{AR^2+4}}$ | Plane lift slope | rad$^{-1}$ |
| 4 | $\alpha_e = \alpha - \dot{z}/U$ | Effective angle of attack | rad |
| 5 | $M_{\text{pitch}} = L \cdot x_p$ | Restoring moment about CG | N·m |
| 6 | $T^2 \ddot{\theta} + 2\zeta T \dot{\theta} + \theta = K \cdot \delta_s$ | 2nd-order pitch response | — |
| 7 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{K/T^2}$ | Natural frequency of pitch | rad/s |
| 8 | $G_{depth}(s) = \dfrac{K_d}{s(1+\tau_d s)}$ | Depth-control plant (approx.) | — |
| 9 | $C_{M_q} = -\dfrac{M_{q}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U L^2 S}$ | Pitch damping derivative | — |
| 10 | $X_{uu} = \dfrac{\partial X}{\partial u^2}$ | Surge resistance derivative | kg/m |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not write absolute values using `|` inside the table** (KTU 2024 markdown breakage pitfall). Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ as shown below when needed in examples.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

These equations are used by naval architects for:
- **Maneuvering simulation** (turning circle, Zig-Zag test).
- **Control system design** (autopilot tuning).
- **Safety certification** (per SUBSAFE / NATO STANAG 4153).
- **Crew training simulators**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Forces on a Stern Plane

Consider a stern plane of area $S_p$ located at distance $x_p$ *aft* of the center of gravity. The plane is set at an angle $\delta_s$ (positive = trailing edge down).

**Step 1 — Velocity at the plane:**

The submarine moves forward at speed $U$, heaves vertically at rate $\dot{z}$, and pitches at rate $q$. The total normal velocity at the plane is:

$$w_{\text{plane}} = -\dot{z} + U\theta - x_p q$$

**Step 2 — Geometric angle of attack:**

The freestream velocity vector at the plane forms an angle with the plane chord:

$$\alpha = \theta - \delta_s$$

**Step 3 — Effective angle of attack** (accounting for pitch rate):

$$\alpha_e = \alpha - \frac{x_p q}{U} = \theta - \delta_s - \frac{x_p q}{U}$$

**Step 4 — Lift force generated** (quasi-steady, linear):

$$L_p = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S_p \, a_p \, \alpha_e$$

**Step 5 — Moment about the CG** (lever arm $x_p$, negative because the plane is aft and produces nose-up moment when $\delta_s > 0$ is *trailing edge up*):

$$M_{\text{plane}} = -L_p \cdot x_p = -\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S_p \, a_p \, x_p \left(\theta - \delta_s - \frac{x_p q}{U}\right)$$

Expanding into the standard derivative form:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{plane}} &= -\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S_p \, a_p \, x_p \, \theta \\
&\quad + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S_p \, a_p \, x_p \, \delta_s \\
&\quad + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S_p \, a_p \, \frac{x_p^2}{U} \, q
\end{aligned}$$

Recognizing the standard hydrodynamic derivatives:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{q} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U \, S_p \, a_p \, x_p^2 \quad \text{(pitch-damping derivative)} \\
M_{\delta_s} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S_p \, a_p \, x_p \quad \text{(plane effectiveness)} \\
M_{\theta} &= -\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S_p \, a_p \, x_p \quad \text{(static stability derivative)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The *sign* of $M_{\theta}$ being negative implies that a positive (nose-up) pitch produces a *nose-down* restoring moment — this is the essence of **static longitudinal stability** in submarines.

## 3.2 Derivation: Second-Order Pitch Response

Linearising around an equilibrium straight-running condition and using small-angle assumptions, the heave–pitch coupled equations reduce to:

$$(I_y - M_{\dot q})\ddot{\theta} + (-M_q)\dot{\theta} + (-M_{\theta})\theta = M_{\delta_s}\,\delta_s$$

Dividing by the moment of inertia about $y$ yields the canonical form:

$$T^2 \ddot{\theta} + 2\zeta T \dot{\theta} + \theta = K \,\delta_s$$

where the equivalent parameters are:

$$T^2 = \frac{I_y - M_{\dot q}}{-M_{\theta}}, \qquad 2\zeta T = \frac{-M_q}{-M_{\theta}}, \qquad K = \frac{M_{\delta_s}}{-M_{\theta}}$$

The natural frequency and damping ratio are then:

$$\omega_n = \frac{1}{T} = \sqrt{\frac{-M_{\theta}}{I_y - M_{\dot q}}}$$

$$\zeta = \frac{-M_q}{2 \sqrt{(-M_{\theta})(I_y - M_{\dot q})}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Stability Criterion (KTU 2024):** For an *underwater* vehicle to be **dynamically stable in pitch**, the denominator must be positive, requiring $M_{\theta} < 0$. For submarines this is naturally satisfied by the hull and stern-plane combination — unlike surface ships, where free-surface effects complicate the picture.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:**
- $U = 7.7$ m/s (≈ 15 knots)
- $S_p = 12$ m²
- $x_p = 7$ m (aft of CG)
- $a_p = 2.4$ rad$^{-1}$
- $\delta_s = 0.10$ rad (≈ 5.7°)
- $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ (seawater)

**Find:** The steady-state pitch moment produced.

**Step 1 — Dynamic pressure:**

$$q_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(7.7)^2 = 30{,}388 \ \text{Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Plane effectiveness derivative:**

$$M_{\delta_s} = q_d \cdot S_p \cdot a_p \cdot x_p = (30{,}388)(12)(2.4)(7)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{\delta_s} &= 30{,}388 \times 12 \times 2.4 \times 7 \\
&= 30{,}388 \times 201.6 \\
&= 6.126 \times 10^{6} \ \text{N·m/rad}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Moment at $\delta_s = 0.10$ rad:**

$$M = M_{\delta_s} \cdot \delta_s = 6.126 \times 10^{6} \times 0.10 = 6.13 \times 10^{5} \ \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{M \approx 613 \ \text{kN·m}}$$

This moment would pitch a 6,000-tonne submarine at an initial angular acceleration of approximately $0.07 \ \text{rad/s}^2$ — small but sufficient over a few seconds to change depth by tens of metres.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Surface Ship vs Submarine Control

| Feature | Surface Ship | Submerged Submarine |
|---------|--------------|---------------------|
| Dominant restoring force | Gravitational (GM) | Hydrodynamic (stern planes) |
| Free-surface effect | Significant | **Negligible (submerged)** |
| Heave coupling | Decoupled from pitch | Strongly coupled |
| Roll control | Active fins + bilge keels | Minimal — usually uncontrolled |
| Time constant (depth change) | N/A | **30–90 seconds** |
| Primary depth controller | N/A | Stern / bow planes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Control Architecture

## 4.1 6-DOF Motion Visualization (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FORCES["External Forces and Moments"]
        FH["Hydrodynamic Force H"]
        FP["Propulsive Force X"]
        FG["Gravity G"]
        FB["Buoyancy B"]
        FC["Control Plane Forces Cp"]
    end

    subgraph KIN["Kinematic Coupling"]
        BODY["Submarine Body G"]
        SURGE["Surge u"]
        SWAY["Sway v"]
        HEAVE["Heave w"]
        ROLL["Roll p"]
        PITCH["Pitch q"]
        YAW["Yaw r"]
    end

    subgraph SENS["Sensors"]
        DEPTH["Depth Sensor z"]
        HDG["Heading Sensor psi"]
        VEL["Speed Sensor U"]
        RATE["Rate Gyros"]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["Control System"]
        COMP["Controller Ks"]
        ACT["Actuators Servo"]
        PLANES_OUT["Stern / Bow Planes delta"]
        RUDDER["Rudder delta_r"]
    end

    FORCES --> KIN
    KIN --> SENS
    SENS --> CTRL
    CTRL --> PLANES_OUT
    CTRL --> RUDDER
    PLANES_OUT --> FORCES
    RUDDER --> FORCES
```

## 4.2 Depth-Control Closed-Loop Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ZREF["Depth Reference z_ref"] --> SUM[("SUM JUNCTION")]
    ZACT["Actual Depth z"] --> SUM
    SUM --> ERROR["Error e_z"]
    ERROR --> PID["PID Controller Kp Ki Kd"]
    PID --> AMP["Amplifier and Servo"]
    AMP --> STERN["Stern Plane Actuator delta_s"]
    STERN --> HULL["Submarine Hull Dynamics"]
    HULL --> ZACT
    ZACT -. feedback .-> SUM
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Take-away:** The depth-control loop is fundamentally a **Type-1 system** (one integrator from the stern-plane deflection to depth). This means a *step change* in depth reference produces a *ramp* in steady-state error that goes to zero — i.e., zero steady-state error, but finite rise time. Proper tuning requires **phase margin ≥ 45°** to avoid oscillatory depth overshoot.

## 4.3 Heading (Yaw) Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PSIREF["Heading Reference psi_ref"] --> S1[("SUM")]
    PSIACT["Actual Yaw psi"] --> S1
    S1 --> ERR["Heading Error e_psi"]
    ERR --> RATE["Yaw Rate Filter"]
    RATE --> RUDDER_CTRL["Rudder Controller K_r"]
    RUDDER_CTRL --> SERVO["Hydraulic Servo"]
    SERVO --> RUDDER["Rudder delta_r"]
    RUDDER --> YAW_DYN["Yaw Dynamics Y_v Y_r N_r"]
    YAW_DYN --> PSIACT
```

## 4.4 Maneuvering Modes Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MODE{"Operating Mode"}
    MODE --> SURFACE["Surface Running - Stern Planes Free"]
    MODE --> PERISCOPE["Periscope Depth - Hull Near Free Surface"]
    MODE --> SUBMERGED["Fully Submerged - 6 DOF Active"]
    MODE --> EMERGENCY["Emergency Deep - High Speed Submerged"]

    SURFACE --> CTRL1["Control: Rudder Dominant"]
    PERISCOPE --> CTRL2["Control: Stern Planes Limited"]
    SUBMERGED --> CTRL3["Control: Stern + Rudder + Trim"]
    EMERGENCY --> CTRL4["Control: Full Plane Deflection Allowed"]

    CTRL1 --> HYD1["Wave Piercing Hydrodynamics"]
    CTRL2 --> HYD2["Squat and Ventilation Effects"]
    CTRL3 --> HYD3["Clean Submerged Flow"]
    CTRL4 --> HYD4["Cavitation Risk High"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Explain the concept of added mass in submarine dynamics and state why it is important for control design.**

**Model Answer:**
Added mass is the *virtual* inertia of the water that must be accelerated together with the submarine hull when the hull accelerates. Because water has density ≈ **1000 kg/m³**, the added mass for a streamlined body can be 20–80% of the hull's actual mass. It appears in the equations of motion as the $X_{\dot u}, Y_{\dot v}, Z_{\dot w}, K_{\dot p}, M_{\dot q}, N_{\dot r}$ terms. It is **important for control design** because:

- It increases the *effective inertia* → slows down all transient responses.
- It couples heave–pitch motions (via $Z_{\dot q}, M_{\dot w}$).
- It governs **damping and natural frequency** $\omega_n$ of depth/pitch loops.
- Mistaking it for hull mass leads to grossly mistuned autopilots.

$$\boxed{\text{Total effective inertia} = m + X_{\dot u}, \quad I_{y,\text{eff}} = I_y - M_{\dot q}}$$

---

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Distinguish between static and dynamic stability of a submarine in pitch.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Static Stability | Dynamic Stability |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|
| Definition | Initial tendency to return to equilibrium *without considering motion* | Tendency to return to equilibrium *via damped oscillation* |
| Math criterion | $M_{\theta} < 0$ | $\zeta > 0$ and $\omega_n > 0$ |
| Test method | Trim test at small angle | Step-input response of $\theta$ vs $t$ |
| Failure mode | Capsize / diverge monotonically | Sustained oscillation |

For a fully submerged submarine, static stability is provided by the **stern planes** acting through the metacenter-like lever arm $x_p$, and dynamic stability by **pitch damping** $M_q < 0$.

$$\boxed{\zeta = \frac{-M_q}{2\sqrt{(-M_\theta)(I_y - M_{\dot q})}}, \quad \text{stable if } \zeta > 0}$$

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU ESE - Module 3 Choice 1]`

**(a)** *Derive the equation of motion for the pitch of a fully submerged submarine, including the contributions of hydrostatic, hydrodynamic, and stern-plane forces. Clearly identify the stability derivatives. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** *A submarine operates at $U = 10$ m/s with stern plane area $S_p = 10$ m², located $x_p = 6$ m aft of CG. The lift slope of the plane is $a_p = 2.6$ rad$^{-1}$. If a step deflection $\delta_s = 0.15$ rad is applied, calculate (i) the steady-state moment, (ii) the pitch-damping derivative $M_q$ if the pitching-rate contribution is to be represented, and (iii) comment on whether the motion is statically stable. Take $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:** (outlines — full derivation per Section 3.1)

1. Resolve all forces in body axes; only the $z$-component lift on stern plane produces a pitch moment about $y$.
2. Express the lift as $L_p = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S_p \, a_p \, (\theta - \delta_s - x_p q / U)$.
3. The moment about CG is $M = -L_p x_p$, giving:

$$M = \underbrace{-\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S_p a_p x_p}_{M_\theta}\,\theta \;+\; \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S_p a_p \tfrac{x_p^2}{U}}_{M_q}\,q \;+\; \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S_p a_p x_p}_{M_{\delta_s}}\,\delta_s$$

4. The hydrodynamic stability derivatives are therefore:

$$M_\theta = -\tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S_p a_p x_p, \quad M_q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U S_p a_p x_p^2, \quad M_{\delta_s} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S_p a_p x_p$$

5. Static stability requires $M_\theta < 0$, satisfied when stern planes are *aft* of the CG.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Dynamic pressure:**

$$q_d = \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(10)^2 = 51{,}250 \ \text{Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Plane effectiveness:**

$$M_{\delta_s} = q_d \cdot S_p \cdot a_p \cdot x_p = (51{,}250)(10)(2.6)(6) = 7.995 \times 10^{6} \ \text{N·m/rad}$$

**Step 3 — (i) Steady-state moment at $\delta_s = 0.15$ rad:**

$$M = M_{\delta_s} \cdot \delta_s = 7.995 \times 10^{6} \times 0.15 = 1.199 \times 10^{6} \ \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{M \approx 1.20 \ \text{MN·m}}$$

**Step 4 — (ii) Pitch-damping derivative:**

$$M_q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U S_p a_p x_p^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(10)(10)(2.6)(36)$$

$$M_q = 5125 \times 936 = 4.797 \times 10^{6} \ \text{N·m·s/rad}$$

$$\boxed{M_q \approx 4.80 \ \text{MN·m·s/rad}}$$

**Step 5 — (iii) Stability comment:**

$M_\theta = -M_{\delta_s} = -7.995 \times 10^{6} \ \text{N·m/rad}$, which is **negative**. Therefore the submarine is **statically stable in pitch** at $U = 10$ m/s with these stern-plane dimensions.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU ESE - Module 3 Choice 2]`

**(a)** *Explain the depth-control problem of a submerged submarine using a block diagram. Discuss why the open-loop response is a *Type-1* integrator. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** *A submarine at 200 m depth is commanded to dive to 250 m. The depth plant is modelled as $G(s) = \dfrac{0.05}{s(1 + 8s)}$. Design a PI controller $C(s) = K_p + K_i/s$ such that the steady-state error to a step demand is zero and the closed-loop dominant poles have damping ratio $\zeta = 0.7$. Compute $K_p$ and $K_i$. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Depth-Control Explanation:**

The open-loop depth response has the form (linearised, small-angle):

$$G_{depth}(s) = \frac{K_d \, e^{-\tau s}}{s(1 + T s)}$$

The pole at the **origin** ($s=0$) arises because a *constant* stern-plane deflection integrates into a *constant rate of change of depth*. Mathematically:

$$\dot{z}(t) = K_d \cdot \delta_s \Rightarrow z(s) = \frac{K_d \cdot \Delta_s}{s^2}$$

Hence a step in $\delta_s$ gives a *ramp* in depth — characteristic of a **Type-1 system**. In closed loop, the integrator ensures **zero steady-state error** to a step demand in depth.

The block diagram is shown in **Section 4.2** of these notes.

**(b) PI Design:**

**Step 1 — Open-loop with PI:**

$$L(s) = \frac{(K_p s + K_i)}{s} \cdot \frac{0.05}{s(1 + 8s)} = \frac{0.05(K_p s + K_i)}{s^2(1 + 8s)}$$

**Step 2 — Characteristic equation:**

$$8s^3 + s^2 + 0.05 K_p s + 0.05 K_i = 0$$

**Step 3 — Specify desired dominant poles** ($\zeta = 0.7$ gives a characteristic $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2$). Choose $\omega_n = 0.3$ rad/s:

$$\text{Desired 2nd-order polynomial: } s^2 + 0.42 s + 0.09$$

**Step 4 — Pole-placement via coefficient matching:** divide characteristic equation by 8 to normalise:

$$s^3 + 0.125 s^2 + 0.00625 K_p s + 0.00625 K_i = 0$$

Match dominant poles by ensuring the remaining real pole is far left. Using dominant-pole approximation:

$$0.00625 K_p \approx 0.42 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_p \approx 67.2$$

For zero steady-state error, **any** non-zero $K_i$ works in Type-1; pick $K_i = 5$ for adequate integral action without instability.

**Step 5 — Verification:**

Closed-loop denominator $8s^3 + s^2 + 3.36 s + 0.25 = 0$. Routh-Hurwitz check:

- $s^3$: $8$
- $s^2$: $1$
- $s^1$: $\tfrac{1 \cdot 3.36 - 8 \cdot 0.25}{1} = 1.36 > 0$
- $s^0$: $0.25 > 0$

All first column entries positive → **closed-loop stable**.

$$\boxed{K_p \approx 67, \quad K_i \approx 5}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Sign confusion on $M_\theta$**: Forgetting that the stern plane being *aft* of the CG gives $M_\theta < 0$ (stable), not $> 0$. (-2 marks typical)
> 2. **Missing the added-mass terms** $X_{\dot u}, Z_{\dot w}, M_{\dot q}$ when writing equations of motion. (-1 to -2 marks)
> 3. **Mixing earth-fixed and body-fixed velocities** without proper Euler-angle transformation. (-2 marks)
> 4. **Forgetting $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³** for seawater — using 1000 gives wrong numerical answers. (-1 mark)
> 5. **Not stating the stability criterion** explicitly after a numerical problem — always end with *"Therefore $M_\theta < 0$, statically stable."* (-1 mark)
> 6. **Drawing block diagrams without arrows** or with wrong signal-flow direction. (-1 mark)
> 7. **Writing `|` inside tables** — this BREAKS KTU's online submission parser. Use `\vert` in LaTeX instead.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **6-DOF** motions: surge $u$, sway $v$, heave $w$, roll $p$, pitch $q$, yaw $r$.
- **Equations of motion** are written in *body-fixed* axes using Newton-Euler; cross-coupling terms ($vr, wq$, etc.) appear automatically.
- **Added mass tensor** $\mathbf{M}_A$ represents the inertia of entrained water; diagonal terms dominate ($X_{\dot u}, Y_{\dot v}, Z_{\dot w}$), off-diagonal terms are smaller but crucial for coupling.
- **Stern plane effectiveness derivative** $M_{\delta_s} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S_p a_p x_p$.
- **Pitch-damping derivative** $M_q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U S_p a_p x_p^2$.
- **Static stability** in pitch requires $M_\theta < 0$ ⇒ stern planes placed **aft** of CG.
- **Dynamic stability** requires both $M_\theta < 0$ and $M_q < 0$.
- **Depth control** is a Type-1 system → zero steady-state error to step demands.
- **Natural frequency** $\omega_n = \sqrt{(-M_\theta)/(I_y - M_{\dot q})}$ and **damping ratio** $\zeta = -M_q / [2\sqrt{(-M_\theta)(I_y - M_{\dot q})}]$ completely characterise the pitch response.
- **Heading (yaw)** control uses rudders; the rudder effectiveness is $N_{\delta_r} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^2 S_R a_R x_R$.
- **Coupling**: heave–pitch are coupled via $Z_q, M_w$; sway–yaw are coupled via $Y_r, N_v$.
- **Standard seawater density** to use in all numericals: $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ (not 1000).
- **KTU 2024 numerical units check**: $U$ in m/s, $S_p$ in m², $x_p$ in m, gives $M_{\delta_s}$ in **N·m/rad** and $M_q$ in **N·m·s/rad**.
- **Always close numerical answers with a stability statement** for full marks.
- **Block diagrams for control loops** must have clear arrows, summing junctions ($\Sigma$), and labelled signal lines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Revision Mnemonic — "SHEAR STAB":**
> **S**tern plane aft → **H**ydrodynamic damping → **E**quations of motion → **A**dded mass → **R**estoring moment.
> **S**tatic stability ($M_\theta < 0$) → **T**ype-1 depth plant → **A**ctuator limits → **B**lock-diagram closed loop.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equation of Motion of a Submarine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST634 - SUBMARINES AND SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Equation of Motion of a Submarine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
The **Equation of Motion of a Submarine** is a set of six coupled, non-linear differential equations derived from Newton's Second Law that mathematically describe the dynamic response of a fully submerged body in a fluid medium under the action of external forces (gravity, buoyancy, hydrodynamic, and propulsive forces) and moments. These equations govern the **translational** (surge, sway, heave) and **rotational** (roll, pitch, yaw) degrees of freedom of the submarine.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, this is treated using a body-fixed coordinate system aligned with the principal axes of the vessel, where the resultant force equals the rate of change of linear momentum, and the resultant moment equals the rate of change of angular momentum.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine pushing a **bottle** underwater in a swimming pool and then letting go.
1. The bottle has a **weight** pulling it down and a **buoyancy force** pushing it up.
2. As it moves, the **water resists** the motion (drag) — this is like trying to run in a pool.
3. The bottle also seems "heavier" when you try to accelerate it because it has to push water out of the way — this is **added mass**.
4. If the bottle has fins or a rudder, they redirect the water flow and steer it.

The Equation of Motion is simply the **mathematical balance sheet** of all these forces and moments for a submarine, telling us exactly how it will accelerate, decelerate, dive, surface, or turn.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A submarine has **6 Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF)** — 3 translations and 3 rotations. The Equation of Motion is the cornerstone for **stability analysis**, **Maneuvering**, and **control system design** (depth control, heading control).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Density of seawater:** $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (standard KTU value)
- **Density of freshwater:** $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Total displacement volume:** $\nabla$ (m³)
- **Displacement mass:** $m = \rho \nabla$ (kg)
- **Center of Gravity (G)** and **Center of Buoyancy (B)** are the two pivotal reference points.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF) Framework

A submarine moving in three-dimensional space can perform six independent motions. They are conventionally named as follows:

| Index | DOF Name | Linear/Angular | Axis of Motion | Positive Direction |
|:-----:|:--------:|:--------------:|:--------------:|:------------------:|
| 1 | **Surge** | Translation | $x$-axis | Forward (bow direction) |
| 2 | **Sway** | Translation | $y$-axis | Starboard (right) |
| 3 | **Heave** | Translation | $z$-axis | Downward (keel direction) |
| 4 | **Roll** | Rotation | About $x$-axis | Starboard down |
| 5 | **Pitch** | Rotation | About $y$-axis | Bow up |
| 6 | **Yaw** | Rotation | About $z$-axis | Bow to starboard |

### 2.2 Fundamental Forces and Moments

For a submarine, the external forces are categorized as:

1. **Gravitational Force ($W$):** Acts at the Center of Gravity (G), directed downward.
$$W = m \cdot g$$
2. **Buoyant Force ($B$):** Acts at the Center of Buoyancy (B), directed upward, equal to the weight of the displaced fluid (Archimedes' Principle).
$$B = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$$
3. **Hydrodynamic Forces ($X, Y, Z$):** Forces generated by fluid pressure and viscous shear on the hull. They comprise **damping** (proportional to velocity) and **restoring** terms.
4. **Propulsive Forces ($T$):** Thrust from propellers, acting along the surge axis.
5. **Control Forces:** Forces from stern planes, bow planes, and rudders.

### 2.3 Newton's Second Law in Body-Fixed Axes

The general vector form of the equation of motion is:
$$\sum \vec{F}_{ext} = m \cdot \vec{a}_G$$
$$\sum \vec{M}_{ext, G} = \frac{d}{dt}(\vec{H}_G) = I_G \cdot \vec{\alpha} + \vec{\omega} \times (I_G \cdot \vec{\omega})$$

For a submarine, the most important equations for KTU examinations are the **vertical plane (longitudinal)** equations: **Surge, Heave, and Pitch**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|:------:|:-------:|:------------:|
| $m$ | Mass of submarine (including added mass) | kg |
| $m_{11}, m_{22}, m_{33}$ | Added mass in surge, sway, heave | kg |
| $I_{xx}, I_{yy}, I_{zz}$ | Moments of inertia (with added moment of inertia) | kg·m² |
| $X, Y, Z$ | External force components in $x, y, z$ | N |
| $K, M, N$ | External moment components about $x, y, z$ axes | N·m |
| $u, v, w$ | Linear velocities (surge, sway, heave) | m/s |
| $p, q, r$ | Angular velocities (roll, pitch, yaw) | rad/s |
| $U$ | Total speed $\sqrt{u^2 + v^2 + w^2}$ | m/s |
| $\rho$ | Fluid density | kg/m³ |
| $L$ | Length of submarine | m |
| $\nabla$ | Displacement volume | m³ |
| $x_G, z_G$ | Center of gravity coordinates (relative to CB) | m |
| $W, B$ | Weight and Buoyancy | N |
| $X_{\dot{u}}, Z_{\dot{w}}$ | Added mass derivatives $\partial X/\partial \dot{u}$ | kg |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in KTU Exams:** Out of the 6-DOF, examiners predominantly test the **3-DOF vertical plane** (surge, heave, pitch) for a depth-changing maneuver and the **yaw equation** for a turning maneuver. Memorize these terms precisely.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: General 6-DOF Equation of Motion

We begin with Newton's Second Law for a rigid body in a body-fixed coordinate system $(x, y, z)$ that translates and rotates with the submarine. Let the origin $O$ be at the Center of Buoyancy $B$ (a common KTU convention for stability analysis).

**Linear Momentum Equation:**
$$\vec{F}_{ext} = m \left( \vec{a}_G + \vec{\dot{\omega}} \times \vec{r}_G + \vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_G) + 2\vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}_G \right)$$

**Angular Momentum Equation** (about the Center of Gravity $G$):
$$\vec{M}_{ext} = I_G \vec{\dot{\omega}} + \vec{\omega} \times (I_G \vec{\omega}) + m \vec{r}_G \times \vec{a}_G$$

where:
- $m$ = mass of the submarine,
- $\vec{r}_G$ = position vector of $G$ relative to the body-fixed origin,
- $I_G$ = inertia tensor of the rigid body about $G$,
- $\vec{\omega}$ = angular velocity vector $(p, q, r)^T$.

### 3.2 Derivation: The Three Vertical Plane Equations (Surge, Heave, Pitch)

For a submarine in the **vertical (x-z) plane**, the only non-zero motion variables are:
- $u$ (surge velocity),
- $w$ (heave velocity),
- $q$ (pitch rate),
- $z$ (depth/heave displacement),
- $\theta$ (pitch angle).

The external forces are $X$ (surge), $Z$ (heave), and $M$ (pitch moment). Expanding the general 6-DOF equations and including **hydrodynamic added mass** terms $X_{\dot{u}}, Z_{\dot{w}}, M_{\dot{q}}$ (which represent the inertial resistance of the fluid to acceleration):

**Step 1 — Surge Equation (X-direction):**
$$m(\dot{u} + qw - vr) = X$$

Since the body is in the $x$-$z$ plane, $v = r = 0$. Including damping $-X_u u$ and added mass $X_{\dot{u}} \dot{u}$:
$$(m + X_{\dot{u}})\dot{u} = X - m q w - X_u u - X_{\vert u \vert u} u \vert u \vert - X_{\delta_s}\delta_s - X_{prop}$$

**Step 2 — Heave Equation (Z-direction):**
$$m(\dot{w} - uq + vp) = Z$$

In the vertical plane, $v = p = 0$. With added mass $Z_{\dot{w}} \dot{w}$ and damping $-Z_w w$:
$$(m + Z_{\dot{w}})\dot{w} = Z + m u q - Z_w w - Z_{\vert w \vert w} w \vert w \vert - Z_{\delta_b}\delta_b - Z_{\delta_s}\delta_s$$

**Step 3 — Pitch Equation (about y-axis):**
$$I_{yy}\dot{q} + (I_{zz} - I_{xx})rp = M$$

In the vertical plane, $p = r = 0$. Adding added inertia $M_{\dot{q}}$:
$$(I_{yy} + M_{\dot{q}})\dot{q} = M - (I_{zz} - I_{xx}) \cdot 0 \cdot 0 - M_w w - M_{\dot{w}}\dot{w} - M_q q - M_{\theta}\theta - M_{\delta_b}\delta_b - M_{\delta_s}\delta_s$$

**Step 4 — Kinematic Equations** (relating velocities to displacements):
$$\dot{x} = u \cos\theta + w \sin\theta \approx u \quad (\text{for small } \theta)$$
$$\dot{z} = -u \sin\theta + w \cos\theta \approx w \quad (\text{for small } \theta)$$
$$\dot{\theta} = q$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Steady-State Equilibrium (Trim and Draft Condition)

In **static equilibrium** (submarine neither accelerating nor rotating), all time derivatives and velocities are zero:
$$\dot{u} = \dot{w} = \dot{q} = u = w = q = 0$$

The equations reduce to **static force/moment balance**:

**Force balance along x:**
$$X = 0 \implies T = R(u)$$

where $T$ is the propeller thrust and $R$ is the calm-water resistance.

**Force balance along z:**
$$Z = 0 \implies B = W$$

This is the **condition of floating equilibrium** — the submarine must displace a volume of water whose weight exactly equals its weight.

**Moment balance about y (Pitch/Trim):**
$$M = 0 \implies \overline{BG} \sin\theta = 0$$

For small angles, this implies $\theta = 0$ when $B$ and $G$ are vertically aligned — the **trim condition**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static vs. Dynamic Equilibrium:** In KTU problems, students often confuse static and dynamic conditions. A submarine in **steady forward motion** at constant depth is in **dynamic equilibrium** (forces balance, but $u \neq 0$). A submarine **hovering motionless** is in **static equilibrium**.

### 3.4 Linearized Equations for Small Disturbances

For control system design (e.g., autopilot), the non-linear equations are linearized about an equilibrium operating point using Taylor series expansion. The classic linearized vertical-plane equations of a submarine (depth-control problem) take the matrix form:

$$\begin{aligned}
(m - X_{\dot{u}})\dot{u} &= X_u u + X_w w + X_q q + X_{\delta_s}\delta_s \\
(m - Z_{\dot{w}})\dot{w} - m u_0 q &= Z_u u + Z_w w + (U_0 + Z_q) q + Z_{\delta_b}\delta_b + Z_{\delta_s}\delta_s \\
(I_{yy} - M_{\dot{q}})\dot{q} - m z_G u_0 q &= M_u u + M_w w + M_q q + M_{\delta_b}\delta_b + M_{\delta_s}\delta_s
\end{aligned}$$

The corresponding state-space form is $\dot{\vec{x}} = A\vec{x} + B\vec{u}$ where:
$$\vec{x} = [u, w, q, z, \theta]^T, \quad \vec{u} = [\delta_b, \delta_s]^T$$

This matrix is the foundation for **stability analysis** using Routh-Hurwitz criteria and **eigenvalue analysis** — favorite KTU exam topics.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: 6-DOF Equation of Motion Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Newton's 2nd Law] --> inputB[Body-Fixed Axes x y z]
    inputB --> linearSys[Translational EOM]
    inputB --> angularSys[Rotational EOM]
    linearSys --> surgeEq[Surge Equation: m u-dot = X]
    linearSys --> swayEq[Sway Equation: m v-dot = Y]
    linearSys --> heaveEq[Heave Equation: m w-dot = Z]
    angularSys --> rollEq[Roll Equation: Ixx p-dot = K]
    angularSys --> pitchEq[Pitch Equation: Iyy q-dot = M]
    angularSys --> yawEq[Yaw Equation: Izz r-dot = N]
    surgeEq --> f1[Forces: Weight, Buoyancy, Thrust, Drag]
    swayEq --> f1
    heaveEq --> f1
    rollEq --> f2[Moments: Hydrodynamic, Control, Restoring]
    pitchEq --> f2
    yawEq --> f2
    f1 --> f3[Added Mass and Damping Terms]
    f2 --> f3
    f3 --> outputA[Steady-State Equilibrium]
    f3 --> outputB[Linearized State-Space Model]
    outputA --> outputC[Stability and Control Design]
    outputB --> outputC
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Force Balance on a Submerged Submarine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Submarine Hull] --> B[Weight W acts at G downward]
    A --> C[Buoyancy B acts at B upward]
    A --> D[Thrust T acts along x-axis forward]
    A --> E[Drag Df acts along x-axis backward]
    A --> F[Stern Plane Force Fs]
    A --> G[Bow Plane Force Fb]
    A --> H[Rudder Force Fr]
    B --> I[Vertical Force Balance]
    C --> I
    I --> J{Static: B = W}
    D --> K[Longitudinal Force Balance]
    E --> K
    K --> L{Dynamic: T = Drag}
    F --> M[Pitch Moment]
    G --> M
    M --> N{Pitch Trim: M = 0}
    H --> O[Yaw Moment]
    O --> P{Yaw Trim: N = 0}
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Equation of Motion Block Reduction for KTU Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Full 6-DOF Nonlinear EOM] --> simplifyA{Simplification}
    simplifyA -->|Vertical Plane Only| v1[3-DOF: Surge, Heave, Pitch]
    simplifyA -->|Horizontal Plane Only| v2[3-DOF: Surge, Sway, Yaw]
    simplifyA -->|Static Equilibrium| v3[Trim and Draft Conditions]
    v1 --> linearA[Linearize about operating point]
    v2 --> linearA
    v3 --> designA[Stability Criterion]
    linearA --> designB[State-Space A B C D matrices]
    designB --> designC[Time Response and Eigenvalue Analysis]
    designA --> end[Hydrostatic Stability]
    designC --> end[Dynamic Maneuverability]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The first diagram shows how the fundamental law branches into 6 equations, the second shows the physical forces feeding into them, and the third shows the KTU-recommended path to reduce complexity for exam problems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*State the six degrees of freedom of a submarine in body-fixed coordinates. Define the terms "surge" and "pitch" with positive directions.*

**Model Answer:**
The six degrees of freedom (6-DOF) of a submarine are:
- **Surge** — Translation along the body's longitudinal ($x$) axis. Positive direction is toward the bow (forward).
- **Sway** — Translation along the transverse ($y$) axis. Positive direction is to starboard.
- **Heave** — Translation along the vertical ($z$) axis. Positive direction is downward.
- **Roll** — Rotation about the $x$-axis. Positive when starboard side goes down.
- **Pitch** — Rotation about the $y$-axis. Positive when the bow pitches up.
- **Yaw** — Rotation about the $z$-axis. Positive when the bow turns to starboard.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is meant by "added mass" in the context of a submarine's equation of motion? Why is it non-zero?*

**Model Answer:**
**Added mass** (or hydrodynamic added inertia) is the effective increase in the inertia of a body due to the surrounding fluid that must be accelerated along with the body. It is non-zero because when a submarine accelerates, it must push aside a portion of the surrounding water, which itself possesses inertia. This displaced water effectively adds to the submarine's mass for the purposes of acceleration. Mathematically, it appears as a coefficient (e.g., $X_{\dot{u}}$, $Z_{\dot{w}}$, $M_{\dot{q}}$) multiplied by the acceleration term. It is significant in submarine hydrodynamics because the density of water is comparable to the submarine's effective density.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Derive the equation of motion for a fully submerged submarine in the **surge direction**, clearly defining all terms. State the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A submarine of mass **2400 tonnes** is moving steadily at a forward speed of **8 knots** (1 knot = 0.5144 m/s). The total hydrodynamic drag at this speed is **$3.2 \times 10^5$ N**. The propeller thrust is **$3.5 \times 10^5$ N**. Determine the net accelerating force and the resulting surge acceleration. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of the Surge Equation:**

**Step 1:** Apply Newton's Second Law along the $x$-axis (surge direction) in a body-fixed coordinate system. The submarine's mass is $m$, surge velocity is $u$, and the angular velocities are $p, q, r$.

**Step 2:** The general translational equation is:
$$m(\dot{u} + qw - vr + x_G(q^2 + r^2) - z_G(qp + \dot{r})) = X$$

For a body symmetric in the $x$-$z$ plane, $v = 0$, $r = 0$, and the cross-coupling terms simplify.

**Step 3:** Substituting the external forces $X = T - R(u) + X_{control} + X_{hydrostatic}$:
$$m\dot{u} = T - R(u) - X_u u - X_{\vert u \vert u} u \vert u \vert + X_{control}$$

**Step 4:** Include the **added mass** effect $X_{\dot{u}}\dot{u}$, which represents the inertial resistance of water:
$$(m + X_{\dot{u}})\dot{u} = T - R(u) - X_u u - X_{\vert u \vert u} u \vert u \vert + X_{control}$$

where:
- $T$ = propeller thrust (N),
- $R(u)$ = calm-water resistance (N),
- $X_u, X_{\vert u \vert u}$ = linear and quadratic damping coefficients,
- $X_{control}$ = force from control surfaces.

**Assumptions:**
1. The submarine is fully submerged (no wave effects).
2. Fluid is incompressible and the flow is irrotational far from the body.
3. Earth-fixed frame is inertial.
4. The body is bilaterally symmetric.

**(b) Numerical Problem Solution:**

**Given:**
- $m = 2400 \text{ tonnes} = 2.4 \times 10^6 \text{ kg}$
- $U = 8 \text{ knots} = 8 \times 0.5144 = 4.1152 \text{ m/s}$
- $R(u) = 3.2 \times 10^5 \text{ N}$
- $T = 3.5 \times 10^5 \text{ N}$

**Step 1:** Net force in surge direction:
$$F_{net} = T - R(u) = 3.5 \times 10^5 - 3.2 \times 10^5 = 0.3 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 3.0 \times 10^4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2:** Since the problem does not specify added mass, we assume added mass is included in the given $m$ (or neglected as a simplification):
$$\dot{u} = \frac{F_{net}}{m} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^4}{2.4 \times 10^6} = 0.0125 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Answer:** Net surge force = **$3.0 \times 10^4$ N**, Surge acceleration = **0.0125 m/s²**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Sample Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the static equilibrium conditions for a fully submerged submarine. Derive the conditions of **floating equilibrium (B = W)** and **trim equilibrium** mathematically. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A submarine has a **displacement volume of 3000 m³** in seawater ($\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$). The total weight of the submarine is **$2.95 \times 10^7$ N**. The center of gravity is located **0.4 m below** the center of buoyancy along the vertical axis. Determine if the submarine is in static equilibrium. If not, what action must be taken to achieve equilibrium? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Static Equilibrium Derivation:**

For a submarine in static equilibrium, all time derivatives and velocities are zero. The 6-DOF equations reduce to:

**Force equilibrium:**
$$\sum F_x = 0 \implies T = R \quad \text{(if moving)}$$
$$\sum F_y = 0 \implies Y = 0$$
$$\sum F_z = 0 \implies B - W = 0 \implies B = W$$

**Moment equilibrium:**
$$\sum M_x = 0 \implies K = 0 \quad \text{(no roll)}$$
$$\sum M_y = 0 \implies \overline{BG} \sin\theta + \text{control moments} = 0$$
$$\sum M_z = 0 \implies N = 0 \quad \text{(no yaw)}$$

The **trim condition** is:
$$\overline{BG} \sin\theta = 0 \implies \theta = 0 \text{ (or } \overline{BG} = 0\text{)}$$

**Floating equilibrium** requires that the **buoyancy force equals the weight**, and **trim equilibrium** requires that the moments about the center of buoyancy are zero.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\nabla = 3000 \text{ m}^3$
- $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $W = 2.95 \times 10^7 \text{ N}$

**Step 1:** Calculate the buoyancy force:
$$B = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 3000 = 3.0166 \times 10^7 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2:** Compare with weight:
$$B - W = 3.0166 \times 10^7 - 2.95 \times 10^7 = 0.0666 \times 10^7 \text{ N} = 6.66 \times 10^5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3:** Since $B > W$, the submarine experiences a **net upward force of $6.66 \times 10^5$ N** and will **rise** (positive buoyancy).

**Step 4:** To achieve static equilibrium ($B = W$), the crew must either:
- **Pump water into the ballast tanks** to increase the weight, OR
- **Reduce displacement volume** by trimming a variable ballast.

The required additional weight is:
$$\Delta W = 6.66 \times 10^5 \text{ N} \quad \text{(i.e., } \Delta m = 6.79 \times 10^4 \text{ kg of seawater)}$$

**Answer:** The submarine is **NOT in equilibrium** (it has positive buoyancy of $6.66 \times 10^5$ N). It will rise. To restore equilibrium, **$6.79 \times 10^4$ kg of seawater must be taken in** through the ballast tanks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Blunder:** Many students mix up positive directions (e.g., heave is positive DOWNWARD by SNAME convention, but students assume UP). A single sign error loses 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting Added Mass:** In numerical problems involving acceleration, the question may provide $m$ that *includes* or *excludes* added mass. Read the problem statement carefully.
> 3. **Static vs. Dynamic Confusion:** "Steady forward motion at constant speed" is **dynamic equilibrium** ($T = R$, but $u \neq 0$), not static.
> 4. **Units:** Always convert knots to m/s, tonnes to kg, and use $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ consistently.
> 5. **6-DOF vs. 3-DOF:** KTU often asks for the vertical plane (3-DOF) or horizontal plane (3-DOF) — do not write all 6 equations when only 3 are required.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The Equation of Motion** is a direct application of **Newton's Second Law** for a rigid body in a fluid, written in body-fixed axes.
- **6 Degrees of Freedom**: Surge ($u$), Sway ($v$), Heave ($w$), Roll ($p$), Pitch ($q$), Yaw ($r$).
- **Three critical forces**: Weight ($W = mg$, down at G), Buoyancy ($B = \rho g \nabla$, up at B), and Hydrodynamic forces (drag + added mass + damping).
- **Added mass** ($X_{\dot{u}}, Z_{\dot{w}}, M_{\dot{q}}$, etc.) is the fluid's inertial resistance to acceleration — it is **non-zero** because of the high density of water.
- **Static equilibrium** requires **$B = W$** and **trim moment = 0**.
- **Dynamic equilibrium** (constant velocity) requires **$T = R$** (thrust equals drag) and zero net moment.
- **Damping forces** are typically modeled as linear ($X_u u$) and quadratic ($X_{\vert u \vert u} u \vert u \vert$) in velocity.
- The **linearized state-space form** $\dot{\vec{x}} = A\vec{x} + B\vec{u}$ is the gateway to **stability and control analysis**.
- The standard **seawater density** is **$\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$** and **$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$** — memorize these for KTU problems.
- For the **vertical plane** (most common KTU problem), only Surge, Heave, and Pitch equations are needed.
- For **maneuvering problems** (turning circle), the **Yaw equation** with rudder force is the focus.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stability and Control in the Horizontal Plane
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST634 - SUBMARINES AND SUBMERSIBLES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Stability and Control in the Horizontal Plane ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition **Stability and Control in the Horizontal Plane** refers to the submerged (or surface) maneuvering characteristics of a submarine in the **yaw plane**, i.e., its ability to maintain a steady course, resist unwanted heading deviations, and execute controlled turns about its vertical (yaw) axis $Z_b$. The horizontal plane involves three degrees of freedom: **surge** (forward motion along $X_b$), **sway** (lateral motion along $Y_b$), and **yaw** (rotation about $Z_b$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):** > *"Horizontal plane stability is the tendency of a submarine, when disturbed in heading, to develop restoring yawing moments and damping moments that bring it back to, or oscillate about, the original course. Horizontal plane control is the deliberate generation of yawing moments using control surfaces (rudders) to alter heading."* The key control surface in the horizontal plane is the **stern rudder** (and optionally the **bow planes in X-rudder configuration** or **fairwater planes** for combined depth/yaw control). The governing coordinate system is the **body-fixed reference frame** $X_b Y_b Z_b$ with origin at the center of gravity. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: "The Shopping Cart on a Slippery Floor"** > > Imagine pushing a loaded shopping cart in a straight line. If you momentarily let go of one handle (a disturbance), the cart will tend to either: > - (a) Self-correct and continue straight (**stable**), > - (b) Wander off in a curve without correction (**unstable**), or > - (c) Oscillate side-to-side and slowly settle (**damped stable**). > > A submarine in the horizontal plane behaves similarly. The **hull's asymmetric pressure distribution** (since the rudder is behind the CG) provides the self-correcting "spring" force, while **water resistance to angular motion** acts as the "shock absorber" (damper). The **rudder** is simply the steering wheel — turn it, and the water pushes the stern sideways, swinging the bow. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Sea water density:** $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (standard oceanographic value). - **Kinematic viscosity of sea water:** $\nu \approx 1.19 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. - **Yaw rate standard notation:** $r = \dot{\psi}$ in rad/s. - **Metacentric height is irrelevant in horizontal plane** (it is a transverse-plane quantity, $GM_T$). - **Submarine speed range:** $U = 4$ to $25$ knots ($2.06$ to $12.86$ m/s). - **Stern rudder area ratio:** typically $A_R / L^2 \approx 1/50$ to $1/100$ where $L$ is submarine length. - **Weather helm / Lee helm angle:** typically $\pm 1°$ to $\pm 3°$ in well-designed submarines. ### 1.4 Why Horizontal Plane is Treated Separately > [!IMPORTANT] > In KTU Board questions, you are expected to clearly state: *"The horizontal plane problem is decoupled from the vertical plane because the cross-coupling terms (e.g., yaw–pitch coupling through $M_{rv}$, $N_{rq}$) are an order of magnitude smaller than direct terms, due to the slender body geometry (length-to-diameter ratio $L/D \approx 7$ to $10$ for submarines)."* This decoupling allows independent analysis of: - **Vertical plane** (depth, pitch): covered in Module 4. - **Horizontal plane** (heading, yaw): covered in this topic. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Coordinate System & Degrees of Freedom A submarine moving in the horizontal plane has **3 DOF**: | DOF | Symbol | Axis | Physical Meaning | |-----|--------|------|------------------| | Surge | $u$ | $X_b$ | Forward translational velocity | | Sway | $v$ | $Y_b$ | Lateral translational velocity | | Yaw | $r$ | $Z_b$ | Angular velocity about vertical axis | Body-fixed velocities: $U_0$ (forward), $v$ (sway), $r$ (yaw rate). Heading angle $\psi$ satisfies $\dot{\psi} = r \sec\theta$ (for small $\theta$, $\dot{\psi} \approx r$). ### 2.2 Forces and Moments in the Horizontal Plane The forces and moments acting on a submarine in the horizontal plane arise from: 1. **Hull hydrodynamic forces** — pressure and viscous drag on the cylindrical body. 2. **Appendage forces** — stern rudder, fairwater planes (in X-config), sail planes. 3. **Propeller forces** — thrust $T$ along $X_b$, lateral force (usually small). 4. **Damping forces** — resistance to sway velocity $v$ and yaw rate $r$. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL: All equations below are standard KTU Board-tested formulas. Memorize the symbols and signs.** | # | Equation | Description | Units | |---|----------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $\sum F_Y = m(\dot{v} + U_0 r)$ | Sway force equilibrium | N | | 2 | $\sum N = I_{zz}\dot{r}$ | Yaw moment equilibrium | N·m | | 3 | $Y_v = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^2 Y_{\dot{v}}' \cdot \dot{v}$ term linearized | Sway force per sway accel | N/(m/s) | | 4 | $N_r = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^4 N_r' \cdot r$ | Yaw damping moment coefficient | N·m·s/rad | | 5 | $Y_{\delta_r} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^2 Y_{\delta_r}' \cdot \delta_r$ | Rudder sway force | N/rad | | 6 | $N_{\delta_r} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^3 N_{\delta_r}' \cdot \delta_r$ | Rudder yawing moment | N·m/rad | | 7 | $K = T / ( \sqrt{2\rho A_R \cdot C_L^{\alpha} \cdot U_0^2 \cdot \cos\delta_r}) $ | Rudder effectiveness factor | — | | 8 | $L_{rudder} = C_L \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_R^2 A_R$ | Lift on rudder | N | | 9 | $D / L = \text{Tactical Diameter} / U_0$ | Non-dimensional turning index | — | | 10 | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{I_{zz} / N_r}$ | Natural yaw period (without control) | s | | 11 | $N_{\text{weather}} = N_{\delta_r} \cdot \delta_{helm}$ | Restoring moment from helm | N·m | | 12 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{-N_r / I_{zz}}$ approx. | Yaw natural frequency (stat. unstable) | rad/s | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sign Convention (KTU 2024 Standard):** > - Positive rudder angle $\delta_r$ → **starboard** rudder (pushes stern to port, bow to starboard) → positive yaw rate $r$. > - Positive yaw moment $N$ → turns bow to starboard. > - Positive $N_r$ coefficient → stabilizing damping (resists yaw rate). > - Positive $N_{\delta_r}$ coefficient → **destabilizing** (rudder is *behind* CG, so deflection creates moment that *amplifies* heading change in a restoring sense only if sign is right). ### 2.4 Stability Criteria — Static and Dynamic #### 2.4.1 Static Directional Stability A submarine is **statically stable in heading** if, when given a small yaw disturbance $\Delta\psi$, it generates a **restoring yawing moment** $N_{restore}$ that opposes the disturbance. $$ N_{restore} = -K_h \cdot \Delta\psi $$ where $K_h > 0$ is the **weather-helm stiffness** (also called $N_{\psi}$ or $C_h$). - If $K_h > 0$ → **Weather helm** (positive): must hold rudder to leeward to maintain straight course. *Statically stable.* - If $K_h < 0$ → **Lee helm** (negative): must hold rudder to windward. *Statically unstable.* - If $K_h = 0$ → Neutral. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Origin of Weather Helm:** > When the submarine yaws by a small angle $\Delta\psi$ from its heading, the hull is no longer aligned with the flow. The asymmetric pressure on the **aft body** (behind CG) creates a moment that pushes the stern back into alignment, restoring the original course. This is why all submarines are designed with **weather helm** (positive static stability). #### 2.4.2 Dynamic Directional Stability A submarine is **dynamically stable** if, after a heading disturbance, the yaw rate $r(t)$ and heading error $\Delta\psi(t)$ decay to zero without sustained oscillation or divergence. The linearized yaw equation is: $$ I_{zz}\dot{r} + N_r r + N_{\dot{r}} \dot{r} + N_{\psi} \psi = N_{\delta_r} \delta_r $$ For natural response (no input, $\delta_r = 0$), the characteristic equation is: $$ (I_{zz} + N_{\dot{r}})s^2 + N_r s + N_{\psi} = 0 $$ Stability requires: - $N_r > 0$ (positive damping) - $N_{\psi} > 0$ (positive static stability — weather helm) - Damping ratio $\zeta = \dfrac{N_r}{2\sqrt{(I_{zz}+N_{\dot{r}})N_{\psi}}}$ should be $0.3 < \zeta < 1.0$ for good course-keeping without sluggish response. ### 2.5 The Nomoto Models (First and Second Order) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favorite — Nomoto's Models are tested almost every year in Part B.** **Nomoto's First-Order Model:** $$ T \dot{r} + r = K \delta_r $$ where: - $T = (I_{zz} + N_{\dot{r}}) / N_r$ — **yaw time constant** (typically 5–15 s for submarines) - $K = N_{\delta_r} / N_r$ — **yaw control gain** (rad/s per rad of rudder) **Nomoto's Second-Order Model:** $$ T_1 T_2 \ddot{r} + (T_1 + T_2)\dot{r} + r = K \delta_r + K T_3 \dot{\delta}_r $$ where $T_1 > T_2$ are the two time constants (slow and fast modes). For most submarines, $T_1 \gg T_2$, and the response is dominated by $T_1$. ### 2.6 Turning Circle Geometry When a steady rudder angle $\delta_{r,0}$ is applied: | Parameter | Definition | Formula | |-----------|------------|---------| | **Steady yaw rate** $r_s$ | Constant turn rate | $r_s = K \delta_{r,0}$ | | **Tactical diameter** $D_t$ | Diameter of steady turning circle | $D_t = 2 U_0 / r_s$ | | **Advance** $A$ | Distance traveled to reach 90° | $A \approx U_0 / r_s$ | | **Transfer** $T_r$ | Lateral distance at 90° | $T_r \approx U_0 / r_s$ | | **Period of turn** | Time for full 360° | $T_{turn} = 2\pi / r_s$ | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** A well-designed submarine achieves a tactical diameter of 4–8 ship lengths $L$ at design speed. Lower speed → larger turning circle (poor maneuverability). Submarines are typically less maneuverable than surface ships of the same size because of lower $N_{\delta_r}$ per unit rudder area. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Yaw Equation of Motion from Newton's Second Law **Step 1 — Define the yawing moment balance.** In the body-fixed frame, the moment about the $Z_b$ axis equals the rate of change of angular momentum about $Z_b$: $$ \sum M_Z = \frac{d}{dt}(I_{zz} r) + (I_{yy} - I_{xx})pq - (I_{xx} - I_{zz})pq + \text{cross terms} $$ For horizontal plane motion only ($p = q = 0$, no roll, no pitch), all cross products vanish and $I_{zz}$ is constant: $$ \sum N = I_{zz} \dot{r} $$ **Step 2 — Identify the contributing moments.** $$ \sum N = N_{\text{hull}} + N_{\text{rudder}} + N_{\text{appendage}} + N_{\text{propeller}} $$ Linearizing about straight-running equilibrium using Taylor series expansion about $v=0, r=0, \psi=0$: $$ N_{\text{hull}} = N_v \cdot v + N_r \cdot r + N_{\dot{r}} \cdot \dot{r} + N_{\psi} \cdot \psi $$ $$ N_{\text{rudder}} = N_{\delta_r} \cdot \delta_r $$ **Step 3 — Substitute back into the moment balance.** $$ I_{zz} \dot{r} = N_v v + N_r r + N_{\dot{r}} \dot{r} + N_{\psi} \psi + N_{\delta_r} \delta_r $$ **Step 4 — Rearrange to canonical form.** $$ (I_{zz} - N_{\dot{r}}) \dot{r} - N_v v - N_r r - N_{\psi} \psi = N_{\delta_r} \delta_r $$ But by standard sign convention in marine hydrodynamics, the added mass $N_{\dot{r}} > 0$ *adds* to $I_{zz}$: $$ (I_{zz} + N_{\dot{r}}) \dot{r} + N_r r + N_{\psi} \psi + N_v v = N_{\delta_r} \delta_r $$ **Step 5 — Apply the slender-body assumption to eliminate $v$ coupling.** For $L/D \gg 1$, the sway-velocity-induced moment $N_v$ is small. The dominant coupling is via the sway force equation: $$ m \dot{v} + m U_0 r = Y_v v + Y_r r + Y_{\delta_r} \delta_r $$ Steady-state (lateral acceleration negligible for slow turns): $v \approx (Y_r - mU_0)r/Y_v$, and substituting into the yaw equation yields the **effective yaw damping**: $$ (I_{zz} + N_{\dot{r}}) \dot{r} + N_r^{\text{eff}} r + N_{\psi} \psi = N_{\delta_r}^{\text{eff}} \delta_r $$ where: $$ N_r^{\text{eff}} = N_r - \frac{N_v(Y_r - mU_0)}{Y_v} $$ **Step 6 — Convert to Nomoto's first-order form.** Divide through by $N_r^{\text{eff}}$: $$ \frac{(I_{zz} + N_{\dot{r}})}{N_r^{\text{eff}}} \dot{r} + r + \frac{N_{\psi}}{N_r^{\text{eff}}} \psi = \frac{N_{\delta_r}^{\text{eff}}}{N_r^{\text{eff}}} \delta_r $$ For heading control, since $r = \dot{\psi}$, and for course-keeping the $\psi$ term dominates at low frequency, we define: $$ T \triangleq \frac{I_{zz} + N_{\dot{r}}}{N_r^{\text{eff}}}, \quad K \triangleq \frac{N_{\delta_r}^{\text{eff}}}{N_r^{\text{eff}}} $$ giving the **Nomoto first-order model**: $$ \boxed{\,T \dot{r} + r = K \delta_r\,} $$ ### 3.2 Derivation: Rudder Force and Yawing Moment **Step 1 — Rudder local velocity.** The rudder sees the cross-flow velocity component due to yaw and sway: $$ U_R = \sqrt{(U_0 + u_R)^2 + v_R^2} \approx U_0 \quad \text{(slender body, $v_R \ll U_0$)} $$ **Step 2 — Lift coefficient on rudder.** For a low-aspect-ratio fin typical of submarine rudders: $$ C_L = C_{L\alpha} \sin\alpha_{\text{eff}} \approx C_{L\alpha} \cdot \alpha_{\text{eff}} $$ where the effective angle of attack is: $$ \alpha_{\text{eff}} = \delta_r - \arctan(v_R / U_0) \approx \delta_r - (v_R / U_0) $$ **Step 3 — Rudder normal force.** $$ F_N = C_L \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_R^2 A_R \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_0^2 A_R C_{L\alpha} \left(\delta_r - \frac{v_R}{U_0}\right) $$ **Step 4 — Yaw moment from rudder (force × arm).** The rudder is located at distance $x_R$ aft of the CG (negative $x_R$ in body frame). The moment arm is approximately $-x_R$: $$ N_{\delta_r} = -x_R \cdot F_N = -x_R \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_0^2 A_R C_{L\alpha} \delta_r $$ In non-dimensional form: $$ N_{\delta_r}' = \frac{N_{\delta_r}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho L^3 U_0^2} = -\frac{x_R}{L} \cdot \frac{A_R}{L^2} C_{L\alpha} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** Since $x_R < 0$ (rudder is aft of CG) and $C_{L\alpha} > 0$, the product gives $N_{\delta_r} > 0$ for positive $\delta_r$ — i.e., a starboard rudder produces a positive (starboard-turning) yawing moment. The factor $\vert x_R / L \vert \cdot (A_R / L^2)$ defines the rudder's **leverage** — bigger $A_R$ or longer arm = more control authority. ### 3.3 Derivation: Steady Turning Radius In a steady turn at constant $r_s$ and constant rudder $\delta_0$: $$ T \cdot 0 + r_s = K \delta_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_s = K \delta_0 $$ The centrifugal force equals the centripetal force required for circular motion of radius $R$: $$ \frac{m U_0^2}{R} = Y_{\text{net}} $$ For balanced turn, the lateral rudder force provides centripetal acceleration: $$ R = \frac{U_0}{r_s} = \frac{U_0}{K \delta_0} $$ **Tactical diameter** is twice the radius: $$ \boxed{\,D_t = 2R = \frac{2 U_0}{K \delta_0} = \frac{2 U_0 \cdot N_r^{\text{eff}}}{N_{\delta_r}^{\text{eff}} \cdot \delta_0}\,} $$ ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A submarine has $I_{zz} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ kg·m}^2$, $N_r = 4 \times 10^6 \text{ N·m·s}$, $N_{\delta_r} = 8 \times 10^6 \text{ N·m/rad}$, $U_0 = 7.7 \text{ m/s}$ (15 knots). A constant rudder angle of $\delta_0 = 15° = 0.262 \text{ rad}$ is applied. Calculate the steady yaw rate, turning radius, and tactical diameter. Neglect added mass $N_{\dot{r}}$. **Solution:** Step 1: Compute Nomoto gain and time constant (ignoring $N_{\dot{r}}$): $$ T = \frac{I_{zz} + N_{\dot{r}}}{N_r} \approx \frac{5 \times 10^6}{4 \times 10^6} = 1.25 \text{ s} $$ $$ K = \frac{N_{\delta_r}}{N_r} = \frac{8 \times 10^6}{4 \times 10^6} = 2.0 \text{ s}^{-1} $$ Step 2: Steady yaw rate: $$ r_s = K \delta_0 = 2.0 \times 0.262 = 0.524 \text{ rad/s} = 30°/\text{s} $$ Step 3: Turning radius: $$ R = \frac{U_0}{r_s} = \frac{7.7}{0.524} = 14.69 \text{ m} $$ Step 4: Tactical diameter: $$ D_t = 2R = 29.4 \text{ m} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note for KTU Students:** The above example uses simplified values. A real submarine at 15 knots turning with 15° rudder would have $D_t \approx 400$–$600$ m due to the much smaller actual $K$ values (typical $K \approx 0.05$–$0.2$ s⁻¹). The numbers here are scaled for algebraic clarity. ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison: Surface Ship vs Submarine | Parameter | Surface Ship | Submarine (Submerged) | |-----------|--------------|------------------------| | $Y_v$ (sway force coeff.) | Moderate | Low (no hull above water) | | $N_r$ (yaw damping) | High (large bilge keels) | Low (smooth hull) | | $N_{\delta_r}$ (rudder moment) | High | Lower (smaller rudder area ratio) | | $T$ (yaw time constant) | 30–100 s | 5–20 s | | Weather helm tendency | Strong | Weaker (less asymmetric form) | | Sensitivity to depth | N/A | Strong (clearance effects) | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Submerged submarines are *inherently* less directionally stable than surface ships because the lack of a free waterplane removes the metacentric leverage. This is why submarines require **constant small rudder corrections** even on a "straight" course — a fact well-known to KTU examiners. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Submarine Horizontal-Plane Control Architecture (Block Diagram) The following block diagram illustrates the closed-loop heading control of a submarine in the horizontal plane, showing all the major functional blocks: sensor → controller → actuator → plant → disturbance → feedback. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Desired Heading
psi_des] --> B[Heading Error
e = psi_des minus psi] B --> C[Autopilot
PID Controller] C --> D[Rudder Command
delta_r_cmd] D --> E[Servo Actuator
Hydraulic] E --> F[Submarine Hull
Nomoto Plant
T dot r + r = K delta_r] F --> G[Yaw Rate r
psi_dot] G --> H[Integrator
psi] H --> I[Compass Sensor
Gyro] I --> J[Feedback Signal] J --> B K[Disturbance
Current, Wave] --> F L[Propeller RPM
Speed U0] --> F M[Bow Rudder
X-config] --> F classDef sensor fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef controller fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef actuator fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef plant fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef dist fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class I sensor class B,C controller class E actuator class F,G,H plant class K,L,M dist ``` ### 4.2 Forces, Moments, and Coordinate System Schematic The following diagram maps the body-fixed coordinate system, the acting forces ($X$, $Y$), moments ($N$), and the rudder geometry. This block-level functional mapping captures the physical setup that a photograph or sketch would otherwise illustrate. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Submarine Hull Body CG[Center of Gravity
Origin O] CB[Center of Buoyancy
Above CG for stability] end subgraph Forward Direction Xb P[Propeller Thrust T
along +Xb] Bow[Bow +Xb] end subgraph Lateral Direction Yb Yaxis[+Yb Starboard Side] SwayVel[Sway Velocity v
along -Yb during turn] end subgraph Yaw Axis Zb Out of Plane Zaxis[+Zb Downward] YawRate[Yaw Rate r dot psi] end subgraph Rudder Aft Rudder[Stern Rudder
at xR aft of CG
Area AR] RudderAngle[Rudder Angle delta_r] RudderForce[Normal Force F_N] YawMoment[Yaw Moment N] end CG --- P CG --- Rudder Rudder --- RudderAngle Rudder --- RudderForce RudderForce --- YawMoment YawMoment --- YawRate classDef geo fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef force fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef moment fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#CD5C5C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class CG,CB,Bow,Yaxis,Zaxis geo class P,SwayVel,RudderForce force class YawMoment,RudderAngle,YawRate moment ``` ### 4.3 Turning Circle Geometry — Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Initial Straight Course
at speed U0] --> B[Apply Rudder delta_0] B --> C[Transient Phase
T dot r + r = K delta_0
r approaches r_s] C --> D[Steady Turn
r = r_s = K delta_0
psi_dot constant] D --> E[90 deg turn
Advance A traveled] E --> F[180 deg turn
Tactical Diameter D_t reached] F --> G[360 deg turn
Returns to original bearing] classDef trans fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef steady fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A,B,C trans class D,E,F,G steady ``` ### 4.4 Stability Classification Tree (Static & Dynamic) ```mermaid flowchart TB Start[Directional Stability
of Submarine] --> Q1{Static Stability
sign of N_psi ?} Q1 -->|N_psi greater than 0| StatStable[Weather Helm
Statically Stable] Q1 -->|N_psi less than 0| StatUnstable[Lee Helm
Statically Unstable] Q1 -->|N_psi equals 0| StatNeutral[Neutral] Start --> Q2{Dynamic Stability
roots of char eqn ?} Q2 -->|Both roots in LHP
Real negative| OverDamped[Over-damped
No oscillation] Q2 -->|Complex pair in LHP| UnderDamped[Under-damped
Decaying oscillation] Q2 -->|Any root in RHP| Unstable[Dynamic Instability
Spiral or divergence] classDef good fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef bad fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef neutral fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class StatStable,OverDamped,UnderDamped good class StatUnstable,Unstable bad class StatNeutral neutral ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part A questions are compulsory, 2 marks for direct answer + 1 mark for brief explanation. Total ~3 marks each. --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define static directional stability of a submarine. Distinguish between weather helm and lee helm.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** *Static directional stability* is the ability of a submarine, when momentarily disturbed in heading, to develop a restoring yawing moment that tends to return it to its original course without any control input **(1 mark)**. The linearized static stability condition is $N_{\psi} = \partial N / \partial \psi > 0$ **(1 mark)**. - **Weather helm** ($N_{\psi} > 0$): To maintain a straight course in a steady disturbance (e.g., cross-current), the helmsman must apply a small rudder angle to the *leeward* side. The submarine is statically stable. - **Lee helm** ($N_{\psi} < 0$): The helmsman must apply rudder to *windward*, indicating a self-amplifying yaw tendency. The submarine is statically unstable **(1 mark)**. --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **What is the significance of the Nomoto time constant $T$ and gain $K$ in submarine yaw dynamics?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Nomoto model $T \dot{r} + r = K \delta_r$ reduces the complex 6-DOF submarine dynamics into a first-order input-output relationship between rudder angle and yaw rate **(1 mark)**. - **Time constant $T$** represents the *inertia* of the submarine's yaw response. A large $T$ means the submarine responds slowly to rudder commands; typical submarine values are 5–15 seconds. $T = (I_{zz} + N_{\dot{r}})/N_r$ **(1 mark)**. - **Gain $K$** represents the *control authority* of the rudder — yaw rate produced per radian of rudder angle. A larger $K$ gives a smaller tactical diameter (tighter turn). $K = N_{\delta_r} / N_r$ **(1 mark)**. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students choose between OR options. --- ### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Set A]` **Q3(a).** Derive the linearized equation of motion of a submarine in the horizontal plane (yaw plane). Clearly state the assumptions used. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Assumptions (2 marks):** 1. Submarine moves in a calm, infinite fluid. 2. Motions are small — linearization about straight-running equilibrium is valid ($v \ll U_0$, $r \ll U_0/L$, $\delta_r$ small). 3. Horizontal plane is decoupled from vertical plane (slender body, $L/D \gg 1$). 4. Roll and pitch are negligible ($p = q = 0$). 5. Constant forward speed $U_0$ (surge decoupled, $u \approx 0$). 6. Constant mass and inertia (no mass shedding). **Derivation (5 marks):** Starting from Newton's second law in body-fixed frame for the yaw moment $N$ about $Z_b$: $$ \sum N = I_{zz} \dot{r} \quad \text{(1 mark)} $$ Linearizing the total moment using Taylor expansion about $v = r = \psi = 0$: $$ \sum N = N_v v + N_r r + N_{\dot{r}} \dot{r} + N_{\psi} \psi + N_{\delta_r} \delta_r \quad \text{(1 mark)} $$ Equating: $$ I_{zz} \dot{r} = N_v v + N_r r + N_{\dot{r}} \dot{r} + N_{\psi} \psi + N_{\delta_r} \delta_r \quad \text{(1 mark)} $$ Coupling with sway force equation: $m(\dot{v} + U_0 r) = Y_v v + Y_r r + Y_{\delta_r} \delta_r$ In quasi-steady sway ($\dot{v} \approx 0$): $$ v \approx \frac{(mU_0 - Y_r)r - Y_{\delta_r} \delta_r}{Y_v} \quad \text{(1 mark)} $$ Substituting back and eliminating $v$: $$ (I_{zz} + N_{\dot{r}}) \dot{r} + N_r^{\text{eff}} r + N_{\psi} \psi = N_{\delta_r}^{\text{eff}} \delta_r \quad \text{(1 mark)} $$ This is the **linearized horizontal-plane equation of motion** of the submarine. --- **Q3(b).** A submarine has the following horizontal-plane derivatives at $U_0 = 7.7$ m/s: $I_{zz} = 1.0 \times 10^7$ kg·m², $N_r = 8.0 \times 10^6$ N·m·s, $N_{\delta_r} = 4.0 \times 10^6$ N·m/rad, $N_{\psi} = 5.0 \times 10^5$ N·m/rad. Neglect added mass. Compute the Nomoto constants $T$ and $K$, the steady yaw rate for $\delta_0 = 20°$, the turning radius, and the tactical diameter. Comment on the directional stability. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Nomoto time constant (1 mark):** $$ T = \frac{I_{zz}}{N_r} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^7}{8.0 \times 10^6} = 1.25 \text{ s} $$ **Step 2 — Nomoto gain (1 mark):** $$ K = \frac{N_{\delta_r}}{N_r} = \frac{4.0 \times 10^6}{8.0 \times 10^6} = 0.5 \text{ s}^{-1} $$ **Step 3 — Steady yaw rate (1 mark):** $$ \delta_0 = 20° = 0.349 \text{ rad} $$ $$ r_s = K \delta_0 = 0.5 \times 0.349 = 0.1745 \text{ rad/s} = 10°/\text{s} $$ **Step 4 — Turning radius (1 mark):** $$ R = \frac{U_0}{r_s} = \frac{7.7}{0.1745} = 44.13 \text{ m} $$ **Step 5 — Tactical diameter (1 mark):** $$ D_t = 2R = 88.26 \text{ m} $$ **Step 6 — Comment on stability (2 marks):** Since $N_{\psi} = 5.0 \times 10^5 > 0$, the submarine exhibits **weather helm** and is **statically stable in heading** (1 mark). The damping $N_r = 8.0 \times 10^6 > 0$ ensures **positive yaw damping**, so the system is also **dynamically stable** (1 mark). The natural period of oscillation in heading is: $$ T_{osc} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{I_{zz}}{N_{\psi}}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{1.0 \times 10^7}{5.0 \times 10^5}} = 28.1 \text{ s} $$ This relatively long period indicates sluggish but well-damped course-keeping — typical of large submarines. --- ### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks, Alternative) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Set B]` **Q4(a).** Explain with neat sketches the different types of rudder configurations used in submarines. Compare their control effectiveness in the horizontal plane. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Submarine Rudder Configurations (5 marks):** 1. **Stern Rudder Only (Cruciform-Aft)** — Single rudder behind the propeller. Simplest, lightest. Control authority limited at low speeds. Ineffective when propeller is not running. **(1 mark)** 2. **Stern + Fairwater Planes (X-configuration)** — Rudder in cruciform on the sail/fairwater plus separate stern planes. The fairwater planes can deflect to provide combined depth and heading control. **(1 mark)** 3. **X-Rudder (4-rudder cruciform at stern)** — Four rudders in X-pattern. Any two opposing rudders can act as a single rudder; the other two can be used for redundancy or fine control. Provides excellent low-speed maneuverability. Used on modern submarines like Type 212, Virginia class. **(1 mark)** 4. **Bow Planes** — Movable planes at the bow (used on some older US submarines like Los Angeles class). Provide better flow at low speeds (no propeller wash interference). However, they limit torpedo tube arrangement. **(1 mark)** 5. **Combined X-rudder and Sail Planes** — Most modern configuration. Allows independent depth and heading control with high redundancy. **(1 mark)** **Comparison of Control Effectiveness (2 marks):** | Configuration | Low-speed control | High-speed control | Redundancy | Cavitation risk | |---------------|-------------------|--------------------|-----------:|-----------------| | Stern only | Poor | Good | Low | High at stern | | X-rudder stern | Excellent | Good | High (4 surfaces) | Distributed | | Bow planes | Good | Moderate | Medium | Low | | Sail (fairwater) X | Excellent | Good | High | Low | **Conclusion:** Modern submarines prefer X-configurations at stern for combined low-speed maneuverability, redundancy, and reduced cavitation. **(As required for full marks.)** --- **Q4(b).** A submarine is moving at $U_0 = 10$ m/s with rudder deflection $\delta_0 = 10°$. The effective yaw gain $K = 0.3 \text{ s}^{-1}$ and the damping time constant $T = 8$ s. Find (i) the steady yaw rate, (ii) the time for the yaw rate to reach 95% of its steady value, (iii) the steady turning radius, and (iv) the tactical diameter. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $U_0 = 10$ m/s, $\delta_0 = 10° = 0.1745$ rad, $K = 0.3$ s⁻¹, $T = 8$ s. **Step 1 — Steady yaw rate (1.5 marks):** $$ r_s = K \delta_0 = 0.3 \times 0.1745 = 0.0524 \text{ rad/s} \approx 3°/\text{s} $$ **Step 2 — Time to reach 95% of steady value (2 marks):** The first-order Nomoto response to a step rudder input is: $$ r(t) = r_s (1 - e^{-t/T}) $$ For $r(t) = 0.95 \, r_s$: $$ 0.95 = 1 - e^{-t/T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{-t/T} = 0.05 $$ $$ t = -T \ln(0.05) = 8 \times 2.996 = 23.97 \text{ s} \approx 24 \text{ s} $$ So the submarine takes about **24 seconds** to reach 95% of its steady yaw rate — quite slow, indicating low directional responsiveness. **Step 3 — Steady turning radius (1.5 marks):** $$ R = \frac{U_0}{r_s} = \frac{10}{0.0524} = 190.8 \text{ m} $$ **Step 4 — Tactical diameter (2 marks):** $$ D_t = 2R = 381.6 \text{ m} $$ **Comment:** With $L \approx 70$ m for a typical submarine, $D_t / L \approx 5.4$ — reasonable for a submarine at 10 m/s. The long time constant $T = 8$ s means the helmsman must anticipate turns well in advance. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (costing 2–4 marks each):** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** in $\delta_0$ — leads to nonsense values for $r_s$ and $R$. *Always show the conversion step explicitly.* > > 2. **Confusing $N_{\dot{r}}$ (added mass) sign.** The convention is $+N_{\dot{r}}$, so it *adds* to $I_{zz}$. Writing $(I_{zz} - N_{\dot{r}})$ is a 1-mark deduction. > > 3. **Mixing up weather helm and lee helm signs.** Weather helm = stable = $N_{\psi} > 0$. Lee helm = unstable = $N_{\psi} < 0$. Many students get this reversed under exam pressure. > > 4. **Forgetting the sway-velocity coupling** when deriving the yaw equation.
