The full derivation should reference the sway equation $m(\dot{v} + U_0 r) = Y_v v + Y_r r + Y_{\delta_r}\delta_r$ even if you ultimately assume quasi-steady $\dot{v} \approx 0$. > > 5. **Not stating assumptions.** KTU Board Examiners award up to 2 marks just for listing assumptions clearly (slender body, linearization, decoupled planes, etc.). > > 6. **Using $K$ in two different senses** — the Nomoto gain $K$ (s⁻¹) is *not* the same as a turning-radius constant. Always define the symbol the first time it appears. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 3, Topic: Stability and Control in the Horizontal Plane** - ☐ The horizontal plane involves **surge, sway, yaw** — three DOF; roll and pitch are neglected. - ☐ The **body-fixed reference frame** $X_b Y_b Z_b$ with origin at CG is the standard KTU convention. - ☐ **Static stability** criterion: $N_{\psi} = \partial N / \partial \psi > 0$ (weather helm). - ☐ **Dynamic stability** requires: $N_r > 0$ (damping) and $N_{\psi} > 0$ (restoring). - ☐ **Nomoto's first-order model:** $T \dot{r} + r = K \delta_r$ — **memorize this**. - ☐ **Nomoto constants:** $T = (I_{zz} + N_{\dot{r}})/N_r$ and $K = N_{\delta_r}/N_r$. - ☐ **Steady yaw rate:** $r_s = K \delta_0$ (in rad/s if $\delta_0$ in rad). - ☐ **Turning radius:** $R = U_0 / r_s$. **Tactical diameter:** $D_t = 2R = 2U_0/(K\delta_0)$. - ☐ **Yaw time constant** $T$ for submarines is typically **5–20 s** (much smaller than surface ships). - ☐ **Weather helm** is the design goal — derived from asymmetric pressure on the afterbody. - ☐ **Rudder moment** $N_{\delta_r} = -x_R \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U_0^2 A_R C_{L\alpha}$ — sign comes from the lever arm. - ☐ **X-rudder and bow planes** improve low-speed maneuverability and provide redundancy. - ☐ **Cross-coupling** with vertical plane is neglected because $L/D \gg 1$. - ☐ **Damping ratio** $\zeta$ for good course-keeping: $0.3 < \zeta < 1.0$. - ☐ **Linearization** is valid when $v/U_0 < 0.2$, $rL/U_0 < 0.2$, and $\delta_r < 25°$. - ☐ Always state **assumptions** at the start of derivations — KTU awards 2 marks for this. - ☐ Always convert **degrees to radians** before substituting into formulas. > **END OF TOPIC NOTES — Module 3, Stability and Control in the Horizontal Plane**

topics.title: Stability and Control in the Vertical Plane
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST634 - SUBMARINES AND SUBMERSIBLES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Stability and Control in the Vertical Plane # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stability in the Vertical Plane** of a submarine refers to the inherent ability of the submerged vessel to return to (static stability) or oscillate about (dynamic stability) its equilibrium depth and trim angle when subjected to small external disturbances such as wave action, depth-keeping errors, or control surface deflections. **Control in the vertical plane** is the deliberate manipulation of hydrostatic, hydrodynamic, and buoyancy forces—through ballast tanks, trim tanks, and stern/bow planes—to regulate depth, pitch angle, and vertical velocity. The vertical plane in submarine hydrodynamics is the plane containing the longitudinal axis (X-body) and the vertical axis (Z-body) of the submarine. Motion in this plane comprises two coupled degrees of freedom: 1. **Heave (w)** — Linear translation along the vertical Z-axis. 2. **Pitch (θ)** — Rotational motion about the transverse Y-axis. These two DOFs are intrinsically coupled, making the vertical-plane dynamics of a submarine a classic **2-DOF coupled oscillatory system** analogous to a spring-mass-damper pendulum. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Underwater Seesaw > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a cork held underwater by your palm. The cork has natural **buoyancy** (upward push) and **gravity** (downward pull). When you release it slightly off its neutral depth, it does one of three things: > - **Returns smoothly** → Statically and dynamically stable (submarine at neutral buoyancy). > - **Oscillates and damps out** → Stable but under-damped (normal operating condition). > - **Runs away to the surface or bottom** → Unstable (catastrophic — uncontrolled depth excursion). > > The **"see-saw" effect** arises because the center of buoyancy (B) shifts when the submarine tilts, while the center of gravity (G) stays put. The horizontal separation between G and the new B creates a **restoring or capsizing couple** — exactly the same physics as a capsizing ship, but in the depth direction. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Submarine) | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² | | Sea-water density | $\rho$ | **1025** | kg/m³ | | Submerged displacement | $\Delta$ | 3000 – 24000 | tonnes | | Submarine length | $L$ | 70 – 170 | m | | Submarine beam (diameter) | $D$ | 7 – 13 | m | | Metacentric height (vertical) | $\overline{GM}_V$ | 0.05 – 0.30 | m | | Operating depth | $h$ | 200 – 900 | m | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Stability in the vertical plane is **not the same** as transverse (roll) stability of a surface ship. Because the submarine is a fully submerged axisymmetric body, the metacentric height is computed differently, and the restoring moment in the vertical plane arises primarily from the **shift of the center of buoyancy** during pitch, NOT from a change in the waterplane area. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Force and Moment Components The forces and moments acting on a submarine in the vertical plane can be categorized as follows: ### 2.1.1 Hydrostatic Forces (Always Present) - **Weight (W)** acting downward through the Center of Gravity (G). - **Buoyancy (B)** acting upward through the Center of Buoyancy (CB). - At equilibrium depth: $W = B$ exactly. ### 2.1.2 Hydrodynamic Forces (Velocity-Dependent) - **Heaving force** ($Z_{\dot{w}}, Z_w$) — damping and restoring in heave. - **Pitching moment** ($M_{\dot{q}}, M_q$) — damping and restoring in pitch. - **Cross-coupling terms** ($Z_q, M_w$) — coupling between heave and pitch DOFs. ### 2.1.3 Control Forces (Deliberately Applied) - **Bow planes / Stern planes deflection** → producing lift forces $L_{bp}, L_{sp}$. - **Main ballast tank (MBT) flooding/blowing** → producing net buoyancy change $\Delta B$. - **Trim tank transfer** → shifting G fore/aft to adjust pitch moment. ## 2.2 Static Stability in the Vertical Plane Static stability refers to the condition where the submarine, when displaced from its equilibrium, experiences a **restoring moment** that tends to return it to the original trim. For a submarine at small pitch angle $\theta$ with **fixed displacement** ($\nabla$ = submerged volume remains constant), the center of buoyancy shifts both: - **Vertically** by an amount proportional to $\sin\theta$ - **Longitudinally** by an amount proportional to $\cos\theta$ The longitudinal shift of B is what produces the **restoring or upsetting moment** about G. ### 2.2.1 The Critical Condition > [!NOTE] > A submarine is **statically stable in the vertical plane** if and only if its center of gravity lies **below** the metacenter for vertical-plane motion. For modern submarines this is ensured by placing heavy machinery (turbines, batteries, reactor) in the lower portion of the pressure hull. ## 2.3 Dynamic Stability — Equations of Motion In the vertical plane, the linearized equations of motion for a submarine are: $$m(\dot{w} - u_0 q) = Z_w \, w + Z_q \, q + Z_{\delta_s} \, \delta_s + (W - B)\cos\theta$$ $$I_y \dot{q} = M_w \, w + M_q \, q + M_{\delta_s} \, \delta_s$$ where: - $u_0$ = forward speed (m/s) - $w$ = heave velocity (m/s) - $q$ = pitch rate (rad/s) - $\delta_s$ = stern plane deflection (rad) - $m$ = mass of submarine - $I_y$ = moment of inertia about transverse axis - $W$ = weight, $B$ = buoyancy ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $W = mg = \rho g \nabla$ | Weight equals displaced water weight at equilibrium | N | | 2 | $B = \rho g V_{disp}$ | Buoyancy force on submerged hull | N | | 3 | $\Delta W = \rho g \Delta V$ | Change in weight by flooding MBT | N | | 4 | $\overline{GM}_V = \overline{KB} - \overline{KG}$ | Vertical-plane metacentric height | m | | 5 | $\overline{KB} = \dfrac{I_T}{V}$ | Height of metacenter above keel ($I_T$ = 2nd moment of waterplane) | m | | 6 | $M_{restoring} = W \cdot \overline{GM}_V \cdot \sin\theta$ | Restoring moment in pitch | N·m | | 7 | $L_{plane} = C_L \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}\rho u_0^2 S_{plane}$ | Lift produced by stern/bow plane | N | | 8 | $\Delta B_{MBT} = \rho g A_{MBT} \Delta h$ | Buoyancy change by MBT operation | N | | 9 | $\tau = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{I_y}{W \cdot \overline{GM}_V}}$ | Natural period of pitch oscillation | s | | 10 | $\zeta = \dfrac{M_q}{2\sqrt{I_y \cdot W \cdot \overline{GM}_V}}$ | Damping ratio in pitch | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Condition:** At neutral buoyancy, $W = B$ exactly. Any deviation $W \neq B$ produces a net vertical force that causes the submarine to either sink ($\Delta W > 0$ where $\Delta W = W - B$) or surface ($\Delta W < 0$). ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Depth-keeping autopilot:** Uses stern-plane feedback to maintain depth to within $\pm 3$ m at operating depth. - **Emergency blow:** Rapid MBT blowing with high-pressure air to surface a disabled submarine. - **Hovering systems:** Modern submersibles (e.g., ALVIN, DSV) use variable ballast + thrusters to maintain depth in strong currents. - **AUV depth control:** Pitch-and-heave decoupling enables stable survey lines at 6000 m depth. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Vertical-Plane Metacentric Height $\overline{GM}_V$ We derive the metacentric height in the vertical plane from first principles using Archimedes' principle and rigid-body geometry. ### Step 1: Equilibrium Condition For a submarine floating at neutral buoyancy at depth $h$: $$W = B = \rho g \nabla$$ where $\nabla$ is the displaced volume equal to the enclosed hull volume. ### Step 2: Application of Small Pitch Angle $\theta$ Now tilt the submarine by a small angle $\theta$ about its transverse axis. The volume displaced remains $\nabla$ (incompressible fluid), but the **center of buoyancy shifts**. The new center of buoyancy B' is located at the centroid of the new displaced volume. By the principle of **virtual work** or **center of gravity shift of a tilted volume**: The horizontal (longitudinal) shift of B from the centerline is: $$\overline{BB_{horiz}} = \overline{KB} \cdot \sin\theta$$ where $\overline{KB}$ is the height of the original center of buoyancy above the keel. ### Step 3: Definition of Metacenter M The **metacenter M** is defined as the point where the vertical line through the new center of buoyancy B' intersects the original vertical centerline of the submarine. The height of M above B is: $$\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}$$ where $I_T$ is the **second moment of area** of the waterplane (cross-sectional area at the design waterline) about the longitudinal axis. ### Step 4: Metacentric Radius The metacentric radius $\overline{KM}$ becomes: $$\overline{KM} = \overline{KB} + \overline{BM} = \overline{KB} + \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}$$ ### Step 5: Vertical-Plane Metacentric Height The **vertical-plane metacentric height** is the distance from G to M: $$\overline{GM}_V = \overline{KM} - \overline{KG} = \left(\overline{KB} + \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}\right) - \overline{KG}$$ Since $I_T$ for an axisymmetric circular cross-section hull of diameter $D$ is: $$I_T = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64}$$ we obtain: $$\overline{BM} = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64 \nabla}$$ ### Step 6: Final Compact Form $$\boxed{\overline{GM}_V = \overline{KB} + \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64 \nabla} - \overline{KG}}$$ > **Interpretation:** If $\overline{GM}_V > 0$, then G is below M, and the moment produced by shifting B is **restoring** (stable). If $\overline{GM}_V < 0$, the moment is **capsizing** (unstable). --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Restoring Moment in Pitch ### Step 1: Set Up the Geometry When the submarine is pitched by a small angle $\theta$, the center of buoyancy moves from CB to CB'. The horizontal displacement of B from the centerline is: $$e = \overline{GM}_V \cdot \sin\theta$$ For small angles, $\sin\theta \approx \theta$: $$e \approx \overline{GM}_V \cdot \theta$$ ### Step 2: Compute the Restoring Moment The couple formed by weight W (acting down at G) and buoyancy B (acting up at B') produces a moment about G: $$M_{restoring} = W \cdot e = W \cdot \overline{GM}_V \cdot \sin\theta$$ Linearized for small angles: $$M_{restoring} \approx W \cdot \overline{GM}_V \cdot \theta$$ ### Step 3: Sign Convention Check - If $\overline{GM}_V > 0$ and $\theta > 0$ (bow up), the moment is **negative** (tending to push bow down) → **restoring**. - If $\overline{GM}_V < 0$, the moment is positive → **capsizing**. $$\boxed{M_{restoring} = -W \cdot \overline{GM}_V \cdot \theta \quad \text{(with sign convention)}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Depth Control Using Stern Plane ### Step 1: Force on Stern Plane The stern plane (a small hydrofoil at the tail) deflects by an angle $\delta_s$ and produces a lift force: $$L_{sp} = C_{L_\alpha} \cdot \alpha_{eff} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}\rho u_0^2 S_{sp}$$ For a control surface, the effective angle of attack is $\alpha_{eff} = \delta_s$ (small angles), and the moment arm from the plane to G is $x_{sp}$. ### Step 2: Resulting Pitching Moment $$M_{\delta_s} = L_{sp} \cdot x_{sp} = C_{L_\alpha} \cdot \delta_s \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}\rho u_0^2 S_{sp} \cdot x_{sp}$$ ### Step 3: Equation of Motion for Pitch Using Newton's second law for rotation about the transverse Y-axis: $$I_y \ddot{\theta} = M_{restoring} + M_{\delta_s} + M_{damping}$$ $$I_y \ddot{\theta} = -W \cdot \overline{GM}_V \cdot \theta + C_{L_\alpha} \cdot \delta_s \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}\rho u_0^2 S_{sp} \cdot x_{sp} - M_{\dot{q}} \dot{\theta}$$ ### Step 4: Standard Second-Order Form Rearranging: $$I_y \ddot{\theta} + M_{\dot{q}} \dot{\theta} + W \cdot \overline{GM}_V \cdot \theta = C_{L_\alpha} \cdot \delta_s \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}\rho u_0^2 S_{sp} \cdot x_{sp}$$ Dividing by $I_y$: $$\ddot{\theta} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{\theta} + \omega_n^2 \theta = K_\delta \cdot \delta_s$$ where: $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{W \cdot \overline{GM}_V}{I_y}} \quad \text{(natural frequency in pitch)}$$ $$\zeta = \dfrac{M_{\dot{q}}}{2\sqrt{I_y \cdot W \cdot \overline{GM}_V}} \quad \text{(damping ratio)}$$ $$K_\delta = \dfrac{C_{L_\alpha} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}\rho u_0^2 S_{sp} \cdot x_{sp}}{I_y} \quad \text{(control gain)}$$ ### Step 5: Numerical Example **Given:** - $W = 24 \times 10^6$ N (≈ 2400 tonne submarine) - $\overline{GM}_V = 0.15$ m - $I_y = 1.2 \times 10^8$ kg·m² - $M_{\dot{q}} = 4.5 \times 10^7$ N·m·s/rad **Compute natural frequency:** $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{24 \times 10^6 \times 0.15}{1.2 \times 10^8}} = \sqrt{0.030} = 0.1732 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$\boxed{f_n = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = 0.0276 \text{ Hz} \quad ; \quad T_n = 36.2 \text{ s}}$$ **Compute damping ratio:** $$\zeta = \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^7}{2\sqrt{1.2 \times 10^8 \times 24 \times 10^6 \times 0.15}}$$ $$\zeta = \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^7}{2\sqrt{4.32 \times 10^{14}}} = \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^7}{2 \times 2.078 \times 10^7} = \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^7}{4.156 \times 10^7}$$ $$\boxed{\zeta \approx 1.083}$$ > **Interpretation:** $\zeta > 1$ means the system is **overdamped** — the submarine returns to its trim without oscillation. This is the design goal for depth control: stable, smooth, non-oscillatory response. --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Depth Change from MBT Flooding ### Step 1: Buoyancy Change Flooding the Main Ballast Tank (MBT) by a volume $\Delta V$ changes the **displacement** without changing the **weight**: $$B_{new} = W + \rho g \Delta V$$ ### Step 2: Net Downward Force $$F_{net} = W - B_{new} = -\rho g \Delta V$$ (A negative net force means downward — the submarine sinks.) ### Step 3: Resulting Depth Rate By Newton's second law (neglecting drag for short interval): $$m \ddot{z} = -\rho g \Delta V$$ For terminal velocity (drag balances net force), drag force on a streamlined body is: $$F_D = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A \dot{z}^2$$ Setting $F_D = \rho g \Delta V$: $$\dot{z}_{terminal} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 g \Delta V}{C_D A}}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{z}_{terminal} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 g \Delta V}{C_D A}}}$$ For typical values $\Delta V = 20$ m³, $C_D = 0.4$, $A = 100$ m²: $$\dot{z}_{terminal} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 9.81 \times 20}{0.4 \times 100}} = \sqrt{9.81} \approx 3.13 \text{ m/s}$$ --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Vertical-Plane Stability Architecture (Block Diagram) The following mermaid block diagram maps the interactions between hydrostatic, hydrodynamic, and control forces in the vertical plane: ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Disturbance Input
Wave Action, Current, Maneuver] --> B[Submarine Hull
Submerged Body] B --> C[Hydrostatic Forces
Weight W and Buoyancy B] B --> D[Hydrodynamic Forces
Heave and Pitch Damping] B --> E[Control Surfaces
Stern Plane and Bow Plane] C --> F[Net Vertical Force
W minus B] D --> G[Heave Velocity w] E --> H[Lift Force on Plane
L equals half rho u squared S CL delta] F --> I[Equilibrium Check
W equals B Condition] G --> I H --> J[Pitching Moment
M equals L times x arm] J --> K[Coupled 2 DOF System
Heave plus Pitch] I --> K K --> L[Depth Response z of t] K --> M[Pitch Angle theta of t] L --> N[Depth Autopilot
Feedback Controller] M --> N N --> E ``` ## 4.2 Stability Analysis Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD P0[Start: Submarine at Depth h] --> P1{Trim Condition?} P1 --|Neutral Buoyancy| P2[W equals B] P1 --|Positive Buoyancy| P3[Submarine Rises] P1 --|Negative Buoyancy| P4[Submarine Sinks] P2 --> P5{Pitch Disturbance Applied} P5 --|Small Theta| P6[Compute GM Vertical] P5 --|Large Theta| P7[Nonlinear Regime
Loss of Stability] P6 --> P8{GM Vertical Greater Than Zero?} P8 --|Yes| P9[Statically Stable
Restoring Moment Exists] P8 --|No| P10[Statically Unstable
Capsizing Tendency] P9 --> P11{Damping Ratio Check} P11 --|Zeta Less Than One| P12[Underdamped Oscillation] P11 --|Zeta Greater Than One| P13[Overdamped Smooth Return] P11 --|Zeta Equals One| P14[Critically Damped] P12 --> P15[Acceptable with Autopilot] P13 --> P16[Ideal Operating Condition] P14 --> P16 P15 --> P17[Activate Stern Plane Control] P16 --> P17 P17 --> P18[Depth Maintained] P10 --> P19[Emergency Ballast Adjustment] P7 --> P19 P3 --> P20[Reduce Buoyancy
Venti MBT] P4 --> P21[Increase Buoyancy
Blow MBT] ``` ## 4.3 Force and Moment Geometry Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Eq[Equilibrium Position] G0[Center of Gravity G] B0[Center of Buoyancy B] M0[Metacenter M] end subgraph Pt[Pitched Position at Angle Theta] G1[Center of Gravity G
Unchanged Position] B1[Center of Buoyancy B Prime
Shifted Forward by e] M1[Metacenter M
New Intersection Point] end Eq --> Pt B1 -.Restoring Couple.-> G1 M1 --- B1 G0 --- G1 M0 --- M1 ``` ## 4.4 Control System Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR R[Desired Depth
z ref] --> S[Summing Junction
Plus Minus] S --> E[Error Signal
e equals z ref minus z] E --> C[Depth Controller
PID Gains] C --> A[Actuator
Stern Plane Servo] A --> P[Plant
Submarine Hull] P --> Y[Measured Depth z] Y --> S P -.Disturbance.-> D[Wave Force, Current] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define the term "Vertical-Plane Metacentric Height" $\overline{GM}_V$ for a submarine. State its significance for static stability in the vertical plane.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1.5 marks):** Vertical-plane metacentric height $\overline{GM}_V$ is the distance between the center of gravity G of the submarine and the metacenter M, measured along the vertical centerline, for the submerged condition. - **Mathematical form (1 mark):** $\overline{GM}_V = \overline{KB} + \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64 \nabla} - \overline{KG}$ - **Significance (0.5 mark):** If $\overline{GM}_V > 0$, the submarine is statically stable in pitch; if $\overline{GM}_V < 0$, it is unstable and will capsize. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between static and dynamic stability of a submarine in the vertical plane.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Static Stability | Dynamic Stability | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Tendency to develop restoring moment when displaced | Tendency to return to equilibrium without oscillation | | **Governing parameter** | $\overline{GM}_V$ | Damping ratio $\zeta$ | | **Condition** | $\overline{GM}_V > 0$ | $\zeta > 0$ with appropriate magnitude | | **Analysis tool** | Force/moment balance | Solution of EOM (2nd order ODE) | (2 marks for table + 1 mark for definition clarity) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 3 Choice Q1A]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the vertical-plane metacentric height $\overline{GM}_V$ of a fully submerged submarine of circular cross-section with diameter $D$ and submerged volume $\nabla$. Clearly state the assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A submarine has the following particulars: - Length: $L = 100$ m - Diameter: $D = 10$ m - Submerged displacement: $\nabla = 7854$ m³ - Height of CG above keel: $\overline{KG} = 4.2$ m - Height of CB above keel: $\overline{KB} = 5.0$ m Calculate: (i) The metacentric height $\overline{GM}_V$ (ii) The restoring moment when the submarine is pitched by $\theta = 5°$ (iii) Comment on the static stability. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** 1. Submarine at equilibrium: $W = \rho g \nabla$ 2. Apply small pitch angle $\theta$ — volume displaced is unchanged but centroid shifts. 3. Horizontal shift of B: $\overline{BB_{horiz}} = \overline{KB} \cdot \sin\theta \approx \overline{KB} \cdot \theta$ 4. Metacenter M defined such that $\overline{BM} = I_T / \nabla$ where $I_T$ is second moment of waterplane area. 5. For circular cross-section of diameter $D$: $I_T = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64}$ 6. Therefore $\overline{KM} = \overline{KB} + \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64 \nabla}$ 7. Hence: $\overline{GM}_V = \overline{KM} - \overline{KG} = \overline{KB} + \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64 \nabla} - \overline{KG}$ **Assumptions (any 3 for full marks):** - Submarine fully submerged (no free surface effect) - Incompressible, homogeneous sea water - Small pitch angle (linearization valid) - Rigid hull (no deformation) - Constant $\rho$ and $g$ **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **(i) Metacentric height:** $$\overline{KB} = 5.0 \text{ m}, \quad \overline{KG} = 4.2 \text{ m}$$ Compute $I_T$ and $\overline{BM}$: $$I_T = \dfrac{\pi (10)^4}{64} = \dfrac{\pi \times 10000}{64} = 490.87 \text{ m}^4$$ $$\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla} = \dfrac{490.87}{7854} = 0.0625 \text{ m}$$ $$\overline{KM} = \overline{KB} + \overline{BM} = 5.0 + 0.0625 = 5.0625 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{\overline{GM}_V = \overline{KM} - \overline{KG} = 5.0625 - 4.2 = 0.8625 \text{ m}}$$ **(ii) Restoring moment at $\theta = 5°$:** $$W = \rho g \nabla = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 7854 = 7.896 \times 10^7 \text{ N}$$ $$M_{restoring} = W \cdot \overline{GM}_V \cdot \sin(5°)$$ $$M_{restoring} = 7.896 \times 10^7 \times 0.8625 \times 0.0872$$ $$\boxed{M_{restoring} = 5.94 \times 10^6 \text{ N·m} = 5.94 \text{ MN·m}}$$ **(iii) Comment on stability:** Since $\overline{GM}_V = +0.8625$ m $> 0$, the submarine is **statically stable** in the vertical plane. The positive metacentric height ensures a restoring moment is generated when the submarine is pitched, returning it toward the original trim. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 3 Choice Q1B]** **(a)** Derive the second-order equation of motion for pitch angle $\theta$ of a submarine in the vertical plane, including the natural frequency $\omega_n$ and damping ratio $\zeta$. Explain what range of $\zeta$ is desirable for depth control. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A submarine with weight $W = 20$ MN and pitch moment of inertia $I_y = 1.0 \times 10^8$ kg·m² has a metacentric height $\overline{GM}_V = 0.20$ m and pitch damping coefficient $M_{\dot{q}} = 3.0 \times 10^7$ N·m·s/rad. Compute: (i) The natural frequency $\omega_n$ and natural period $T_n$ of pitch oscillation (ii) The damping ratio $\zeta$ (iii) State whether the system is underdamped, critically damped, or overdamped. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** Starting from Newton's second law for rotation about the transverse axis: $$I_y \ddot{\theta} = M_{restoring} + M_{control} + M_{damping}$$ $$I_y \ddot{\theta} = -W \overline{GM}_V \theta + L_{sp} \cdot x_{sp} - M_{\dot{q}} \dot{\theta}$$ For free oscillation (no control input, $L_{sp} = 0$): $$I_y \ddot{\theta} + M_{\dot{q}} \dot{\theta} + W \overline{GM}_V \theta = 0$$ Dividing by $I_y$: $$\ddot{\theta} + \dfrac{M_{\dot{q}}}{I_y} \dot{\theta} + \dfrac{W \overline{GM}_V}{I_y} \theta = 0$$ Comparing with standard form $\ddot{\theta} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{\theta} + \omega_n^2 \theta = 0$: $$\boxed{\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{W \overline{GM}_V}{I_y}} \quad ; \quad \zeta = \dfrac{M_{\dot{q}}}{2\sqrt{I_y W \overline{GM}_V}}}$$ **Desirable range for depth control (1 mark):** $\zeta$ between **0.7 and 1.0** is ideal — fast response with minimal overshoot. For pure depth holding, $\zeta \geq 1$ (critically or overdamped) is preferred to avoid oscillations that could cause depth excursions. **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **(i) Natural frequency and period:** $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{W \overline{GM}_V}{I_y}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{20 \times 10^6 \times 0.20}{1.0 \times 10^8}}$$ $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{4.0 \times 10^6}{1.0 \times 10^8}} = \sqrt{0.04} = 0.20 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$\boxed{\omega_n = 0.20 \text{ rad/s} \quad ; \quad T_n = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = \dfrac{2\pi}{0.20} = 31.42 \text{ s}}$$ **(ii) Damping ratio:** $$\zeta = \dfrac{M_{\dot{q}}}{2\sqrt{I_y W \overline{GM}_V}}$$ Denominator: $$2\sqrt{1.0 \times 10^8 \times 20 \times 10^6 \times 0.20} = 2\sqrt{4.0 \times 10^{14}} = 2 \times 2.0 \times 10^7 = 4.0 \times 10^7$$ $$\boxed{\zeta = \dfrac{3.0 \times 10^7}{4.0 \times 10^7} = 0.75}$$ **(iii) Damping classification:** Since $\zeta = 0.75 < 1$, the system is **underdamped**. It will oscillate about the equilibrium trim with the oscillations decaying exponentially. For this value, depth control will be acceptably responsive with small overshoot. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Stability in the Vertical Plane:** > 1. **Confusing vertical-plane and transverse-plane stability** — Do not use the surface-ship roll formula. For a fully submerged submarine, $I_T$ is the second moment of the cross-sectional area about the longitudinal axis, NOT the waterplane area in the traditional sense. > 2. **Forgetting the linearization assumption** — Always state "for small pitch angles" when using $\sin\theta \approx \theta$. > 3. **Sign convention errors** — In the restoring moment equation, clearly indicate whether the moment is restoring (negative if $\theta > 0$ and $\overline{GM}_V > 0$) or upsetting. > 4. **Units inconsistency** — $\overline{GM}_V$ in meters, $W$ in N, $I_y$ in kg·m². Mixing kgf with N loses 1 mark minimum. > 5. **Forgetting to comment on stability** — Numerical answers must END with a stability comment. Examiners allot 1–2 marks explicitly for this. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Stability and Control in the Vertical Plane:** - ☐ **Two coupled DOFs**: heave (w) and pitch (θ). They are ALWAYS coupled — never analyze separately. - ☐ **Vertical-plane metacentric height**: $\overline{GM}_V = \overline{KB} + \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64 \nabla} - \overline{KG}$ - ☐ **Stability criterion**: $\overline{GM}_V > 0$ ⇒ statically stable in pitch. - ☐ **Restoring moment**: $M_{restoring} = W \cdot \overline{GM}_V \cdot \sin\theta$ - ☐ **Natural pitch frequency**: $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{W \overline{GM}_V}{I_y}}$ - ☐ **Damping ratio**: $\zeta = \dfrac{M_{\dot{q}}}{2\sqrt{I_y W \overline{GM}_V}}$ - ☐ **Desired damping**: $\zeta \approx 0.7$–$1.0$ for optimal depth control (fast + smooth). - ☐ **Stern plane control**: Produces pitching moment $M_{\delta_s} = C_{L_\alpha} \delta_s \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho u_0^2 S_{sp} \cdot x_{sp}$ - ☐ **MBT flooding**: Adds mass without changing buoyancy → submarine sinks. - ☐ **MBT blowing**: Removes mass (water replaced by air) → submarine becomes more buoyant → rises. - ☐ **Trim tanks**: Shift G fore/aft to adjust pitch without changing depth. - ☐ **Cross-coupled terms**: $Z_q$ and $M_w$ link heave and pitch — critical in autopilot design. - ☐ **Operating condition**: Modern submarines operate with $\overline{GM}_V \approx 0.1$–$0.3$ m to balance stability and maneuverability. - ☐ **Standard sea water**: $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s² (use these unless otherwise stated). ---

topics.title: Structure and Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST634 - SUBMARINES AND SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Structure and Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining "Structure and Systems" in Submarine Engineering

In the context of modern submarine design, **Structure and Systems** refers to the integrated engineering framework comprising the **load-bearing pressure envelope** (the hull and its stiffeners) along with all the **mission-critical subsystems** that allow the vessel to submerge, navigate, remain submerged, surface safely, and support human life or unmanned operations for extended durations.

The KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus frames this module as the bridge between hydrostatics (buoyancy, stability) and operational submarine architecture. According to naval architectural convention, a submarine is not merely a "boat that goes underwater" — it is a **self-contained, pressure-rated, life-supporting engineering platform** whose internal arrangement must withstand external hydrostatic pressure while housing propulsion, power, navigation, and crew-support machinery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PENST634 - Module 4):**
> *Structure and Systems* encompasses the **pressure hull geometry**, **external fairing**, **internal compartmentalization**, **main ballast tanks (MBT)**, **trim tanks**, **auxiliary systems**, **propulsion arrangement**, and **auxiliary machinery layout** required for safe and sustained underwater operation.

---

## 1.2 Structural Hierarchy of a Submarine

A submarine is built as a **multi-layered structural sandwich**, where each layer performs a distinct function. Let us define them hierarchically:

### A. Pressure Hull (Inner Hull)
- A **cylindrical or ellipsoidal pressure vessel** made from high-yield-strength steel (HY-80, HY-100) or titanium alloys.
- Rated to withstand **full ocean depth pressure**, computed using the collapsing pressure formula.
- Houses the crew, machinery, control spaces, and all operational systems.

### B. Outer Hull (Light Hull / Free-Flooding Hull)
- A **streamlined, non-pressure-bearing fairing** that reduces hydrodynamic drag.
- Floods with seawater during submerged operations (free-flooding).
- Provides a smooth external surface for efficient submerged running.

### C. Main Ballast Tanks (MBT)
- Large **variable-volume tanks** located between the pressure and outer hulls (in a double-hull submarine).
- **Flooded to dive**, **blown empty to surface**.
- Volume determines the magnitude of the negative buoyancy change.

### D. Trim Tanks (Forward and Aft)
- Smaller tanks used for **fine buoyancy and fore-aft trim adjustments**.
- Allow the submarine to achieve neutral buoyancy precisely at any depth.

### E. Conning Tower / Sail
- A streamlined superstructure on the upper deck housing the **periscopes, masts, and communication antennas**.
- On modern designs, also contains retractable planes for depth control.

---

## 1.3 Systems Hierarchy

The "systems" component covers all mechanical, electrical, fluid, and control subsystems:

| System Group | Core Subsystems | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Propulsion** | Diesel engines, electric motors, shaft, propeller | Convert stored energy → thrust |
| **Power** | Batteries, generators, nuclear reactor (if applicable) | Generate and store electrical energy |
| **Ballast & Trim** | MBT blow valves, trim pumps, vent valves | Control depth and attitude |
| **Auxiliary** | Compressed air, hydraulic, lube oil, cooling | Support main machinery |
| **Life Support** | O₂ generation, CO₂ scrubbers, chillers, sanitation | Sustain crew habitability |
| **Navigation & C4I** | Sonar, periscopes, inertial nav, communications | Situational awareness and control |
| **Emergency** | Emergency blow, rescue, firefighting | Casualty control |

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The Submarine as a "Building Under Pressure"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a modern high-rise office building that is required to operate **fully submerged in a lake**. The structural engineers must design the building's outer walls (pressure hull) to resist the lake's water pressure at maximum depth. Between the outer "decorative cladding" (outer hull) and the structural walls, there must be huge **air-filled chambers** (ballast tanks) that can be emptied with a single button press to make the building float to the surface. Inside, every office (compartment) needs its own power supply, oxygen, plumbing, and communication — exactly like a submarine's internal systems.

This analogy captures the essence: a submarine is a **pressure-rated, self-sustaining, mobile platform** — its structures give it form and safety, while its systems give it function and life.

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used in Submarine Structural Design

For accurate calculations, the following constants and standard metrics must be memorized for KTU examinations:

- **Density of seawater:** $\rho_{sw} = \mathbf{1025 \; kg/m^3}$ (standard KTU value)
- **Density of fresh water:** $\rho_{fw} = \mathbf{1000 \; kg/m^3}$
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = \mathbf{9.81 \; m/s^2}$
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = \mathbf{101325 \; Pa}$
- **Hydrostatic pressure gradient:** $P_h = \mathbf{10.05 \; kPa/m}$ of seawater depth
- **Typical yield strengths:** HY-80 → $\mathbf{550 \; MPa}$, HY-100 → $\mathbf{690 \; MPa}$, Titanium Grade 5 → $\mathbf{880 \; MPa}$
- **Typical submerged speed:** Conventional → $\mathbf{20\text{–}25 \; knots}$, Nuclear → $\mathbf{30+\; knots}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Submarine Structure

### 2.1.1 Pressure Hull Theory

The pressure hull is fundamentally a **thin or moderately thick cylindrical pressure vessel** closed by hemispherical or ellipsoidal end caps. Its structural integrity depends on:

1. **Material yield strength** ($\sigma_y$)
2. **Wall thickness** ($t$)
3. **Hull radius** ($R$)
4. **Safety factor** (typically $SF = 1.5$ to $2.0$)

A submarine is unique because the hull is loaded externally rather than internally (unlike a gas cylinder). When submerged, the external hydrostatic pressure creates a **compressive hoop stress** in the cylindrical hull section.

### 2.1.2 Stiffener Rings (Frames) and Bulkheads

To prevent **buckling collapse** (which occurs at pressures well below the material's yield strength), the pressure hull is reinforced with:
- **Ring frames** (circumferential stiffeners) at regular intervals
- **Longitudinal stiffeners** (for very large diameter hulls)
- **Transverse bulkheads** dividing the hull into watertight compartments
- **Cone-cylinder intersections** at the ends (special reinforced joints)

### 2.1.3 Collapse vs. Yield — Why Buckling Controls Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A submarine hull does **not** fail by yielding first — it fails by **elastic instability (buckling)**. The classical collapse pressure of a long unstiffened cylinder under external pressure (von Mises formula) is far below the yield-collapse pressure. This is why ring frames are mandatory.

The transition between buckling-dominated and yield-dominated failure is governed by the **radius-to-thickness ratio** ($R/t$). For deep-diving submarines, a small $R/t$ ratio is critical.

---

## 2.2 Systems Architecture — How the Subsystems Interact

The **propulsion-power-ballast triad** is the operational heart of any submarine. Understanding how they interact is essential for KTU questions:

1. **Surface running** → Diesel engines charge batteries and propel the boat. MBTs are empty (full buoyancy).
2. **Diving** → MBTs flooded through Kingston valves at the top; negative buoyancy pulls the boat under. Forward and aft trim tanks adjusted.
3. **Snorkel running** → At periscope depth, the snorkel mast extends; diesel engines breathe air through it while MBTs remain full.
4. **Deep submerged running** → Diesel engines shut down. Electric motors powered by batteries (or AIP/nuclear) drive the propeller.
5. **Surfacing** → MBTs blown empty using high-pressure compressed air from storage banks; positive buoyancy returns the boat to the surface.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table summarizes every formula you must know for Module 4. Memorize the symbol definitions and units before attempting any derivation.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic Pressure | $P_h = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$ | $\rho_{sw}$ = seawater density (kg/m³), $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $h$ = depth (m) | Computing external pressure on hull at operational depth |
| 2 | Total Collapse Pressure (Thin Cylinder, von Mises) | $P_c = \dfrac{2 \cdot E}{1-\nu^2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^3$ | $E$ = Young's modulus (Pa), $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio, $t$ = thickness (m), $D$ = diameter (m) | Predicts buckling collapse pressure of an unstiffened cylinder |
| 3 | Hoop Stress (Internal Pressure Cylinder) | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{t}$ | $P$ = internal pressure (Pa), $R$ = radius (m), $t$ = thickness (m) | Stress in cylindrical pressure vessel |
| 4 | Required Wall Thickness | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{\sigma_y / SF}$ | $\sigma_y$ = yield strength (Pa), $SF$ = safety factor | Minimum thickness for yield-based design |
| 5 | Reserve Buoyancy | $RB = W_{surfaced} - W_{submerged\;neutral}$ | All in tonnes or N | Indicates margin above neutral buoyancy |
| 6 | Ballast Volume Required to Submerge | $V_{MBT} = \dfrac{\Delta W_{sub}}{\rho_{sw}}$ | $\Delta W_{sub}$ = weight increase to dive (N), $\rho_{sw}$ = 1025 kg/m³ | Volume of seawater that must enter MBT |
| 7 | Reserve Buoyancy Fraction | $RBF = \dfrac{V_{free\;flood}}{V_{total\;hull}}$ | Both in m³ | Design metric (typically 0.30–0.45 for submarines) |
| 8 | Compressed Air Required to Blow MBT | $V_{air} = V_{MBT} \cdot \dfrac{P_{final}}{P_{initial}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\eta_{comp}}$ | $\eta_{comp}$ = compressor efficiency (0.85–0.95) | Sizing HP air flasks for emergency blow |
| 9 | Submerged Speed (Power-Speed) | $P = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V}{\eta_{prop}}$ | $R_T$ = total resistance (N), $V$ = speed (m/s), $\eta_{prop}$ = propulsive efficiency | Power required to maintain submerged speed |
| 10 | Battery Endurance | $t_{end} = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{P_{prop}}$ | $E_{bat}$ = battery energy (kWh), $P_{prop}$ = propulsion power (kW) | Hours of submerged electric running |

---

## 2.4 Why These Formulas Matter in Real Submarine Design

- **Formulas 1–4** are used by **naval architects** during preliminary hull sizing for new submarine classes.
- **Formulas 5–7** are critical for **submarine operators and stability officers** to compute safe operating envelopes.
- **Formula 8** determines the size of the **high-pressure air (HPA) system**, a life-safety-critical subsystem.
- **Formulas 9–10** define the **mission profile and operational range** — directly linked to fuel/energy capacity planning.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** In Module 4 questions, formulas 1, 2, and 6 appear most frequently. Always write down the **known quantities with units** before substituting — this earns the "method" marks even if the final arithmetic slips.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hoop Stress in a Submarine Pressure Hull (Thin-Walled Cylinder)

We will derive the circumferential (hoop) stress experienced by a cylindrical hull segment under external pressure. Although submarine hulls see external pressure, the magnitude of stress is identical in form to internal pressure loading, with sign reversed.

### Step 1 — Free Body Setup
Consider a half-cylinder of length $L$, radius $R$, and wall thickness $t$, cut along a longitudinal diameter. The internal pressure $P$ acts outward on this half-surface.

### Step 2 — Identify Forces Acting on the Cut Section
- **Pressure force** pushing the half-cylinder apart: $F_P = P \cdot (2R \cdot L)$
- **Hoop stress resisting the separation**, acting on the two cut walls (each of area $t \cdot L$): $F_\sigma = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L$

### Step 3 — Apply Static Equilibrium
For equilibrium, the resisting force must equal the separating force:

$$
2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L = P \cdot 2R \cdot L
$$

### Step 4 — Cancel Common Terms
Dividing both sides by $2L$:

$$
\sigma_h \cdot t = P \cdot R
$$

### Step 5 — Solve for Hoop Stress

$$
\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{t}
$$

**Textual interpretation of the result:**
- Hoop stress is **directly proportional** to applied pressure and hull radius.
- Hoop stress is **inversely proportional** to wall thickness.
- For a deeper-diving submarine (larger $P$), the wall must be thicker or the radius smaller.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Minimum Wall Thickness for Yield-Based Design

Starting from the hoop stress equation, we want the minimum thickness such that stress never exceeds the allowable value:

### Step 1 — Allowable Stress
$$
\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{SF}
$$

### Step 2 — Set Hoop Stress Equal to Allowable Stress

$$
\dfrac{P \cdot R}{t} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{SF}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Wall Thickness

$$
t = \dfrac{P \cdot R \cdot SF}{\sigma_y}
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Demonstration
A submarine is to operate at depth $h = 300$ m with hull radius $R = 4$ m. Material is HY-80 with $\sigma_y = 550 \times 10^6$ Pa, and design safety factor $SF = 2.0$.

**Step 4a — Compute hydrostatic pressure at operating depth:**

$$
P_h = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 300
$$

$$
P_h = 3.017 \times 10^6 \; Pa \approx 3.02 \; MPa
$$

**Step 4b — Substitute into the wall thickness formula:**

$$
t = \dfrac{(3.02 \times 10^6) \cdot 4 \cdot 2.0}{550 \times 10^6}
$$

**Step 4c — Simplify numerator:**

$$
t = \dfrac{2.416 \times 10^7}{5.50 \times 10^8}
$$

**Step 4d — Final answer:**

$$
t = 0.0439 \; m \approx 44 \; mm
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Collapse Pressure of an Unstiffened Cylindrical Hull (von Mises Equation)

This is the equation that controls the actual failure mode of submarine hulls — **elastic buckling**, not yielding.

### Step 1 — Underlying Physics
When external pressure exceeds a critical value, the cylindrical shell **buckles outward** in a characteristic diamond-pattern deformation. The classical analysis by von Mises (1914) gives the critical external pressure.

### Step 2 — Governing Differential Equation
For an infinitely long, perfectly circular cylinder, classical small-deflection buckling theory yields:

$$
\dfrac{D}{R^4} \cdot \nabla^8 w + \dfrac{E \cdot t}{R^2} \cdot \nabla^4 w = P \cdot \nabla^4 w
$$

where $D$ is the **flexural rigidity per unit width** of the shell:

$$
D = \dfrac{E \cdot t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the Critical Pressure
Substituting the trial buckled-shape solution $w = A \sin(n\theta) \cos(m\pi z/L)$ and minimizing with respect to mode number $n$ (which yields $n = 2$ for an infinitely long cylinder), the **von Mises critical pressure** emerges:

$$
P_c = \dfrac{2 \cdot E}{1-\nu^2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^3
$$

### Step 4 — Physical Interpretation
- $P_c$ scales with the **cube of the thickness-to-diameter ratio**.
- Doubling the thickness increases collapse pressure by **8 times**.
- This is why deep-diving submarines (e.g., research submersibles to 6000 m) require **very thick walls or small diameters**, plus titanium construction.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example
A submarine hull has $D = 8$ m, $t = 0.05$ m, $E = 2.0 \times 10^{11}$ Pa (steel), and $\nu = 0.30$.

**Step 5a — Compute the thickness ratio cubed:**

$$
\left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^3 = \left(\dfrac{0.05}{8}\right)^3 = (6.25 \times 10^{-3})^3 = 2.44 \times 10^{-7}
$$

**Step 5b — Compute the elastic modulus term:**

$$
\dfrac{2 \cdot E}{1-\nu^2} = \dfrac{2 \cdot (2.0 \times 10^{11})}{1 - (0.30)^2} = \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^{11}}{0.91}
$$

$$
\dfrac{2 \cdot E}{1-\nu^2} = 4.40 \times 10^{11} \; Pa
$$

**Step 5c — Multiply:**

$$
P_c = 4.40 \times 10^{11} \cdot 2.44 \times 10^{-7}
$$

**Step 5d — Final collapse pressure:**

$$
P_c = 1.07 \times 10^5 \; Pa \approx 0.107 \; MPa
$$

This corresponds to a collapse depth of approximately $\dfrac{P_c}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g} \approx 10.6$ m — confirming that an **unstiffened cylinder is far too weak** for any real submarine, justifying the use of ring frames.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study of Submarine Systems

| System | Conventional Diesel-Electric | AIP (Air-Independent) | Nuclear-Powered |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Power Source** | Diesel + Lead-acid batteries | Diesel + batteries + AIP (Stirling/MES/FC) | Nuclear reactor + steam turbines |
| **Submerged Endurance** | 1–4 days | 7–21 days | Unlimited (limited only by food) |
| **Submerged Speed** | 6–10 knots | 4–8 knots | 25–30+ knots |
| **Air Independence** | No (must snorkel every 1–2 days) | Partial (weeks without surfacing) | Yes (full) |
| **Crew Habitability** | Moderate | Good | Excellent |
| **Example Class** | Type 209 (HDW) | Type 212 (HDW), Scorpène | Virginia (US), Akula (Russia) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Note:** A common 7-mark Part B question asks: *"Compare three propulsion systems for a modern submarine and justify the choice for a coastal patrol vessel."* Use this table as the backbone of your answer.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the function of the main ballast tanks in a submarine and state what happens to them during a normal surfacing operation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Main ballast tanks (MBTs) are large variable-volume tanks located between the pressure hull and outer hull in a double-hull submarine. Their function is to **change the submarine's overall density** to control depth. To dive, MBTs are **flooded** through top-mounted Kingston valves and bottom vents, admitting seawater by gravity and negative buoyancy. To surface, high-pressure compressed air from storage flasks is **blown into the MBTs**, displacing the seawater through the bottom vents. As water leaves, buoyancy becomes positive and the submarine rises. Thus, MBT operation directly controls the transition between surface and submerged states. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between the pressure hull and the outer hull of a submarine.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Pressure Hull | Outer Hull |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Resists external hydrostatic pressure; contains crew and systems | Streamlines external shape; reduces drag |
| Material | HY-80/HY-100 steel or titanium | Mild steel or composite fairing |
| Flooding | Never flooded during operation | Free-flooding during submerged running |
| Shape | Cylindrical with hemispherical ends | Streamlined hydrodynamic profile |

The pressure hull is **safety-critical**, while the outer hull is **performance-critical**. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — CHOICE A

**A new coastal patrol submarine is to be designed with a cylindrical pressure hull of diameter $D = 6$ m and operating depth $h = 250$ m. The hull material is HY-80 steel ($\sigma_y = 550$ MPa, $E = 2.0 \times 10^{11}$ Pa, $\nu = 0.30$). Adopt a safety factor $SF = 2.0$.**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the minimum wall thickness based on yield criterion and compute its numerical value for the given submarine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the von Mises equation, compute the theoretical collapse pressure of an unstiffened cylinder of the same diameter with the thickness obtained in part (a). Comment on whether this collapse pressure is adequate for the operating depth. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B1(a)

**Step 1 — State the yield-based design equation:**
Starting from hoop stress equilibrium:

$$
\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{t} \leq \sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{SF}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for thickness:**

$$
t_{min} = \dfrac{P \cdot R \cdot SF}{\sigma_y}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute hydrostatic pressure at operating depth:**
Take $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $h = 250$ m:

$$
P = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 250 = 2.514 \times 10^6 \; Pa
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**
With $R = D/2 = 3$ m, $\sigma_y = 550 \times 10^6$ Pa, $SF = 2.0$:

$$
t_{min} = \dfrac{(2.514 \times 10^6) \cdot 3 \cdot 2.0}{550 \times 10^6}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute numerator and divide:**

$$
t_{min} = \dfrac{1.508 \times 10^7}{5.50 \times 10^8} = 0.0274 \; m \approx 27.4 \; mm
$$

**Answer (a):** The minimum wall thickness is **approximately 27.4 mm**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B1(b)

**Step 1 — State the von Mises collapse equation:**

$$
P_c = \dfrac{2 \cdot E}{1-\nu^2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^3
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:** $t = 0.0274$ m, $D = 6$ m, $E = 2.0 \times 10^{11}$ Pa, $\nu = 0.30$.

**Step 3 — Compute the geometric ratio:**

$$
\dfrac{t}{D} = \dfrac{0.0274}{6} = 4.567 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 4 — Cube the ratio:**

$$
\left(4.567 \times 10^{-3}\right)^3 = 9.52 \times 10^{-8}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the modulus term:**

$$
\dfrac{2 \cdot 2.0 \times 10^{11}}{1 - 0.09} = \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^{11}}{0.91} = 4.40 \times 10^{11} \; Pa
$$

**Step 6 — Multiply:**

$$
P_c = 4.40 \times 10^{11} \cdot 9.52 \times 10^{-8} = 4.19 \times 10^4 \; Pa \approx 0.0419 \; MPa
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to equivalent depth:**

$$
h_{collapse} = \dfrac{P_c}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g} = \dfrac{4.19 \times 10^4}{1025 \times 9.81} \approx 4.17 \; m
$$

**Answer (b):** The collapse pressure corresponds to only **~4.2 m of seawater depth**, which is grossly inadequate for the 250 m operating depth. This confirms that an **unstiffened cylinder cannot be used**; ring frames (stiffeners) must be added to prevent buckling — a fundamental requirement in submarine hull design. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — CHOICE B

**(a)** With the aid of a neat schematic, describe the **structural layout of a modern double-hull submarine**, identifying the pressure hull, outer hull, main ballast tanks, trim tanks, and bulkheads. Explain the role of ring stiffeners. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **diesel-electric, AIP, and nuclear propulsion systems** for submarines in terms of submerged endurance, power rating, complexity, and crew habitability. State which system you would recommend for a 1500-tonne coastal patrol submarine and justify your choice. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B2(a)

**Structural Layout (description for 5 marks; schematic in text form for 2 marks):**

A modern double-hull submarine consists of **two concentric hulls separated by ballast tank spaces**:

1. **Inner pressure hull:** A cylindrical steel shell of high-yield material, capped at each end by hemispherical or ellipsoidal pressure bulkheads. It houses crew spaces, control room, machinery, and weapons. It is divided by **transverse watertight bulkheads** into 6–8 compartments for damage control.

2. **Outer hull (free-flooding fairing):** Surrounds the pressure hull at a radial standoff. During submerged operations, the space between is filled with seawater through free-flooding ports.

3. **Main ballast tanks (MBTs):** The annular space between the pressure and outer hulls, divided into port and starboard or forward/aft groups. Flooded to dive; blown empty to surface.

4. **Trim tanks:** Smaller dedicated tanks inside the pressure hull, used for fine fore-aft and athwartships trim adjustment.

5. **Ring stiffeners:** Circumferential frames welded to the outside of the pressure hull at regular intervals (typically every 0.5–1.0 m). They dramatically increase the **buckling collapse pressure** of the hull and divide it into short, stable shell panels.

**Textual schematic (cross-section, viewed from bow):**

```
[      OUTER HULL      ] [      OUTER HULL      ]
[                      ] [                      ]
[  MBT (floodable)      ] [  MBT (floodable)      ]
[                      ] [                      ]
[   ┌──────────────┐   ] [   ┌──────────────┐    ]
[   │  PRESSURE    │   ] [   │  PRESSURE    │    ]
[   │  HULL (steel)│   ] [   │  HULL (steel)│    ]
[   │   crew +     │   ] [   │   crew +     │    ]
[   │   systems    │   ] [   │   systems    │    ]
[   └──────────────┘   ] [   └──────────────┘    ]
[   Ring frames at       ] [   Ring frames at      ]
[   regular intervals    ] [   regular intervals   ]
```

**Role of ring stiffeners (2 marks):** They subdivide the hull into short shell segments, each of which has a much higher buckling resistance than a long unstiffened cylinder. This raises the collapse pressure from a few meters to several hundred meters of depth, making modern submarine operations possible.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B2(b)

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | Diesel-Electric | AIP | Nuclear |
|---|---|---|---|
| Submerged Endurance | 1–4 days | 7–21 days | Unlimited |
| Power Output | 2–5 MW (diesels), 1–3 MW (electric) | 0.1–0.5 MW (AIP) | 20–50 MW (shaft) |
| System Complexity | Low | Medium | Very high |
| Crew Habitability | Moderate (frequent snorkeling) | Good | Excellent |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Very high |
| Air Independent | No | Partial | Yes |

**Recommendation for 1500-tonne coastal patrol submarine (2 marks):**

I would recommend the **AIP (Air-Independent Propulsion) system**, specifically a Stirling-cycle or fuel-cell variant. Justification:

- The vessel is small (1500 t) and coastal — a full nuclear plant is operationally and economically unjustifiable.
- Pure diesel-electric requires frequent snorkeling, which compromises stealth in contested coastal waters.
- AIP provides 7–21 days of submerged endurance with low acoustic signature, ideal for patrol missions.
- Maintenance and infrastructure costs are reasonable for a mid-sized navy.

**Conclusion:** AIP offers the optimal balance of stealth, endurance, and cost for a coastal patrol submarine of this tonnage. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatches:** Always convert depth to meters and pressure to Pascals before substituting. Half of all lost marks in Module 4 come from inconsistent units.
> 2. **Confusing pressure hull with outer hull:** Examiners specifically test this distinction — never describe the outer hull as pressure-bearing.
> 3. **Skipping the buoyancy sign convention:** When deriving diving force, explicitly state "negative buoyancy = $W_{sub} > F_B$". Avoid verbal ambiguity.
> 4. **Omitting safety factor:** Always multiply by $SF$ when computing minimum thickness. A solution without $SF$ is considered incomplete.
> 5. **Schematic requirement:** In B1-style questions, marks are reserved for labeled diagrams. Even a hand-drawn sketch earns 1–2 marks; a missing sketch can cost up to 3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this section for last-minute revision before the KTU exam.**

### A. Structural Concepts
- A submarine's **pressure hull** is a thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel loaded externally.
- **Ring stiffeners** prevent buckling collapse; they are mandatory for any operational depth.
- The **outer hull** is a non-pressure fairing; it free-floods when submerged.
- **Transverse bulkheads** compartmentalize the pressure hull for damage control.

### B. Systems Concepts
- **MBTs** control dive/surface; flooded to dive, blown empty to surface.
- **Trim tanks** fine-tune buoyancy and longitudinal/athwartships trim.
- **Propulsion triad:** Diesel (surface/snorkel) → Generator → Batteries → Electric motor (submerged).
- **AIP and nuclear** extend submerged endurance beyond battery limits.
- **Life support** (O₂ generators, CO₂ scrubbers) is critical for human-occupied submarines.
- **Emergency blow** uses high-pressure compressed air for rapid surfacing.

### C. Key Numerical Anchors to Memorize
- $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³
- Hydrostatic gradient = **10.05 kPa per meter depth**
- HY-80 yield strength = **550 MPa**
- HY-100 yield strength = **690 MPa**
- Typical reserve buoyancy fraction for submarines = **0.30 to 0.45**

### D. Top Five Formula Recalls
1. $P_h = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$
2. $\sigma_h = P \cdot R / t$
3. $t_{min} = P \cdot R \cdot SF / \sigma_y$
4. $P_c = \dfrac{2E}{1-\nu^2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^3$
5. $V_{MBT} = \Delta W_{sub} / \rho_{sw}$

### E. Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid
- Forgetting to convert **knots to m/s** in speed calculations (1 knot = 0.5144 m/s).
- Mixing up **internal and external pressure** sign conventions.
- Drawing the pressure hull as a single skin without showing ring stiffeners.
- Confusing **MBT** with **trim tank** functions.
- Omitting units in the final answer.

### F. Module 4 KTU Bloom's Taxonomy Distribution
- **Remember (L1):** ~15% — Definitions of hull types, systems names.
- **Understand (L2):** ~25% — Why MBTs work, role of stiffeners.
- **Apply (L3):** ~30% — Wall thickness calculations, collapse pressure.
- **Analyze (L4):** ~20% — Comparative studies, system selection.
- **Evaluate (L5):** ~10% — Justification and design recommendation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST634 - SUBMARINES AND SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Submarine Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Submarine Structure** refers to the engineered load-bearing framework of a submarine, comprising the **pressure hull**, **outer hull (light hull / casing)**, **internal bulkheads**, **ring frames**, **longitudinal stiffeners**, **platforms and decks**, and the **forward and aft end closures (hemispherical/ellipsoidal domes)**. It is designed to resist **external hydrostatic pressure**, **localized point loads**, **shock loads** (from underwater explosions), and **fatigue cycling** throughout the submarine's operational life.

The structure is classified functionally into:

- **Pressure Hull (Inner Strength Hull):** The watertight, pressure-resisting envelope that protects the crew and vital machinery. For a modern nuclear submarine operating at **300 m**, the cylindrical pressure hull is typically forged from **HY-80 or HY-100 steel** with a wall thickness between **70 mm and 100 mm**.
- **Outer Hull (Casing / Fairwater):** A non-pressure-resistant streamlined shell that reduces hydrodynamic drag and provides free-flooding volume for ballast tanks and storage.
- **Internal Structure:** Composed of **transverse ring frames** (typically spaced at **600 mm** to **1000 mm** pitch), **longitudinal stringers**, **flat or corrugated bulkheads**, and **deck plating**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — "The Deep-Sea Egg":** Imagine a chicken egg dropped into the deep ocean. The eggshell (brittle but shaped as a dome) resists the crushing pressure of the water column above it. A submarine works on the **same principle**, but with two layers:
> 1. The **inner pressure hull** is like a thick steel egg (cylinder closed with domed ends) — it is the actual pressure-resisting structure.
> 2. The **outer hull** is like a teardrop-shaped aerodynamic fairing — it does **NOT** resist pressure, but it streamlines the submarine and creates space for ballast water tanks.
>
> **Why a cylinder with domed ends?** A perfect sphere is the most efficient pressure vessel, but it wastes internal volume. A cylinder with hemispherical/ellipsoidal end caps is a **compromise between strength and usable interior space** — the cylinder wall is strong in hoop tension, and the domes distribute pressure evenly into compression.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Submarine Depth Terminology (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **Operating Depth:** The maximum depth at which the submarine can operate routinely (e.g., **250 m** for typical SSK, **300–400 m** for SSN).
> - **Collapse Depth:** The depth at which the pressure hull will structurally fail catastrophically. Design rule: **Collapse Depth = (1.5 to 2.0) × Operating Depth**.
> - **Design Factor of Safety (FoS):** Typically **2.0** for pressure hull material yielding.
> - **Design Pressure:** $P_d = \rho g h_{op} \times \text{FoS}$ where $\rho$ is seawater density (**1025 kg/m³**), $g$ is **9.81 m/s²**, and $h_{op}$ is operating depth.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pressure Hull — Step-by-Step Design Logic

**Step 1 — Establish Operating Depth**
The customer specification (e.g., Indian Navy) defines the operating depth. This is the **first input** to the design process.

**Step 2 — Calculate Design Pressure**
External hydrostatic pressure is the dominant load. For seawater at depth $h$:

$$P_{ext} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$$

> **Why?** The pressure at any depth is the weight of the water column above per unit area. This is the **Pascal-Hydrostatic Principle**.

**Step 3 — Select Hull Geometry**
Submarines use a **cylinder with hemispherical/ellipsoidal end caps** because:
- Cylinders offer maximum usable internal volume.
- Spherical/ellipsoidal ends transfer hoop loads smoothly into the cylindrical section, eliminating stress concentrations.
- A 2:1 ellipsoid is **40% more efficient** than a flat end cap.

**Step 4 — Apply Membrane Stress Theory**
For a thin-walled cylinder, **hoop stress** dominates:

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P \cdot r}{t}$$

where $r$ is the mean hull radius and $t$ is the wall thickness.

**Step 5 — Apply a Material Failure Criterion**
Two criteria are valid in KTU exams:
- **Tresca (Maximum Shear Stress):** $\sigma_{yield} = 2 \cdot \tau_{max}$
- **Von Mises (Distortion Energy):** More accurate for ductile steels.

**Step 6 — Check Buckling**
Thin shells fail by **elastic instability** (buckling) before yielding. The **Bresse-Timoshenko collapse pressure** must be checked.

**Step 7 — Apply Factor of Safety**
Divide the allowable material stress by a factor of **2.0** to account for corrosion, weld defects, and uncertainty.

### 2.2 Hull Configuration Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Classification of Submarine Hulls:**

| Hull Type | Description | Pros | Cons | Used In |
|-----------|-------------|------|------|---------|
| **Single Hull** | Only one pressure hull; outer fairing is minimal | Lightweight, simple | Less safe, no redundancy | WW-II era boats (Type VII U-boat) |
| **Double Hull** | Inner pressure hull + outer pressure hull with inter-hull space used for ballast | Damage-tolerant, quieter, more storage | Wider beam, heavier | Modern SSN/SSBN (e.g., Virginia-class, Akula-class) |
| **Partial Double Hull** | Inner pressure hull with outer casing only on the upper portion (saddle tank design) | Compromise between weight and safety | Complex tank management | Russian Romeo class, early SSK |

### 2.3 Structural Framing Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Frame Systems in Submarine Hulls (Examiner's Favourite):**
> - **Ring Frames (Transverse):** Closed ring sections (T-bar, flat-bar, or built-up). Spaced at **0.6 m to 1.0 m** pitch. They maintain the **circular cross-section** under external pressure and prevent ovalisation.
> - **Longitudinal Stringers:** Run parallel to the hull axis. They increase the **moment of inertia** of the combined hull-frame section, raising the **critical buckling load**.
> - **Triangulated Brackets:** At frame-to-stiffener intersections, to prevent local stress concentrations.
> - **Bulkheads:** Ring-stiffened flat or corrugated plates dividing the submarine into **3 to 7 watertight compartments** (Forward Torpedo Room, Control Room, Reactor/Machinery Compartment, Auxiliary Machinery, Engine Room, Aft Torpedo Room).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for Submarine Structure (Kerala University Exam Ready):**

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | $P = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P r}{t}$ | Hoop (circumferential) stress in thin cylinder | Pa |
| 3 | $\sigma_{long} = \dfrac{P r}{2 t}$ | Longitudinal stress in closed cylinder | Pa |
| 4 | $t = \dfrac{P r}{\sigma_{allow}}$ | Required wall thickness (membrane theory) | m |
| 5 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6 \cdot E \cdot (t/D)^{2.5}}{(L/D)}$ | Bresse–Timoshenko collapse pressure (ring-stiffened cylinder) | Pa |
| 6 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2 \cdot E \cdot t^{2}}{R^{2} \cdot \sqrt{3(1-\nu^{2})}}$ | Inelastic buckling of unstiffened shell (von Mises) | Pa |
| 7 | $\text{FoS} = \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{working}}$ | Factor of Safety against yielding | dimensionless |
| 8 | $P_{collapse} = \text{FoS} \times P_{operating}$ | Collapse pressure requirement | Pa |
| 9 | $d_{collapse} = \text{FoS} \times d_{operating}$ | Collapse depth requirement | m |
| 10 | $M_{critical} = \dfrac{P \cdot L^{4}}{E \cdot I}$ | Ring frame collapse moment (under uniform external pressure) | N·m |

> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used in the **preliminary design phase** of every submarine and deep submersible. Naval architects iterate with these to size the hull before running detailed **Finite Element Analysis (FEA)** in software like **ABAQUS** or **NASTRAN**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hydrostatic Pressure on a Submerged Submarine

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Premise:** Consider a small horizontal surface of area $dA$ submerged at depth $h$ below the free surface of seawater. The weight of the water column above this surface exerts a force $dF$ on it.

**Step 1 — Express the weight of the water column above the area.**

The vertical column of water directly above $dA$ has:
- Volume: $dV = h \cdot dA$
- Mass: $dm = \rho_{sw} \cdot dV = \rho_{sw} \cdot h \cdot dA$

where $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$ is the density of standard seawater.

**Step 2 — Calculate the gravitational force on this column.**

$$dF = dm \cdot g = \rho_{sw} \cdot h \cdot dA \cdot g$$

where $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$ is the acceleration due to gravity.

**Step 3 — Divide by area to get pressure.**

Since pressure is force per unit area, we obtain:

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{dF}{dA} \\
P &= \frac{\rho_{sw} \cdot h \cdot dA \cdot g}{dA} \\
P &= \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Account for atmospheric pressure at the free surface.**

For a submarine, we also add the atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} \approx 101.3 \text{ kPa}$ acting on the sea surface:

$$P_{absolute} = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 5 — Convert to engineering units.**

For $h$ in meters and $P$ in bar, since $1 \text{ bar} = 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$:

$$P \text{ (bar)} = \frac{101325 + 1025 \times 9.81 \times h}{10^{5}} \approx \frac{100 + 0.1006 \cdot h}{10}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example.**

For a submarine at operating depth $h = 300 \text{ m}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{ext} &= 101325 + 1025 \times 9.81 \times 300 \\
P_{ext} &= 101325 + 3,016,575 \\
P_{ext} &\approx 3.12 \text{ MPa} \approx 31.2 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Every additional **10 m** of depth adds approximately **1 bar (100 kPa)** of pressure. This is why submarine design is dominated by **compressive external loading** — the structure is essentially being crushed inward from all sides simultaneously.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Hoop Stress in a Thin-Walled Cylindrical Pressure Hull

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Premise:** Consider a cylindrical pressure hull of mean radius $r$ and wall thickness $t$, with internal gauge pressure $P$ (or equivalently, treat external pressure $P$ on a vacuum-interior hull). The cylinder is assumed **thin-walled** ($t/r < 0.05$) and loaded symmetrically.

**Step 1 — Consider equilibrium of a half-cylinder element.**

Cut the cylinder along a diametrical plane. The external pressure $P$ acts on the projected area $2r \cdot L$ (where $L$ is the length of the section considered). The hoop stress $\sigma_h$ acts on the two cut edges, each of area $t \cdot L$.

**Step 2 — Apply force equilibrium.**

The total outward force on the half-cylinder (from internal pressure) must equal the total restoring force from hoop stress:

$$P \cdot (2r \cdot L) = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot (t \cdot L)$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

Cancelling $2L$ on both sides:

$$P \cdot r = \sigma_h \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Solve for hoop stress.**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot r}{t}$$

**Step 5 — For a submarine in compression (external crushing):**

Since the hull is loaded by **external** pressure, $\sigma_h$ becomes **compressive**. The wall thickness required to keep $\sigma_h$ below the allowable compressive stress is:

$$t \geq \frac{P \cdot r}{\sigma_{allow}}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example for a typical SSK.**

Given: Operating depth $h = 250 \text{ m}$, hull radius $r = 3.0 \text{ m}$, allowable compressive stress for HY-80 steel $\sigma_{allow} = 550 \text{ MPa / 2} = 275 \text{ MPa}$ (with FoS = 2).

First, calculate external pressure:

$$P = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 250 \approx 2.51 \text{ MPa}$$

Then, calculate required thickness:

$$t = \frac{2.51 \times 10^{6} \times 3.0}{275 \times 10^{6}} \approx 0.0274 \text{ m} \approx 27.4 \text{ mm}$$

> **Note:** This is a **membrane (membrane-stress) thickness**. In practice, due to buckling concerns, the actual thickness is increased to **70–80 mm** for a real HY-80 pressure hull. The discrepancy arises because **buckling controls the design** — not yielding.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Critical Buckling Pressure of a Ring-Stiffened Cylinder (Bresse–Timoshenko)

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Premise:** A long cylinder of radius $R$, wall thickness $t$, length $L$ between ring frames, and Young's modulus $E$ will buckle elastically into **multiple lobes** around its circumference. We assume the cylinder is **ring-stiffened** (not stiffened longitudinally), which is the classic case analyzed by Bresse (1866) and refined by Timoshenko.

**Step 1 — Define the lobe geometry.**

When a cylinder buckles under external pressure, it deforms into $n$ lobes around its circumference and $m$ half-waves along its axis. The radial deflection is:

$$w = A \cdot \cos\left(\frac{m \pi x}{L}\right) \cdot \cos(n \theta)$$

where $n$ is the number of circumferential waves, $m$ is the number of axial half-waves.

**Step 2 — Express the strain energy of bending.**

For a thin shell, the bending strain energy per unit length is:

$$U_{bend} = \frac{D}{2} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{L} \left[\left(\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial y^{2}}\right)^{2} - 2(1-\nu)\left(\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}} \cdot \frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial y^{2}} - \left(\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x \partial y}\right)^{2}\right)\right] dx \cdot R \, d\theta$$

where $D = \dfrac{E t^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$ is the **flexural rigidity** of the shell.

**Step 3 — Express the work done by external pressure.**

The work done by external pressure $P$ as the shell deforms by $w$ is:

$$W = \frac{P}{2} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{L} w^{2} \cdot R \, d\theta \, dx$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the assumed mode shape and integrate.**

After substitution and integration, the integrals evaluate to:

$$\begin{aligned}
U_{bend} &= \frac{\pi R D L A^{2}}{4} \left[\left(\frac{m \pi}{L}\right)^{4} + 2\left(\frac{m \pi}{L}\right)^{2} \cdot \frac{n^{2}}{R^{2}} + \frac{n^{4}}{R^{4}}\right] \\
W &= \frac{\pi R L A^{2}}{4} \cdot P
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the principle of stationary potential energy.**

For equilibrium at the buckling load, the second variation of total potential energy vanishes, which gives the **critical pressure** for a given $n$ and $m$:

$$P_{cr}(n,m) = D \cdot \left[\left(\frac{m \pi}{L}\right)^{2} + \frac{n^{2}}{R^{2}}\right]^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Find the minimum pressure over all integer $m$ and $n$.**

For long cylinders ($L \gg R$), the axial wave number $m = 1$ is optimal. Substituting $m = 1$:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{D}{R^{2}} \cdot \left(\frac{\pi^{2} R^{2}}{L^{2}} + n^{2}\right)^{2}$$

Minimizing with respect to $n$ (treating $n$ as continuous for the lower bound) and substituting $D = E t^{3} / [12(1-\nu^{2})]$:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \cdot E}{1 - \nu^{2}} \cdot \left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^{2.5} \cdot \frac{D}{L}$$

For steel with $\nu = 0.3$, the **Bresse–Timoshenko collapse pressure** reduces to:

$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \cdot E \cdot (t/D)^{2.5}}{(L/D)}}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that $P_{cr}$ is **proportional to $(t/D)^{2.5}$** — not to $t/D$. This is why simply increasing wall thickness gives **diminishing returns**. To achieve higher collapse depths, designers must also **reduce frame spacing $L$** (i.e., add more ring frames), which is why modern submarines have closely-spaced ring frames every 600–800 mm.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison of Failure Modes in a Submarine Pressure Hull (Kerala University Exam Table):**

| Failure Mode | Triggering Condition | Governing Parameter | Design Mitigation |
|--------------|----------------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **Material Yielding** | $\sigma_{hoop} \geq \sigma_{yield}$ | $t/r$ ratio | Increase wall thickness; use HY-100 or HY-130 steel |
| **Elastic Buckling** | $P_{ext} \geq P_{cr}$ | $(t/R)^{2.5}$ and $L/R$ | Add ring frames; reduce $L$ |
| **Plastic Collapse** | Pressure exceeds elastic limit, large deformations | Strain-hardening modulus | Use ductile steels (HY-series) |
| **Fatigue Cracking** | Cyclic loading (repeated dives) | Stress range $\Delta K$ | Weld quality control; radiography of welds |
| **Corrosion** | Long-term seawater exposure | Wall loss rate | Cathodic protection; coatings |
| **Shock Loading** | Underwater explosion (UNDEX) | Peak pressure, impulse | Double hull; shock-isolated mountings |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** 
*Distinguish between a single hull and a double hull submarine. Mention one advantage of each.*

**Model Answer:**

> A **single hull submarine** has only one structural envelope — the pressure hull itself — which is also the outer fairing. Its advantage is **low displacement and simpler construction**, leading to reduced cost.

> A **double hull submarine** has an inner pressure hull surrounded by an outer hull, with an inter-hull space used for ballast tanks and auxiliary storage. Its advantages are **superior damage tolerance** (one hull can be breached without flooding the boat), **reduced radiated noise** (the inter-hull space dampens machinery noise), and **greater payload capacity** for sonar arrays and missiles.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is meant by the "collapse depth" of a submarine, and how is it related to the operating depth?*

**Model Answer:**

> **Collapse depth** is the depth at which the pressure hull undergoes **catastrophic structural failure** — typically elastic or plastic buckling — under the external hydrostatic pressure. Beyond this depth, the hull can no longer maintain its integrity, and the submarine is crushed.

> The relationship is governed by a design margin. The **collapse depth is generally 1.5 to 2.0 times the operating depth**. For a submarine rated to operate at **300 m**, the collapse depth is designed to be at least **450–600 m**. This provides a safety margin against material defects, corrosion, fatigue, and operational errors.

> Mathematically: $d_{collapse} = \text{FoS} \times d_{operating}$, where the typical **Factor of Safety (FoS)** is **2.0**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model — 7 Marks]**
*With the help of a neat sketch, explain the structural arrangement of a modern nuclear submarine. List the materials used for the pressure hull and justify the choice.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sketch of the structural arrangement:**

The structural arrangement consists of the following components, from outside to inside:

1. **Outer Hull (Casing / Fairwater):** A non-pressure-resistant, streamlined shell that reduces hydrodynamic drag and provides free-flooding volume for ballast tanks.
2. **Pressure Hull (Inner Strength Hull):** The primary load-bearing envelope, a **cylindrical section with hemispherical/ellipsoidal end closures**.
3. **Ring Frames:** Closed ring sections (typically T-bars) at **0.6 to 1.0 m pitch**, supporting the cylindrical shell against external pressure.
4. **Longitudinal Stringers:** Run along the length of the pressure hull, increasing the section's moment of inertia.
5. **Transverse Bulkheads:** Ring-stiffened flat or corrugated plates dividing the submarine into **3 to 7 watertight compartments** — forward torpedo room, control room, reactor compartment, machinery spaces, engine room, and aft torpedo room.
6. **Decks and Platforms:** Internal horizontal plating dividing each compartment vertically for operational use.

**Step 2 — Materials for the Pressure Hull:**

| Material | Yield Strength | Application |
|----------|----------------|-------------|
| **HY-80 Steel** | 550 MPa (80 ksi) | Operating depths up to 300 m |
| **HY-100 Steel** | 690 MPa (100 ksi) | Operating depths 300–400 m |
| **HY-130 Steel** | 900 MPa (130 ksi) | Deep-diving research submarines |
| **Titanium Alloy (Ti-6Al-4V)** | 880 MPa | Russian Alfa-class (deep, non-magnetic) |
| **Austenitic Stainless Steel (Type 304)** | 215 MPa | Some early deep submersibles |

**Step 3 — Justification for material choice:**

HY-80 and HY-100 are **nickel-copper-molybdenum low-alloy steels** with high yield strength, excellent weldability (critical for thick-section fabrication), and good toughness at low temperatures. The pressure hull must withstand **compressive hoop stresses** of hundreds of MPa while remaining **weldable in thick sections** — ordinary mild steel would require an impractically thick hull.
