Wait — this needs correction. The full Von Mises result includes a membrane prebuckling stress that *lowers* the effective buckling load. The corrected classical result (accounting for prebuckling deformation) is:

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{2 \, E}{\sqrt{3(1-\nu^2)}} \cdot \frac{t}{R}
$$

Let us derive this rigorously. The prebuckling *average* hoop compressive stress in a long cylinder is $\sigma_h = P R / t$. This pre-stress softens the shell. A refined energy balance including the membrane work done by the prebuckling stress gives the corrected expression, which after minimization over the integer $n$ and asymptotic matching yields the *Von Mises* equation:

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{2 E}{\sqrt{3(1-\nu^2)}} \cdot \frac{t}{R}
$$

**Numerical substitution for steel** ($\nu = 0.30$):

$$
\frac{2}{\sqrt{3(1-0.30^2)}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3 \times 0.91}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{2.73}} = \frac{2}{1.652} \approx 1.21
$$

So the practical formula for an infinitely long steel cylinder is:

$$
P_{cr} \approx 1.21 \, E \left(\frac{t}{R}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for hull shape:** This formula shows $P_{cr}$ is *linear* in $t/R$, not in $(t/R)^2$ as one might naively guess. Halving the thickness cuts the collapse pressure in half. Doubling the radius halves it. This is why *small-diameter* spherical hulls (Alvin: 2.1 m dia, 49 mm Ti wall) survive at 4,500 m, while large-diameter cylinders (Ohio-class: 10 m dia, ≈ 80 mm steel wall) are limited to ≈ 300 m without excessive weight.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A cylindrical submarine pressure hull has mean radius $R = 4.0$ m, wall thickness $t = 0.060$ m, and length $L = 60$ m. The hull material is HY-80 steel with $E = 207$ GPa and $\nu = 0.30$. Compute:
> (a) The critical buckling pressure treating the hull as an *infinitely long* cylinder.
> (b) The critical buckling pressure treating the hull as a *short* cylinder.
> (c) The maximum safe operating depth with a safety factor of 1.5 against the *short-cylinder* collapse pressure.

#### Part (a) — Long Cylinder

$$
P_{cr,\text{long}} = \frac{2 E}{\sqrt{3(1-\nu^2)}} \cdot \frac{t}{R}
$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$
P_{cr,\text{long}} = \frac{2 \times 207 \times 10^9}{\sqrt{3 \times (1-0.09)}} \times \frac{0.060}{4.0}
$$

$$
= \frac{414 \times 10^9}{1.652} \times 0.015
$$

$$
= 2.506 \times 10^{11} \times 0.015 = 3.76 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa} = 3.76 \text{ GPa}
$$

This enormous value (3.76 GPa = 38,600 bar ≈ 386 km of water) is the *theoretical* upper limit for an infinitely long shell — and it confirms that an *unstiffened* long cylinder would, in principle, be capable of extreme depths. The reason military submarines are not unlimited-depth vehicles is that the *inter-ring* length (between stiffeners) is what governs buckling, not the full hull length — leading us to the *short-cylinder* formula.

#### Part (b) — Short Cylinder (inter-ring buckling)

The distance between ring stiffeners is typically $L \approx 1.0$ m. The Von Mises short-cylinder formula:

$$
P_{cr,\text{short}} = \frac{2.6 \, E}{\sqrt{1-\nu^2}} \cdot \left(\frac{t}{R}\right)^{5/2} \cdot \frac{R}{L}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\frac{2.6 \times 207 \times 10^9}{\sqrt{1-0.09}} = \frac{538.2 \times 10^9}{0.9539} = 5.642 \times 10^{11} \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\left(\frac{0.060}{4.0}\right)^{5/2} = (0.015)^{2.5} = 2.764 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\frac{R}{L} = \frac{4.0}{1.0} = 4.0
$$

Multiplying:

$$
P_{cr,\text{short}} = 5.642 \times 10^{11} \times 2.764 \times 10^{-5} \times 4.0
$$

$$
= 5.642 \times 10^{11} \times 1.106 \times 10^{-4} = 6.24 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} \approx 62.4 \text{ MPa}
$$

#### Part (c) — Maximum Safe Depth

$$
P_{allow} = \frac{P_{cr,\text{short}}}{SF} = \frac{62.4}{1.5} = 41.6 \text{ MPa}
$$

Using $P = \rho g h$:

$$
h_{max} = \frac{P_{allow}}{\rho_{sw} g} = \frac{41.6 \times 10^6}{1025 \times 9.81} = \frac{41.6 \times 10^6}{10{,}055} \approx 4{,}137 \text{ m}
$$

> **Result:** The un-stiffened (with $L = 1$ m inter-ring spacing) cylindrical hull could in principle operate to ≈ 4,100 m. In practice, **imperfections knock down** the theoretical value by a factor of ≈ 3–5, so the real collapse depth is closer to 1,000–1,500 m, which matches the actual crush depth of modern SSNs operating at ≈ 600 m.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison: Cylinder vs. Sphere

For a sphere of the same radius $R$ and thickness $t$:

$$
P_{cr,\text{sphere}} = \frac{2 E}{\sqrt{3(1-\nu^2)}} \cdot \frac{t}{R} = P_{cr,\text{long cylinder}}
$$

**Wait — they are equal?** Yes, the *membrane* buckling pressure of a complete sphere equals that of an infinite cylinder. The reason spheres are still preferred for *very deep* diving is that the **shell of revolution** stabilizes all buckling modes (there is no preferred lobe direction), whereas the cylinder has weak axial modes. With **stiffening rings**, the cylinder can be made to approach sphere-like performance, but only at the cost of added weight.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** A submarine hull is a *ring-stiffened cylinder* (not a bare cylinder). The role of the rings is to break the shell into short panels, forcing the buckling mode to a higher circumferential wave number $n$, which raises $P_{cr}$ dramatically. The trade-off: more rings = more steel = heavier = less payload.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Hull-Shape Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Submarine Mission Profile] --> B{Max Operating Depth}
    B -->|Depth less than 600 m| C[Cylindrical HY-80 Steel Hull]
    B -->|Depth 600 m to 2000 m| D[Ring-Stiffened Cylinder with HY-100 Steel]
    B -->|Depth greater than 2000 m| E[Titanium Ti-6Al-4V Hull]

    C --> C1[Long cylinder buckling formula]
    C --> C2[Inter-ring short cylinder formula]
    C --> C3[Stringer-stiffened plating]

    D --> D1[Closer ring spacing]
    D --> D2[HAS elliptical endcaps]
    D --> D3[Thicker plate sections]

    E --> E1{Geometry}
    E1 --> E2[Spherical pressure hull]
    E1 --> E3[Cylindrical with hemispherical endcaps]

    E2 --> E3a[Highest Pcr per unit weight]
    E3 --> E3b[Volume optimization for crew]

    C3 --> F[Collapse Depth]
    D3 --> F
    E3a --> F
    E3b --> F

    F --> G[Safety Factor 1.5 to 2.0]
    G --> H[Maximum Operating Depth]
    H --> I[Submerged Displacement and Payload Capacity]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Buckling Mode Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[External Hydrostatic Pressure P] --> M{P increases}
    M -->|Low P| L1[Elastic Ovalization n equals 2]
    M -->|Medium P| L2[Higher Mode n equals 3 4 5]
    M -->|Critical P| L3[Elastic Buckling Collapse]
    M -->|Above Pcr| L4[Plastic Collapse]

    L1 --> R1[Countermeasure: Ring Stiffeners]
    L2 --> R2[Countermeasure: Closer Ring Spacing]
    L3 --> R3[Countermeasure: Thicker Plate or Higher Strength Steel]
    L4 --> R4[Design Forbidden Region]

    R1 --> O[Increase Critical Collapse Depth]
    R2 --> O
    R3 --> O
```

### 4.3 Hull-Shape Comparison Table

| Feature | Cylinder | Sphere | Ellipsoid |
|---|---|---|---|
| Buckling efficiency | Moderate (with rings) | Highest | Intermediate |
| Internal volume per unit length | Highest | Lowest | Medium |
| Hydrodynamic drag | Low (with fairing) | High | Low–Medium |
| Manufacturability | Easy (rolled plate) | Difficult (forged hemispheres) | Moderate |
| Material used | HY-80 / HY-100 steel | Titanium / Acrylic | Steel |
| Typical depth | 300–600 m | 4,000–11,000 m | Bow/stern caps |
| Application | Military submarines | Research submersibles | End closures |

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagrams above are the recommended Mermaid adaptations for this topic. A physical drawing of a ring-stiffened cylindrical shell with 2-lobe and 3-lobe buckling modes can be sketched in student notes, but is not amenable to Mermaid syntax.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*Why is a spherical shape preferred over a cylindrical shape for deep-diving submersible pressure hulls, even though both are made of the same material and weight?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A spherical pressure hull is more efficient at resisting external hydrostatic pressure than a cylinder of the same radius, wall thickness, and material. The reasons are:
1. **(1 mark)** A sphere has *biaxial curvature* at every point, so external pressure is carried by uniform biaxial membrane stress. A cylinder has curvature only in the hoop direction, leaving it prone to *ovalization* (2-lobe elastic buckling) between stiffeners.
2. **(1 mark)** The Von Mises critical buckling pressure for a sphere, $P_{cr,sph} = \dfrac{2E}{\sqrt{3(1-\nu^2)}} \cdot \dfrac{t}{R}$, is the highest achievable for any shell geometry of equal weight. The cylinder must be *ring-stiffened* to approach this performance.
3. **(1 mark)** Therefore, for *very deep* diving (4,000 m+), a sphere of titanium gives the highest collapse pressure per unit mass. The cylinder is preferred only when long internal length is required (e.g., missile tubes), and only for shallower depths.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Define "ring stiffener" in a submarine pressure hull and explain its role in preventing elastic buckling.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **(1 mark)** A *ring stiffener* (or ring frame) is a circumferential T- or L-section structural member welded to the inside of a cylindrical pressure hull at regular intervals along the hull length.
2. **(1 mark)** Its function is to break the long cylinder into a series of *shorter* cylindrical panels (of length equal to the ring spacing $L$). Because the short-cylinder Von Mises buckling pressure $P_{cr}$ scales linearly with $R/L$, reducing $L$ by adding more rings *raises* the critical collapse pressure.
3. **(1 mark)** The rings also resist the *ovalization* tendency of the shell by providing hoop-direction stiffness, forcing the buckling mode to a higher lobe number $n$ (e.g., $n=3$ or 4 instead of $n=2$), which is structurally stable.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Format:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]**

*(a) [7 marks]* With the aid of the Von Mises equation, derive the expression for the **critical buckling pressure of an infinitely long thin cylindrical shell** under uniform external hydrostatic pressure. State all assumptions clearly.

*(b) [7 marks]* A submarine pressure hull is a cylinder of mean radius $R = 3.5$ m and wall thickness $t = 0.045$ m, fabricated from HY-80 steel ($E = 207$ GPa, $\nu = 0.30$). Ring stiffeners are fitted at a spacing of $L = 0.8$ m.
- (i) Calculate the *short-cylinder* critical buckling pressure $P_{cr}$.
- (ii) Determine the *maximum safe operating depth* with a factor of safety of **2.0**.
- (iii) If the design team proposes replacing the steel with titanium (Ti-6Al-4V, $E = 114$ GPa, $\nu = 0.34$) of the same thickness, will the collapse depth increase or decrease? Justify with a numerical estimate.

---

**Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]**

**[1 mark]** State assumptions: thin shell ($t \ll R$); isotropic linear-elastic material; uniform external pressure $P$; no end-cap effects; small deflections.

**[2 marks]** Write the strain energy of bending and the work done by external pressure:

$$
U_b = \frac{D_s}{2} \int \int \left\{ (\nabla^2 w)^2 - 2(1-\nu)[\ldots] \right\} R\, d\theta\, dx, \quad D_s = \frac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}
$$

$$
W = \frac{P}{2} \int \int \left[ \left(\frac{\partial w}{\partial x}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{1}{R}\frac{\partial w}{\partial \theta}\right)^2 + 2w + \frac{w^2}{R^2} \right] R\, d\theta\, dx
$$

**[2 marks]** Assume trial buckled shape $w = A\cos(n\theta)$ for an infinitely long shell, substitute, and equate $U_b = W$ (Rayleigh–Ritz):

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2) R^3} \, n^2(n^2-1)
$$

**[1 mark]** Apply prebuckling membrane-stress correction (Von Mises refinement) and minimize over $n$ for the long-cylinder asymptotic case to obtain:

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{2 E}{\sqrt{3(1-\nu^2)}} \cdot \frac{t}{R}
$$

**[1 mark]** Comment: $P_{cr}$ is *linear* in $t/R$, indicating that a thinner shell collapses proportionally faster, justifying the need for stiffeners to keep $t/R$ high in the loaded state.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]**

**(i) Short-cylinder buckling pressure [2 marks]:**

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \, E}{\sqrt{1-\nu^2}} \cdot \left(\frac{t}{R}\right)^{5/2} \cdot \frac{R}{L}
$$

Step 1:

$$
\frac{2.6 \times 207 \times 10^9}{\sqrt{1-0.09}} = \frac{538.2 \times 10^9}{0.9539} = 5.642 \times 10^{11} \text{ Pa}
$$

Step 2:

$$
\left(\frac{0.045}{3.5}\right)^{5/2} = (0.01286)^{2.5} = 1.870 \times 10^{-5}
$$

Step 3:

$$
\frac{R}{L} = \frac{3.5}{0.8} = 4.375
$$

Step 4:

$$
P_{cr} = 5.642 \times 10^{11} \times 1.870 \times 10^{-5} \times 4.375 = 4.615 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} \approx 46.2 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(ii) Maximum safe depth [2 marks]:**

$$
P_{allow} = \frac{P_{cr}}{SF} = \frac{46.2}{2.0} = 23.1 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
h_{max} = \frac{23.1 \times 10^6}{1025 \times 9.81} = \frac{23.1 \times 10^6}{10{,}055} \approx 2{,}297 \text{ m}
$$

**(iii) Titanium substitution [3 marks]:**

New short-cylinder buckling pressure:

$$
P_{cr,Ti} = \frac{2.6 \times 114 \times 10^9}{\sqrt{1-0.34^2}} \times 1.870 \times 10^{-5} \times 4.375
$$

Step 1:

$$
\frac{2.6 \times 114 \times 10^9}{\sqrt{0.8844}} = \frac{296.4 \times 10^9}{0.9404} = 3.152 \times 10^{11} \text{ Pa}
$$

Step 2:

$$
P_{cr,Ti} = 3.152 \times 10^{11} \times 1.870 \times 10^{-5} \times 4.375 = 2.580 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} \approx 25.8 \text{ MPa}
$$

Comparison: $P_{cr,Ti} / P_{cr,steel} = 25.8 / 46.2 = 0.558$. The collapse pressure *decreases by ≈ 44%* because titanium's Young's modulus (114 GPa) is only ≈ 55% that of steel. Although titanium is *stronger* per unit weight, its *elastic stiffness* is lower, so a thin titanium hull buckles earlier. **(Conclusion: depth would decrease — titanium is used for deep-diving *spheres* where the geometry compensates, not for cylinders where stiffness dominates.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]**

*(a) [7 marks]* Discuss the **three principal pressure hull geometries** (cylindrical, spherical, ellipsoidal) used in modern submarines and submersibles, with reference to:
- Internal volume efficiency
- Hydrodynamic performance
- Manufacturing complexity
- Typical depth rating

*(b) [7 marks]* A deep-diving *spherical* titanium hull has mean radius $R = 1.05$ m and wall thickness $t = 0.049$ m. The material properties are $E = 114$ GPa and $\nu = 0.34$. Using the Von Mises formula for a sphere, calculate:
- (i) The theoretical critical collapse pressure.
- (ii) Applying a *knockdown factor* of $K = 0.30$ (to account for geometric imperfections), determine the allowable collapse pressure.
- (iii) Compute the safe operating depth with a safety factor of 1.5 and compare with the depth rating of the *Alvin* submersible (4,500 m).

---

**Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — Comparison [7 marks]**

Allocate marks as follows:
- **(2 marks) Cylindrical hull** — most common, used in all military submarines; provides the largest internal volume per unit length for accommodating crew, machinery, and missile tubes; prone to *elastic ovalization* (2-lobe buckling) between ring stiffeners; rated typically 300–600 m for HY-80 steel.
- **(2 marks) Spherical hull** — mathematically the most efficient shape for resisting external pressure; every point carries uniform biaxial stress; lowest drag-adjusted weight; used in bathyscaphes and deep-submergence vehicles; rated up to 11,000 m (e.g., *DSV Limiting Factor*).
- **(2 marks) Ellipsoidal hull (HAS — Hemispherical / Asymmetric / Spherical endcaps)** — used as bow and stern closures of cylindrical hulls; provide smooth hydrodynamic entry; manufactured by hot-press forging or spin-forming; structurally efficient as biaxial curvature is preserved.
- **(1 mark)** Concluding summary: the geometry must match the *mission* — long hulls for transit/military, spheres for science/exploration.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]**

**(i) Theoretical critical collapse pressure [3 marks]:**

For a sphere:

$$
P_{cr,sph} = \frac{2 E}{\sqrt{3(1-\nu^2)}} \cdot \frac{t}{R}
$$

Step 1:

$$
\sqrt{3(1-\nu^2)} = \sqrt{3 \times (1-0.1156)} = \sqrt{3 \times 0.8844} = \sqrt{2.6532} = 1.629
$$

Step 2:

$$
\frac{2 \times 114 \times 10^9}{1.629} = \frac{228 \times 10^9}{1.629} = 1.400 \times 10^{11} \text{ Pa}
$$

Step 3:

$$
P_{cr,sph} = 1.400 \times 10^{11} \times \frac{0.049}{1.05} = 1.400 \times 10^{11} \times 0.04667 = 6.534 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa} \approx 6.53 \text{ GPa}
$$

**(ii) Allowable collapse pressure with knockdown factor [2 marks]:**

$$
P_{allow} = K \cdot P_{cr,sph} = 0.30 \times 6.53 \text{ GPa} = 1.96 \text{ GPa} = 1{,}960 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(iii) Safe operating depth [2 marks]:**

$$
P_{safe} = \frac{P_{allow}}{SF} = \frac{1{,}960}{1.5} = 1{,}307 \text{ MPa} = 1.307 \text{ GPa}
$$

$$
h_{safe} = \frac{1.307 \times 10^9}{1025 \times 9.81} = \frac{1.307 \times 10^9}{10{,}055} \approx 130{,}000 \text{ m}
$$

> **Comment:** The theoretical depth is ≈ **130 km** of water — clearly impossible since no ocean is that deep (Mariana Trench ≈ 11,000 m). The result confirms that *theoretical* Von Mises pressures vastly overestimate real collapse depths. The knockdown factor $K$ is essential to bring the analysis in line with reality. After applying $K = 0.30$ and $SF = 1.5$, the design comfortably accommodates *Alvin*'s 4,500 m rating (which corresponds to a hydrostatic pressure of only ≈ 45 MPa — far below $P_{allow} = 1{,}960$ MPa). The huge margin reflects the conservatism built into KTU-style analytical estimates.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — "Shape of the Pressure Vessel"**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{1-\nu^2}$ term in the Von Mises formula.** Many students write $P_{cr} = 2E \cdot t/R$, which *overestimates* the buckling pressure by ≈ 15% for steel. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Confusing long-cylinder and short-cylinder formulas.** The "long cylinder" formula has $P_{cr} \propto t/R$, while the "short cylinder" formula has $P_{cr} \propto (t/R)^{5/2} \cdot (R/L)$. KTU examiners routinely set numerical problems where students apply the *wrong* formula and lose 3–4 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring the prebuckling stress correction.** The naive derivation gives $P_{cr} \propto (t/R)^3$ (from the $D_s \cdot t^3$ term), which is a *stability* answer, not a *strength* answer. Examiners expect the Von Mises result with the $2/\sqrt{3(1-\nu^2)}$ factor.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency.** Hydrostatic pressure must be in **Pa**, depth in **m**, and density in **kg/m³**. Mixing MPa and bar (1 bar = 0.1 MPa) is a frequent source of silly errors.
> 5. **Forgetting the knockdown factor.** Real collapse pressures are 3–5× *lower* than the Von Mises theoretical value because of geometric imperfections, residual stresses, and welding distortions. In KTU Part B problems, always multiply by $K \approx 0.25$–$0.40$ before applying the safety factor.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Shape of the Pressure Vessel**

- **Why shape matters:** Geometry (not material strength) governs the collapse of thin-walled submarine hulls. The pressure hull must resist *elastic buckling*, not yielding.
- **Three primary shapes:** Cylinder (volume), Sphere (depth), Ellipsoid (transition). Choice depends on mission profile.
- **Hydrostatic pressure gradient:** **0.1 MPa per 10 m** of seawater. Memorize this.
- **Hoop stress in a cylinder (internal pressure):** $\sigma_h = pD/(2t)$.
- **Long-cylinder Von Mises buckling pressure:** $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2E}{\sqrt{3(1-\nu^2)}} \cdot \dfrac{t}{R}$.
- **Short-cylinder Von Mises buckling pressure:** $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6 E}{\sqrt{1-\nu^2}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{R}\right)^{5/2} \cdot \dfrac{R}{L}$.
- **Bryan (simplified) formula:** $P_{cr} = 2.6 E (t/D)^{2.5}$ — useful for quick estimates when $\nu$ is ignored.
- **Sphere is most efficient** per unit weight for deep diving, but its $P_{cr}$ *equals* that of a long cylinder of the same $t/R$. Its advantage is *isotropic stability* — no preferred buckling direction.
- **Ring stiffeners** are mandatory for cylindrical hulls — they break the shell into short panels, raising $P_{cr}$ linearly with $1/L$.
- **Knockdown factor** $K \approx 0.25$–$0.40$ must be applied to theoretical $P_{cr}$ before applying a safety factor (typically 1.5–2.0).
- **Material matters** but less than shape: HY-80 steel is used for cylinders, titanium (Ti-6Al-4V) for deep-diving spheres. Acrylic is used for viewports.
- **Safety factor practice** at KTU: Part B problems almost always ask for "safe operating depth" — so the chain is $P_{cr} \to K \cdot P_{cr} \to P_{allow} = P_{cr}/SF \to h_{max} = P_{allow}/(\rho g)$.
- **Common exam trick:** KTU may quote a result in bar (1 bar ≈ 0.1 MPa) and expect the answer in meters of seawater, or vice-versa. Be careful with unit conversions.
- **Theoretical vs. real collapse depth:** Always show the theoretical value first, apply $K$, then the SF, *then* compute depth. This three-step structure is exactly what KTU examiners reward with full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST634 - SUBMARINES AND SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Buckling Deformation of the Shell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Buckling Deformation of the Shell** is defined as a sudden, large-amplitude, geometric instability failure mode of a thin-walled shell structure (cylindrical, conical, or spherical pressure hull of a submarine/submersible) that occurs when the applied external hydrostatic pressure reaches a critical value known as the **Critical Buckling Pressure (p_cr)**, even though the actual compressive stress in the shell wall remains well below the material's yield strength.

In the context of **submarine pressure hull design**, buckling is a *geometric* (stability) failure rather than a *strength* (material yield) failure. The pressure hull of a deep-diving submersible is typically a **thin-walled cylindrical shell** (radius $R$, wall thickness $t$, length $L$) made of high-tensile steel (e.g., HY-80, HY-100) and is subjected to uniform external pressure $p$ due to the surrounding seawater.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Criterion**: The structure fails not because the material gives up, but because its *shape* (curvature, geometry) suddenly gives way, like an inverted pop can crushed by finger pressure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine holding an empty, **soda/drink can (350 ml)** vertically and pressing the thumb gently on its open end... It suddenly collapses with a sharp *crunch*, producing diamond-shaped facets along its side. Now examine the wall: the **aluminium** is barely scratched. The wall thickness is around **0.1 mm**, but the can still took only a few kilograms of force to buckle. This is exactly the failure mode threatening a submarine's pressure hull, scaled up to **75–100 mm thick** steel and depths of **300–1000+ metres**.

Three intuitive "Why" reasons for shell buckling:

1. **Curvature loss**: A thin shell relies on its curved geometry to redistribute pressure as membrane (in-plane) tension. When $p$ reaches a threshold, the curvature flattens locally, and the geometry "snaps" into a non-developable deformation pattern.
2. **Slenderness**: The radius-to-thickness ratio ($R/t$) of submarine hulls is very large (**50–150**). Slender structures are inherently unstable.
3. **Imperfections matter**: Real shells deviate from perfect circles (ovality, weld dimples). These imperfections *lower* the actual buckling pressure well below the theoretical classical value — a phenomenon captured by **knock-down factors (KDF)** in KTU/US Navy practice.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Importance |
|-----------|--------|---------------|------------|
| Young's Modulus (Steel) | $E$ | **$2.07 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm²** | Governs elastic stiffness |
| Poisson's Ratio | $\nu$ | **0.30** | Enters the flexural rigidity |
| Yield Stress (HY-80) | $\sigma_y$ | **552 N/mm²** | Defines elastoplastic threshold |
| Density of Seawater | $\rho_{sw}$ | **1025 kg/m³** | Hydrostatic head: $p = \rho_{sw} g h$ |
| Radius/Thickness ratio | $R/t$ | **20 – 150** | Determines buckling class |
| Length/Radius ratio | $L/R$ | **1 – 20** | Determines buckling class |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hydrostatic Pressure Conversion**: $p_{ext}$ (in MPa) = $0.01005 \times h$ (in metres). Therefore **300 m depth** ≈ **3.0 MPa** external pressure on the hull.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Buckling Modes in a Submarine Pressure Hull

A thin cylindrical shell under external hydrostatic pressure can collapse in three distinct geometrical modes. The actual mode is dictated by the **$L/R$** and **$R/t$** ratios.

**Mode 1 — Axisymmetric (Ring) Buckling** *(Short Cylinders, $L/R < 1.7$):*
- Multiple ring-shaped depressions form around the circumference.
- Each ring drops inward by an amount on the order of $t$.
- Associated with **membrane compression** plus **circumferential bending**.

**Mode 2 — Non-Axisymmetric (Diamond/Yoshimura) Buckling** *(Intermediate Cylinders, $1.7 < L/R < 6$):*
- The wall deforms into a pattern of inward and outward lobes (like a checkerboard / the soda can).
- This is the **classic shell-buckling mode** and the one observed in most submarine-scale shells.
- General form: $m$ circumferential waves, $n$ axial half-waves.

**Mode 3 — Euler (Column) Buckling** *(Long Cylinders, $L/R > 6$):*
- The cylinder as a whole bends like a column.
- Critical load is governed by axial-flexural stiffness, not by hoop stress.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For typical **submarine hulls**, $L/R$ is moderate (2–8) and $R/t$ is large — so **Mode 2 (Yoshimura pattern)** dominates. This is the focus of KTU Module 4.

## 2.2 Derivation Logic — The Critical Pressure Equations

The classical buckling analysis starts from **Donnell's shallow-shell equations** (1933) for a cylindrical shell under combined axial load $N_x$ and external pressure $p$. For pure external pressure, the linear stability determinant yields:

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= \frac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)} \quad &\text{(flexural rigidity of the shell wall)} \\[4pt]
p_{cr} \cdot R &= \frac{D}{R^2} \cdot \frac{(m^2 + n^2 \beta^2 - 1)(m^2 + n^2 \beta^2 - 2)}{(m^2 + n^2 \beta^2)^2} \cdot 2R/t \cdot (\text{strain terms})
\end{aligned}$$

Minimising the critical pressure w.r.t. the number of circumferential waves $m$ gives the three classical asymptotic formulas developed below.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise these three formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU university exam question on this topic.**

| # | Shell Class / Mode | Critical Pressure Formula | Validity Range |
|---|--------------------|---------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Long Cylinder — Bryan (1888)** | $p_{cr} = \dfrac{2E}{1-\nu^{2}} \left(\dfrac{t}{2R}\right)^{3}$ | $L/R \geq 6$ and $R/t \geq 100$ |
| 2 | **Intermediate — Von Mises (1914)** | $p_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6 \, E \, (t/2R)^{5/2}}{(L/2R) - 0.45 \, (t/2R)^{1/2}}$ | $1.7 \leq L/R \leq 6$ |
| 3 | **Short Cylinder — Pure Ring Buckling** | $p_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6 \, E}{1-\nu^{2}} \left(\dfrac{t}{2R}\right)^{2.5} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(L/2R)}$ | $L/R \leq 1.7$ |
| 4 | **Sphere (Membrane)** | $p_{cr} = \dfrac{2E}{\sqrt{3(1-\nu^{2})}} \left(\dfrac{t}{R}\right)^{2}$ | All $R/t$ |
| 5 | **Stiffened Cylinder (Bryan–modified)** | $p_{cr} = \dfrac{2E}{1-\nu^{2}} \left(\dfrac{t}{2R}\right)^{3} \left(1 + \dfrac{I_{s}}{L_{s}\,t^{3}/12}\right)$ | Ring-stiffened |
| 6 | **Knock-Down Factor (KDF)** | $KDF = \dfrac{p_{cr,\,actual}}{p_{cr,\,classical}}$ | $KDF \approx 0.5$–$0.7$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $2$ in $t/2R$ reflects the outer-radius-based convention of naval architects. The factor $2.6$ in Mises' and ring-buckling formulas is a numerical constant that emerges from minimising Bryan's expression with respect to the integer $m$ (circumferential wave number).

## 2.4 Why the Knock-Down Factor Exists (Engineering Reality)

**Classical formulas predict ~50–100% higher** critical pressures than are observed in real shells. Three root causes:

1. **Geometric imperfections** — ovality $\delta_0/R$ (typically 0.001–0.005), weld depressions, fabrication tolerance.
2. **Boundary asymmetries** — end-cap interaction, eccentric loading, ring-stiffener fit-up gaps.
3. **Material anisotropy** — rolling-direction effects, residual stresses, asymmetric yielding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **U.S. Navy** uses design rules (e.g., *DDS-039*, *MIL-STD-1629*) that bake in a KDF of **0.75** for cylinders and **0.70** for spheres relative to classical theory. The **ASME BPVC Section VIII, Division 2** uses a slightly different reduction scheme.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

| Industry | Application |
|----------|-------------|
| **Submarine Design** | Pressure hull collapse depth |
| **Deep-sea Manned Submersibles (e.g., *Alvin*, *Shinkai 6500*)** | Titanium sphere integrity |
| **Oil & Gas** | Subsea pipeline collapse (external pressure + bending) |
| **Aerospace** | Monocoque fuselage buckling at altitude |
| **Civil — Storage Tanks** | Roof buckling under partial vacuum |
| **Cryogenics** | LNG tank outer shell collapse under vapour pressure |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bryan's Formula for a Long Cylinder

**Starting Point:** A long, simply-supported cylindrical shell of radius $R$, thickness $t$, length $L \gg R$ is subjected to uniform external pressure $p$. The pre-buckling stress is purely **hoop** (circumferential):
$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{p R}{t}$$

**Step 1 — Effective modulus in plane stress:**
The shell is a 2-D continuum under biaxial hoop–axial restraint. The effective modulus governing buckling is:
$$E^{*} = \frac{E}{1-\nu^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Energy of a perturbation mode** $w = A \sin\left(\frac{m\pi x}{L}\right) \cos(n\theta)$. The increase in strain energy (bending) and decrease in work done by pressure both depend on $m$ and $n$. The energy balance gives:
$$p_{cr} = \frac{E^{*} t}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{m^{2}} \cdot \left[\frac{1}{m^{2}} + \frac{n^{2} t^{2}}{12 R^{2}(1-\nu^{2})}\right] \cdot \text{(geometric factor)}$$

**Step 3 — Minimisation w.r.t. axial wave number $m$** for large $L$ gives $m \to 1$ and yields the well-known **Bryan (1888) result**:
$$\boxed{\, p_{cr} = \frac{2E}{1-\nu^{2}} \left(\frac{t}{2R}\right)^{3} \,}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical check (submarine-like geometry):** Take $R = 2.5$ m, $t = 0.05$ m, $E = 2.07 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm², $\nu = 0.30$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{t}{2R} &= \frac{0.05}{5.0} = 0.01 \\[4pt]
1-\nu^{2} &= 1-0.09 = 0.91 \\[4pt]
p_{cr} &= \frac{2 \times 2.07\times 10^{5}}{0.91} \times (0.01)^{3} \;\text{N/mm}^{2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{4.14\times 10^{5}}{0.91} \times 10^{-6} = 0.455 \,\text{N/mm}^{2} = 0.455 \,\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

Applying KDF = 0.6: $p_{cr,\,design} = 0.273$ MPa, corresponding to a collapse depth of:
$$h = \frac{0.273}{0.01005} \approx 27 \,\text{m — clearly too shallow!}$$

This proves that **real submarine hulls are not Bryan's long cylinder**; they are stiffened, often with **intermediate $L/R$** values, and use **HY-100 or HY-130** steel with $\sigma_y$ above 690 MPa.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Von Mises Formula for Intermediate Cylinders

**Step 1 — Start from the observed wave pattern.** Tests show that for $1.7 \leq L/R \leq 6$, the buckling pressure depends on $L$ (unlike the long-cylinder limit). The general $p_{cr}$ has the form:
$$p_{cr} = \frac{E \, t^{2}}{R \cdot L} \cdot f\left(\frac{L^{2} \sqrt{1-\nu^{2}}}{R t}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Mises fitted experimental data** with the empirical but very accurate expression (valid for intermediate cylinders):
$$\boxed{\, p_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \, E \, (t/2R)^{5/2}}{(L/2R) - 0.45 \, (t/2R)^{1/2}} \,}$$

**Step 3 — Limit check:** Let $L \to \infty$ (so denominator $\to \infty$ in Von Mises, but $L$ must not be infinite). For $L/2R$ very large, the $(t/2R)^{1/2}$ term in denominator becomes negligible — but Von Mises is *not* supposed to apply in the long-cylinder regime. Apply it only between $L/2R = 0.85$ and $L/2R = 3.0$.

**Step 4 — Numerical example:** $R = 2.5$ m, $t = 0.05$ m, $L = 7.5$ m, $E = 2.07 \times 10^{5}$ MPa:
$$\begin{aligned}
t/2R &= 0.01, \quad L/2R = 1.5 \\[4pt]
(t/2R)^{5/2} &= (0.01)^{2.5} = 1.0\times 10^{-5} \\[4pt]
(L/2R) - 0.45(t/2R)^{1/2} &= 1.5 - 0.45(0.1) = 1.455 \\[4pt]
p_{cr} &= \frac{2.6 \times 2.07\times 10^{5} \times 1.0\times 10^{-5}}{1.455} = \frac{5.382}{1.455} \approx 3.70 \,\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

With KDF = 0.6: $p_{cr,\,design} \approx 2.22$ MPa ⇒ collapse depth ≈ **221 m**. More reasonable, and still enhanced by ring stiffeners.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spherical Shell Critical Pressure

A spherical shell's pre-buckling stress is uniform membrane:
$$\sigma = \frac{p R}{2 t}$$

**Step 1 — Buckling criterion from classical shell theory** (Zoelly, 1915). The critical pressure for an *ideal* sphere is:
$$p_{cr} = \frac{2E}{\sqrt{3(1-\nu^{2})}} \left(\frac{t}{R}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Derivation logic:** Equate bending-strain energy of a perturbation mode $w(\theta,\phi)$ to the work done by pressure. The eigenvalue problem yields the smallest eigenvalue giving the buckling load. The $\sqrt{3}$ factor emerges from the optimisation over the Legendre modes $P_n(\cos\theta)$.

**Step 3 — Numerical example** — submersible crew sphere (e.g., *Shinkai 6500* scale): $R = 1.1$ m, $t = 0.0735$ m (titanium), $E = 1.10 \times 10^{5}$ MPa, $\nu = 0.33$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{t}{R} &= 0.0668, \quad (t/R)^{2} = 4.47 \times 10^{-3} \\[4pt]
\sqrt{3(1-\nu^{2})} &= \sqrt{3(0.891)} = 1.635 \\[4pt]
p_{cr} &= \frac{2 \times 1.10\times 10^{5} \times 4.47\times 10^{-3}}{1.635} = 601 \,\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

Applying KDF = 0.45 (sphere is more imperfection-sensitive):
$p_{cr,\,design} = 270.5$ MPa ⇒ **colossous** reserve, sufficient for 6500 m depth (≈ 65 MPa).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick-Reference Summary

| Quantity | Symbol | Long Cyl. | Int. Cyl. (Mises) | Sphere |
|----------|--------|-----------|-------------------|--------|
| Exponent on $t/R$ | — | 3 | 2.5 | 2 |
| Depends on $L$? | — | NO | YES | NO |
| Typical KDF | — | 0.5–0.7 | 0.6 | 0.4–0.5 |
| Failure mode | — | Lobes | Yoshimura | Local dimple |

> [!NOTE]
> **Design philosophy** — submarines are NOT designed on the *classical* pressure alone. The design margin is `p_collapse_actual / p_operating ≥ 1.5` for *human-occupied* vehicles. The classical formulas above are then paired with a **Finite Element (FE) eigenvalue analysis** that includes geometric imperfections (e.g., eigenvalue buckling in ANSYS/ABAQUS) to obtain a "realistic" collapse pressure.

## 3.5 Ring-Stiffened Cylinders (Module-4 Linked Topic)

A bare cylinder is buckling-limited. To push the collapse depth, naval architects add **ring stiffeners (frames)** at intervals $L_s$ along the length. The composite section's effective bending stiffness becomes:
$$I_{eff} = I_{s} + \frac{L_{s} t^{3}}{12}$$
where $I_s$ is the second moment of area of the ring-stiffener cross-section.

Substituting into Bryan's formula:
$$p_{cr} = \frac{2E}{1-\nu^{2}} \left(\frac{t}{2R}\right)^{3} \left[1 + \frac{12 \, I_{s}}{L_{s} \, t^{3}}\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A typical ring-stiffener can boost $p_{cr}$ by a factor of **3–5**, at modest weight penalty. This is why every real submarine hull has a *ribbed* silhouette — those visible bands are the frames that resist buckling.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Submarine Hull Buckling Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Inputs: R, t, L, Material, Depth] --> B[Compute Hydrostatic Pressure p_ext]
    B --> C{L/R Classification}
    C -->|L/R ≥ 6| D[Long Cylinder - Bryan Formula]
    C -->|1.7 to 6| E[Intermediate - Von Mises Formula]
    C -->|L/R < 1.7| F[Short Cylinder - Ring Buckling]
    C -->|Spherical| G[Sphere - Zoelly Formula]
    D --> H[Compute Classical p_cr]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Apply Knock-Down Factor KDF]
    I --> J[Compute Design p_cr_design]
    J --> K{Margin Check}
    K -->|p_cr_design ≥ 1.5 × p_ext| L[ACCEPT Design]
    K -->|Otherwise| M[INCREASE t or ADD Ring Stiffeners]
    M --> A
    L --> N[Final Submarine Pressure Hull]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Three Buckling Modes Visual Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SHORT[Short Cylinders L over R less than 1.7]
        S1[Axisymmetric Ring Buckling] --> S2[Multiple Inward Depressions]
    end
    subgraph INTER[Intermediate 1.7 to 6]
        I1[Non-Axisymmetric Yoshimura Pattern] --> I2[Diamond Lobes in Plastic Bag Crinkle Form]
    end
    subgraph LONG[Long Cylinders L over R greater than 6]
        L1[Euler Column Mode] --> L2[Whole Cylinder Bows Laterally]
    end
    subgraph SPHERE[Spherical Shells]
        P1[Local Dimple Buckling] --> P2[Single Inward Pit]
    end
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Engineering Reality Chain (Why KDF < 1)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Perfect Shell Ideal Geometry] --> Q[Classical p_cr - Bryan / Mises / Zoelly]
    Q --> R1[Geometric Imperfections Ovality]
    Q --> R2[Residual Welding Stresses]
    Q --> R3[Material Inhomogeneity]
    Q --> R4[Boundary Conditions Asymmetry]
    R1 --> S[Reduced Actual p_cr]
    R2 --> S
    R3 --> S
    R4 --> S
    S --> T[Knock-Down Factor KDF approximately 0.5 to 0.7]
    T --> U[Final Design p_cr]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Pressure Hull Build-Up Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inner Pressure Hull Thin Cylinder] --> B[Ring Frames T Section Stiffeners]
    B --> C[Longitudinal Stringers]
    C --> D[Outer Fairing Non Pressure Bearing]
    D --> E[Combined Buckling Resistance Boost factor 3 to 5]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define the term "critical buckling pressure" of a submarine pressure hull. Why is it lower than the classical theoretical value in practice?**

**Model Answer:**
The *critical buckling pressure* ($p_{cr}$) is the external hydrostatic pressure at which a thin-walled shell (the pressure hull) suddenly loses its stable geometric form and collapses, even though the wall's average hoop stress ($\sigma_\theta = pR/t$) is still well below the yield stress of the material.

The *classical* $p_{cr}$ is calculated for a **perfect** geometry. In practice the real value is **lower** because:
1. **Geometric imperfections** — ovality, weld depressions, dimples.
2. **Residual stresses** from welding and rolling.
3. **Material anisotropy** and inhomogeneity.
4. **Boundary condition asymmetries** (e.g., end-cap fit-up).

The ratio of actual to classical is the **Knock-Down Factor (KDF)**, typically **0.5–0.7** for cylinders and **0.4–0.5** for spheres.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**List the three classical formulas for the critical buckling pressure of a cylindrical shell under external pressure and state the validity range of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Formula | Name | Validity Range |
|---|---------|------|----------------|
| 1 | $p_{cr} = \dfrac{2E}{1-\nu^{2}}\left(\dfrac{t}{2R}\right)^{3}$ | Bryan | $L/R \geq 6$ (long cylinder) |
| 2 | $p_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6\,E\,(t/2R)^{5/2}}{(L/2R) - 0.45\,(t/2R)^{1/2}}$ | Von Mises | $1.7 \leq L/R \leq 6$ (intermediate) |
| 3 | $p_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6\,E}{1-\nu^{2}}\left(\dfrac{t}{2R}\right)^{2.5}\dfrac{1}{(L/2R)}$ | Ring buckling | $L/R < 1.7$ (short cylinder) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. *Do not* apply Bryan's long-cylinder formula to a hull where $L/R < 6$ — full mark deduction. **Always** classify by $L/R$ first.
> 2. *Always* apply a **knock-down factor (KDF)** in the final answer. A calculation that produces the "classical" $p_{cr}$ but does not apply KDF is **incomplete** for naval-architecture design and loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. *Watch the unit conversion*: hydrostatic pressure $p = 0.01005 h$ (in metres to MPa). Most mark-loss in numerical problems is due to unit mistakes.
> 4. *Show $L/R$ and $R/t$ ratios explicitly* before choosing the formula.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

The pressure hull of a deep-diving submersible is a **circular cylinder** of mean radius $R = 1.5$ m, wall thickness $t = 40$ mm and unsupported length $L = 9$ m between ring stiffeners. The hull material is **HY-80 steel** with $E = 2.07 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm² and $\nu = 0.30$.

**(a)** Classify the cylinder (long / intermediate / short) and select the **appropriate classical buckling formula**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Compute the **classical critical buckling pressure** and the **design allowable pressure** using a knock-down factor $KDF = 0.60$. State the corresponding **collapse depth in metres of seawater**. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Classification:**

Compute the geometric ratios:
$$\frac{L}{R} = \frac{9.0}{1.5} = 6.0 \quad ; \quad \frac{R}{t} = \frac{1.5}{0.040} = 37.5$$

The ratio $L/R = 6.0$ lies at the **upper boundary of the intermediate regime** (Von Mises range: $1.7 \leq L/R \leq 6$). The hull is therefore classified as an **intermediate cylinder**, and we use the **Von Mises formula**:
$$p_{cr} = \frac{2.6\,E\,(t/2R)^{5/2}}{(L/2R) - 0.45\,(t/2R)^{1/2}}$$

(If the student is more conservative, they may note that $L/R = 6$ is on the boundary, and could also verify using Bryan's formula as a cross-check.) *(Full 7 marks.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Evaluation:**

Compute the auxiliary quantities:
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{t}{2R} &= \frac{40}{2 \times 1500} = 0.01333 \\[4pt]
\left(\frac{t}{2R}\right)^{5/2} &= (0.01333)^{2.5} = 2.05 \times 10^{-5} \\[4pt]
\left(\frac{t}{2R}\right)^{1/2} &= (0.01333)^{0.5} = 0.1155 \\[4pt]
\frac{L}{2R} &= \frac{9.0}{3.0} = 3.0
\end{aligned}$$

Denominator:
$$3.0 - 0.45 \times 0.1155 = 3.0 - 0.05198 = 2.948$$

Classical critical pressure:
$$p_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \times 2.07 \times 10^{5} \times 2.05 \times 10^{-5}}{2.948}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 2.6 \times 2.07 \times 10^{5} \times 2.05 \times 10^{-5} \\[4pt]
&= 2.6 \times 4.2435 = 11.033 \;\text{N/mm}^{2} \\[4pt]
p_{cr} &= \frac{11.033}{2.948} = 3.742 \;\text{N/mm}^{2} \approx 3.74 \;\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

Applying the knock-down factor:
$$p_{cr,\,design} = KDF \times p_{cr} = 0.60 \times 3.74 = 2.245 \;\text{MPa}$$

Collapse depth:
$$h = \frac{p_{cr,\,design}}{0.01005} = \frac{2.245}{0.01005} = 223.4 \;\text{m}$$

**Final Answer:** $p_{cr} \approx 3.74$ MPa (classical), $p_{cr,design} \approx 2.25$ MPa, collapse depth ≈ **223 m**. *(Full 7 marks.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**(a)** With the aid of a clear sketch, describe the **three classical buckling modes** of a thin cylindrical shell under external hydrostatic pressure. Indicate the $L/R$ range where each mode dominates. (7 Marks)

**(b)** The crew sphere of a deep submersible is a **spherical shell** of radius $R = 1.1$ m and wall thickness $t = 70$ mm, made of titanium alloy with $E = 1.10 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm² and $\nu = 0.33$. Compute the **classical critical buckling pressure** and the **design collapse depth** (use KDF = 0.45). Comment on whether this is sufficient for a **6500 m** operating depth. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Three Buckling Modes:**

*(Please refer to the block diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes for a schematic.)*

A thin cylindrical shell under external pressure buckles in one of three modes, depending on the slenderness ratio $L/R$:

1. **Mode I — Axisymmetric (Ring) Buckling** — observed in **short cylinders** ($L/R < 1.7$). The cross-section deforms into a series of regular **ring-shaped inward depressions**, each separated axially by approximately one ring wavelength $\lambda = 2.6 \sqrt{Rt}$. The wave pattern is purely axisymmetric (no circumferential variation).

2. **Mode II — Non-Axisymmetric (Yoshimura/Diamond) Buckling** — observed in **intermediate cylinders** ($1.7 \leq L/R \leq 6$). The surface develops a pattern of **alternating inward and outward lobes** forming a diamond/checkerboard pattern (called the *Yoshimura pattern*, after the geometrician who studied the analogous paper-folding mode). The buckling pattern has $m$ circumferential waves and $n$ axial half-waves.

3. **Mode III — Euler (Column) Buckling** — observed in **long cylinders** ($L/R > 6$). The cylinder behaves as a beam-column and bows out laterally with a single axial half-wave (or a few). Critical load is governed by flexural rigidity $EI$ rather than membrane stress. *(Full 7 marks.)*

**Part (b) — Spherical Shell Buckling:**

Formula (Zoelly, 1915):
$$p_{cr} = \frac{2E}{\sqrt{3(1-\nu^{2})}} \left(\frac{t}{R}\right)^{2}$$

Compute:
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{t}{R} &= \frac{70}{1100} = 0.06364 \\[4pt]
\left(\frac{t}{R}\right)^{2} &= 4.05 \times 10^{-3} \\[4pt]
1-\nu^{2} &= 1 - 0.1089 = 0.8911 \\[4pt]
\sqrt{3 \times 0.8911} &= \sqrt{2.6733} = 1.6347
\end{aligned}$$

Classical critical pressure:
$$p_{cr} = \frac{2 \times 1.10\times 10^{5} \times 4.05 \times 10^{-3}}{1.6347}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
&= \frac{891.0}{1.6347} = 545.0 \;\text{N/mm}^{2} \approx 545 \;\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

Design (KDF = 0.45):
$$p_{cr,\,design} = 0.45 \times 545 = 245.3 \;\text{MPa}$$

Collapse depth:
$$h = \frac{245.3}{0.01005} = 24{,}408 \;\text{m} \approx 24.4 \;\text{km}$$

Operating pressure at 6500 m:
$$p_{op} = 0.01005 \times 6500 = 65.3 \;\text{MPa}$$

**Comment:** Design collapse pressure (245.3 MPa) is **3.76 times** the operating pressure (65.3 MPa), giving a comfortable design margin above the typical 1.5 safety factor. The design is **safe for 6500 m operation** with a substantial margin. *(Full 7 marks.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Buckling = geometric instability, not material yield.** Collapse can occur at $\sigma \ll \sigma_y$.
- ✅ Three **classical formulas** for cylinders: **Bryan** (long), **Von Mises** (intermediate), **Ring** (short).
- ✅ **Validity ranges**: $L/R \geq 6$ (Bryan), $1.7 \leq L/R \leq 6$ (Mises), $L/R < 1.7$ (ring buckling).
- ✅ **Spherical shells** use Zoelly's formula, with $p_{cr} \propto (t/R)^{2}$ — *not* $(t/R)^{3}$ as in long cylinders.
- ✅ **Hydrostatic pressure**: $p$ (MPa) = $0.01005 \times h$ (metres).
- ✅ **Knock-down factor (KDF)** must always be applied: KDF ≈ 0.5–0.7 for cylinders, 0.4–0.5 for spheres.
- ✅ Typical submarine values: $R/t \approx 30$–$100$, $L/R \approx 2$–$8$ → **intermediate regime dominates**.
- ✅ **Ring stiffeners** (frames) boost $p_{cr}$ by a factor of 3–5; they appear as the visible "ribs" on every submarine hull.
- ✅ Design safety factor: $p_{cr,design} / p_{op} \geq 1.5$ for *human-occupied* vessels.
- ✅ **Imperfections** (ovality, welds) are the dominant reason classical formulas over-predict real shell strength.
- ✅ For exam: always **classify** by $L/R$, **show** both classical and design $p_{cr}$, and **convert** to depth in metres.
- ✅ For titanium spheres, very high $p_{cr}$ is achievable (e.g., Shinkai 6500), which is why deep submersibles use spheres rather than cylinders for the crew compartment.
- ✅ **External pressure** is *more critical* than internal pressure for buckling in submarines, because external pressure creates **compressive** hoop stress in the wall.
- ✅ Most *common exam pitfall*: using Bryan's formula when $L/R < 6$ — full mark deduction in KTU 2024 scheme.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST634 - SUBMARINES AND SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Failure Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of submarine and submersible pressure-hull design (as prescribed under KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4), **Failure Modes** refer to the distinct, physically observable mechanisms by which a pressure hull or any load-bearing structural component loses its functional integrity under operational and environmental loading. These modes are categorized by the dominant physical phenomenon — elastic instability, plastic deformation, fracture, fatigue, or material degradation — that ultimately governs the collapse or loss-of-mission of the hull.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition Highlight**
> For a submarine pressure hull, *failure* is **NOT** synonymous with material rupture alone. The classical KTU/Naval-Architecture definition treats failure as any event where the hull ceases to maintain its intended pressure-resisting function — which includes elastic buckling, inelastic buckling, yielding, brittle fracture, fatigue cracking, and corrosion-induced thickness loss.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine crushing an empty aluminum soft-drink can in your hand from the sides.

- If the can is thin and long, it **buckles** (it "pops" with a sudden snap, even though the aluminum never breaks) — this is **instability failure**.
- If you keep squeezing, the aluminum eventually **tears** along a crease — this is **material rupture**.
- If you squeeze and release it a thousand times, a **fatigue crack** slowly grows at the crease — this is **fatigue failure**.
- If the can is left in seawater, rust eats away the wall until it gets so thin that it buckles at a fraction of the original load — this is **corrosion-driven failure**.

A submarine hull behaves the same way. The hull must be designed so that under **hydrostatic pressure** (water load) and **dynamic cyclic loading** (depth changes, wave slap), **none of these modes occur** within the design depth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Naval-Architecture Insight**
> For modern submarines operating at depths of 300 m – 900 m (and up to 1000+ m for deep-diving submersibles), **buckling is the governing failure mode**, NOT yielding. This is the *single most counter-intuitive fact* in submarine structural design: the hull is *almost always* too thin to fail by yielding first — it buckles elastically or in elastically long before the steel reaches its yield strength.

### 1.3 Classification of Failure Modes

Failure modes are broadly classified into five families under the KTU Naval-Architecture framework:

| # | Failure Family | Physical Phenomenon | Typical Sub-Classification |
|---|----------------|---------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Instability (Buckling)** | Sudden loss of load-carrying capacity due to geometric instability | Elastic, Inelastic, Plastic, Crippling |
| 2 | **Material Yielding** | Permanent plastic deformation beyond elastic limit | First-yield, Gross-yield, Limit-load |
| 3 | **Rupture / Fracture** | Material separation into two or more parts | Ductile, Brittle, Stress-corrosion cracking |
| 4 | **Fatigue** | Progressive damage under cyclic loading | High-cycle (HCF), Low-cycle (LCF), Corrosion-fatigue |
| 5 | **Environmental / Time-Dependent** | Loss of effective thickness or strength over time | General corrosion, Pitting, Creep, Hydrogen embrittlement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Anchor (Module 4):** The KTU module on *Failure Modes* emphasizes the pressure hull because it is the **single safety-critical component** of a submarine — its failure is catastrophic and non-recoverable. All other systems (propulsion, ballast, control) can be made redundant; the hull cannot.

### 1.4 Pressure Hull as the System of Interest

The pressure hull is a **ring-stiffened or unstiffened cylindrical shell** (often with hemispherical or ellipsoidal end closures) made of high-tensile steel (e.g., **HY-80, HY-100, HY-130** — each number denotes the **yield strength in ksi**). The external hydrostatic pressure $P_e$ at operating depth $H$ is given by:

$$P_e = \rho_w \, g \, H$$

where $\rho_w \approx \mathbf{1025~kg/m^3}$ (seawater density), $g = \mathbf{9.81~m/s^2}$, and $H$ is the depth in metres. At **300 m**, $P_e \approx \mathbf{3.0~MPa}$; at **900 m**, $P_e \approx \mathbf{9.05~MPa}$.

These pressures drive every failure mode considered in this module.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Deconstructing Each Failure Mode

#### 2.1.1 Elastic (Classical) Buckling

- **What it is:** A geometric instability where the cylindrical shell suddenly transitions from a stable axisymmetric shape to a deformed, lobed (non-axisymmetric) pattern with **no material yielding**.
- **Why it matters:** For submarine hulls with $R/t$ ratios in the range 50–300, classical elastic buckling is the *upper bound* on collapse pressure.
- **How it occurs:** Small imperfections (out-of-roundness, eccentricity of stiffeners) dramatically reduce the *actual* buckling pressure compared to the theoretical (perfect-shell) value. Real hulls fail at **30–60 %** of the classical value.

> [!NOTE]
> The classic formula used is **Timoshenko's modified Bryan formula** with knockdown factors (often denoted $k_c$ or $\gamma$).

#### 2.1.2 Inelastic (Plastic) Buckling

- **What it is:** Buckling that occurs after **partial yielding** of the shell wall — i.e., the material has exceeded its proportional limit but the section still carries load.
- **Why it matters:** Governs failure of medium-thick hulls where some yielding precedes instability.
- **How it occurs:** Combines the elastic stiffness matrix with the plastic modulus $E_t$ (tangent modulus).

#### 2.1.3 Crippling (Local Shell Buckling Between Rings)

- **What it is:** Local buckling of the shell plating between two adjacent ring stiffeners, treated as a cylindrical panel under axial + circumferential compression + external pressure.
- **Why it matters:** Sets the **maximum permitted ring spacing** $L$ for a given shell thickness $t$.

#### 2.1.4 Material Yielding (First-Yield Criterion)

- **What it is:** The pressure at which the **maximum equivalent stress** reaches the material yield strength.
- **Yield criteria used in KTU syllabus:**
  - **Tresca:** $\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \le \sigma_y$
  - **Von Mises:** $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 - \sigma_2\sigma_3 - \sigma_3\sigma_1} \le \sigma_y$

For a thick-walled cylinder under external pressure, yielding initiates at the **inner surface**.

#### 2.1.5 Fracture (Brittle / Ductile)

- **What it is:** Sudden separation of material driven by a stress concentration at a crack tip.
- **Key parameter:** **Stress Intensity Factor** $K$. Failure occurs when $K \ge K_{IC}$ (fracture toughness).
- **Linear Elastic Fracture Mechanics (LEFM)** regime when the crack is long compared to the plastic zone.

#### 2.1.6 Fatigue Failure

- **What it is:** Progressive, sub-yield damage accumulation under repeated loading.
- **Three regimes:**
  - **HCF** ($N > 10^5$ cycles): governed by **Basquin's law**.
  - **LCF** ($N < 10^4$): governed by **Coffin-Manson** strain-life law.
  - **Corrosion-fatigue:** synergistic effect of cyclic stress + seawater → drastically lowers fatigue life.
- **Cumulative damage rule:** **Miner's rule** predicts failure when $\sum (n_i/N_i) = 1$.

#### 2.1.7 Creep and Stress Relaxation

- Relevant only for **deep-submersible** pressure hulls operating at high temperatures (e.g., titanium hulls of certain research submersibles) or for long-duration submerged operation.
- Creep is usually negligible for steel hulls at ocean temperatures but is examined in the KTU syllabus for completeness.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Formulas** — every KTU exam on this topic will draw from this set.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic pressure | $P_e = \rho_w g H$ | $\rho_w$ = kg/m³, $g$ = m/s², $H$ = m | Drives all hull loading |
| 2 | Hoop stress in thin cylinder | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P R}{t}$ | $P$ = Pa, $R$ = m, $t$ = m | Open-ended cylinder approximation |
| 3 | Critical buckling pressure (Timoshenko/Bryan) | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6 \, E \, (t/D)^{2.5}}{(L/D)}$ | $E$ = Pa, $t, D, L$ = m | Classical, perfect shell |
| 4 | Modified Bryan (with knock-down) | $P_{cr,real} = k_c \cdot P_{cr,ideal}$ | $k_c \approx 0.3 - 0.6$ | Accounts for imperfections |
| 5 | Crippling stress (von Mises) | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{0.6 \, E \, t}{R}$ | $E$ = Pa, $t, R$ = m | Local panel buckling between rings |
| 6 | Von Mises yield criterion | $\sigma_{eq}^2 = \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 - \sigma_2\sigma_3 - \sigma_3\sigma_1$ | Stress in Pa | For 3D stress state |
| 7 | Stress Intensity Factor (mode I) | $K_I = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | $Y$ = geometry factor, $a$ = crack half-length (m) | LEFM |
| 8 | Fracture criterion | $K_I \ge K_{IC}$ | $K_{IC}$ = MPa√m | Material property |
| 9 | Paris law (fatigue crack growth) | $\dfrac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^m$ | $C, m$ = material constants | Mid-range fatigue growth |
| 10 | Basquin's law (HCF) | $\sigma_a = \sigma_f' (2N_f)^b$ | $\sigma_a$ = stress amplitude, $b, \sigma_f'$ = constants | High-cycle fatigue life |
| 11 | Coffin-Manson (LCF) | $\dfrac{\Delta \varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon_f' (2N_f)^c$ | $\varepsilon_f', c$ = constants | Low-cycle, strain-based |
| 12 | Miner's cumulative damage | $D = \sum_i \dfrac{n_i}{N_i} = 1$ | $n_i$ = applied cycles, $N_i$ = cycles to failure | Failure predicted at $D = 1$ |
| 13 | Stress concentration factor | $K_t = \dfrac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma_{nom}}$ | Dimensionless | At holes, notches, welds |
| 14 | Effective stress with corrosion | $t_{eff} = t_0 - r_c \cdot T$ | $r_c$ = mm/yr corrosion rate, $T$ = yr | Time-dependent wall loss |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe `\|` Rule Followed**: All absolute-value bars (e.g., $\vert \sigma \vert$) are written using `\vert` to prevent table breakage.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HY-80 / HY-100 steel selection:** HY-80 ($Y = \mathbf{80~ksi} \approx 552$ MPa) gives a $50\%$ higher yield margin than mild steel, increasing collapse depth at moderate weight penalty.
- **Stiffener design:** Ring stiffeners convert a long cylinder into a series of shorter, stiffer panels — increasing $P_{cr}$ dramatically.
- **Non-destructive evaluation (NDE):** Failure-mode awareness dictates inspection intervals — ultrasonic testing for fatigue cracks, magnetic particle for surface cracks, eddy current for cladding degradation.
- **Salvage and forensic analysis:** The *Titan* submersible (OceanGate, 2023) failed by **compressive buckling + brittle composite failure** due to cyclic fatigue in a non-engineered pressure vessel — a tragic real-world case study.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Classical Buckling Pressure for a Long Cylinder Under External Pressure

**Starting point:** Consider a long, thin, perfectly cylindrical shell of mean radius $R$, wall thickness $t$, and length $L$, subjected to uniform external pressure $P_e$. We seek the pressure at which the shell first becomes unstable.

**Step 1: Identify the strain energy stored in the shell wall under deformation.**

For a cylindrical shell undergoing axisymmetric and non-axisymmetric deformations, the total potential energy $U$ is the sum of bending strain energy $U_b$ and membrane strain energy $U_m$. For a perturbation of the form $w = A \cos(n\theta) \cos(\pi x / L)$, the bending strain energy per unit length is:

$$U_b = \frac{D}{2} \int_0^L \int_0^{2\pi} \left[ \nabla^4 w \right]^2 R \, d\theta \, dx$$

where $D = \dfrac{Et^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ is the flexural rigidity and $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio.

**Step 2: Write the work done by external pressure during the buckling deflection.**

$$W = \frac{P_e}{2} \int_0^L \int_0^{2\pi} \left( \frac{\partial w}{\partial x} \right)^{\!2} R \, d\theta \, dx$$

This term represents the decrease in potential energy of the external pressure as the shell displaces inward.

**Step 3: Apply the principle of stationary potential energy (Ritz/Trefftz criterion).**

At the bifurcation point, $\delta(U - W) = 0$, which for the assumed deflection mode reduces to:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{D}{R^3} \left[ \frac{n^4}{\left(n^2 - 1\right)} \right] \left[ 1 + \left(\frac{\pi R}{n L}\right)^2 \right]^{-2}$$

**Step 4: Minimize with respect to the circumferential wave number $n$.**

For a long cylinder ($L \gg R$), the optimum $n$ satisfies $n^2 = 2\pi R / L \cdot \sqrt{1 - \nu^2}$. Substituting and simplifying yields the **classical Bryan formula**:

$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \, E \, (t/R)^{2.5}}{L/R}}$$

In terms of the diameter $D = 2R$:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \, E \, (t/D)^{2.5}}{L/D}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notice the exponent 2.5 on $(t/R)$** — this is why even a small increase in wall thickness produces a *huge* increase in collapse depth. Doubling $t$ increases $P_{cr}$ by a factor of $\mathbf{2^{2.5} \approx 5.66}$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A submarine pressure hull has mean radius $R = 2.5$ m, wall thickness $t = 40$ mm, unsupported length between rings $L = 1.0$ m, made of HY-80 steel with $E = \mathbf{207~GPa}$, $\nu = 0.3$, $\sigma_y = \mathbf{552~MPa}$. Find:
(a) Critical buckling pressure (classical).
(b) Knockdown-adjusted collapse pressure (use $k_c = 0.5$).
(c) First-yield pressure using Tresca.
(d) Verify which failure mode governs.

**(a) Classical buckling pressure:**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \times 207 \times 10^9 \times (0.040 / 5.0)^{2.5}}{1.0 / 5.0}$$

Step-by-step:

$$\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^{2.5} &= \left(\frac{0.040}{5.0}\right)^{2.5} = (0.008)^{2.5}
\end{aligned}$$

Computing $(0.008)^{2.5}$:

$$(0.008)^2 = 6.4 \times 10^{-5}$$
$$\sqrt{0.008} = 0.0894$$
$$(0.008)^{2.5} = (0.008)^2 \cdot \sqrt{0.008} = 6.4 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.0894 = 5.724 \times 10^{-6}$$

Now:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \times 207 \times 10^9 \times 5.724 \times 10^{-6}}{0.2}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
2.6 \times 207 \times 10^9 &= 5.382 \times 10^{11} \\
5.382 \times 10^{11} \times 5.724 \times 10^{-6} &= 3.080 \times 10^{6} \\
\frac{3.080 \times 10^{6}}{0.2} &= 1.540 \times 10^{7}~\text{Pa}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr} \approx 15.4~\text{MPa}}$$

**(b) Knockdown-adjusted collapse:**

$$P_{collapse} = 0.5 \times 15.4 = 7.7~\text{MPa}$$

**(c) First-yield pressure (Tresca):**

For a thin cylinder, hoop stress is dominant:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P R}{t} = \sigma_y$$

$$P_y = \frac{\sigma_y \, t}{R} = \frac{552 \times 10^6 \times 0.040}{2.5} = 8.832~\text{MPa}$$

**(d) Governing mode:**

Comparing:
- $P_{collapse}$ (buckling, realistic) $= 7.7$ MPa
- $P_y$ (yielding) $= 8.83$ MPa

Since $P_{collapse} < P_y$, **buckling governs**. The safety factor against buckling:

$$SF = \frac{P_y}{P_{collapse}} = \frac{8.83}{7.7} \approx 1.15$$

> [!NOTE]
> This narrow margin confirms the KTU principle: **buckling is the governing mode**, and design must incorporate the knockdown factor to achieve realistic safety.

---

### 3.3 Fatigue Life Estimation Using Miner's Rule

**Problem:** A submarine hull experiences three loading spectra over a service life:
- **Block 1:** $n_1 = 5000$ cycles at $\Delta\sigma_1 = 200$ MPa → cycles-to-failure $N_1 = 10^6$
- **Block 2:** $n_2 = 200$ cycles at $\Delta\sigma_2 = 350$ MPa → $N_2 = 10^4$
- **Block 3:** $n_3 = 50$ cycles at $\Delta\sigma_3 = 500$ MPa → $N_3 = 500$

Compute cumulative damage $D$:

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= \sum_i \frac{n_i}{N_i} \\
&= \frac{5000}{1{,}000{,}000} + \frac{200}{10{,}000} + \frac{50}{500} \\
&= 0.005 + 0.020 + 0.100 \\
&= 0.125
\end{aligned}$$

Since $D = 0.125 < 1$, the hull has **not** reached end-of-life and retains a margin of $1 - 0.125 = 0.875$ (≈ 87.5% life remaining).

> [!WARNING]
> **Miner's rule is linear and ignores load-interaction effects.** Conservative KTU practice applies a *safety factor* of 0.5–0.7 on the critical $D = 1$ threshold.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Functional Architecture — Failure-Mode Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Hydrostatic Pressure Load P_ext] --> B{Shell Geometry}
    B --> B1[Thin Cylinder: R/t greater than 50]
    B --> B2[Thick Cylinder: R/t less than 50]

    B1 --> C{Stress Level vs Yield}
    C --> C1[Stress below Yield Strength]
    C --> C2[Stress above Yield Strength]

    C1 --> D[Elastic Buckling Mode]
    D --> D1[Classical Bryan Equation]
    D --> D2[Apply Knockdown Factor kc]
    D2 --> D3[Predicted Collapse Pressure P_coll]

    C2 --> E[Inelastic Buckling Mode]
    E --> E1[Use Tangent Modulus Et]
    E --> E2[Use Reduced Flexural Rigidity]
    E2 --> E3[Predicted Collapse Pressure P_coll]

    B2 --> F{Yield Criterion}
    F --> F1[Tresca Check]
    F --> F2[Von Mises Check]
    F1 --> F3[Compare P_yield with P_ext]
    F2 --> F3

    A --> G{Time Dependent Loading}
    G --> G1[Constant Load Long Duration]
    G --> G2[Repeated Sub yield Loading]

    G1 --> H[Creep Failure Mode]
    H --> H1[Use Norton Bailey Law]

    G2 --> I[Fatigue Failure Mode]
    I --> I1[Apply Miners Rule]
    I1 --> I2[Compare Cumulative Damage D with 1]

    D3 --> J{Safety Factor Check}
    E3 --> J
    F3 --> J
    H1 --> J
    I2 --> J

    J --> J1[SF greater than 1.5: SAFE]
    J --> J2[SF between 1.0 and 1.5: MARGINAL]
    J --> J3[SF less than 1.0: REDESIGN]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Failure Mode Interdependencies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L1[LOAD INPUTS]
        loadA[Hydrostatic Pressure]
        loadB[Dynamic Wave Loading]
        loadC[Thermal Load]
    end

    subgraph L2[STRUCTURE]
        structA[Pressure Hull Cylinder]
        structB[Ring Stiffeners]
        structC[End Closures]
    end

    subgraph L3[FAILURE MODES]
        fmA[Buckling]
        fmB[Yielding]
        fmC[Fracture]
        fmD[Fatigue]
        fmE[Corrosion]
    end

    subgraph L4[MITIGATION]
        mitA[Knockdown Factor]
        mitB[Safety Factor 1.5 to 2.0]
        mitC[Periodic NDE Inspection]
        mitD[Corrosion Allowance]
    end

    loadA --> structA
    loadB --> structA
    loadC --> structA
    structA --> structB
    structA --> structC

    structA --> fmA
    structA --> fmB
    structA --> fmC
    loadB --> fmD
    loadA --> fmE

    fmA --> mitA
    fmB --> mitB
    fmC --> mitB
    fmD --> mitC
    fmE --> mitD

    mitA --> safe[Acceptable Design]
    mitB --> safe
    mitC --> safe
    mitD --> safe
```

### 4.3 Failure Mode Hierarchy Schematic (ASCII Visualization)

```
                +-------------------------------------+
                |   OPERATING HYDROSTATIC PRESSURE    |
                |       (Drives All Failure Modes)    |
                +-----------------+-------------------+
                                  |
            +---------------------+---------------------+
            |                     |                     |
            v                     v                     v
   +----------------+    +----------------+    +----------------+
   | INSTABILITY    |    | MATERIAL       |    | TIME-DEPENDENT |
   | (Geometric)    |    | (Strength)     |    | (Service Life) |
   +-------+--------+    +-------+--------+    +-------+--------+
           |                     |                     |
   +-------+-------+     +-------+-------+     +-------+-------+
   | Elastic       |     | First Yield   |     | Fatigue       |
   | Inelastic     |     | Limit Load    |     | Corrosion     |
   | Crippling     |     | Rupture       |     | Creep         |
   +---------------+     +---------------+     +---------------+
           |                     |                     |
           v                     v                     v
     P_collapse             P_yield             D = 1, t_eff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between elastic buckling and inelastic buckling of a submarine pressure hull. Which one is more critical in modern submarine hulls?**

**Model Answer:**
Elastic buckling occurs at stresses entirely below the proportional limit of the material — the hull loses stability purely due to geometry. Inelastic buckling involves partial yielding of the shell wall before instability. In modern submarines with high $R/t$ ratios, **elastic buckling** is more critical because the shell becomes unstable at stresses significantly below the yield strength. *(3 marks — 1 mark for each difference + 1 for correct identification)*

---

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**State Miner's rule. What does a cumulative damage index $D = 0.6$ imply about component life?**

**Model Answer:**
Miner's rule is the linear cumulative damage hypothesis stating that fatigue failure occurs when $\sum (n_i/N_i) = 1$. A value of $D = 0.6$ implies the component has consumed **60% of its fatigue life** and retains **40% remaining life**. In safety-critical submarine design, a $D$-limit of **0.5** is often adopted as the inspection threshold. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Module 4 (Failure Modes) appears as a full 14-mark question with internal choice. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**A ring-stiffened submarine pressure hull has the following design data:**
- Mean radius $R = 3.0$ m
- Wall thickness $t = 50$ mm
- Unsupported length between rings $L = 1.2$ m
- Material: HY-80 steel with $E = 207$ GPa, $\sigma_y = 552$ MPa, $\nu = 0.3$
- Knockdown factor $k_c = 0.45$

**(a)** Compute the classical critical buckling pressure and the knockdown-adjusted collapse pressure. Comment on the safety margin. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the first-yield pressure using the Von Mises criterion for the hoop-stress-dominant state. Compare with the buckling collapse pressure and identify the governing failure mode. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Classical Critical Buckling Pressure (7 marks)**

Using the Bryan/Timoshenko formula:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \, E \, (t/D)^{2.5}}{L/D}$$

Step 1: Compute $t/D = 0.050 / 6.0 = 8.333 \times 10^{-3}$.

Step 2: Compute $(t/D)^{2.5}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
(t/D)^{2.5} &= (8.333 \times 10^{-3})^{2.5} \\
&= (8.333 \times 10^{-3})^2 \cdot \sqrt{8.333 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= 6.944 \times 10^{-5} \times 9.129 \times 10^{-2} \\
&= 6.339 \times 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 3: Compute $L/D = 1.2 / 6.0 = 0.2$.

Step 4: Substitute:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{cr} &= \frac{2.6 \times 207 \times 10^9 \times 6.339 \times 10^{-6}}{0.2} \\
&= \frac{2.6 \times 207 \times 10^9 \times 6.339 \times 10^{-6}}{0.2}
\end{aligned}$$

Intermediate: $2.6 \times 207 \times 10^9 = 5.382 \times 10^{11}$ Pa.

Then: $5.382 \times 10^{11} \times 6.339 \times 10^{-6} = 3.412 \times 10^{6}$ Pa.

Finally: $P_{cr} = 3.412 \times 10^{6} / 0.2 = 1.706 \times 10^{7}$ Pa.

$$\boxed{P_{cr} \approx 17.06~\text{MPa}}$$

Step 5: Apply knockdown factor:

$$P_{collapse} = k_c \cdot P_{cr} = 0.45 \times 17.06 = 7.68~\text{MPa}$$

Step 6: Operating pressure at design depth $H = 600$ m:

$$P_{op} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 600 = 6.03~\text{MPa}$$

Step 7: Safety factor:

$$SF = \frac{7.68}{6.03} \approx 1.27$$

**Comment:** The safety factor of 1.27 is **below the KTU-recommended minimum of 1.5**. The design needs revision — either increase $t$, reduce $L$ (closer ring spacing), or upgrade to HY-100.

---

**(b) First-Yield Pressure — Von Mises Criterion (7 marks)**

For a thin cylinder under external pressure, the biaxial stress state at the inner surface is:

$$\sigma_\theta = -\frac{P R}{t}, \quad \sigma_x = -\frac{P R}{2t}, \quad \sigma_r \approx 0$$

Von Mises equivalent stress:

$$\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_\theta^2 + \sigma_x^2 - \sigma_\theta \sigma_x}$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{eq} &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{PR}{t}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{PR}{2t}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{PR}{t}\right)\left(\frac{PR}{2t}\right)} \\
&= \frac{PR}{t}\sqrt{1 + 0.25 - 0.5} \\
&= \frac{PR}{t}\sqrt{0.75} \\
&= 0.866 \, \frac{PR}{t}
\end{aligned}$$

Setting $\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_y$:

$$P_y = \frac{\sigma_y \, t}{0.866 \, R} = \frac{552 \times 10^6 \times 0.050}{0.866 \times 3.0}$$

$$\boxed{P_y = 10.62~\text{MPa}}$$

**Comparison:**
- $P_{collapse}$ (buckling) = $7.68$ MPa
- $P_y$ (yielding) = $10.62$ MPa

Since $P_{collapse} < P_y$, **buckling governs**. The hull will buckle before yielding — the expected behavior for a thin-walled submarine hull.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the various **failure modes** of a submarine pressure hull with neat sketches. Discuss why **buckling** is the governing mode for modern deep-diving submarines. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A submarine pressure hull experiences the following load spectrum during one patrol:
- 10,000 cycles at $\Delta\sigma = 150$ MPa → $N = 5 \times 10^6$
- 1,000 cycles at $\Delta\sigma = 250$ MPa → $N = 5 \times 10^4$
- 100 cycles at $\Delta\sigma = 400$ MPa → $N = 10^3$

Using **Miner's rule**, determine the cumulative damage index and predict whether the hull is safe for the next patrol. Suggest remedial measures if unsafe. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Failure Modes of Submarine Pressure Hull (7 marks)**

The major failure modes are:

1. **Elastic buckling** — geometric instability at stresses below the yield strength.
2. **Inelastic buckling** — buckling after partial yielding, governed by tangent modulus.
3. **Crippling** — local buckling of shell panels between ring stiffeners.
4. **Material yielding** — onset of permanent plastic deformation.
5. **Brittle fracture** — sudden failure driven by stress concentrations and low fracture toughness.
6. **Fatigue** — progressive damage under repeated pressure cycling (diving and surfacing).
7. **Corrosion-driven failure** — loss of effective thickness reducing $P_{collapse}$.

**Why buckling governs modern submarines:**

Modern deep-diving hulls have very high $R/t$ ratios to maximize interior volume and minimize displacement. This makes the shell *geometrically compliant* — it buckles elastically at pressures far below those required to cause yielding. For example, the hull analyzed in Question A buckles at **7.68 MPa** but does not yield until **10.62 MPa** — a 38% margin. Hence buckling, not yielding, sets the operational depth.

*(Award: 4 marks for listing and explaining modes, 3 marks for buckling-governing explanation)*

---

**(b) Miner's Rule Calculation (7 marks)**

$$D = \sum_i \frac{n_i}{N_i}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= \frac{10{,}000}{5 \times 10^6} + \frac{1{,}000}{5 \times 10^4} + \frac{100}{10^3} \\
&= 0.002 + 0.020 + 0.100 \\
&= 0.122
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** $D = 0.122 < 1$, so the hull is **safe** for the next patrol, with **87.8%** of fatigue life remaining.

**Remedial measures if $D$ were approaching 1 (for completeness):**
- Reduce cyclic stress amplitudes (lower maximum diving depth).
- Apply compressive residual stresses via autofrettage.
- Add weld dressing / peening at high-stress locations.
- Increase NDE inspection frequency.
- Use corrosion-fatigue-resistant coatings.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Areas in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the knockdown factor** — students use $P_{cr}$ directly as the collapse pressure. KTU examiners deduct **3–4 marks** for this. Always state: "real collapse pressure is reduced by $k_c$ due to imperfections."
> 2. **Confusing hoop stress with longitudinal stress** — for a closed cylinder under external pressure, the hoop stress is *twice* the longitudinal. Misidentification loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Incorrect use of Miner's rule** — applying it to non-proportional loading or forgetting to sum across all load blocks. Always show three lines of summation explicitly.
> 4. **Mixing up Tresca and Von Mises formulas** — Von Mises gives ≈ 15% higher allowable pressure than Tresca. State which criterion is being used.
> 5. **Ignoring corrosion allowance** — every KTU pressure-hull problem expects a corrosion allowance (typically 1–3 mm). Omitting it costs 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Buckling, not yielding, is the governing failure mode** for modern submarine pressure hulls with $R/t > 50$.
- **Knockdown factor $k_c$** (0.3–0.6) converts the *theoretical* buckling pressure to the *real* collapse pressure.
- **The Bryan/Timoshenko equation** $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6 E (t/D)^{2.5}}{L/D}$ is the cornerstone formula — memorize it.
- **Tresca** is the more conservative yield criterion (factor 1.15 lower than Von Mises).
- **Ring stiffeners** reduce unsupported length $L$, dramatically increasing $P_{cr}$.
- **Fatigue** is governed by **Miner's rule**: failure at $D = \sum n_i/N_i = 1$.
- **Fracture mechanics** uses stress intensity factor $K_I$; failure when $K_I \ge K_{IC}$.
- **Corrosion** subtracts from effective thickness: $t_{eff} = t_0 - r_c \cdot T$.
- **Required safety factor** against collapse: **≥ 1.5** for manned submarines.
- **Inspection regimes** must address the dominant mode: NDT for cracks, UT for thickness loss, dye-penetrant for surface defects.
- **Units consistency:** Use SI (Pa, m, kg, s) throughout — mixed-unit errors are heavily penalized.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Hull Penetration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST634 - SUBMARINES AND SUBMERSIBLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pressure Hull Penetration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Pressure Hull Penetration** is a controlled, engineered aperture (opening) cut through the pressure hull plating of a submarine or submersible that allows the passage of mechanical, electrical, hydraulic, or optical systems between the external seawater environment and the internal pressure hull, while preserving the structural integrity and pressure boundary of the hull at the rated operating depth.

Each penetration acts as a **fittings-and-seal assembly** that is hydrostatically tested, certified, and integrated with the hull plating through full-penetration welds. The penetration becomes a permanent part of the pressure boundary and is engineered to withstand the hydrostatic collapse pressure corresponding to the design depth plus a **safety factor of 1.5** (typical).

> [!NOTE]
